i need to make the ingestion generalizable to differnt courses. from only uploading the slides. Good — this is the moment to lift the structure from "Computer Vision study vault" to "study vault that handles any course." The trick is replacing course-specific paths with placeholders that get filled in once per course, and adding a one-time setup step when a new course shows up. Let me think through what's actually course-specific vs. course-agnostic in your current setup, then give you the rewrite. ## What's coupled to Computer Vision right now Looking at your current vault and CLAUDE.md, the course-specific bits are: 1. **Hardcoded paths** — `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/...` appears in dozens of places 2. **Hardcoded filenames** — examples like `12_feature_detectors2.pdf`, `16_CNNs/slide-07.png` 3. **The example image embed paths** in the diagram rules 4. **Color/category palette suggestions** that assume CV content (conv, pool, activation) 5. **The implicit assumption** that there's exactly one course The *workflow* is course-agnostic — slide ingest → atomic concepts → flashcards → clusters → cheat sheets. That stays. The *wiring* needs parameterization. ## The design Two layers: **Layer 1 — Per-course manifest.** Each course gets a `_course.md` file at the root of its source folder that captures the course-specific bits: ```yaml --- course-code: Computer-Vision display-name: Computer Vision semester: Spring 2026 exam-date: 2026-04-29 exam-format: | 25 MCQ × 0.3 pts + 2 conceptual open × 0.75 pts + 1 algorithmic open × 1.0 pts Duration: 1h 30m diagram-categories: - conv: "#e3f2fd" - pool: "#fff3e0" - activation: "#f3e5f5" - loss: "#ffebee" --- ``` **Layer 2 — CLAUDE.md uses `{course}` placeholders everywhere.** Course-specific paths become `30-Sources/{course}/...` with the convention that `{course}` is substituted from the manifest. When you start a new course (say History 101), you: 1. Drop slides in `30-Sources/History-101/` 2. Create `30-Sources/History-101/_course.md` with the manifest 3. Tell Claude: "ingest the History 101 slides" — Claude reads the manifest, knows the course code, slots into the existing workflow. No CLAUDE.md changes per course. New courses cost ~2 minutes of setup. ## The rewritten CLAUDE.md This is a full rewrite. Save it over your existing one — the structure is preserved, just generalized. ```bash cat > "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/CLAUDE.md" << 'EOF' # Study Vault Instructions This vault holds study materials for multiple university courses. The structure and workflow are course-agnostic; per-course details live in each course's manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. ## Folders - `00-Inbox/` — raw captures, untriaged - `10-Courses/{course}/` — your work for the course (lecture notes, problem sets, syllabus) - `20-Concepts/` — atomic concept notes, **shared across all courses**, one idea per file, kebab-case filenames - `30-Sources/{course}/` — readings, papers, slides for that course - `40-Reviews/{course}/` — flashcards, study guides, clusters, practice mocks for that course - `99-Templates/` — Templater templates, prompt templates `{course}` is the course code, e.g. `Computer-Vision`, `History-101`. Use kebab-case, no spaces. ## Per-course manifest Each course has a manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` that captures course-specific details: ```yaml --- course-code: Computer-Vision # exact folder name used in paths display-name: Computer Vision # human-readable semester: Spring 2026 exam-date: 2026-04-29 exam-format: | Free-form description of the exam structure, marks, duration diagram-categories: # optional, for Mermaid color coding category-name: "#hex-color" --- # {Display Name} Notes about this course's quirks: prof's grading style, common pitfalls, abbreviations used in slides, etc. ``` **Always read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` before working on any artifact for that course.** It tells you the exam format, the prof's idiosyncrasies, and any course-specific conventions. If a course manifest doesn't exist when the user references a new course, ask the user for the exam format and basic course details, then create the manifest before proceeding. ## Concept notes are shared across courses `20-Concepts/` is a single shared library across all courses — concepts genuinely overlap (Bayes' theorem appears in stats, ML, and epistemology; gradient descent in optimization and deep learning). Frontmatter on every concept note must include a `courses:` array listing every course that touches the concept: ```yaml --- tags: [concept] courses: [Computer-Vision, Deep-Learning] sources: - course: Computer-Vision file: 16_CNNs.pdf - course: Deep-Learning file: 03_optimization.pdf created: YYYY-MM-DD --- ``` When ingesting from a new course's slides, **search 20-Concepts/ first**. If a concept exists, EXTEND it with this course's framing, add the course to the `courses:` array, and add the new source. Don't duplicate. ## Rules for note creation - **Atomic** — one concept per file in `20-Concepts/` - **Search before creating** — extend or link, don't duplicate - **Link aggressively** with `[[wikilinks]]`; orphans are failures - **YAML frontmatter required** on every note - **Never invent facts.** If a source doesn't say it, don't write it. - When extracting from `30-Sources/`, cite the exact source filename in the note ## When generating flashcards Format: **multi-line Spaced Repetition** — question on its own line, then `?` alone on the next line, then the answer. Cards separated by a blank line: ``` question text ? answer text ``` Rules: - Do NOT use the older `Q:: / A::` inline format - Atomic recall, not paragraph dumps - Equations stay as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display); preserve verbatim - If a card has an existing `` review-history comment, place it on the line directly after the answer - Save to `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` - Frontmatter: `tags: [flashcards, {course}]` ## Style - Plain language. Define jargon on first use. - Equations as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display). --- ## Slide ingest workflow When the user asks to ingest a lecture slide deck: **Pre-flight (every time):** 1. Identify the course from the user's request or the slide path. Read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. If it doesn't exist, ask for course details and create it. 2. Confirm the slides are PDF + slides-png pre-rendered. If not, run `bin/ingest-slides.sh {course}` first. **Per-slide-deck:** 1. Read the PDF in `30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{NN}_{topic}.pdf` 2. Create ONE lecture note in `10-Courses/{course}/lectures/lecture-{NN}-{topic}.md` - Frontmatter: ```yaml tags: [lecture] course: {course} source: {pdf-filename} lecture: NN slides: "[[30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{pdf-filename}|Open slides ↗]]" created: YYYY-MM-DD ``` - The `slides` value is a vault-root wikilink so Obsidian renders it as a clickable "Open slides ↗" link. Use the exact source filename including any `(1)` suffix and the `.pdf` extension. - Sections: Overview, Key concepts (linked), Equations, Diagrams, Open questions 3. Extract atomic concepts to `20-Concepts/{kebab-case-name}.md` - One concept per file - Frontmatter must include: `courses: [{course}]`, source entry referencing `{pdf-filename}`, `created: YYYY-MM-DD` - SEARCH `20-Concepts/` first; if a concept exists, EXTEND it — don't duplicate. Add this course to its `courses:` array if missing, and add the new source. 4. Create flashcards in `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` (multi-line `?` format) - Atomic recall pairs, exam-oriented - Cover definitions, when-to-use, common pitfalls - 15–25 cards per lecture Show me a summary of what you'll create BEFORE writing any files. Wait for "go". --- ## Diagrams: Mermaid first, always **Default to Mermaid.** Every diagram in a slide must first be attempted as Mermaid. Image embeds are a fallback, not a convenience. ### Process for every diagram 1. Identify what the diagram represents: architecture, flow, hierarchy, state, sequence, comparison, or visualization? 2. Pick the Mermaid type: - Sequential pipelines, network architectures, layer stacks → `flowchart LR` or `flowchart TD` - Algorithms, training loops, data pipelines → `flowchart TD` - Class hierarchies, model genealogies → `classDiagram` or `flowchart` - State machines, training phases → `stateDiagram-v2` - Forward/backward sequences → `sequenceDiagram` or `flowchart` - Comparisons → markdown table, never an image 3. Write the Mermaid using actual labels and dimensions from the slide — not generic "Layer 1." If the slide says "Conv 3×3, 64 filters → ReLU → MaxPool 2×2", the Mermaid nodes say exactly that. 4. Embed an image only if the content matches the allowlist below. ### Image embed allowlist (narrow) Only permitted for: - Pixel-level visualizations (filter kernels, learned weights, feature map activations) - Photographic examples (sample images, real photos demonstrating an effect) - Plotted graphs with specific data (loss curves, accuracy plots — where shape/values matter) - Spatial geometry that loses meaning when abstracted (receptive fields on real images, bounding box examples) - Mathematical visualizations on real data (Fourier spectrum images, frequency domain plots) If it's not on this list, it must be Mermaid. "It would be complex" is not a reason. ">10 nodes" is not a reason — Mermaid handles 40+ nodes fine. ### Forbidden - NEVER describe a diagram in prose ("the figure shows three boxes connected by arrows…") - NEVER embed a slide image as a substitute for a diagram you could Mermaid - NEVER skip a diagram silently — if you can't Mermaid it and it's not on the allowlist, ask ### Mermaid quality bar - Use real labels from the slide, including dimensions (e.g. `224×224×3`) - Group related nodes with `subgraph` when the slide groups them - Add edge labels for non-obvious transitions: `-->|"stride 2"|` - For repeated blocks, use one subgraph + a note about repetition rather than copy-pasting - Split diagrams exceeding ~40 nodes into two views ### Course-specific diagram styling If the course manifest defines `diagram-categories`, use those colors via `classDef`. Otherwise use a calm default palette (avoid neon). ### Image sources Two pre-extracted sources per lecture: - `30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png` — full slide screenshots. Default when an image is needed. - `30-Sources/{course}/images/{lecture-name}/img-NNN.png` — extracted bitmaps. Use only when an isolated clean figure is materially better than the full slide. ### Image embed format ```markdown ![[30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png]] *Figure: {what it shows} (slide N).* ``` Use vault-root-absolute paths (no `../../` walks). Always caption with what it shows + slide number. --- ## Mehrmaid for markdown-rich diagrams For diagrams where node labels need full markdown (LaTeX, tables, internal links, multi-line lists), use ```` ```mehrmaid ```` instead of ```` ```mermaid ````. Same syntax; node content wrapped in `("...")` is rendered as Obsidian markdown. Default to plain `mermaid`. Reach for `mehrmaid` only when the content genuinely needs markdown features. Don't use it to paper over bad diagram structure — restructure first. ### Mermaid syntax constraints (forbidden inside `mermaid` blocks) These break rendering: - `>` blockquote lines - `**bold**`, `_italic_`, `==highlight==` - `- bullet` or `* bullet` lines - `# header` lines - `` (use `%% Mermaid comments` instead) - Raw `$LaTeX$` inside unquoted node labels For emphasis, use HTML inside quoted labels: `A["bold"]`. For line breaks: `A["line one
line two"]`. --- ## Answering study questions When the user asks a conceptual or content question, **explain it clearly first** — read the relevant lecture and concept notes (and the underlying PDF if needed), then teach the concept in flowing prose. **Format:** - Write naturally, like teaching a peer studying for an exam. Full sentences, normal paragraph flow. - Use markdown freely where it helps: **bold** for key terms, *italics* for emphasis or asides, equations in `$...$` and `$$...$$`, numbered/bulleted lists for steps, Mermaid diagrams for flows. - **No `> blockquote` formatting in the body** — it fragments the flow. **Inline source citations — must be clickable links with `#page=N`, not plain text:** Bracketed plain text is broken — Markdown renders it as nothing. Every slide citation must be a real Markdown link with a `#page=N` fragment. **Wrong:** `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]` **Right:** `[slide 7](30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/10_fourier_transform2.pdf#page=7)` **Path rules** depend on where the link lives: - Lecture notes (`10-Courses/{course}/lectures/`) → `../../30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N` - Concept notes (`20-Concepts/`) → `../30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N` - Cluster files (`40-Reviews/{course}/clusters/{NN-name}/`) → `../../../30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N` - Chat output → vault-root-absolute: `30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{filename}.pdf#page=N` **Citation patterns:** - Single slide: `[slide 14](30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{file}.pdf#page=14)` - Slide range: `[slides 14–17](30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{file}.pdf#page=14)` - Multiple slides: stack — `[slide 14](path#page=14) [slide 22](path#page=22)` - Synthesis across many slides: `[synthesis of slides 14–22](path#page=14)` - Lecture note section: `[[lecture-12-feature-detectors-2#Hough Transform]]` (wikilink, no `#page=`) - Concept note: `[[hough-transform]]` (wikilink) **Compact text preferred.** Use `[slide 7]` as visible text when the lecture is clear from context. Use the long form `[slide 7 of 10_fourier_transform2.pdf]` only when citing across multiple lectures and the filename is needed for disambiguation. **Filename rules:** - Use the EXACT PDF filename, including any `(1)` suffix - Don't strip `.pdf`, don't URL-encode parentheses - Don't invent slide numbers — verify by checking the matching slide PNG if uncertain **Always prefer slide references** over concept-note references when possible — slides are the original source. Concept notes were derived from slides. If a claim has no source support, mark it inline as `[not in source]`. Never invent specifics. **End every grounded answer with three short sections:** 1. **The source's exact framing** — short paragraph in *italics* (no blockquote), capturing how the lecture phrases the concept. End with a clickable slide link. 2. **Remember this** — one-line atomic fact for flashcards. 3. **Beyond the lecture** — what you added that wasn't in the source, plain prose. Skip if nothing. **Hard rules:** - Stay grounded — vague hand-waves are fine, fabricated specifics aren't. - For exam prep, lead with the lecture's framing. Flag textbook discrepancies. - Diagrams welcome — use Mermaid per the rules above. --- ## Templates - `99-Templates/concept.md` — atomic concept stub for in-class capture - `99-Templates/lecture.md` — lecture stub for in-class capture - `99-Templates/grounded-question.md` — prompt template for source-grounded explanations - `99-Templates/generate-mock.md` — prompt template for generating practice exams These are live-capture stubs, intentionally lightweight. They are NOT the structure for slide-ingest output — slide ingest produces a richer post-hoc artifact (Overview / Key concepts / Equations / Diagrams / Open questions). EOF ``` ## Set up the Computer Vision manifest ```bash cat > "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/Computer-Vision/_course.md" << 'EOF' --- course-code: Computer-Vision display-name: Computer Vision semester: Spring 2026 exam-date: 2026-04-29 exam-format: | 25 MCQ × 0.3 pts + 2 conceptual open × 0.75 pts + 1 algorithmic open × 1.0 pts = 10.0 pts total Duration: 1h 30m Format references: 40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md and exam-style-guide.md diagram-categories: conv: "#e3f2fd" pool: "#fff3e0" activation: "#f3e5f5" loss: "#ffebee" --- # Computer Vision ## About this course Lectures 10–20 covering Fourier-domain image analysis through to autoencoders. ## Conventions - Lecture notes use the verbatim slide filenames (some have `(1)` suffixes — preserve them) - Concept notes that overlap with future ML/DL courses should add those courses to `courses:` when those decks land - The prof grades open-ended questions strictly on lecture wording; match the lecture's framing over textbook framing ## Quirks - Slide PNGs map 1-to-1 with PDF page numbers (no title-page offset to worry about) EOF ``` ## Generalize the ingest script Your current `bin/ingest-slides.sh` is already course-parameterized — you pass the course name as `$1`. Verify it still works: ```bash cat "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/bin/ingest-slides.sh" ``` Should still work without changes. To use for a new course: ```bash # After dropping slides into 30-Sources/History-101/ ./bin/ingest-slides.sh History-101 ``` That converts PPTX → PDF and PDF → slide PNGs for the new course, in the same structure. ## Adding a new course end-to-end When a new course shows up: ```bash # 1. Create the course folder mkdir -p "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/History-101" # 2. Drop PPTX slides into that folder # 3. Convert to PDF + PNG cd "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School" ./bin/ingest-slides.sh History-101 # 4. Create the manifest $EDITOR "/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/30-Sources/History-101/_course.md" # Fill in course-code, display-name, semester, exam-date, exam-format ``` Then in Agent Client: > Ingest the History 101 lectures. Read `30-Sources/History-101/_course.md` first to load course context, then process every PDF in `30-Sources/History-101/pdf/` per the slide ingest workflow. Claude reads the manifest, knows what course it's working on, slots into the existing structure. No CLAUDE.md edits. ## Concept notes get more interesting The shared `20-Concepts/` library is the killer feature once you have multiple courses. Concepts that appear in multiple courses get extended rather than duplicated. Their `courses:` array grows. The graph view connects across courses automatically. For the Computer Vision concepts you've already built, retrofitting the new frontmatter format is one prompt: > For every file in `20-Concepts/`, ensure the frontmatter has a `courses:` array (currently most have `courses: [Computer-Vision]` or just `course: Computer-Vision`). Standardize to `courses: [Computer-Vision]` as a list. Also ensure each has a `sources:` list with at least the originating Computer Vision PDF. > > Show me a sample of three files before applying. After "go", apply to all. ## A few things to watch for **The cluster pattern is course-specific.** Your `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/clusters/` is a great structure but it's tied to that course's exam blueprint. When a new course lands, it gets its own `40-Reviews/{new-course}/clusters/` after you've built that course's exam blueprint. The clusters aren't a shared resource the way concepts are. **Don't try to share flashcards across courses.** Each course's `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-NN.md` is course-specific because the questions are framed for that course's exam. If a concept is genuinely shared (gradient descent), the *concept note* gets the cross-course frontmatter, but the flashcards drilling it for one course's exam stay in that course's review folder. **The CLAUDE.md is now a contract.** When it says `30-Sources/{course}/...` everywhere, you can't have one course where the slides live in `Sources/CV/` instead. Stick to the convention. If a course has a weird wrinkle (different folder names, weird PDF page-to-slide mapping), capture it in that course's `_course.md`, not by deviating from the structure. ## Verify it works After you replace CLAUDE.md and create the CV manifest, test with a small ask: > Read `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/_course.md` and tell me back: course code, exam date, exam format, and the diagram color for "conv" category. If it reads the manifest correctly and answers, the wiring works. Then the real test — pretend a new course is starting: > I'm starting a new course called "Linear Algebra" with course code `Linear-Algebra`. Walk me through what I need to do to set it up before ingesting any slides. The answer should reference the manifest, the folder structure, and the ingest script. If it does, you're set up for as many courses as you want to add.
The ingest script is already course-parameterized. The two changes needed are: rewrite CLAUDE.md with `{course}` placeholders, and create the Computer Vision manifest.

# Study Vault Instructions This vault holds study materials for multiple university courses. The structure and workflow are course-agnostic; per-course details live in each course's manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. ## Folders - `00-Inbox/` — raw captures, untriaged - `10-Courses/{course}/` — your work for the course (lecture notes, problem sets, syllabus) - `20-Concepts/` — atomic concept notes, **shared across all courses**, one idea per file, kebab-case filenames - `30-Sources/{course}/` — readings, papers, slides for that course - `40-Reviews/{course}/` — flashcards, study guides, clusters, practice mocks for that course - `99-Templates/` — Templater templates, prompt templates `{course}` is the course code, e.g. `Computer-Vision`, `History-101`. Use kebab-case, no spaces. ## Per-course manifest Each course has a manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` that captures course-specific details: ```yaml --- course-code: Computer-Vision # exact folder name used in paths display-name: Computer Vision # human-readable semester: Spring 2026 exam-date: 2026-04-29 exam-format: | Free-form description of the exam structure, marks, duration diagram-categories: # optional, for Mermaid color coding category-name: "#hex-color" --- # {Display Name} Notes about this course's quirks: prof's grading style, common pitfalls, abbreviations used in slides, etc. ``` **Always read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` before working on any artifact for that course.** It tells you the exam format, the prof's idiosyncrasies, and any course-specific conventions. If a course manifest doesn't exist when the user references a new course, ask the user for the exam format and basic course details, then create the manifest before proceeding. ## Concept notes are shared across courses `20-Concepts/` is a single shared library across all courses — concepts genuinely overlap (Bayes' theorem appears in stats, ML, and epistemology; gradient descent in optimization and deep learning). Frontmatter on every concept note must include a `courses:` array listing every course that touches the concept: ```yaml --- tags: [concept] courses: [Computer-Vision, Deep-Learning] sources: - course: Computer-Vision file: 16_CNNs.pdf - course: Deep-Learning file: 03_optimization.pdf created: YYYY-MM-DD --- ``` When ingesting from a new course's slides, **search 20-Concepts/ first**. If a concept exists, EXTEND it with this course's framing, add the course to the `courses:` array, and add the new source. Don't duplicate. ## Rules for note creation - **Atomic** — one concept per file in `20-Concepts/` - **Search before creating** — extend or link, don't duplicate - **Link aggressively** with `[[wikilinks]]`; orphans are failures - **YAML frontmatter required** on every note - **Never invent facts.** If a source doesn't say it, don't write it. - When extracting from `30-Sources/`, cite the exact source filename in the note ## When generating flashcards Format: **multi-line Spaced Repetition** — question on its own line, then `?` alone on the next line, then the answer. Cards separated by a blank line: ``` question text ? answer text ``` Rules: - Do NOT use the older `Q:: / A::` inline format - Atomic recall, not paragraph dumps - Equations stay as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display); preserve verbatim - If a card has an existing `` review-history comment, place it on the line directly after the answer - Save to `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` - Frontmatter: `tags: [flashcards, {course}]` ## Style - Plain language. Define jargon on first use. - Equations as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display). --- ## Slide ingest workflow When the user asks to ingest a lecture slide deck: **Pre-flight (every time):** 1. Identify the course from the user's request or the slide path. Read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. If it doesn't exist, ask for course details and create it. 2. Confirm the slides are PDF + slides-png pre-rendered. If not, run `bin/ingest-slides.sh {course}` first. **Per-slide-deck:** 1. Read the PDF in `30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{NN}_{topic}.pdf` 2. Create ONE lecture note in `10-Courses/{course}/lectures/lecture-{NN}-{topic}.md` - Frontmatter: ```yaml tags: [lecture] course: {course} source: {pdf-filename} lecture: NN slides: "[[30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{pdf-filename}|Open slides ↗]]" created: YYYY-MM-DD ``` - The `slides` value is a vault-root wikilink so Obsidian renders it as a clickable "Open slides ↗" link. Use the exact source filename including any `(1)` suffix and the `.pdf` extension. - Sections: Overview, Key concepts (linked), Equations, Diagrams, Open questions 3. Extract atomic concepts to `20-Concepts/{kebab-case-name}.md` - One concept per file - Frontmatter must include: `courses: [{course}]`, source entry referencing `{pdf-filename}`, `created: YYYY-MM-DD` - SEARCH `20-Concepts/` first; if a concept exists, EXTEND it — don't duplicate. Add this course to its `courses:` array if missing, and add the new source. 4. Create flashcards in `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` (multi-line `?` format) - Atomic recall pairs, exam-oriented - Cover definitions, when-to-use, common pitfalls - 15–25 cards per lecture Show me a summary of what you'll create BEFORE writing any files. Wait for "go". --- ## Diagrams: Mermaid first, always **Default to Mermaid.** Every diagram in a slide must first be attempted as Mermaid. Image embeds are a fallback, not a convenience. ### Process for every diagram 1. Identify what the diagram represents: architecture, flow, hierarchy, state, sequence, comparison, or visualization? 2. Pick the Mermaid type: - Sequential pipelines, network architectures, layer stacks → `flowchart LR` or `flowchart TD` - Algorithms, training loops, data pipelines → `flowchart TD` - Class hierarchies, model genealogies → `classDiagram` or `flowchart` - State machines, training phases → `stateDiagram-v2` - Forward/backward sequences → `sequenceDiagram` or `flowchart` - Comparisons → markdown table, never an image 3. Write the Mermaid using actual labels and dimensions from the slide — not generic "Layer 1." If the slide says "Conv 3×3, 64 filters → ReLU → MaxPool 2×2", the Mermaid nodes say exactly that. 4. Embed an image only if the content matches the allowlist below. ### Image embed allowlist (narrow) Only permitted for: - Pixel-level visualizations (filter kernels, learned weights, feature map activations) - Photographic examples (sample images, real photos demonstrating an effect) - Plotted graphs with specific data (loss curves, accuracy plots — where shape/values matter) - Spatial geometry that loses meaning when abstracted (receptive fields on real images, bounding box examples) - Mathematical visualizations on real data (Fourier spectrum images, frequency domain plots) If it's not on this list, it must be Mermaid. "It would be complex" is not a reason. ">10 nodes" is not a reason — Mermaid handles 40+ nodes fine. ### Forbidden - NEVER describe a diagram in prose ("the figure shows three boxes connected by arrows…") - NEVER embed a slide image as a substitute for a diagram you could Mermaid - NEVER skip a diagram silently — if you can't Mermaid it and it's not on the allowlist, ask ### Mermaid quality bar - Use real labels from the slide, including dimensions (e.g. `224×224×3`) - Group related nodes with `subgraph` when the slide groups them - Add edge labels for non-obvious transitions: `-->|"stride 2"|` - For repeated blocks, use one subgraph + a note about repetition rather than copy-pasting - Split diagrams exceeding ~40 nodes into two views - **Direction:** prefer `flowchart LR` for sequential pipelines (data → model → output), `flowchart TD` for hierarchies and decision flows. Match the slide's natural reading direction. - **Use subgraphs to chunk visual complexity.** Group related layers (e.g. "Encoder", "Decoder", "Classifier head") into named subgraphs with `subgraph Name [Display Label]`. - **Edge labels only when meaningful.** `-->|"stride 2"|` is useful; `-->|"then"|` is noise. - **Node shape signals type.** Use: - `[Rectangle]` for layers / operations - `([Stadium])` for inputs/outputs - `{Diamond}` for decisions or branching - `[(Cylinder)]` for data stores / datasets - `((Circle))` for sums / merge points (e.g. ResNet skip-add) - **One concept per diagram.** If a slide tries to show training loop + architecture + loss in one figure, split into 2-3 Mermaid blocks each focused on one aspect. - **Annotate dimensions on the edge, not the node**, when feasible: `Conv1 -->|"112×112×64"| Pool1`. Keeps node labels short. - **Skip connections with explicit merge nodes:** for ResNet-style skips, draw `Input --> Conv1 --> Conv2 --> Add((+)); Input --> Add; Add --> Output`. Don't fake skips with curved arrows — they don't render predictably. ### Course-specific diagram styling If the course manifest defines `diagram-categories`, use those colors via `classDef`. Otherwise use a calm default palette (avoid neon). ### Image sources Two pre-extracted sources per lecture: - `30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png` — full slide screenshots. Default when an image is needed. - `30-Sources/{course}/images/{lecture-name}/img-NNN.png` — extracted bitmaps. Use only when an isolated clean figure is materially better than the full slide. Indices do not correspond to slide order — identifying the right one requires opening the PDF. ### Image embed format ```markdown ![[30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png]] *Figure: {what it shows} (slide N).* ``` Use vault-root-absolute paths (no `../../` walks). Always caption with what it shows + slide number. ### Where diagrams go - Lecture notes: Mermaid liberally throughout. Equations as LaTeX. # Study Vault Instructions This vault holds study materials for multiple university courses. The structure and workflow are course-agnostic; per-course details live in each course's manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. ## Folders - `00-Inbox/` — raw captures, untriaged - `10-Courses/{course}/` — your work for the course (lecture notes, problem sets, syllabus) - `20-Concepts/` — atomic concept notes, **shared across all courses**, one idea per file, kebab-case filenames - `30-Sources/{course}/` — readings, papers, slides for that course - `40-Reviews/{course}/` — flashcards, study guides, clusters, practice mocks for that course - `99-Templates/` — Templater templates, prompt templates `{course}` is the course code, e.g. `Computer-Vision`, `History-101`. Use kebab-case, no spaces. ## Per-course manifest Each course has a manifest at `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` that captures course-specific details: ```yaml --- course-code: Computer-Vision # exact folder name used in paths display-name: Computer Vision # human-readable semester: Spring 2026 exam-date: 2026-04-29 exam-format: | Free-form description of the exam structure, marks, duration diagram-categories: # optional, for Mermaid color coding category-name: "#hex-color" --- # {Display Name} Notes about this course's quirks: prof's grading style, common pitfalls, abbreviations used in slides, etc. ``` **Always read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md` before working on any artifact for that course.** It tells you the exam format, the prof's idiosyncrasies, and any course-specific conventions. If a course manifest doesn't exist when the user references a new course, ask the user for the exam format and basic course details, then create the manifest before proceeding. ## Concept notes are shared across courses `20-Concepts/` is a single shared library across all courses — concepts genuinely overlap (Bayes' theorem appears in stats, ML, and epistemology; gradient descent in optimization and deep learning). Frontmatter on every concept note must include a `courses:` array listing every course that touches the concept: ```yaml --- tags: [concept] courses: [Computer-Vision, Deep-Learning] sources: - course: Computer-Vision file: 16_CNNs.pdf - course: Deep-Learning file: 03_optimization.pdf created: YYYY-MM-DD --- ``` When ingesting from a new course's slides, **search 20-Concepts/ first**. If a concept exists, EXTEND it with this course's framing, add the course to the `courses:` array, and add the new source. Don't duplicate. ## Rules for note creation - **Atomic** — one concept per file in `20-Concepts/` - **Search before creating** — extend or link, don't duplicate - **Link aggressively** with `[[wikilinks]]`; orphans are failures - **YAML frontmatter required** on every note - **Never invent facts.** If a source doesn't say it, don't write it. - When extracting from `30-Sources/`, cite the exact source filename in the note ## When generating flashcards Format: **multi-line Spaced Repetition** — question on its own line, then `?` alone on the next line, then the answer. Cards separated by a blank line: ``` question text ? answer text ``` Rules: - Do NOT use the older `Q:: / A::` inline format - Atomic recall, not paragraph dumps - Equations stay as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display); preserve verbatim - If a card has an existing `` review-history comment, place it on the line directly after the answer - Save to `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` - Frontmatter: `tags: [flashcards, {course}]` ## Style - Plain language. Define jargon on first use. - Equations as LaTeX (`$...$` inline, `$$...$$` display). --- ## Slide ingest workflow When the user asks to ingest a lecture slide deck: **Pre-flight (every time):** 1. Identify the course from the user's request or the slide path. Read `30-Sources/{course}/_course.md`. If it doesn't exist, ask for course details and create it. 2. Confirm the slides are PDF + slides-png pre-rendered. If not, run `bin/ingest-slides.sh {course}` first. **Per-slide-deck:** 1. Read the PDF in `30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{NN}_{topic}.pdf` 2. Create ONE lecture note in `10-Courses/{course}/lectures/lecture-{NN}-{topic}.md` - Frontmatter: ```yaml tags: [lecture] course: {course} source: {pdf-filename} lecture: NN slides: "[[30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{pdf-filename}|Open slides ↗]]" created: YYYY-MM-DD ``` - The `slides` value is a vault-root wikilink so Obsidian renders it as a clickable "Open slides ↗" link. Use the exact source filename including any `(1)` suffix and the `.pdf` extension. - Sections: Overview, Key concepts (linked), Equations, Diagrams, Open questions 3. Extract atomic concepts to `20-Concepts/{kebab-case-name}.md` - One concept per file - Frontmatter must include: `courses: [{course}]`, source entry referencing `{pdf-filename}`, `created: YYYY-MM-DD` - SEARCH `20-Concepts/` first; if a concept exists, EXTEND it — don't duplicate. Add this course to its `courses:` array if missing, and add the new source. 4. Create flashcards in `40-Reviews/{course}/flashcards/lecture-{NN}.md` (multi-line `?` format) - Atomic recall pairs, exam-oriented - Cover definitions, when-to-use, common pitfalls - 15–25 cards per lecture Show me a summary of what you'll create BEFORE writing any files. Wait for "go". --- ## Diagrams: Mermaid first, always **Default to Mermaid.** Every diagram in a slide must first be attempted as Mermaid. Image embeds are a fallback, not a convenience. ### Process for every diagram 1. Identify what the diagram represents: architecture, flow, hierarchy, state, sequence, comparison, or visualization? 2. Pick the Mermaid type: - Sequential pipelines, network architectures, layer stacks → `flowchart LR` or `flowchart TD` - Algorithms, training loops, data pipelines → `flowchart TD` - Class hierarchies, model genealogies → `classDiagram` or `flowchart` - State machines, training phases → `stateDiagram-v2` - Forward/backward sequences → `sequenceDiagram` or `flowchart` - Comparisons → markdown table, never an image 3. Write the Mermaid using actual labels and dimensions from the slide — not generic "Layer 1." If the slide says "Conv 3×3, 64 filters → ReLU → MaxPool 2×2", the Mermaid nodes say exactly that. 4. Embed an image only if the content matches the allowlist below. ### Image embed allowlist (narrow) Only permitted for: - Pixel-level visualizations (filter kernels, learned weights, feature map activations) - Photographic examples (sample images, real photos demonstrating an effect) - Plotted graphs with specific data (loss curves, accuracy plots — where shape/values matter) - Spatial geometry that loses meaning when abstracted (receptive fields on real images, bounding box examples) - Mathematical visualizations on real data (Fourier spectrum images, frequency domain plots) If it's not on this list, it must be Mermaid. "It would be complex" is not a reason. ">10 nodes" is not a reason — Mermaid handles 40+ nodes fine. ### Forbidden - NEVER describe a diagram in prose ("the figure shows three boxes connected by arrows…") - NEVER embed a slide image as a substitute for a diagram you could Mermaid - NEVER skip a diagram silently — if you can't Mermaid it and it's not on the allowlist, ask ### Mermaid quality bar - Use real labels from the slide, including dimensions (e.g. `224×224×3`) - Group related nodes with `subgraph` when the slide groups them - Add edge labels for non-obvious transitions: `-->|"stride 2"|` - For repeated blocks, use one subgraph + a note about repetition rather than copy-pasting - Split diagrams exceeding ~40 nodes into two views - **Direction:** prefer `flowchart LR` for sequential pipelines (data → model → output), `flowchart TD` for hierarchies and decision flows. Match the slide's natural reading direction. - **Use subgraphs to chunk visual complexity.** Group related layers (e.g. "Encoder", "Decoder", "Classifier head") into named subgraphs with `subgraph Name [Display Label]`. - **Edge labels only when meaningful.** `-->|"stride 2"|` is useful; `-->|"then"|` is noise. - **Node shape signals type.** Use: - `[Rectangle]` for layers / operations - `([Stadium])` for inputs/outputs - `{Diamond}` for decisions or branching - `[(Cylinder)]` for data stores / datasets - `((Circle))` for sums / merge points (e.g. ResNet skip-add) - **One concept per diagram.** If a slide tries to show training loop + architecture + loss in one figure, split into 2-3 Mermaid blocks each focused on one aspect. - **Annotate dimensions on the edge, not the node**, when feasible: `Conv1 -->|"112×112×64"| Pool1`. Keeps node labels short. - **Skip connections with explicit merge nodes:** for ResNet-style skips, draw `Input --> Conv1 --> Conv2 --> Add((+)); Input --> Add; Add --> Output`. Don't fake skips with curved arrows — they don't render predictably. ### Course-specific diagram styling If the course manifest defines `diagram-categories`, use those colors via `classDef`. Otherwise use a calm default palette (avoid neon). ### Image sources Two pre-extracted sources per lecture: - `30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png` — full slide screenshots. Default when an image is needed. - `30-Sources/{course}/images/{lecture-name}/img-NNN.png` — extracted bitmaps. Use only when an isolated clean figure is materially better than the full slide. Indices do not correspond to slide order — identifying the right one requires opening the PDF. ### Image embed format ```markdown ![[30-Sources/{course}/slides-png/{lecture-name}/slide-NN.png]] *Figure: {what it shows} (slide N).* ``` Use vault-root-absolute paths (no `../../` walks). Always caption with what it shows + slide number. ### Where diagrams go - Lecture notes: Mermaid liberally throughout. Equations as LaTeX. Images sparingly, only per the allowlist. - Concept notes: at most one diagram per note. Mermaid strongly preferred. Image only when the concept is fundamentally visual (e.g. "feature map" warrants an image; "convolution operation" warrants Mermaid). --- ## Mehrmaid for markdown-rich diagrams For diagrams where node labels need full markdown (LaTeX, tables, internal links, multi-line lists), use ```` ```mehrmaid ```` instead of ```` ```mermaid ````. Same syntax; node content wrapped in `("...")` is rendered as Obsidian markdown. Default to plain `mermaid`. Reach for `mehrmaid` only when the content genuinely needs markdown features. Don't use it to paper over bad diagram structure — restructure first. ### Mermaid syntax constraints (forbidden inside `mermaid` blocks) These break rendering: - `>` blockquote lines - `**bold**`, `_italic_`, `==highlight==` - `- bullet` or `* bullet` lines - `# header` lines - `` (use `%% Mermaid comments` instead) - Raw `$LaTeX$` inside unquoted node labels For emphasis, use HTML inside quoted labels: `A["bold"]`. For line breaks: `A["line one
line two"]`. --- ## Templates vs. ingest output `99-Templates/lecture.md` and `99-Templates/concept.md` are live-capture stubs for in-class use — intentionally lightweight. content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf lecture: 02 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 02 — Data terminology in recommender systems ## Overview Defines the **three components** of every recommender — user, item, interaction — and walks through the kinds of data those interactions can produce (explicit vs implicit feedback, user/item features, knowledge-based data). Closes with the **cold-start problem** and the standard data-engineering moves for reco: transformations (count, weighted count, time decay, one-hot, binarize), **negative sampling** for implicit-only data, and **data splits** (random, stratified, chronological). ## Key concepts - [[reco-data-terminology]] — user, item, interaction - [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]] — pros/cons of each - [[cold-start-problem]] — cold users, cold items - [[negative-sampling-reco]] — generating negatives from implicit-only data - [[reco-data-splits]] — random, stratified, chronological - [[reco-data-transformations]] — count, weighted count, time decay, one-hot, binarize ## The three components (slide 1) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **user** | a customer who interacted with the company and to whom the system recommends products | | **item** | a product that can be recommended to a user | | **interaction / feedback** | a type of interaction between a user and an item | ## Feedback types (slides 2–4) ```mermaid flowchart TD Data[Reco data] --> Exp[Explicit feedback] Data --> Imp[Implicit feedback] Data --> Feat[User/item features] Data --> KB[Knowledge-based] Exp --> ER[Ratings] Exp --> ES[Survey results] Exp --> ERV[Reviews] Imp --> IC[Click / no click] Imp --> IP[Purchase / no purchase] Imp --> IE[Engagement time] classDef metric fill:#ffebee class Exp,Imp metric ``` **Explicit** ([slide 3](../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2002.pdf#page=3)): informative, can express negatives, but costly, biased, and may need calibration. **Implicit** ([slide 4](../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2002.pdf#page=4)): plentiful from logs, low statistical bias, but less graded and **negatives are hard to define**. ## Cold start (slide 7) - **Cold user** — too little interaction history to recommend accurately (new user, infrequent user). Example: buying a house. - **Cold item** — too few interactions to learn the item's profile. Example: new releases. ## Data transformations (slides 8–13) | Transform | What it does | |---|---| | **Count** | Count interactions between user and item — affinity = interaction count | | **Weighted count** | Count weighted by interaction type (view < click < buy) | | **Time decay** | Reduce relevance of older interactions; half-life parameter $T$ | | **Weighted time decay** | Combine the two | | **One-hot encoding** | Categorical → indicator vector (can increase sparsity) | | **Binarize** | Numerical → ordinal (e.g. rating ≥ 4 → 1) | ## Negative sampling (slide 14) Implicit feedback is positive-only. To train a discriminative model you must **generate negative interactions** by sampling unobserved user-item pairs. The risk: a sampled "negative" might actually be a positive the user simply hasn't seen. See [[negative-sampling-reco]]. ## Data splits (slides 15–18) ```mermaid flowchart LR D[(Full dataset)] --> R[Random split] D --> S[Stratified split] D --> C[Chronological split] R --> RU[risk: cold users in test] S --> SU[same users in train + test
avoids cold-start in eval] C --> CU[respects time
used when tastes drift] ``` - **Random** — easiest baseline, but can produce cold users in test. - **Stratified** — same set of users (or items) appears in both train and test → avoids artificial coldness during evaluation. Example: movie recommendation. - **Chronological** — split by timestamp; required when preferences drift (fashion). ## Equations Time-decay weight, with half-life $T$: $$w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$$ An interaction $T$ time units before $t_0$ gets half the weight of one at $t_0$. ## Open questions - What's the right negative-sampling ratio? (Lecture defers — comes up again in [[lecture-09-bpr]].) - How do you choose $T$ for time decay? (Domain-dependent; tune on validation.)

content.newText:
---
name: Reco data terminology
description: The three components every recommender system models — user, item, interaction
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Reco data terminology

Every recommender system has **exactly three components**:

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **user** | the customer who interacted with the company; recommendations are made *to* the user |
| **item** | the product that *can be* recommended |
| **interaction / feedback** | one event linking a user and an item (purchase, click, rating, view…) |

If you can't identify all three in a system, it isn't a recommender — it's a search engine, ranker, or ad targeting system.

## Storage shape

A recommender's input is a (sparse) **interaction matrix** $R \in \mathbb{R}^{|U| \times |I|}$:

- Rows index users $u$
- Columns index items $i$
- Entries are interaction values (rating, count, binary signal); most entries are missing

This is the canonical "R matrix" used throughout collaborative filtering ([[collaborative-filtering]]).

## Related

- [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]] — what the entries of $R$ can be
- [[cold-start-problem]] — what happens when a row or column has too few entries
- [[collaborative-filtering]] — using $R$ across users

content.newText:
---
name: Explicit vs implicit feedback
description: Two types of recommender input data — direct ratings vs behavioral signals — with opposing pros and cons
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Explicit vs implicit feedback

The two ways recommenders learn what users like.

## Explicit feedback

User actively states a preference: numerical rating, survey, written review.

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| More informative, richer | Costly to obtain |
| Easier for algorithms to interpret | Inconvenient to users |
| **Can express negative feedback** | Biased toward certain users (only some bother) |
| | May need calibration (different users use scales differently) |

Examples: IMDB/Netflix star ratings, satisfaction surveys, written reviews.

## Implicit feedback

User behavior inferred to be a preference signal: clicks, purchases, dwell time, impressions.

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Plentiful — already in transaction logs | Less graded (often binary) |
| Not disruptive to users | **Hard to define negatives** — "no click" could mean "didn't see" or "saw and ignored" |
| Less statistical bias | Less personalised signal per event |

The negatives problem is the headline issue: implicit feedback is **positive-only**, so training a discriminative model requires [[negative-sampling-reco]], and ranking-aware losses like [[bpr-loss]] outperform pointwise classification on implicit data.

## When each fits

- **Explicit** when ratings are core to the product (IMDB, Netflix's old ★ ratings).
- **Implicit** when behavior is abundant and ratings would interrupt the UX (e-commerce clicks, news consumption).
- Most production systems blend both.

## Related

- [[reco-data-terminology]]
- [[negative-sampling-reco]]
- [[bpr-loss]]
- [[cold-start-problem]]

content.newText:
---
name: Cold-start problem
description: When a recommender lacks enough interaction history to recommend accurately for a user or item
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Cold-start problem

A recommender can't recommend well when it has too little signal. Two flavours:

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold user** | Too few prior interactions to estimate preferences | New customer; infrequent buyer of high-ticket items (a house) |
| **Cold item** | Too few interactions to learn the item's profile | New product release; one-of-a-kind catalog entry |

## Why it matters

Pure [[collaborative-filtering]] is **fundamentally broken on cold-start**: with no row entries (cold user) or no column entries (cold item) in the [[reco-data-terminology|interaction matrix]], the algorithm has nothing to correlate.

## Mitigations

- **[[content-based-filtering]]** — use user/item features (demographics, item metadata) so a brand-new item with no interactions still has a representation. **The textbook cold-start fix.**
- **Knowledge-based recommenders** — encode rules / constraints / cases (good for high-value, infrequent purchases like cars or houses).
- **Hybrid models** like **[[wide-and-deep]]** — combine collaborative and content signals so cold cases fall back gracefully.
- **Explicit onboarding** — ask new users a few questions to bootstrap a profile.

## Related

- [[collaborative-filtering]] — the algorithm class that suffers from cold-start
- [[content-based-filtering]] — handles cold-start
- [[reco-data-terminology]]

---
name: Cold-start problem
description: When a recommender lacks enough interaction history to recommend accurately for a user or item
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Cold-start problem

A recommender can't recommend well when it has too little signal. Two flavours:

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold user** | Too few prior interactions to estimate preferences | New customer; infrequent buyer of high-ticket items (a house) |
| **Cold item** | Too few interactions to learn the item's profile | New product release; one-of-a-kind catalog entry |

## Why it matters

Pure [[collaborative-filtering]] is **fundamentally broken on cold-start**: with no row entries (cold user) or no column entries (cold item) in the [[reco-data-terminology|interaction matrix]], the algorithm has nothing to correlate.

## Mitigations

- **[[content-based-filtering]]** — use user/item features (demographics, item metadata) so a brand-new item with no interactions still has a representation. **The textbook cold-start fix.**
- **Knowledge-based recommenders** — encode rules / constraints / cases (good for high-value, infrequent purchases like cars or houses).
- **Hybrid models** like **[[wide-and-deep]]** — combine collaborative and content signals so cold cases fall back gracefully.
- **Explicit onboarding** — ask new users a few questions to bootstrap a profile.

## Related

- [[collaborative-filtering]] — the algorithm class that suffers from cold-start
- [[content-based-filtering]] — handles cold-start
- [[reco-data-terminology]]

content.newText:
---
name: Negative sampling (recommenders)
description: Generating negative training examples from implicit-only feedback by sampling unobserved user-item pairs
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 09.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 10a.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Negative sampling (recommenders)

Implicit feedback (clicks, purchases) is **positive-only** — no explicit "I dislike X" signal. To train a discriminative or pairwise-ranking model you need negatives, so you **sample unobserved user-item pairs** as negatives.

## The procedure

For each observed positive $(u, i^+)$:

1. Sample $k$ items $i^-$ uniformly at random from items the user has *not* interacted with.
2. Train as if $(u, i^-)$ has label 0 (or as if user prefers $i^+$ over $i^-$).

Common choice: $k = 4$ to $10$ negatives per positive.

## The fundamental problem

A sampled "negative" can be one of two very different things:

- **True negative** — user saw it and rejected it.
- **Missing positive** — user simply hasn't been exposed to it yet.

Treating missing positives as negatives biases the model away from items the user might actually like — precisely the items you want to recommend.

## Mitigations

- **[[bpr-loss|Pairwise loss (BPR)]]** — only require that observed > unobserved, not that unobserved = 0. This is the headline fix.
- **Popularity-biased sampling** — popular items as negatives are more likely to be true negatives (the user saw them and didn't engage).
- **Hard negative mining** — sample items the model currently scores high but the user didn't interact with.

## Related

- [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]
- [[bpr-loss]] — the principled response to the negative-sampling problem
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — uses negative sampling with binary cross-entropy

content.newText:
---
name: Reco data splits
description: Three ways to partition a recommender dataset into train/test — random, stratified, chronological — each correct for a different evaluation question
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, evaluation]
---

# Reco data splits

How you split your reco dataset *defines what you're measuring*. Three standard options, each appropriate for a different evaluation question.

## Random split

Randomly assign each interaction to train or test.

- **Risk:** users with all interactions in test become artificially cold; you measure cold-start performance, not the metric you wanted.
- Useful as a baseline when the dataset is dense per user.

## Stratified split

Randomly split *within* each user (or item), so the same set of users appears in both train and test.

- **Avoids artificial cold users in evaluation.** This is what you want when measuring how well you recommend to *known* users.
- Can apply a minimum-interactions filter (e.g. keep only users with ≥ 5 events).
- **Default for movie-recommendation experiments.**

## Chronological split

Split by interaction timestamp: everything before $t_{\text{cutoff}}$ is train, everything after is test.

- Respects causality — the model never sees the future during training.
- **Required when preferences drift over time** (fashion, news, trending products).
- Closer to how the system performs in production.

## Decision rule

| Question you're asking | Use |
|---|---|
| "How well do I recommend to known users?" | Stratified |
| "How well will my deployed system perform tomorrow?" | Chronological |
| "Quick baseline on dense data" | Random |
| "How well do I handle cold users?" | Hold out *entire users* (a user-level split) |

## Related

- [[reco-data-terminology]]
- [[cold-start-problem]]

content.newText:
---
name: Reco data transformations
description: Standard preprocessing moves on raw user-item interactions — count, weighted count, time decay, one-hot, binarize
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Reco data transformations

Five recurring preprocessing moves to convert raw interaction logs into a usable affinity matrix.

| Transform | What it does | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Count** | $A_{ui} = \#$ interactions between user $u$ and item $i$ | Implicit feedback baseline |
| **Weighted count** | Same, weighted by interaction type ($w_\text{view} < w_\text{click} < w_\text{buy}$) | Multiple interaction types differ in strength |
| **Time decay** | $A_{ui} = \sum_t 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$ | Recent interactions matter more (most domains) |
| **Weighted time decay** | Both above combined | Both effects apply |
| **One-hot encoding** | Categorical → indicator vector | Categorical features for content-based models. **Increases sparsity.** |
| **Binarize** | Numerical → ordinal (e.g. rating ≥ 4 → 1) | Convert ratings to implicit-style positives |

## The half-life $T$

Time decay uses an exponential with parameter $T$:

$$w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$$

An interaction $T$ time units before $t_0$ gets **half the weight** of one happening at $t_0$. Choice of $T$ is domain-specific — fashion needs short half-life (weeks), durable goods need long (months/years).

## Related

- [[reco-data-terminology]]
- [[sar-algorithm]] — uses an affinity matrix derived from these transforms
- [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 02
source: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 02 — Data terminology (flashcards)

What three components are present in every recommender system?
?
**user**, **item**, and **interaction (feedback)**.

What does the "user" mean in reco terminology?
?
A customer who interacted with the company and to whom the system recommends products.

What does the "item" mean?
?
A product that can be recommended to a user.

What is "feedback" or "interaction" in reco?
?
A type of interaction between a user and an item (rating, click, purchase, view, etc.).

Name three forms of explicit feedback.
?
Numerical ratings (e.g. IMDB stars), survey results, and text reviews.

Name three forms of implicit feedback.
?
Clicks, purchases, and engagement time (e.g. dwell / visualization time).

What is the headline disadvantage of explicit feedback?
?
It's **costly to obtain**, inconvenient for users, and biased toward users willing to leave ratings.

What is the headline disadvantage of implicit feedback?
?
**Negatives are hard to define** — "no click" could mean "didn't see" or "saw and rejected".

What is a cold user?
?
A user who has not provided enough interaction data for accurate recommendations — usually new or infrequent.

What is a cold item?
?
An item that has too few interactions to make accurate recommendations — usually new releases or unique products.

What is the count transformation in reco data?
?
Count the interactions between each user and item to produce affinity scores.

What does time-decay weighting do?
?
Reduces the relevance of older interactions; with half-life $T$, an event $T$ units ago has half the weight of one happening now: $w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$.

Why is one-hot encoding tricky in reco?
?
It can make data even more sparse — and reco data is already extremely sparse.

What is negative sampling and why is it needed?
?
For implicit-only data we have no negative labels. We sample unobserved user-item pairs as negatives. **Risk:** a sampled negative might be a missing positive (item the user just hasn't seen).

What's the difference between a random and a stratified split?
?
**Random** assigns each interaction independently to train/test (risks cold users in test). **Stratified** ensures the same set of users (or items) appears in both train and test, eliminating artificial coldness.

When should you use a chronological split?
?
When user preferences drift over time (fashion, news, trends) — the model should never train on data from the future.

What's a typical use of a stratified split?
?
Movie recommendation — same users in train and test so the evaluation isn't dominated by cold-start.

What's a typical use of a chronological split?
?
Fashion recommendation — tastes shift over time, so you want to test on later events than training events.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf lecture: 03 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 03 — Collaborative vs content-based filtering ## Overview The two foundational families of recommender algorithms. - **Collaborative filtering (CF)** — correlate *behaviors* across users. "Jeremy loved A and B, Tao loved A, B, and C → Jeremy will probably love C." Uses only the [[reco-data-terminology|R matrix]]. - **Content-based filtering (CB)** — match users to items via *features* (demographics, item attributes, descriptions). Same-zip-code, same-age users get the same recommendations. CF dominates in dense-interaction domains (movies, e-commerce). CB is the textbook fix for [[cold-start-problem|cold-start]] — it doesn't need interaction history because items have features from day one. ## Key concepts - [[collaborative-filtering]] - [[content-based-filtering]] - [[cold-start-problem]] ## Side-by-side | | Collaborative filtering | Content-based filtering | |---|---|---| | **Inputs** | User-item interaction matrix only | User features + item features | | **Intuition** | "Users who behave alike will like alike" | "Users with similar profiles like similar items" | | **Strength** | Captures latent structure no one labelled | Works on day one for cold items/users | | **Weakness** | Fails on cold items/users | Limited to what features capture; less personalisation | | **Example signal** | Purchase history, ratings, clicks | Zip code, age, brand, genre, description | ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CF[Collaborative filtering] R[(R matrix:
users × items)] --> CFM[Model] --> CFR[Recommendations] end subgraph CB[Content-based filtering] UF[User features] --> CBM[Model] IF[Item features] --> CBM CBM --> CBR[Recommendations] end classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd class CFM,CBM reco ``` ## What "content" can be (slide 4) For content-based models, *content* can mean any of: - User features (location, age, gender, occupation) - Item features (price, brand, description) - Review comments (text) - Knowledge graph (related entities) - Multi-domain info (image, video) - Contextual info (time, device, location at time of interaction) ## Why CB mitigates cold start CF requires entries in the R matrix. A brand-new item has zero entries → CF can't represent it. CB only needs the item's **feature vector**, which exists from the moment the item enters the catalog. Same logic for cold users. ## Open questions - When do you choose CB over CF when both are feasible? (Lecture: CB if cold-start is the binding constraint OR if item features carry strong signal; otherwise CF wins on density.) - Hybrid models? See [[lecture-10b-wide-and-deep]].

content.newText:
---
name: Collaborative filtering
description: Recommend by correlating user behaviors — users with similar interaction patterns get similar recommendations
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Collaborative filtering

A recommender that uses **only** user-item interactions (no demographics, no item features). It correlates *behaviors* across users.

> **Intuition:** users who give similar ratings to the same items will have similar preferences, so we should recommend each one items that the other liked.

## Input

A sparse user-item interaction matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{|U| \times |I|}$:

| | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User A | 4 | ? | 5 | ? |
| User B | ? | 1 | ? | 5 |
| User C | ? | ? | 4 | 4 |

`?` = missing — the recommender's job is to predict those.

## Sub-families

| Type | Example | Captures |
|---|---|---|
| **User-based memory** | k-nearest-neighbor on user rows | "Users like you also bought …" |
| **Item-based memory** | [[sar-algorithm|SAR]] | "If you bought X, you probably want Y" |
| **Model-based** | [[matrix-factorization-reco]], [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] | Latent factors |
| **Graph-based** | [[graph-neural-networks]], LightGCN | High-order user-item connectivity |

## Pros

- Captures *latent* structure no one labelled (a movie's hidden "indie-ness" emerges from who watches it, even if the metadata never says so).
- Doesn't require any item or user attributes.

## Cons

- **[[cold-start-problem|Cold-start]]** — a user with no history or item with no interactions cannot be represented.
- Tends to recommend popular items (popularity bias).
- Sparse data hurts model quality.

## Related

- [[content-based-filtering]] — the alternative paradigm
- [[matrix-factorization-reco]]
- [[sar-algorithm]]
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[bpr-loss]]

> **Intuition:** users who give similar ratings to the same items will have similar preferences, so we should recommend each one items that the other liked.

## Input

| | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 |

| User A | 4 | ? | 5 | ? |
| User B | ? | 1 | ? | 5 |
| User C | ? | ? | 4 | 4 |

`?` = missing — the recommender's job is to predict those.

## Sub-families

| Type | Example | Captures |
|---|---|---|
| **User-based memory** | k-nearest-neighbor on user rows | "Users like you also bought …" |
| **Item-based memory** | [[sar-algorithm|SAR]] | "If you bought X, you probably want Y" |
| **Model-based** | [[matrix-factorization-reco]], [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] | Latent factors |
| **Graph-based** | [[graph-neural-networks]], LightGCN | High-order user-item connectivity |

## Pros

- Captures *latent* structure no one labelled (a movie's hidden "indie-ness" emerges from who watches it, even if the metadata never says so).
- Doesn't require any item or user attributes.

## Cons

- **[[cold-start-problem|Cold-start]]** — a user with no history or item with no interactions cannot be represented.
- Tends to recommend popular items (popularity bias).
- Sparse data hurts model quality.

## Related

- [[content-based-filtering]] — the alternative paradigm
- [[matrix-factorization-reco]]
- [[sar-algorithm]]
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[bpr-loss]]

content.newText:
---
name: Content-based filtering
description: Recommend by matching user features to item features — handles cold-start because it doesn't need interaction history
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Content-based filtering

A recommender that uses **user features and item features** to predict preferences. Doesn't need user-item interaction history at all (though it can use it as one more feature).

> **Intuition:** Nikhil is in zip code A, age group N, and bought item X. Le is in the same zip code and age group → Le is likely to like X.

## What "content" can be

- **User features:** location, age, gender, occupation, demographics
- **Item features:** price, brand, description, category
- **Review text** (sentiment, topics)
- **Knowledge graph** (relationships between entities)
- **Multi-domain** info (image, video, audio)
- **Contextual** info (time, device, location at time of interaction)

## Why it matters

CB is the textbook fix for **[[cold-start-problem|cold-start]]**. A brand-new item has zero interactions, so [[collaborative-filtering|CF]] can't represent it — but the item's **feature vector** exists from day one. Same for cold users.

## How it learns

Train a model (linear, MLP, or tree) on user features × item features → predicted affinity. At inference, score every (user, item) pair and rank.

## Pros / Cons

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Handles cold items and cold users | Limited to what features capture (no latent serendipity) |
| Easy to interpret (feature importances) | Needs good feature engineering |
| Doesn't need interaction history | Less personalised than CF on dense data |

## Hybrid

In practice, production systems blend both — see **[[wide-and-deep]]** for the canonical hybrid. The "wide" part memorises content cross-features; the "deep" part learns CF-style latent embeddings.

## Related

- [[collaborative-filtering]]
- [[cold-start-problem]]
- [[wide-and-deep]]
- [[reco-data-terminology]]

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 03
source: Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 03 — Collaborative vs content-based filtering (flashcards)

In one sentence, what is collaborative filtering?
?
Recommending by **correlating personal behaviors** across users — users with similar interaction patterns get similar recommendations.

In one sentence, what is content-based filtering?
?
Recommending by **understanding user and item profiles** (features) and matching users to items with similar features.

What is the headline weakness of collaborative filtering?
?
**Cold-start** — it cannot handle users or items with little/no interaction history.

What is the headline strength of content-based filtering compared to CF?
?
It **handles cold start** — a brand-new item already has its features even though it has no interactions yet.

What inputs does collaborative filtering rely on?
?
Only the **user-item interaction matrix** (purchases, ratings, clicks). No item or user attributes needed.

Give five examples of "content" in content-based filtering.
?
User features (age, location), item features (price, brand), review comments, knowledge graph, multi-domain information (image/video), contextual information (time, device).

Why does CF tend to recommend popular items?
?
Popular items have many interactions across many users, so they appear in many users' similarity neighborhoods → they get recommended often. (Popularity bias.)

Give the canonical CF example.
?
"If Jeremy loves A and B, and Tao loves A, B, and C, then Jeremy is more likely to love C."

Give the canonical CB example.
?
"Nikhil is in zip code A, age group N, and bought item X; Le is in the same area and age group, so Le is likely to like X."

Why is CB sometimes preferred over CF for high-value items (cars, houses)?
?
High-value items have very few interactions per user (cold-user case), so CF has nothing to correlate. CB can still work because user/item features remain available.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf lecture: 04 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 04 — Evaluating recommenders: offline vs online metrics ## Overview Two evaluation worlds. **Offline metrics** quantify model quality on held-out historical data — rating metrics (RMSE, MAE), ranking metrics (Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP@K, NDCG@K), and non-accuracy metrics (diversity, novelty, serendipity, coverage). **Online metrics** measure business impact in production — CTR, conversion rate, MAU, AOV, LTV — and are evaluated with **A/B tests**. Closes with three deployment **architectures**: batch, real-time, and 2-step (hybrid). ## Key concepts - [[reco-rating-metrics]] — RMSE, MAE, AUC, logloss - [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP@K, NDCG@K - [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — novelty, diversity, serendipity, coverage - [[reco-online-metrics]] — CTR, conversion, AOV, MAU, LTV - [[ab-test]] — comparing model A vs B in production - [[reco-architectures]] — batch, real-time, 2-step ## Offline vs online (slide 1) | | Offline | Online | |---|---|---| | **What it measures** | Model performance on held-out data | Business impact in production | | **Examples** | RMSE, NDCG, MAP | CTR, conversion rate, MAU, AOV, LTV | | **When to use** | Model selection, benchmarking | Final deployment decision | | **A/B tested?** | No — computed once on a fixed dataset | Yes — randomised live traffic | ## Rating metrics (slide 3) For predicted rating $\hat{r}_{ui}$ vs true rating $r_{ui}$: $$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{|K|} \sum_{(u,i)\in K}(\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui})^2}, \qquad \text{MAE} = \frac{1}{|K|} \sum_{(u,i)\in K}|\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui}|$$ - **RMSE** — penalises outliers more, popular default. - **MAE** — better estimator of the true average error. - **AUC** — discriminate positive vs negative classes. - **Logloss / cross-entropy** — heavily penalises confident wrong predictions. ## Ranking metrics (slide 4) These measure how relevant the **top-K** recommendations are. | Metric | What it measures | |---|---| | **Precision@K** | Of the K items recommended, what fraction are relevant? | | **Recall@K** | Of the relevant items, what fraction are in the top-K? | | **MAP@K** | Mean Average Precision — averaged precision per user, position-weighted | | **NDCG@K** | Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain — both *hit* of relevant items AND *correctness of ranking* | ## Diversity metrics (slide 7) Measure value beyond accuracy — important for catalog coverage and avoiding filter bubbles. | Metric | Meaning | |---|---| | **Novelty** | How novel are recommended items? (low recommendation frequency = novel) | | **Diversity** | How different are recommended items from each other? | | **Serendipity** | How surprising are recommendations vs the user's history? | | **Coverage** | What fraction of the catalog gets recommended? | ## Long tail (slide 6) Item distribution follows a **long tail** — a few items are very popular, most are not. Long-tail items can be highly profitable but suffer from cold-start. Models optimising rating accuracy (e.g. ALS) tend to recommend popular items → bad on diversity. ```mermaid flowchart TD Pop["Popular head
(few items, many interactions)"] Tail["Long tail
(many items, few interactions each)"] Pop --> ALS["ALS / accuracy-optimised
recommends mostly here"] Tail --> Random["Random / diversity-focused
covers more of these"] ``` ## Online metrics (slide 9) | Metric | Definition | |---|---| | **CTR** (click-through rate) | Clicks ÷ visits — user engagement | | **Conversion rate** | Conversions (e.g. purchases) ÷ visits | | **AOV** (Average Order Value) | Total revenue ÷ number of orders | | **MAU** (Monthly Active Users) | Unique users engaging in 30 days | | **LTV** (Lifetime Value) | Total revenue per user across their engagement; healthy benchmark: **LTV ≥ 3 × CAC** | ## A/B testing (slide 10) Randomise traffic into two groups: A gets the ML model, B gets a baseline (or random). Compare metrics. With more than two groups it's a **multi-variate test**. ## Architectures (slides 11–13) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Batch["Batch"] ADLS1[(ADLS Gen 2
GB-TB)] --> DB1[Databricks
train+score] --> Cosmos1[(CosmosDB
cache)] --> K8s1[Kubernetes
fetch] end subgraph RT["Real-time"] ADLS2[(ADLS Gen 2
MB-GB)] --> DB2[Databricks
features+train] --> ML[AzureML serve] --> K8s2[Kubernetes
score+rank] end subgraph TS["2-step / hybrid"] ADLS3[(ADLS Gen 2)] --> DB3[Databricks
train+score] --> Cosmos3[(CosmosDB
candidates)] --> ML2[AzureML rerank
100-1000 items] --> K8s3[Kubernetes] end ``` - **Batch** — large data, scores precomputed and cached. Cheap to serve, can't react to real-time signals. - **Real-time** — features and scoring at request time. Fresh, but expensive. - **2-step** — batch generates **candidates (high recall)**, real-time **reranks** them (high precision). Standard in industry. ## ALS vs random (slide 8) - ALS beats random on **ranking metrics** (it's optimised for rating accuracy). - Random beats ALS on **diversity metrics** (it ignores popularity bias). - Implication: pick metrics that match the business goal — accuracy-only models hurt the long tail. ## Open questions - How many users/duration do you need for an A/B test to be statistically powered? (Domain-specific; sample-size formulas live in stats books.) - How do you balance CTR (engagement) against LTV (long-term value)? (Hot debate; click-bait optimisation is a known failure mode.)

content.newText:
---
name: Reco rating metrics
description: Offline metrics that measure how accurately a recommender predicts numeric ratings — RMSE, MAE, R², AUC, logloss
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, evaluation]
---

# Reco rating metrics

Measure how accurately a recommender predicts a *numeric* rating. Use when you're predicting ratings (stars, scores) rather than ranking. **For ranking-style use cases see [[reco-ranking-metrics]] instead.**

## Regression metrics

For predicted $\hat r_{ui}$ vs true $r_{ui}$ over evaluation set $K$:

| Metric | Formula | Property |
|---|---|---|
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{|K|}\sum (\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui})^2}$ | More affected by outliers; popular default |
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{|K|}\sum |\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui}|$ | Better estimator of true average error |
| **R²** | proportion of variance explained | Overall fit |
| **Explained variance** | similar to R² | How much variance the model captures |

## Classification metrics (for binary preference)

| Metric | What it measures |
|---|---|
| **AUC** | Integral under ROC curve — ability to discriminate positives from negatives |
| **Logloss / cross-entropy** | $-\sum y \log \hat y + (1-y)\log(1-\hat y)$ — heavily penalises **confident wrong** predictions |

## When each is right

- **RMSE** when large errors are disproportionately bad (recommending a 1-star movie as 5-star is much worse than as 4-star).
- **MAE** when you want the *average* user-perceived error.
- **AUC** when the question is "ranks positives above negatives", regardless of absolute predicted value.
- **Logloss** when predicted *probabilities* matter (CTR prediction, calibrated outputs).

## Related

- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — for top-K recommendation
- [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — for catalog-coverage metrics
