content.newText:
---
name: Reco ranking metrics
description: Offline metrics for top-K recommendation — Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP@K, NDCG@K
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, evaluation]
---

# Reco ranking metrics

The most-used family of offline reco metrics — they answer *"how good is the top-K list I show the user?"*. **In most practical reco scenarios, ranking metrics matter more than [[reco-rating-metrics|rating metrics]]**, because users see a list, not a scalar.

## Definitions

For each user $u$, let:

- $R_u$ = top-K items the model recommends
- $T_u$ = items that are actually relevant for $u$ (held-out positives)

| Metric | Formula | What it asks |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision@K** | $\frac{|R_u \cap T_u|}{K}$ | Of the K recommended, how many were correct? |
| **Recall@K** | $\frac{|R_u \cap T_u|}{|T_u|}$ | Of all relevant items, how many did we surface in the top-K? |
| **MAP@K** | mean of average precisions across users | Position-weighted precision averaged over users |
| **NDCG@K** | DCG@K / IDCG@K | Both **hit** and **correct ranking** matter — discounts items further down the list |

## Average Precision

For one user with K recommendations:

$$\text{AP@K} = \frac{1}{|T_u|} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathrm{Precision@}k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{item at position } k \in T_u]$$

Hits earlier in the list contribute more (because the partial Precision@k is higher). Average over users → **MAP@K**.

## NDCG@K

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{\text{rel}_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}, \qquad \text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$$

where $\text{rel}_k$ is the relevance score at rank $k$, and IDCG is DCG of the ideal ordering. Because of the $\log_2(k+1)$ discount, **getting the first hit at rank 1 is much better than rank 5**, and the metric reflects that.

## Precision vs recall trade-off

- High **precision** = the top-K is mostly correct items, but you might miss many relevant items further down.
- High **recall** = you covered most relevant items, but had to recommend a lot of irrelevant ones to do so.
- For top-K recommendation lists with small K, **precision usually dominates the user experience.**

## Related

- [[reco-rating-metrics]] — when ratings are the prediction target
- [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — non-accuracy quality dimensions
- [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss aligned with these metrics

| Metric | Formula | What it asks |

Hits earlier in the list contribute more (because the partial Precision@k is higher). Average over users → **MAP@K**.

## NDCG@K

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{\text{rel}_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}, \qquad \text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$$

where $\text{rel}_k$ is the relevance score at rank $k$, and IDCG is DCG of the ideal ordering. Because of the $\log_2(k+1)$ discount, **getting the first hit at rank 1 is much better than rank 5**, and the metric reflects that.

## Precision vs recall trade-off

- High **precision** = the top-K is mostly correct items, but you might miss many relevant items further down.
- High **recall** = you covered most relevant items, but had to recommend a lot of irrelevant ones to do so.
- For top-K recommendation lists with small K, **precision usually dominates the user experience.**

## Related

- [[reco-rating-metrics]] — when ratings are the prediction target
- [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — non-accuracy quality dimensions
- [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss aligned with these metrics

---
name: Reco diversity metrics
description: Non-accuracy metrics for recommender systems — novelty, diversity, serendipity, coverage
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, evaluation]
---

# Reco diversity metrics

Accuracy isn't the whole story. A recommender that always recommends the same five popular items will score well on Precision@K but is a terrible product. These four metrics quantify *quality dimensions beyond accuracy*.

| Metric | What it measures | Slogan |
|---|---|---|
| **Novelty** | How rare are the recommended items? | "Don't keep recommending the obvious" |
| **Diversity** | How different are items in the list from each other? | "Don't recommend ten of the same thing" |
| **Serendipity** | How surprising are recommendations vs the user's history? | "Recommend things they wouldn't predict" |
| **Coverage** | What fraction of the catalog gets recommended? | "Use the whole catalog, not just the head" |

## The accuracy-vs-diversity trade-off

Models optimised purely for [[reco-rating-metrics|rating accuracy]] (like ALS) tend to recommend popular items — popularity is correlated with predicted rating because popular items have many ratings to learn from. **Result:** ALS dominates ranking metrics but loses to a random recommender on diversity. The lecture's [example: ALS vs Random].

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pop["Popular head items"] -->|"high prob to be recommended"| ALSrec["ALS top-K"]
    Tail["Long tail items"] -->|"never seen"| Cold["Cold-start gravesite"]
    Tail -.->|"random recommender catches these"| RandRec["Random top-K"]
    classDef bad fill:#ffebee
    class Cold bad
```

## When diversity matters most

- **Catalog discovery** (music, books) — users want surprises.
- **Long-tail businesses** — Amazon, Netflix make a lot of money on the tail.
- **Filter bubbles** — news, social — homogeneity is socially harmful.

## Common failure modes

- **Echo chambers** — recommending only items similar to what the user already engages with → narrowing of interests over time.
- **Rich-get-richer** — popularity-biased recommenders make popular items more popular, starving the tail.

## Related

- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — the accuracy axis
- [[content-based-filtering]] — can boost diversity via feature-driven recs

content.newText:
---
name: Reco online metrics
description: Production / business metrics for recommenders — CTR, conversion rate, AOV, MAU, LTV
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, evaluation, business]
---

# Reco online metrics

Production metrics that measure how a recommender helps the business. Computed live, evaluated via [[ab-test|A/B tests]]. Also called **business metrics**.

| Metric | Formula | What it measures |
|---|---|---|
| **CTR** (click-through rate) | clicks / visits | User engagement with the recommendations |
| **Conversion rate** | conversions / visits | How often the recommendation leads to a desired action (purchase, signup) |
| **AOV** (Average Order Value) | total revenue / # orders | Per-transaction monetisation |
| **MAU** (Monthly Active Users) | unique users in 30 days | Reach + retention |
| **LTV** (Lifetime Value) | AOV × purchases/year × retention years | Total revenue per user across their lifecycle |

## The LTV / CAC heuristic

A healthy benchmark: **LTV ≥ 3 × CAC** (Customer Acquisition Cost). Below that, you can't profitably acquire customers; above it, the recommender is doing real work.

## Why online metrics matter

Offline metrics (NDCG, MAP) are *proxies* for what we actually want — engagement, revenue, retention. A model can win offline and lose online if it:

- Recommends items users *click* but don't buy (low conversion).
- Surfaces clickbait (high CTR, low LTV).
- Hurts long-term engagement by killing diversity.

## How to evaluate

[[ab-test|A/B test]] in production. Half the traffic gets model A, half gets model B (or a baseline / random). Compare these metrics with proper statistical testing. With more than two variants → multi-variate test.

## Related

- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — offline proxy
- [[ab-test]] — the standard evaluation method
- [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — diversity often correlates with long-term LTV even when it loses on offline accuracy

content.newText:
---
name: A/B test
description: Randomised controlled experiment — split live traffic between two variants, compare metrics, pick the winner
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, evaluation, experimentation]
---

# A/B test

Randomised controlled experiment for online evaluation. The standard way to decide whether a new recommender (or any product change) is actually better in production.

## How it works

1. Randomise users (or sessions) into two arms with equal traffic.
2. Arm A sees the **control** (current model, baseline, random).
3. Arm B sees the **treatment** (new model).
4. Measure [[reco-online-metrics|business metrics]] for each arm.
5. Use a statistical test to decide whether B's lift over A is real (not noise).

## Variants

- **Multi-variate test** — more than two arms (A vs B vs C).
- **Multi-armed bandit** — adaptive allocation, shifts traffic toward the winner during the experiment.
- **Switchback test** — alternate the variant in time slices when randomisation by user is impossible.

## What can go wrong

- **Underpowered** — too few users / too short a window → noise dominates lift.
- **Novelty effect** — users react to *change* itself; the lift fades over weeks.
- **SUTVA violation** — treatment in arm A leaks to arm B (e.g. social network recommendations spill across arms).
- **Goodhart's law** — optimising the A/B metric drifts the product (clickbait optimisation).

## Why offline metrics aren't enough

Offline metrics are *proxies*. The only ground truth for "is this recommender better?" is observed user behavior, which requires a live experiment.

## Related

- [[reco-online-metrics]] — what you usually measure
- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — the offline proxies you'd validate against

--- name: Reco architectures description: Three production deployment patterns for recommenders — batch, real-time, and 2-step (hybrid) type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, recommender-systems, systems] --- # Reco architectures Three standard deployment patterns. Each trades freshness against compute cost. ## Batch ```mermaid flowchart LR Data[(Storage
GB-TB)] --> Train[Train + score offline] Train --> Cache[(Cache results)] Cache --> Serve[Serve fetched scores] ``` - Score every (user, item) pair *offline*, write the top-K to a cache. - **Cheap to serve**, can't react to within-session signals. - Use when interactions are slow-changing (weekly catalog, durable goods). ## Real-time ```mermaid flowchart LR Data[(Storage
MB-GB)] --> Train[Feature engineering + train] Train --> Serve[Serve model] Req[Request] --> Serve Serve --> Score[Score + rank
at request time] ``` - Score on the fly using current request context. - **Fresh** but expensive — every request hits the model. - Use when context matters (location, time, just-clicked item). ## 2-step (hybrid) ```mermaid flowchart LR Data[(Storage)] --> Train[Train] Train --> Cand[(Candidate cache
high recall)] Cand --> Rerank[Rerank online
100-1000 items] Rerank --> Out[Top-K] classDef step fill:#e3f2fd class Cand,Rerank step ``` - **Step 1 — candidate generation:** offline batch produces a high-recall candidate set per user (typically 100-1000 items). - **Step 2 — reranking:** at request time, a smaller real-time model reranks the candidates with fresh features. - **The industry default for large catalogs** — gets the freshness of real-time without scoring the full catalog at every request. ## Trade-off table | Architecture | Latency | Compute cost | Freshness | Catalog size handled | |---|---|---|---|---| | Batch | Low | Low | Stale (hours-days) | Any size | | Real-time | Medium-High | High | Live | Limited (have to score full catalog) | | 2-step | Low-Medium | Medium | Mostly fresh | Any size — candidates filter the catalog | ## Related - [[reco-online-metrics]] — what you optimise once deployed - [[ab-test]] — how you compare architectures live

---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 04
source: Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 04 — Offline vs online metrics (flashcards)

What's the difference between offline and online metrics for recommenders?
?
**Offline metrics** measure model performance on held-out historical data (RMSE, NDCG). **Online metrics** measure business impact in production (CTR, conversion, MAU).

Define RMSE for a recommender.
?
$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{|K|} \sum_{(u,i) \in K}(\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui})^2}$ — root-mean-square error between predicted and true ratings. More affected by outliers than MAE.

When do you use rating metrics vs ranking metrics?
?
**Rating metrics** (RMSE, MAE) when the prediction target is a numerical rating. **Ranking metrics** (NDCG, MAP) when you produce a top-K list — most practical reco use cases use ranking.

Define Precision@K and Recall@K.
?
**Precision@K** = (relevant items in top-K) / K — what fraction of the recommendations were correct. **Recall@K** = (relevant items in top-K) / (total relevant items) — what fraction of relevant items we surfaced.

What does NDCG@K measure that Precision@K does not?
?
**Position of the hit** — NDCG discounts hits further down the list via a $\log_2(k+1)$ factor. Getting the relevant item at rank 1 is worth more than at rank 5.

What does MAP@K stand for and what does it average?
?
**Mean Average Precision @ K**. For each user, compute average precision across hits in the top-K (position-weighted). Then average across users.

Name the four diversity-style metrics for recommenders.
?
**Novelty**, **Diversity**, **Serendipity**, and **Coverage**.

What is novelty in recommender evaluation?
?
How novel recommended items are — measured by their **recommendation frequency** across users. Items recommended often = low novelty.

What is serendipity?
?
How **surprising** recommendations are to a specific user, by comparing them to items the user has already interacted with.

What is coverage?
?
The proportion of the **catalog** that gets recommended — high coverage means the recommender uses the long tail, not just the popular head.

What is the long-tail problem in recommendation?
?
Item distribution is long-tailed: a few items are very popular, most are rare. Long-tail items can be highly profitable but suffer from cold-start. Accuracy-optimised models (ALS) mostly recommend popular items.

Why does ALS lose to a random recommender on diversity metrics?
?
ALS optimises rating accuracy → it learns to recommend items with the most data, which are popular items. The long tail almost never appears, so coverage and novelty are low.

Define CTR.
?
**Click-Through Rate** — number of users who click on a link / total visitors. A measure of user engagement.

Define LTV and the LTV/CAC heuristic.
?
**Lifetime Value** = total revenue per user over their engagement lifetime. A healthy benchmark is **LTV ≥ 3 × CAC** (Customer Acquisition Cost).

What is an A/B test in recommenders?
?
A live experiment — randomise traffic into two arms, A gets the model, B gets a baseline (or vice versa). Compare metrics to decide if the new model is better in production.

What is a multi-variate test?
?
An experiment with **more than two arms** (A vs B vs C…). Same idea as A/B, more variants.

Name the three reco deployment architectures.
?
**Batch** (precompute scores, cache, serve), **real-time** (score at request time), and **2-step / hybrid** (batch generates candidates, real-time reranks them).

When is a 2-step architecture preferred?
?
When you have a **large catalog** and need both freshness and scalability. Step 1 (batch) generates a high-recall candidate set; step 2 (real-time) reranks 100-1000 items for high precision.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf lecture: 05 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 05 — The evolution of state (FSMs to Transformers) ## Overview A bird's-eye view of how chatbots evolved from rule-based finite state machines to transformer-driven, agentic systems. Three eras introduced via a "Software 1.0 → Software 2.0" framing, then a tour of the modern stack: **RAG**, **agentic workflows** (function calling, ReAct), and the **evaluation problem** (LLM-as-judge, faithfulness, guardrails). ## Key concepts - [[finite-state-machine-chatbot]] — the rule-based era - [[rag-pipeline]] — Retrieval-Augmented Generation - [[agentic-workflow]] — function calling, ReAct, the agent loop - [[llm-evaluation]] — LLM-as-a-judge, faithfulness, answer relevance - [[llm-guardrails]] — anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering ## The three eras (slide 1) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Software 1.0
Rule-based"] --> B["FSMs + regex
'If user says X, do Y'
Fragile, no nuance"] A --> C["Software 2.0
Probabilistic"] C --> D["Transformer + Self-Attention
Programming → Training"] D --> E["Context as State"] E --> F["State = n tokens in context window
Predict next token, not navigate FSM"] ``` | Era | State model | Failure mode | |---|---|---| | **Software 1.0** | Database variable | Fragile, unscalable, no nuance | | **Software 2.0** | Trained weights | Probabilistic, less interpretable | | **Modern** | The context window | Hallucination, knowledge cutoff | ## RAG stack (slide 2) The standard solution to **knowledge cutoff** and **hallucination**. ```mermaid flowchart LR Doc[Document] -->|Chunking| Chunks[Chunks] Chunks -->|Embedding| Vec[Vector store] Q[User query] -->|Embed + search| TopK[Top-K chunks] Vec --> TopK TopK -->|Augment prompt| Prompt[System prompt + chunks] Prompt -->|Generate| LLM[LLM] LLM --> Ans[Answer] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class LLM chatbot ``` **Workflow:** Ingestion → Retrieval → Augmentation → Generation. **Key challenge:** garbage in, garbage out. **Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM.** **Tech stack examples:** - Orchestration: LangChain, LlamaIndex - Vector DB: ChromaDB (local), Pinecone (cloud), pgvector (SQL) ## Agentic workflows (slide 3) LLM moves from **chatting** to **acting**. | Pattern | What it is | |---|---| | **Function calling** | LLM identifies when it needs an external tool and outputs structured JSON to trigger an API/SQL/Python call | | **The agent loop** | Autonomous iteration: model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal (e.g. Claude Code) | | **ReAct (Reason + Act)** | Interleave **Thought** → **Action** → **Observation** loops. Example: Thought "I need order status" → Action `get_order_details(order_id="123")` → Observation "shipped yesterday" | ## Evaluation (slide 4) Stochastic models can't be unit-tested. - **LLM-as-a-judge** — use a stronger model to grade a smaller model's output on accuracy, tone, safety. - **Faithfulness** — does the answer match the retrieved context? (Grounded vs hallucinated.) - **Answer relevance** — does the answer actually address the user's prompt? - **Guardrail layer** — PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering. ## Open questions - How do you A/B test an LLM? (Hard — outputs are stochastic, evaluation is expensive.) - Optimal chunk size for retrieval? (Depends on embedding dim, query specificity, context budget.)

Rule-based"] --> B["FSMs + regex
'If user says X, do Y'
Fragile, no nuance"] A --> C["Software 2.0
Probabilistic"] C --> D["Transformer + Self-Attention
Programming → Training"] D --> E["Context as State"] E --> F["State = n tokens in context window
Predict next token, not navigate FSM"] ``` | Era | State model | Failure mode | |---|---|---| | **Software 1.0** | Database variable | Fragile, unscalable, no nuance | | **Software 2.0** | Trained weights | Probabilistic, less interpretable | | **Modern** | The context window | Hallucination, knowledge cutoff | ## RAG stack (slide 2) The standard solution to **knowledge cutoff** and **hallucination**. ```mermaid flowchart LR Doc[Document] -->|Chunking| Chunks[Chunks] Chunks -->|Embedding| Vec[Vector store] Q[User query] -->|Embed + search| TopK[Top-K chunks] Vec --> TopK TopK -->|Augment prompt| Prompt[System prompt + chunks] Prompt -->|Generate| LLM[LLM] LLM --> Ans[Answer] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class LLM chatbot ``` **Workflow:** Ingestion → Retrieval → Augmentation → Generation. **Key challenge:** garbage in, garbage out. **Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM.** **Tech stack examples:** - Orchestration: LangChain, LlamaIndex - Vector DB: ChromaDB (local), Pinecone (cloud), pgvector (SQL) ## Agentic workflows (slide 3) LLM moves from **chatting** to **acting**. | Pattern | What it is | |---|---| | **Function calling** | LLM identifies when it needs an external tool and outputs structured JSON to trigger an API/SQL/Python call | | **The agent loop** | Autonomous iteration: model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal (e.g. Claude Code) | | **ReAct (Reason + Act)** | Interleave **Thought** → **Action** → **Observation** loops. Example: Thought "I need order status" → Action `get_order_details(order_id="123")` → Observation "shipped yesterday" | ## Evaluation (slide 4) Stochastic models can't be unit-tested. - **LLM-as-a-judge** — use a stronger model to grade a smaller model's output on accuracy, tone, safety. - **Faithfulness** — does the answer match the retrieved context? (Grounded vs hallucinated.) - **Answer relevance** — does the answer actually address the user's prompt? - **Guardrail layer** — PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering. ## Open questions - How do you A/B test an LLM? ---
name: Finite-state machine chatbot
description: The pre-LLM "Software 1.0" chatbot architecture — hard-coded state transitions matched against keyword/regex rules
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, history]
---

# Finite-state machine chatbot

The "Software 1.0" chatbot architecture: a hard-coded **finite state machine** plus regex/keyword matching rules. Each user input is classified into an *intent*; each intent triggers a transition and a scripted response.

## How it worked

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Greeting
    Greeting --> Booking: "book"/"reserve"
    Greeting --> FAQ: "what"/"how"/"where"
    Booking --> Confirmation: "confirm"/"yes"
    Booking --> Greeting: "cancel"
    FAQ --> [*]
    Confirmation --> [*]
```

> "If user says X, do Y" — strict intent classification.

## Examples

- **ELIZA (1966)** — first chatbot, matched keywords with scripted Rogerian-therapy responses (`User: "My mother loves flowers" → ELIZA: "Tell me about your mother."`).
- **A.L.I.C.E. (1995)** — heuristic pattern matching, AIML rules. Won awards but never passed the Turing test.
- **Early IVR systems**, customer-service flows.

## Why it failed

- **Fragile.** Any phrasing outside the intent dictionary breaks the bot.
- **No nuance or context.** Can't handle "actually, change my earlier order" without explicit state plumbing.
- **Doesn't scale.** Every new intent needs hand-authored rules.

## Why this matters historically

The transformer revolution replaced "navigate a hand-coded graph" with "predict the next token from the context window". State went from a database variable to **the n tokens currently in context** — a paradigm shift in how chatbots model dialogue.

## Related

- [[rag-pipeline]] — modern fix for the knowledge-cutoff problem
- [[transformer-encoder]] — the architecture that displaced FSMs

content.newText:
---
name: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
description: Pipeline that grounds LLM outputs in retrieved documents — solves knowledge cutoff and reduces hallucination
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, llm, retrieval]
---

# RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

A pipeline that grounds an LLM's response in retrieved documents. The standard fix for two LLM weaknesses: **knowledge cutoff** (model doesn't know recent events) and **hallucination** (model invents facts).

## The four-stage workflow

| Stage | What happens |
|---|---|
| **1. Ingestion** | Document → chunked → each chunk embedded → stored in vector DB |
| **2. Retrieval** | User query → embedded → vector search → top-K relevant chunks |
| **3. Augmentation** | Retrieved chunks injected into the system prompt |
| **4. Generation** | LLM synthesises an answer **based only on the provided context** |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Doc[(Documents)] -->|chunk| Chunks
    Chunks -->|embed| Vec[(Vector store)]
    Q[User query] -->|embed| QE[Query embedding]
    QE -->|top-K search| Vec
    Vec --> Top[Top-K chunks]
    Top -->|augment prompt| Prompt
    Q --> Prompt
    Prompt --> LLM
    LLM --> Ans[Answer]
    classDef store fill:#e3f2fd
    classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0
    class Vec store
    class LLM chatbot
```

## Garbage in, garbage out

The headline insight from the lecture: **embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM**. A great LLM with bad retrieval gives confident answers grounded in irrelevant context.

## Tech stack

| Layer | Examples |
|---|---|
| Orchestration | LangChain, LlamaIndex |
| Vector DB | ChromaDB (local), Pinecone (cloud), pgvector (SQL) |
| Embedding model | OpenAI `text-embedding-3`, Cohere, sentence-transformers |
| LLM | GPT-4, Claude, Llama, etc. |

## Common failure modes

- **Bad chunking** — chunks too small lose context; too large dilute relevance.
- **Embedding mismatch** — using a generic model when domain-specific terms need a fine-tuned encoder.
- **Stale index** — vector store not refreshed when source documents update.
- **No re-ranking** — top-K by cosine similarity isn't always best; cross-encoder reranking helps.

## Related

- [[finite-state-machine-chatbot]] — the previous era
- [[agentic-workflow]] — what comes after RAG when "answer questions" becomes "do tasks"
- [[llm-evaluation]] — faithfulness measures whether the answer matches retrieved context

content.newText:
---
name: Agentic workflow
description: LLM-driven autonomous workflow — function calling, ReAct, the agent loop
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, llm, agents]
---

# Agentic workflow

LLMs evolving from **chatting** to **acting**. Three patterns:

## Function calling

The LLM identifies when it needs an external tool and outputs **structured JSON** to trigger an API, SQL query, Python function, etc. The framework runs the tool and feeds the result back.

```python
{
  "tool": "get_order_details",
  "arguments": {"order_id": "123"}
}
```

## The agent loop

Autonomous iteration: the model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal. Example: Claude Code, OpenAI Agents.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Goal] --> B[LLM thinks]
    B --> C{Need a tool?}
    C -->|yes| D[Call tool]
    D --> E[Observe result]
    E --> B
    C -->|done| F[Answer]
```

## ReAct (Reason + Act)

A prompting pattern that interleaves reasoning and acting:

```
Thought:      I need to find the user's order status.
Action:       get_order_details(order_id="123")
Observation:  Order shipped yesterday.
Thought:      I can answer the user now.
Answer:       Your order shipped yesterday.
```

The Thought-Action-Observation triplet is the core unit. Models trained on ReAct-style traces learn to interleave naturally.

## Comparison

| Pattern | Single-shot? | Autonomy |
|---|---|---|
| Function calling | One tool call → response | Low |
| ReAct | Multiple Thought-Action loops | Medium |
| Agent loop | Open-ended iteration until goal met | High |

## When agentic > one-shot

- Multi-step tasks ("book a trip" needs flight + hotel + car).
- Tasks requiring fresh data (weather, prices, search).
- Tasks requiring side effects (sending email, modifying a database).

## Risks

- **Runaway loops** — agent calls tools indefinitely without converging.
- **Tool misuse** — agent picks the wrong tool or argument.
- **Cost** — each loop iteration burns tokens.
- **Action safety** — sensitive actions (payments, deletes) need human-in-the-loop.

## Related

- [[rag-pipeline]] — agentic workflows often *use* RAG as one tool
- [[llm-guardrails]] — keep agents from going off the rails
- [[llm-evaluation]] — agentic outputs are even harder to evaluate

content.newText:
---
name: LLM evaluation
description: Methods for evaluating stochastic LLM outputs — LLM-as-a-judge, faithfulness, answer relevance
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, llm, evaluation]
---

# LLM evaluation

Stochastic models can't be unit-tested the way deterministic code can. Evaluation has to be approximate, statistical, and often itself uses an LLM.

## LLM-as-a-judge

Use a **stronger model** to grade a smaller (or candidate) model's output on accuracy, tone, safety, etc. The judge is given the user prompt, the model's output, and a rubric, and returns a score or label.

**Why it works:** for many subjective dimensions (helpfulness, conciseness, faithfulness), state-of-the-art LLMs match or beat human raters at lower cost. **Limits:** judge models have biases (favouring verbose answers, agreeing with their own family, etc.).

## Key metrics for RAG / grounded systems

| Metric | What it asks |
|---|---|
| **Faithfulness** | Does the answer match the retrieved context? (Or did the model hallucinate?) |
| **Answer relevance** | Does the answer actually address the user's prompt? |
| **Context precision** | Are the retrieved chunks actually relevant to the question? |
| **Context recall** | Did we retrieve all the chunks needed to answer? |

A faithful but irrelevant answer is still wrong. A relevant but unfaithful answer is a hallucination.

## Other approaches

- **Reference-based** — BLEU, ROUGE, exact match. Useful for translation/summarisation, brittle for open-ended generation.
- **Pairwise preference** — show two outputs, ask which is better. Drives [[reco-online-metrics|preference-based]] training (RLHF, DPO).
- **Test sets** — curated benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval).
- **Production monitoring** — track user feedback signals (👍/👎, regenerations, edits).

## Why this is hard

- Outputs are non-deterministic (temperature > 0).
- "Correct" is often subjective.
- Reference-based metrics miss valid alternatives.
- Adversarial inputs find failure modes the test set never covered.

## Related

- [[rag-pipeline]] — what faithfulness and answer relevance evaluate
- [[llm-guardrails]] — defence layer that's separate from evaluation
- [[ab-test]] — production-side counterpart for live decisions

content.newText:
---
name: LLM guardrails
description: Safety layer around LLM systems — PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 05.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, llm, safety]
---

# LLM guardrails

A safety layer around an LLM system. Three standard concerns:

| Concern | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **PII leakage** | Anonymise personal information before it enters the prompt or before it's logged |
| **Prompt injection** | Detect attempts to override the system prompt ("ignore previous instructions and …") |
| **Toxic / unsafe output** | Filter outputs against a moderation classifier or rule list |

## Where guardrails sit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[User input] -->|guardrail in| GIn[Input filter]
    GIn -->|sanitised| LLM
    LLM -->|raw output| GOut[Output filter]
    GOut -->|safe response| Out[User response]
    GIn -->|blocked| Block[Refuse / sanitise]
    GOut -->|blocked| Block
    classDef guard fill:#ffebee
    class GIn,GOut,Block guard
```

## Common patterns

- **Input guardrails** — anonymise PII, detect prompt injection, validate input format.
- **Output guardrails** — moderation classifier, jailbreak detection, hallucination check (cross-reference with retrieved context).
- **Tool-use guardrails** — restrict which tools agents can call, require human-in-the-loop for sensitive actions.

## Constitutional AI

Anthropic's Claude is trained with **Constitutional AI** — embed ethical principles into the model itself rather than (only) wrapping it in external filters. The model has internalised refusal behaviour.

## Trade-offs

- More guardrails → more refused or sanitised outputs → worse UX on benign queries (false positives).
- Fewer guardrails → easier abuse, regulatory risk.
- The right point depends on the deployment context (consumer chat ≠ internal dev tool).

## Related

- [[agentic-workflow]] — agents are higher-risk because they take actions, not just produce text
- [[rlhf]] — alignment training that shapes default refusal behaviour
- [[llm-evaluation]] — measures whether guardrails work

# Lecture 05 — Evolution of state (flashcards)

What characterised "Software 1.0" chatbots?
?
Hard-coded **finite state machines** + regex. Logic = "If user says X, do Y." Strict intent classification. Fragile, no nuance, doesn't scale.

What is the "context as state" insight in Software 2.0 chatbots?
?
State is no longer a database variable — it's the **n tokens currently in the context window**. The model predicts the next token rather than navigating a predefined graph.

Name the four stages of the RAG workflow.
?
**Ingestion** (chunk → embed → vector store) → **Retrieval** (embed query, vector search top-K) → **Augmentation** (inject chunks into the prompt) → **Generation** (LLM answers based on the provided context).

What two LLM problems does RAG solve?
?
**Knowledge cutoff** (model doesn't know recent events) and **hallucination** (model invents facts). Retrieving fresh, real chunks grounds the answer.

What's the headline lesson about RAG quality?
?
**Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM.** Garbage in, garbage out.

What is function calling in agentic LLMs?
?
The LLM outputs **structured JSON** (tool name + arguments) when it identifies it needs an external tool. The framework executes the tool and feeds back the result.

Describe the ReAct pattern.
?
Interleaved **Thought → Action → Observation** loops. Example: Thought "I need order status" → Action `get_order_details(order_id="123")` → Observation "shipped yesterday" → Answer.

What is the agent loop?
?
Autonomous iteration where the model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal. Example: Claude Code.

What is "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluation?
?
Use a **stronger model** to grade a smaller (or candidate) model's output on accuracy, tone, safety, etc. Cheaper than human raters; risk of judge bias.

Define faithfulness and answer relevance for RAG evaluation.
?
**Faithfulness** — does the answer match the retrieved context? (Or is it a hallucination?) **Answer relevance** — does the answer actually address the user's prompt?

Name three concerns the LLM guardrail layer addresses.
?
**PII anonymisation**, **prompt-injection detection**, and **output filtering** (moderation against unsafe content).

Why can't you unit-test LLM outputs deterministically?
?
LLMs are stochastic — the same input can produce different outputs. Evaluation has to be statistical and approximate.

# Lecture 05 — Evolution of state (flashcards)

What characterised "Software 1.0" chatbots?
?
Hard-coded **finite state machines** + regex. Logic = "If user says X, do Y." Strict intent classification. Fragile, no nuance, doesn't scale.

What is the "context as state" insight in Software 2.0 chatbots?
?
State is no longer a database variable — it's the **n tokens currently in the context window**. The model predicts the next token rather than navigating a predefined graph.

Name the four stages of the RAG workflow.
?
**Ingestion** (chunk → embed → vector store) → **Retrieval** (embed query, vector search top-K) → **Augmentation** (inject chunks into the prompt) → **Generation** (LLM answers based on the provided context).

What two LLM problems does RAG solve?
?
**Knowledge cutoff** (model doesn't know recent events) and **hallucination** (model invents facts). Retrieving fresh, real chunks grounds the answer.

What's the headline lesson about RAG quality?
?
**Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM.** Garbage in, garbage out.

What is function calling in agentic LLMs?
?
The LLM outputs **structured JSON** (tool name + arguments) when it identifies it needs an external tool. The framework executes the tool and feeds back the result.

Describe the ReAct pattern.
?
Interleaved **Thought → Action → Observation** loops. Example: Thought "I need order status" → Action `get_order_details(order_id="123")` → Observation "shipped yesterday" → Answer.

What is the agent loop?
?
Autonomous iteration where the model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal. Example: Claude Code.

What is "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluation?
?
Use a **stronger model** to grade a smaller (or candidate) model's output on accuracy, tone, safety, etc. Cheaper than human raters; risk of judge bias.

Define faithfulness and answer relevance for RAG evaluation.
?
**Faithfulness** — does the answer match the retrieved context? (Or is it a hallucination?) **Answer relevance** — does the answer actually address the user's prompt?

Name three concerns the LLM guardrail layer addresses.
?
**PII anonymisation**, **prompt-injection detection**, and **output filtering** (moderation against unsafe content).

Why can't you unit-test LLM outputs deterministically?
?
LLMs are stochastic — the same input can produce different outputs. Evaluation has to be statistical and approximate.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf lecture: 06 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 06 — SAR (memory-based) and matrix factorization ## Overview Two foundational [[collaborative-filtering|collaborative-filtering]] techniques. - **SAR** (Smart Adaptive Recommendations) — memory-based, item-item co-occurrence + user affinity. Multiply matrices, sort, top-K. No training, just counting. - **Matrix factorization** — model-based. Decompose the rating matrix $R \approx P Q^T$ into latent user/item factors. Solve via **SGD (e.g. SVD with biases)** or **Alternating Least Squares (ALS)**. The lecture closes with the (non-convex) MF objective and explains why ALS is preferred over plain gradient descent. ## Key concepts - [[sar-algorithm]] — memory-based item-item recommender - [[reco-similarity-metrics]] — count, lift, Jaccard, mutual information, cosine, inclusion - [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — $R \approx P Q^T$ with latent factors - [[svd-recommender]] — SGD-based matrix factorization with biases - [[als-recommender]] — Alternating Least Squares - [[reco-data-transformations]] — affinity matrix construction ## Part 1 — SAR ```mermaid flowchart LR Tx[(Transactional
data)] --> Aff["User-item
affinity matrix A"] Tx --> Cooc["Co-occurrence
matrix C"] Cooc --> Sim["Item-item
similarity S"] Aff --> Score["Score = A · S"] Sim --> Score Score --> Filter["(opt.) Remove seen items"] Filter --> Top["Top-K recommendations"] classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd class Aff,Sim reco ``` ### Affinity matrix Captures how strongly user $u$ is connected to item $i$. SAR accepts both **explicit** (1-5 ratings) and **implicit** (clicks, purchases) data; each interaction type has a weight. **Time decay** with half-life $T$ optionally weights recent events more (slide 7). ### Co-occurrence and similarity Co-occurrence matrix $C = U'^T U'$ (where $U'$ is the binarised affinity). Entry $C_{ij}$ counts users who interacted with both items $i$ and $j$. Similarity is computed from $C$ via one of several metrics: | Metric | Property | |---|---| | **Count** | Favours **predictability** — popular items recommended most | | **Lift** | Favours **discoverability / serendipity** — boosts items popular among small subgroups | | **Jaccard** | Compromise between count and lift | | **Mutual information** | Information-theoretic, biased toward low-frequency items | | **Lexicographers MI** | Corrects MI bias by multiplying by co-occurrence frequency | | **Cosine similarity** | Angle between item column vectors | | **Inclusion index** | Overlap between items | ### Scoring $$\text{rec}(u, i) = \sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$$ For each unseen item $i$, score = sum over items $j$ the user has interacted with of (affinity to $j$) × (similarity of $i$ to $j$). The slide-5 worked example: User 1's score for Item 4 = $5 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2.5 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 = 28.5$. ### Practical notes - **CPU and PySpark implementations** exist; possible future ones: GPU (PyTorch), Dask, stored procedure. - No training step — pure linear algebra over counts. ## Part 2 — Matrix factorization ### The latent-factor model Represent each user $u$ by a vector $p_u \in \mathbb{R}^f$ and each item $i$ by a vector $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^f$. Predicted rating: $$\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$$ The number of factors $f$ is also called the **rank** of the model. Item factors might encode "comedy vs drama" or uninterpretable axes; user factors weigh user preferences along those axes. ### Regularised objective $$\min_{P,Q} \sum_{(u,i) \in K} \left(r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u\right)^2 + \lambda \left(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2\right)$$ Sum is over **observed** ratings $K$ — missing entries are excluded, not zeroed. ### Two learning algorithms **SGD (e.g. SVD-with-biases):** iteratively update $p_u$ and $q_i$ by taking small steps against the gradient. Adds **biases** $\mu$ (global mean), $b_u$ (user), $b_i$ (item) to model offsets: $$\hat r_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^T p_u$$ **ALS:** alternate between holding $P$ fixed and solving for $Q$, then vice versa. Each subproblem is a least-squares regression with a closed-form solution. The full loss is non-convex, but each ALS subproblem is convex → no local-minimum trap during the inner solve. ### When to choose which - **SGD/SVD** — explicit ratings, stochastic updates, easy to extend with extra features. - **ALS** — sparse implicit data, easy to **parallelise** (independent subproblems), Spark-friendly. ## Equations Time-decay affinity (slide 7): $$A_{ui} = \sum_t w_{e(t)} \cdot 2^{-(t_0 - t) / T}$$ with $w_{e(t)}$ the weight of the interaction type at time $t$ and $T$ the half-life. ## Open questions - Choosing $f$ (rank): typical 10–200; trade-off between expressiveness and overfitting (use regularization $\lambda$). - ALS doesn't apply directly to implicit feedback without weighting unobserved entries; the **implicit-feedback ALS** variant (Hu et al. 2008) introduces confidence weights — covered briefly on slide 14.

content.newText:
---
name: SAR (Smart Adaptive Recommendations)
description: Memory-based collaborative filter — multiplies a user-item affinity matrix with an item-item similarity matrix to produce top-K recommendations
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, collaborative-filtering]
---

# SAR (Smart Adaptive Recommendations)

A memory-based [[collaborative-filtering|collaborative filter]]. No training step — just multiply two matrices.

## Three matrices

| Matrix | Symbol | Built from |
|---|---|---|
| **Affinity** | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{|U|\times|I|}$ | User-item interactions, weighted by type and time |
| **Co-occurrence** | $C \in \mathbb{R}^{|I|\times|I|}$ | Number of users who interacted with both items $i$ and $j$ |
| **Item-item similarity** | $S \in \mathbb{R}^{|I|\times|I|}$ | Normalisation of $C$ via one of count / lift / Jaccard / cosine / etc. (see [[reco-similarity-metrics]]) |

## Scoring

For user $u$ and unseen item $i$:

$$\text{rec}(u, i) = \sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$$

Sum over all items $j$ the user has touched. Output the top-K items by score, optionally removing items the user has already seen.

## Worked example (slide 5)

User 1's affinity row: $[5, 3, 2.5, 0, 0]$ over items 1-5.
Item 4's similarity column: $[3, 2, 3, 4, 2]$.

$$\text{rec}(\text{User 1}, \text{Item 4}) = 5 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2.5 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 = 15 + 6 + 7.5 + 0 + 0 = 28.5$$

## Why "memory-based"

No latent-factor model is learned. The recommendation is computed entirely from observed data and a similarity rule. Contrast with [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]] (model-based, learns embeddings).

## Pros / cons

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| No training; just compute matrices | Doesn't capture latent factors |
| Easy to explain ("we recommend $i$ because you bought similar item $j$") | Memory-heavy on large catalogs ($I \times I$ similarity matrix) |
| Works on implicit feedback out of the box | Cold start unsolved |

## Implementations

CPU and PySpark implementations exist (lecture). Possible future implementations: GPU (PyTorch), Dask, stored procedure (Redis, SQL).

## Related

- [[collaborative-filtering]]
- [[reco-similarity-metrics]] — choices for $S$
- [[reco-data-transformations]] — building $A$
- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — the model-based alternative

content.newText:
---
name: Reco similarity metrics
description: Choices for the item-item similarity matrix in memory-based recommenders — count, lift, Jaccard, mutual information, cosine, inclusion
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Reco similarity metrics

Memory-based recommenders ([[sar-algorithm|SAR]], item-item collaborative filtering) need a similarity rule between items derived from the co-occurrence matrix $C$, where $C_{ij}$ = number of users who interacted with both items $i$ and $j$.

## The seven standard choices

| Metric | Formula (informal) | Bias |
|---|---|---|
| **Count** | $S_{ij} = C_{ij}$ | Favours **predictability** — popular items recommended most often |
| **Lift** | $S_{ij} = C_{ij} / (C_{ii} \cdot C_{jj})$ | Favours **discoverability / serendipity** — boosts items popular among small subgroups |
| **Jaccard** | $S_{ij} = C_{ij} / (C_{ii} + C_{jj} - C_{ij})$ | Compromise between count and lift |
| **Mutual information** | $S_{ij} = \log\frac{C_{ij}}{C_{ii} C_{jj}}$ | Information-theoretic; biased toward low-frequency items |
| **Lexicographers MI** | MI × co-occurrence frequency | Corrects MI's low-frequency bias |
| **Cosine similarity** | angle between item column vectors | Length-invariant; standard in NLP-style embeddings |
| **Inclusion index** | $C_{ij} / \min(C_{ii}, C_{jj})$ | Measures *overlap* — how much $i$ is contained in $j$ |

## Choosing one

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Recommend popular items | Count |
| Surface niche but loved items | Lift |
| Balanced default | Jaccard or cosine |
| Information-theoretic flavour | Mutual information |

## Why this matters

Two recommenders with the *same* affinity matrix and the *same* scoring rule (multiply $A \cdot S$) can produce wildly different recommendations depending on which similarity metric defines $S$. **Count** will keep recommending top-sellers; **lift** will surface things from the long tail.

## Related

- [[sar-algorithm]] — the recommender that uses these
- [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — count tanks diversity, lift improves it
- [[collaborative-filtering]]

content.newText:
---
name: Matrix factorization (recommenders)
description: Decompose the user-item rating matrix into low-rank latent user and item factors — $R \approx P Q^T$
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 07.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, collaborative-filtering]
---

# Matrix factorization (recommenders)

The model-based [[collaborative-filtering|collaborative-filtering]] workhorse. Approximate the sparse user-item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{|U|\times|I|}$ as a product of two low-rank matrices:

$$R \approx P Q^T$$

where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{|U| \times f}$ is users-by-latent-factors and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{|I| \times f}$ is items-by-latent-factors. The number of factors $f$ is the **rank** of the model.

## Predicted rating

$$\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$$

A simple inner product. Each item factor $q_i$ might encode "comedy vs drama" or uninterpretable axes; each user factor $p_u$ weighs the user's affinity along those axes.

## Regularised objective

$$\min_{P, Q}\ \sum_{(u, i) \in K} \left(r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u\right)^2 + \lambda \left(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2\right)$$

Sum is over **observed** ratings $K$ — missing entries are *excluded*, not zero. Without regularisation $\lambda$, the model overfits.

## Two learning algorithms

| Algorithm | How it works | When |
|---|---|---|
| **[[svd-recommender|SGD / SVD]]** | Stochastic gradient descent on $P$ and $Q$ jointly, often with biases | Explicit ratings, flexible |
| **[[als-recommender|ALS]]** | Hold $P$ fixed, solve for $Q$ in closed form, alternate | Sparse implicit data, parallelisable |

The full loss is non-convex in $(P, Q)$, but each ALS subproblem is convex.

## The fundamental limitation (lecture 7)

Inner-product MF combines latent features **linearly**. Two users with the same dot-product preference for item $i$ are treated identically — but real preferences interact non-linearly. This motivates [[neural-collaborative-filtering|Neural Collaborative Filtering]] (replace inner product with an MLP) and [[factorization-machines|Factorization Machines]] (model second-order feature interactions).

## What MF does well

- Captures **latent structure** invisible in the metadata.
- Handles sparsity by sharing parameters across users and items.
- Closed-form gradients → fast.

## What MF doesn't handle

- Cold-start (no $p_u$ for new user).
- Side features (no place to inject demographics or item attributes — extends with FM).
- Non-linear feature interactions.

## Related

- [[svd-recommender]] — SGD-trained MF with biases
- [[als-recommender]] — alternating least squares
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — replaces inner product with MLP
- [[factorization-machines]] — MF + second-order feature crosses
- [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss for MF on implicit data

---

# Matrix factorization (recommenders)

The model-based [[collaborative-filtering|collaborative-filtering]] workhorse. Approximate the sparse user-item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{|U|\times|I|}$ as a product of two low-rank matrices:

$$R \approx P Q^T$$

where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{|U| \times f}$ is users-by-latent-factors and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{|I| \times f}$ is items-by-latent-factors. The number of factors $f$ is the **rank** of the model.

## Predicted rating

$$\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$$

A simple inner product. Each item factor $q_i$ might encode "comedy vs drama" or uninterpretable axes; each user factor $p_u$ weighs the user's affinity along those axes.

## Regularised objective

$$\min_{P, Q}\ \sum_{(u, i) \in K} \left(r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u\right)^2 + \lambda \left(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2\right)$$

Sum is over **observed** ratings $K$ — missing entries are *excluded*, not zero. Without regularisation $\lambda$, the model overfits.

## Two learning algorithms

| Algorithm | How it works | When |
|---|---|---|
| **[[svd-recommender|SGD / SVD]]** | Stochastic gradient descent on $P$ and $Q$ jointly, often with biases | Explicit ratings, flexible |
| **[[als-recommender|ALS]]** | Hold $P$ fixed, solve for $Q$ in closed form, alternate | Sparse implicit data, parallelisable |

The full loss is non-convex in $(P, Q)$, but each ALS subproblem is convex.

## The fundamental limitation (lecture 7)

Inner-product MF combines latent features **linearly**. Two users with the same dot-product preference for item $i$ are treated identically — but real preferences interact non-linearly. This motivates [[neural-collaborative-filtering|Neural Collaborative Filtering]] (replace inner product with an MLP) and [[factorization-machines|Factorization Machines]] (model second-order feature interactions).

## What MF does well

- Captures **latent structure** invisible in the metadata.
- Handles sparsity by sharing parameters across users and items.
- Closed-form gradients → fast.

## What MF doesn't handle

- Cold-start (no $p_u$ for new user).
- Side features (no place to inject demographics or item attributes — extends with FM).
- Non-linear feature interactions.

## Related

- [[svd-recommender]] — SGD-trained MF with biases
- [[als-recommender]] — alternating least squares
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — replaces inner product with MLP
- [[factorization-machines]] — MF + second-order feature crosses
- [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss for MF on implicit data

---
name: SVD recommender (with biases)
description: SGD-trained matrix factorization with global, user, and item bias terms — Funk SVD-style
type: concept

# SVD recommender (with biases)

A flavour of [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]] trained by SGD, popularised by Simon Funk during the Netflix Prize. **Despite the name, this is not the linear-algebra SVD of the rating matrix** — it's MF + biases, optimised by SGD.

## The model

$$\hat r_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^T p_u$$

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Global average rating across the dataset |
| $b_u$ | User bias (this user rates higher/lower than average) |
| $b_i$ | Item bias (this item is rated higher/lower than average) |
| $q_i^T p_u$ | The personalised latent-factor contribution |

## Why biases matter

Without biases, the model is forced to use latent factors to explain "everyone gives 5-star ratings to everything" or "this user is a harsh rater" — wasting capacity on baseline shifts. Adding $\mu, b_u, b_i$ frees the latent factors to model **personalised preferences** only.

## Objective

$$\min \sum_{(u,i) \in K} \left(r_{ui} - \hat r_{ui}\right)^2 + \lambda \big(\|p_u\|^2 + \|q_i\|^2 + b_u^2 + b_i^2\big)$$

## Update rules (SGD)

For each observed $(u, i)$, error $e_{ui} = r_{ui} - \hat r_{ui}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_u &\leftarrow b_u + \eta(e_{ui} - \lambda b_u) \\
b_i &\leftarrow b_i + \eta(e_{ui} - \lambda b_i) \\
p_u &\leftarrow p_u + \eta(e_{ui} q_i - \lambda p_u) \\
q_i &\leftarrow q_i + \eta(e_{ui} p_u - \lambda q_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

## Pros / cons

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Biases handle baseline effects cleanly | Sequential SGD is hard to parallelise (vs ALS) |
| Easy to extend with extra terms (time bias, etc.) | Needs tuning of $\eta, \lambda$ |
| Strong baseline on explicit ratings | Doesn't handle implicit feedback as cleanly |

## Related

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]]
- [[als-recommender]]
- [[gradient-descent]]

---
name: ALS (Alternating Least Squares)
description: Matrix factorization solved by alternately fixing user factors and solving for item factors in closed form (and vice versa)
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, collaborative-filtering, optimization]
---

# ALS (Alternating Least Squares)

A learning algorithm for [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]]. Instead of doing joint gradient descent on $P$ and $Q$, **alternate** between solving for one while holding the other fixed.

## The trick

The full MF objective is **non-convex** in $(P, Q)$. But:

- **Hold $Q$ fixed** → the objective is a sum of independent **least-squares problems** over each user $u$. Convex. Closed-form solution.
- **Hold $P$ fixed** → same story, one least-squares problem per item.

So alternate:

1. With $Q$ fixed, solve for $P$.
2. With $P$ fixed, solve for $Q$.
3. Repeat until convergence.

Each step monotonically decreases the loss → guaranteed convergence (to a local minimum).

## Closed-form per-user update

$$p_u = (Q^T Q + \lambda I)^{-1} Q^T r_u$$

where $r_u$ is user $u$'s observed-rating vector. The matrix to invert is just $f \times f$ (the rank), so cheap.

## Pros over SGD

- **Embarrassingly parallel** — each user's update is independent, ditto each item's. Spark-friendly.
- **No learning rate** to tune.
- Each subproblem is convex with a closed form → no fragile local-minimum dance.

## Cons

- Each step solves $|U| + |I|$ least-squares problems → expensive per iteration vs SGD.
- Doesn't naturally handle huge feature sets like SGD does.

## ALS for implicit feedback

Standard ALS treats missing entries as missing — fine for explicit ratings. For implicit data (clicks, purchases) **all entries matter**: an unobserved entry is "probably no preference". Hu, Koren, Volinsky (2008) introduced **weighted ALS** with a confidence parameter $c_{ui} = 1 + \alpha \cdot r_{ui}$ that downweights unobserved entries to a small constant.

## When to choose ALS

- Massive sparse implicit datasets (e-commerce clicks).
- Spark / distributed pipelines.
- When you don't want to tune SGD hyperparameters.

## Related

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]]
- [[svd-recommender]] — the SGD alternative
- [[reco-architectures]] — ALS lives in the batch architecture

---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 06
source: Chatbots and Reco 06.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 06 — SAR + Matrix factorization (flashcards)

What does SAR stand for, and what kind of recommender is it?
?
**Smart Adaptive Recommendations** — a memory-based collaborative filter that multiplies a user-item affinity matrix $A$ by an item-item similarity matrix $S$ to score recommendations.

What three matrices does SAR use?
?
**Affinity matrix** $A$ (user-item, weighted by interaction type and time decay), **co-occurrence matrix** $C$ (counts users who interacted with both items), and **item-item similarity matrix** $S$ (normalised $C$).

State the SAR scoring formula.
?
$\text{rec}(u, i) = \sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$ — for each unseen item $i$, sum over items $j$ the user has touched of (affinity to $j$) × (similarity of $i$ to $j$).

In SAR, what's the difference between using count vs lift as similarity?
?
**Count** favours predictability — popular items get recommended most. **Lift** favours discoverability/serendipity — boosts items popular among small subgroups.

What is the Jaccard similarity in SAR?
?
A compromise between count and lift. Roughly $C_{ij} / (C_{ii} + C_{jj} - C_{ij})$.

Why does SAR work without any "training" step?
?
The recommendation is computed entirely from observed data via matrix multiplication — no parameters are learned. Just compute the affinity and similarity matrices and multiply.

Write the matrix factorization model for predicted ratings.
?
$\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$, where $p_u \in \mathbb{R}^f$ is the user factor and $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^f$ is the item factor. $f$ is the rank of the model.

Write the regularised matrix factorization objective.
?
$\min_{P,Q} \sum_{(u,i) \in K} (r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u)^2 + \lambda(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2)$ — sum over **observed** entries only, $\lambda$ controls regularization.

Why does the SVD-style MF model add biases $\mu, b_u, b_i$?
?
To absorb baseline effects (global average, harsh users, popular items) so the latent factors $p_u, q_i$ only need to model **personalised** preferences, not baseline shifts.

Write the SVD-with-biases prediction.
?
$\hat r_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^T p_u$.

What's the core idea of ALS?
?
**Alternate** between holding $P$ fixed and solving for $Q$ in closed form (least squares), then vice versa. Each subproblem is convex even though the joint loss is non-convex.

Why is ALS preferred over SGD for sparse implicit datasets?
?
Each user/item update is independent → **embarrassingly parallel** (Spark-friendly), no learning rate to tune, and closed-form least-squares solves each subproblem exactly.

What's the closed-form ALS update for $p_u$?
?
$p_u = (Q^T Q + \lambda I)^{-1} Q^T r_u$ — invert an $f \times f$ matrix (cheap).

Is the matrix factorization loss convex?
?
**No** — non-convex in $(P, Q)$ jointly. But each ALS subproblem (with one matrix fixed) is convex.

Why does plain ALS not work directly on implicit feedback?
?
Plain ALS treats missing entries as missing. For implicit feedback all entries matter — unobserved means "probably negative". The fix is **weighted ALS** with confidence weights $c_{ui} = 1 + \alpha \cdot r_{ui}$.

---
# Lecture 07 — MF limitations and Factorization Machines

## Overview

[[matrix-factorization-reco|Matrix factorization]] computes predictions as an inner product $q_i^T p_u$ — a *linear* combination of latent features. Real user behavior is non-linear, so MF leaves accuracy on the table. Two responses:

- **[[neural-collaborative-filtering|Neural CF]]** (deck 10a) — replace the inner product with an MLP.
- **[[factorization-machines|Factorization Machines (FM)]]** — extend MF to model **all order-2 cross-features** efficiently. The lecture covers this and its variant **Field-aware FM (FFM)**.

## Key concepts

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — what FM extends
- [[factorization-machines]] — order-2 feature interactions
- [[ffm-field-aware]] — different latent factors per feature group

## The headline limitation

For matrix factorization:

$$\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u = \sum_{k=1}^f p_{u,k} q_{i,k}$$

This is a **linear** combination — each latent dim contributes independently. There's no way to express "user *u* likes item *i* only when context *c* holds" without exploding parameters.

## Factorization Machines (slide 2)

FM extends MF to model **order-2 interactions** between any two features (not just user × item):

$$\hat y(\mathbf{x}) = w_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n w_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=i+1}^n \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j$$

- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a **feature vector** containing one-hot user, one-hot item, plus any side features (demographics, time, context).
- $w_0$, $w_i$ are the global bias and first-order weights.
- $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ are **latent vectors per feature**. The pairwise interaction weight is the dot product of the two features' latent vectors.

The trick: instead of learning $O(n^2)$ pairwise weights $w_{ij}$ explicitly, **factorise** them as $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle$. Same expressive power, $O(nk)$ parameters, and you can compute the sum in linear time:

$$\sum_{i < j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{f=1}^k \left[\left(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i\right)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2\right]$$

Trained with SGD; same gradient form as MF.

## Field-aware FM (FFM, slide 3)

FM uses one latent vector $\mathbf{v}_i$ per feature, regardless of who $i$ is interacting with. **FFM** uses a different latent vector $\mathbf{v}_{i,f_j}$ per pair of (feature, field-of-the-other-feature).

> Same equation as FM, but $V$ varies by field.

| Aspect | FM | FFM |
|---|---|---|
| Latent vectors | $n \times k$ | $n \times F \times k$ where $F$ is the number of fields |
| Captures | "User × Item" interactions in one space | Different interactions for "User × Item" vs "User × Time" vs "User × Device" |
| Cost | Cheaper | More parameters, slower |
| When | First baseline | When features represent *different categories* of information that shouldn't share latent space |

## Equations recap

**FM model equation:**

$$\hat y(\mathbf{x}) = w_0 + \langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x}\rangle + \sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j\rangle x_i x_j$$

**Linear-time interaction sum:**

$$\sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j\rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_f \left[\left(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i\right)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2\right]$$

## Software

The lecture mentions **xLearn**, a popular C++ library implementing LR, FM, and FFM with Python bindings.

## Open questions

- When does FM beat MF? When you have side features beyond user/item IDs.
- When does FFM beat FM? When features cleanly partition into fields whose interactions differ.
- Can you go even higher-order? Yes — order-3 FM exists but rarely worth it.

$$\sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j\rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_f \left[\left(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i\right)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2\right]$$

## Software

The lecture mentions **xLearn**, a popular C++ library implementing LR, FM, and FFM with Python bindings.

## Open questions

- When does FM beat MF? When you have side features beyond user/item IDs.
- When does FFM beat FM? When features cleanly partition into fields whose interactions differ.
- Can you go even higher-order? Yes — order-3 FM exists but rarely worth it.

---
name: Factorization Machines (FM)
description: Generalisation of matrix factorization that models all pairwise feature interactions via factorised latent vectors — efficient with side features
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 07.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Factorization Machines (FM)

A generalisation of [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]] (Rendle 2010) that models **all order-2 feature interactions** efficiently, using factorised latent vectors. Crucially, FM accepts arbitrary side features (demographics, time, context) — not just user-and-item IDs.

## The model

$$\hat y(\mathbf{x}) = w_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n w_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Concatenation of one-hot user, one-hot item, side features |
| $w_0$ | Global bias |
| $w_i$ | First-order weight for feature $i$ |
| $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | Latent vector for feature $i$ (the factorisation trick) |
| $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle$ | Interaction weight between features $i$ and $j$ |

## Why factorise the pairwise weights?

Naively learning $w_{ij}$ for every pair gives $O(n^2)$ parameters and zero generalisation across pairs (each $(i,j)$ trained independently). FM factorises $w_{ij} \approx \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle$ — **same expressivity in $O(nk)$ parameters**, and parameters share information across pairs.

## Linear-time computation

Naive computation of the interaction sum is $O(n^2)$. Rewrite:

$$\sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{f=1}^k \left[\left(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i\right)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2\right]$$

Each term is $O(nk)$ → $O(nk)$ total. **This is what makes FM practical on millions of features.**

## How it relates to MF

Matrix factorization is a special case of FM where $\mathbf{x}$ has only two non-zero entries (one for user, one for item). FM = MF generalised to arbitrary feature sets.

## When to use FM

- **Sparse, high-dimensional categorical features** — CTR prediction, ad ranking.
- **Cold-start mitigation** — side features like demographics give the model something to compute interactions on even when user-item history is missing.
- **Anywhere MF fits but you want to add side features without rewriting the model.**

## Variants

- **[[ffm-field-aware|Field-aware FM (FFM)]]** — different latent vector per (feature, field) pair.
- **DeepFM** — combines FM with a deep MLP, similar in spirit to [[wide-and-deep|Wide & Deep]].

## Related

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — the special case
- [[ffm-field-aware]]
- [[wide-and-deep]] — modern deep alternative
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]]

content.newText:
---
name: Field-aware Factorization Machines (FFM)
description: FM variant where each feature has a different latent vector per "field" of the interacting feature
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 07.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Field-aware Factorization Machines (FFM)

A variant of [[factorization-machines|Factorization Machines]] where each feature has a **separate latent vector per "field"** of the other interacting feature.

## The change from FM

In FM, each feature $i$ has one latent vector $\mathbf{v}_i$ used in **all** interactions:

$$\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle$$

In FFM, $i$ has a different latent vector for each *field* the other feature belongs to. If features are partitioned into fields (User, Item, Time, Device, …):

$$\langle \mathbf{v}_{i, F(j)}, \mathbf{v}_{j, F(i)} \rangle$$

where $F(\cdot)$ returns the field of a feature.

## Why this helps

In FM, a user-feature's latent vector has to do double duty — work well in `User × Item` interactions AND `User × Time` AND `User × Device`. These interactions reflect different things; one shared vector blurs them. FFM gives the user feature **separate vectors per field**, so the model can specialise.

## Cost

- Parameters: $O(n \cdot F \cdot k)$ where $F$ is the number of fields (vs $O(n k)$ for FM).
- Slower training, more memory.
- Often worth it on highly heterogeneous CTR-prediction datasets.

## When to use

- Many distinct field types (CTR-style ad ranking with user, ad, advertiser, page, time, device, geo, etc.).
- Features cleanly partition into fields whose interaction semantics differ.

## When NOT to use

- Few fields (just user + item) → FM is enough.
- Tight memory budget — FFM blows up parameters.

## Related

- [[factorization-machines]] — the parent
- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — the grandparent (special case of FM)

source: Chatbots and Reco 07.pdf

# Lecture 07 — MF limitations + Factorization Machines (flashcards)

What's the fundamental limitation of inner-product matrix factorization?
?
The inner product $q_i^T p_u$ combines latent features **linearly**. Real user-item interactions are non-linear, so MF can't capture complex patterns.

Two common responses to MF's linear-combination limitation?
?
**Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF)** — replace the inner product with an MLP. **Factorization Machines (FM)** — extend MF to model order-2 interactions across all features (with side features included).

Write the FM model equation.
?
$\hat y(\mathbf{x}) = w_0 + \sum_i w_i x_i + \sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j$, where $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is the latent vector for feature $i$.

What does FM's pairwise-interaction factorisation buy you?
?
Naive pairwise weights are $O(n^2)$ parameters with no sharing. Factorising as $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle$ gives **$O(nk)$ parameters** with information shared across pairs.

How does FM compute the pairwise sum in linear time?
?
$\sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_f \big[(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2\big]$ — $O(nk)$ per term.

How is matrix factorization a special case of FM?
?
MF is FM where $\mathbf{x}$ has only two non-zero entries: one for the user, one for the item. FM generalises this to any feature vector.

What does FFM (Field-aware FM) change relative to FM?
?
FFM uses a **different latent vector per (feature, field-of-the-interacting-feature) pair**. Each feature has $F$ vectors instead of one, where $F$ = number of fields.

When is FFM worth the cost over FM?
?
When features cleanly partition into many distinct **fields** (user, item, time, device, advertiser…) whose interactions have different semantics. Standard in CTR-prediction.

What library implements FM and FFM with Python bindings?
?
**xLearn** (C++ with Python bindings).

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf lecture: 08 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 08 — Decision Trees, Random Forest, GBDT ## Overview Tree-based models from the ground up. **Decision trees** split feature space recursively to minimise impurity (Gini or Entropy). They overfit easily — fixed by **bagging** (Random Forest) or **boosting** (GBDT). The latter dominates structured-data competitions; **LightGBM** and **XGBoost** are the two production-grade GBDT frameworks. Closes with engineering details: level-wise vs leaf-wise growth, exact vs histogram split, distributed training. ## Key concepts - [[decision-tree]] — single tree, Gini / Entropy impurity - [[random-forest]] — bagging ensemble of trees - [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]] — boosting ensemble (XGBoost, LightGBM) - [[gini-vs-entropy]] — impurity measures for splits ## Decision trees (slides 1–4) Recursively split feature space into rectangles until each leaf is "pure" (all one class). Two impurity measures: | | Gini index | Entropy | |---|---|---| | Formula | $G = \sum_k p_k(1 - p_k) = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$ | $H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$ | | Interpretation | Variance in the node | Uncertainty in the node | | Range | 0 (pure) → 0.5 (50/50 binary) | 0 (pure) → 1 (50/50 binary, base 2) | | Compute | Faster | Slower (log) | | Used in | scikit-learn default, most production GBDT | C4.5, ID3 | **Pros of DTs:** - Intuitive, easy to explain to businesses. - Cut feature space in (hyper)cuboids. - Handle categorical variables natively. - No feature normalisation needed. **Con:** **overfit easily** → use Random Forest or GBDT. ## Random Forest (slide 6) **Bagging (bootstrap aggregation):** 1. Sample $B$ bootstrap subsets *with replacement*. 2. Train a decision tree on each. 3. Final prediction = average (regression) or majority vote (classification). ```mermaid flowchart TD Data[(Full dataset)] --> B1[Bag 1
w/ replacement] Data --> B2[Bag 2] Data --> Bn[Bag B] B1 --> T1[Tree 1] B2 --> T2[Tree 2] Bn --> Tn[Tree B] T1 --> Avg[Average / vote] T2 --> Avg Tn --> Avg Avg --> Pred[Prediction] ``` - **Trains in parallel** (each tree independent). - Each bag is a representative subsample (sampling with replacement ensures we don't run out of data). - **Bagging is algorithm-agnostic** — used in RF but also applies to GBDT. ## Gradient-Boosting Decision Trees (slides 7–10) Sequential ensemble of weak trees, each fit to the **residual** of the previous ones. ### Additive training $$\hat y_i^{(t)} = \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)$$ where $f_t$ is the new tree at iteration $t$. ### Loss with regularisation $$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} = \sum_i \ell\big(y_i, \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)\big) + \Omega(f_t)$$ The regulariser $\Omega(f_t)$ penalises tree complexity (number of leaves, leaf weights). ### Taylor expansion GBDT (esp. XGBoost / LightGBM) approximates the loss using a 2nd-order Taylor expansion around $\hat y_i^{(t-1)}$: $$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} \approx \sum_i \left[g_i f_t(x_i) + \tfrac{1}{2} h_i f_t(x_i)^2\right] + \Omega(f_t)$$ where $g_i = \partial_{\hat y} \ell$ (gradient) and $h_i = \partial^2_{\hat y} \ell$ (Hessian). This makes the optimal split fast to compute. ### Tree-growth strategies (slide 10) | Strategy | What it does | Best for | |---|---|---| | **Level-wise** | Grow tree level by level | Small datasets | | **Leaf-wise** | Split the leaf with the highest loss change | Large datasets, faster, overfits without tuning | XGBoost defaults to level-wise; **LightGBM defaults to leaf-wise**. ### Split-finding strategies (slide 11) | | Exact | Histogram | |---|---|---| | Approach | Try every feature value | Bin features into a histogram, evaluate bin boundaries | | Speed | Slow | Fast | | Accuracy | Best | Marginally lower; equal in large datasets | LightGBM is histogram-only. XGBoost supports both. ## XGBoost vs LightGBM (slides 12–13) | | XGBoost | LightGBM | |---|---|---| | Author | DMLC | Microsoft | | Default growth | Level-wise | Leaf-wise | | Splits | Exact + histogram | Histogram only | | GPU | Yes | Yes | | Distributed | Yes (Spark, Dask) | Yes (Spark via SynapseML) | | Parameters | ~100 | 130+ | LightGBM on Spark (SynapseML) is **10-30% faster** than SparkML on the Higgs dataset and achieves **15% higher AUC**. ## Distributed LightGBM (slide 14) Data-parallel: dataset partitioned across Spark workers; workers build local histograms; histograms are **merged** for non-overlapping feature subsets across workers; workers find the local best split on the merged histograms; sync up the global best split. ## Open questions - When to use trees vs deep learning? Trees usually win on tabular data; DL on images/text/sequences. - Why is leaf-wise growth not the default everywhere? It overfits small datasets — needs regularisation tuning.

---
name: Decision tree
description: ML algorithm that recursively splits feature space into rectangles to minimise impurity at each leaf
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, machine-learning]
---

# Decision tree

A machine-learning algorithm that **recursively splits feature space** into (hyper)cuboids until each leaf is "pure" — all data points belonging to one class (classification) or close in value (regression).

## How it splits

At each node, find the (feature, threshold) that minimises a **purity / impurity** measure of the resulting children. Two standard measures: **[[gini-vs-entropy|Gini index and Entropy]]**.

A node is **pure** when all its data points are of one class (impurity = 0).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Root: feat A < 5?"]
    Root -->|yes| L1["feat B < 2?"]
    Root -->|no| L2["Class B"]
    L1 -->|yes| L3["Class A"]
    L1 -->|no| L4["Class B"]
```

## Pros

- **Intuitive and explainable** — easy to show stakeholders the split tree.
- Cuts feature space in axis-aligned rectangles.
- **Categorical variables** handled natively (no one-hot needed).
- **No feature normalisation** required (splits are based on ordering, not scale).
- Handles missing values gracefully.

## The one big con

**Overfit easily.** A deep tree memorises the training data and generalises poorly. Mitigations:

- **Pruning** — limit max depth, min samples per leaf.
- **[[random-forest|Random Forest]]** — bagging ensemble.
- **[[gradient-boosting-decision-trees|GBDT]]** — boosting ensemble.

The lecture's framing: trees as a single classifier overfit; the fix is always an ensemble.

## Use cases

- Tabular data (best class of model on most tabular benchmarks, often via GBDT).
- When you need an explainable model.
- As a weak learner inside ensembles.

## Related

- [[gini-vs-entropy]]
- [[random-forest]]
- [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]]
