---

# Decision tree

A machine-learning algorithm that **recursively splits feature space** into (hyper)cuboids until each leaf is "pure" — all data points belonging to one class (classification) or close in value (regression).

## How it splits

At each node, find the (feature, threshold) that minimises a **purity / impurity** measure of the resulting children. Two standard measures: **[[gini-vs-entropy|Gini index and Entropy]]**.

A node is **pure** when all its data points are of one class (impurity = 0).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Root: feat A < 5?"]
    Root -->|yes| L1["feat B < 2?"]
    Root -->|no| L2["Class B"]
    L1 -->|yes| L3["Class A"]
    L1 -->|no| L4["Class B"]
```

## Pros

- **Intuitive and explainable** — easy to show stakeholders the split tree.
- Cuts feature space in axis-aligned rectangles.
- **Categorical variables** handled natively (no one-hot needed).
- **No feature normalisation** required (splits are based on ordering, not scale).
- Handles missing values gracefully.

## The one big con

**Overfit easily.** A deep tree memorises the training data and generalises poorly. Mitigations:

- **Pruning** — limit max depth, min samples per leaf.
- **[[random-forest|Random Forest]]** — bagging ensemble.
- **[[gradient-boosting-decision-trees|GBDT]]** — boosting ensemble.

The lecture's framing: trees as a single classifier overfit; the fix is always an ensemble.

## Use cases

- Tabular data (best class of model on most tabular benchmarks, often via GBDT).
- When you need an explainable model.
- As a weak learner inside ensembles.

## Related

- [[gini-vs-entropy]]
- [[random-forest]]
- [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]]

---
name: Gini index vs Entropy
description: Two impurity measures for choosing splits in decision trees — Gini is faster, Entropy is information-theoretic
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, machine-learning, information-theory]
---

# Gini index vs Entropy

Two standard impurity measures for choosing splits in [[decision-tree|decision trees]]. Both are zero when a node is pure (all one class) and maximal when classes are evenly split.

## Definitions

For class probabilities $p_k$ in a node:

| Measure | Formula | Range (binary) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gini index** | $G = \sum_k p_k(1 - p_k) = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$ | 0 → 0.5 |
| **Entropy** | $H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$ | 0 → 1 |

Both measure *uncertainty* / *variance* in the node:

- 0 → all points in one class (pure).
- Maximal → uniform distribution over classes.

## Differences in practice

| | Gini | Entropy |
|---|---|---|
| Compute | Faster (no log) | Slower (log) |
| Origin | Economics (Gini coefficient — measures inequality) | Information theory (Shannon entropy) |
| Interpretation | Variance of node | Expected information drawn from the distribution |
| Default in | scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM | C4.5, ID3 |

**The numerical splits chosen by Gini and Entropy are usually similar.** Gini is preferred in production for the speed advantage; Entropy gets used in pedagogy because it ties to information theory.

## Mnemonic

- **Gini** — variance / inequality measure → fast → production.
- **Entropy** — information-theoretic uncertainty → slower → academic.

## Related

- [[decision-tree]] — uses these
- [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]] — also uses Gini for split-finding

content.newText:
---
name: Random Forest
description: Bagging ensemble of decision trees — train many trees on bootstrapped subsets in parallel, average their predictions
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, machine-learning, ensemble]
---

# Random Forest

A **bagging ensemble** of [[decision-tree|decision trees]] (Breiman 2001). The textbook fix for decision-tree overfitting.

## Bagging (bootstrap aggregation)

1. Sample $B$ subsets from the training data **with replacement** (so the same point can appear in multiple bags, and any one bag is likely to contain ~63% of the original points).
2. Train one decision tree on each subset.
3. Final prediction = average (regression) or majority vote (classification).

Sampling with replacement ensures we don't run out of datapoints and that each bag is a representative subsample.

## Why bagging reduces overfitting

Each tree overfits to its bag's idiosyncrasies, but the ensemble averages those idiosyncrasies out. Variance of an average of $B$ uncorrelated estimators is $1/B$ times that of one — so the more independent the trees, the lower the variance.

To reduce correlation between trees, RF also samples **a random subset of features** at each split (typically $\sqrt{n}$ for classification).

## Pros / cons

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Trains in **parallel** (each tree independent) | Doesn't reach the accuracy of [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees|GBDT]] on most tabular datasets |
| Robust to overfitting | Less interpretable than a single tree |
| Good baseline | Slow at inference (must traverse $B$ trees) |
| Feature importance for free | |

## Bagging is algorithm-agnostic

The "bagging" part — sampling, training in parallel, aggregating — works for any base learner, not just trees. The lecture flags this: bagging can be applied to GBDT or other ML algorithms too.

## Related

- [[decision-tree]] — the base learner
- [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]] — sequential alternative to bagging
- [[gini-vs-entropy]] — splits within each tree

---
name: Gradient-Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT)
description: Sequential ensemble of weak decision trees, each fit to the residuals of previous trees — XGBoost, LightGBM
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, machine-learning, ensemble]
---

# Gradient-Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT)

A **boosting ensemble**: sequentially add weak [[decision-tree|decision trees]], each fit to the *residual error* of the running prediction. Dominates structured/tabular ML benchmarks. Two production libraries: **XGBoost** and **LightGBM**.

## Additive training

$$\hat y_i^{(t)} = \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)$$

The new tree $f_t$ adds to the previous prediction.

## Loss with regularisation

$$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} = \sum_i \ell\big(y_i, \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)\big) + \Omega(f_t)$$

$\Omega(f_t)$ penalises tree complexity (number of leaves, leaf-weight magnitudes).

## 2nd-order Taylor approximation

XGBoost / LightGBM expand $\ell$ around $\hat y_i^{(t-1)}$ to 2nd order:

$$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} \approx \sum_i \left[g_i\, f_t(x_i) + \tfrac{1}{2} h_i\, f_t(x_i)^2\right] + \Omega(f_t)$$

with $g_i = \partial_{\hat y} \ell$ (gradient) and $h_i = \partial^2_{\hat y} \ell$ (Hessian). This makes the optimal split fast to find in closed form.

## Two growth strategies

| Strategy | Behaviour | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **Level-wise** | Grow tree level by level | Smaller datasets, balanced trees |
| **Leaf-wise** | Split the leaf with largest loss change | Large datasets, faster, can overfit without tuning |

XGBoost defaults to level-wise; **LightGBM defaults to leaf-wise**.

## Two split-finding strategies

| | Exact | Histogram |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Try every feature value | Bin features into a histogram, evaluate bin boundaries |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Accuracy | Best in tiny datasets | Equal in large datasets |

LightGBM is histogram-only; XGBoost supports both.

## XGBoost vs LightGBM

| | XGBoost | LightGBM |
|---|---|---|
| Default growth | Level-wise | Leaf-wise |
| Split-finding | Exact + histogram | Histogram only |
| Distributed | Spark, Dask | Spark via SynapseML |
| Author | DMLC | Microsoft |
| Headline number | — | 10-30% faster than SparkML on Higgs, 15% higher AUC |

## Why GBDT > Random Forest on most tabular data

- **Sequential error correction** — each tree fixes what the ensemble got wrong.
- More aggressive optimisation, often with fewer trees.
- Built-in regularisation via $\Omega(f_t)$.

The trade-off: trains sequentially, so harder to parallelise (vs RF which trains trees independently).

## Related

- [[decision-tree]] — the base learner
- [[random-forest]] — the bagging alternative
- [[gini-vs-entropy]] — split impurity (still applies in GBDT)

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 08
source: Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 08 — Decision Trees, RF, GBDT (flashcards)

In one sentence, what does a decision tree do?
?
**Recursively splits feature space into rectangles** until each leaf contains data points of mostly one class — minimising "impurity" at each split.

What is a "pure" node in a decision tree?
?
A node where all the data points belong to the **same class** — impurity = 0.

State the Gini index formula.
?
$G = \sum_k p_k(1 - p_k) = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$, where $p_k$ is the probability of picking a point from class $k$.

State the entropy formula for impurity.
?
$H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$, where $p_k$ is the probability of picking a point from class $k$.

What's the difference between Gini and entropy in practice?
?
**Gini** is faster to compute (no log) and is variance-flavoured; **entropy** is information-theoretic. Splits chosen by either are usually similar — Gini wins in production for speed.

When are Gini and entropy both zero?
?
When the node is **pure** — all data points belong to the same class.

Name three natural advantages of decision trees.
?
**Intuitive / explainable**, **handles categorical variables natively**, **no feature normalisation needed**.

What is the headline weakness of decision trees, and how is it fixed?
?
**They overfit easily.** Fixed by ensembles: **Random Forest** (bagging) or **GBDT** (boosting).

What is bagging?
?
**Bootstrap aggregation** — sample $B$ subsets of the data with replacement, train one model on each, then average (regression) or majority-vote (classification) the predictions.

Why does Random Forest sample with replacement?
?
Sampling with replacement ensures each bag is **representative** (large enough) and you don't run out of data points. The same point can appear in multiple bags.

How is Random Forest training parallelisable?
?
Each tree is **independent** of the others — you can train all $B$ trees in parallel.

What is GBDT in one sentence?
?
A **sequential ensemble** of weak decision trees, where each new tree is fit to the **residual error** of the previous trees.

Write the GBDT additive update.
?
$\hat y_i^{(t)} = \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)$, where $f_t$ is the new tree at iteration $t$.

What does the 2nd-order Taylor approximation buy GBDT?
?
$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} \approx \sum_i [g_i f_t(x_i) + \tfrac{1}{2} h_i f_t(x_i)^2] + \Omega(f_t)$, with gradient $g_i$ and Hessian $h_i$. This makes the optimal split **fast to find in closed form**.

What's the difference between level-wise and leaf-wise tree growth?
?
**Level-wise** grows the tree level by level (balanced; safer for small datasets). **Leaf-wise** splits the leaf with the **highest loss change** (faster; better for large datasets; overfits without tuning).

Which growth strategy does LightGBM default to?
?
**Leaf-wise** — gives faster training on large datasets at the cost of overfitting risk.

What's the difference between exact and histogram split-finding?
?
**Exact** evaluates every feature value (slow, marginally more accurate). **Histogram** bins features and evaluates bin boundaries (much faster, equal accuracy on large datasets).

Two production GBDT libraries the lecture mentions?
?
**XGBoost** (DMLC, supports exact + histogram) and **LightGBM** (Microsoft, histogram-only, leaf-wise default).

How is LightGBM distributed across Spark workers?
?
Each worker partitions the data, builds **local histograms**, then merges histograms for non-overlapping feature subsets across workers, finds local best splits on merged histograms, and syncs the global best split.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 09.pdf lecture: 09 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 09.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 09 — Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) ## Overview A loss function for **ranking** with implicit feedback. BPR (Rendle et al. 2009) addresses the headline problem of [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback|implicit data]]: pointwise classification treats unobserved items as negatives, but most "negatives" are just **missing positives** (items the user hasn't seen yet). BPR replaces pointwise loss with **pairwise loss**: only require that **observed > unobserved**, not that observed = 1 and unobserved = 0. ## Key concepts - [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss, MAP-style objective - [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]] — why pairwise wins on implicit data - [[negative-sampling-reco]] — what BPR is the principled response to ## Pointwise vs pairwise (slides 2–3) ```mermaid flowchart TD Imp["Implicit feedback
(positive-only)"] --> Pw["Pointwise loss"] Imp --> Pair["Pairwise loss (BPR)"] Pw --> Bug["Trains observed=1, unobserved=0
but unobserved = positive OR negative"] Pair --> Fix["Trains: observed > unobserved
(no commitment about absolute value)"] classDef good fill:#e3f2fd classDef bad fill:#ffebee class Pw,Bug bad class Pair,Fix good ``` **Pointwise (the bug):** model trained to predict 1 for observed pairs, 0 for unobserved. Almost all unobserved pairs are *missing positives* — items the user might love but hasn't seen. Training pushes those toward 0 → the model can't recommend them. **Pairwise (the fix):** for each user $u$, generate triples $(u, i, j)$ where $i$ is observed and $j$ is unobserved. Train so that $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$. The model never has to commit to absolute scores — only to *correct ordering*. ## Why pairwise data is useful (slide 3) - **Training set is structured:** positive, negative, unknown. - **Missing values between two unobserved items are exactly the pairs the model has to rank** at inference time → train and test are aligned. - **From a pairwise perspective, train and test are disjoint.** - The objective directly optimises ranking — not a proxy. ## BPR objective (slide 4) Maximise the posterior probability of the model parameters $\Theta$ given preference data $>_u$: $$p(\Theta | >_u) \propto p(>_u | \Theta) \cdot p(\Theta)$$ The likelihood that user $u$ prefers $i$ to $j$: $$p(i >_u j | \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$$ where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. With a Gaussian prior on $\Theta$ (i.e. L2 regularisation), the **MAP objective** to maximise is: $$\text{BPR-OPT} = \sum_{(u, i, j) \in D_S} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$$ Trained with SGD over sampled triples $(u, i, j)$. ## Where BPR fits - **Loss-function pluggable:** any model that produces $\hat r_{ui}$ can use BPR loss — matrix factorization, neural CF, sequential recommenders, GNNs. - **Implicit feedback default:** when ratings are absent, BPR is usually the right starting point. - **Generalises to explicit feedback:** can express user $u$ prefers $a$ over $b$ from a numerical rating ($r_{ua} > r_{ub}$). ## Equations recap | | | |---|---| | Posterior | $p(\Theta \| >_u) \propto p(>_u \| \Theta)\, p(\Theta)$ | | Likelihood | $p(i >_u j \| \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$ | | Prior | Normal: $\Theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ | | MAP objective | $\sum \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ | ## Open questions - How to choose negative-sampling distribution? (Uniform is the default; popularity-biased sampling helps in practice.) - Pairwise vs listwise (LambdaMART, ListNet)? Pairwise is simpler and usually sufficient.

BPR (Rendle et al. 2009) addresses the headline problem of [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback|implicit data]]: pointwise classification treats unobserved items as negatives, but most "negatives" are just **missing positives** (items the user hasn't seen yet). BPR replaces pointwise loss with **pairwise loss**: only require that **observed > unobserved**, not that observed = 1 and unobserved = 0. ## Key concepts - [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise ranking loss, MAP-style objective - [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]] — why pairwise wins on implicit data - [[negative-sampling-reco]] — what BPR is the principled response to ## Pointwise vs pairwise (slides 2–3) ```mermaid flowchart TD Imp["Implicit feedback
(positive-only)"] --> Pw["Pointwise loss"] Imp --> Pair["Pairwise loss (BPR)"] Pw --> Bug["Trains observed=1, unobserved=0
but unobserved = positive OR negative"] Pair --> Fix["Trains: observed > unobserved
(no commitment about absolute value)"] classDef good fill:#e3f2fd classDef bad fill:#ffebee class Pw,Bug bad class Pair,Fix good ``` **Pointwise (the bug):** model trained to predict 1 for observed pairs, 0 for unobserved. Almost all unobserved pairs are *missing positives* — items the user might love but hasn't seen. Training pushes those toward 0 → the model can't recommend them. **Pairwise (the fix):** for each user $u$, generate triples $(u, i, j)$ where $i$ is observed and $j$ is unobserved. Train so that $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$. The model never has to commit to absolute scores — only to *correct ordering*. ## Why pairwise data is useful (slide 3) - **Training set is structured:** positive, negative, unknown. - **Missing values between two unobserved items are exactly the pairs the model has to rank** at inference time → train and test are aligned. - **From a pairwise perspective, train and test are disjoint.** - The objective directly optimises ranking — not a proxy. ## BPR objective (slide 4) Maximise the posterior probability of the model parameters $\Theta$ given preference data $>_u$: $$p(\Theta | >_u) \propto p(>_u | \Theta) \cdot p(\Theta)$$ The likelihood that user $u$ prefers $i$ to $j$: $$p(i >_u j | \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$$ where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. With a Gaussian prior on $\Theta$ (i.e. L2 regularisation), the **MAP objective** to maximise is: $$\text{BPR-OPT} = \sum_{(u, i, j) \in D_S} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$$ Trained with SGD over sampled triples $(u, i, j)$. ## Where BPR fits - **Loss-function pluggable:** any model that produces $\hat r_{ui}$ can use BPR loss — matrix factorization, neural CF, sequential recommenders, GNNs. - **Implicit feedback default:** when ratings are absent, BPR is usually the right starting point. - **Generalises to explicit feedback:** can express user $u$ prefers $a$ over $b$ from a numerical rating ($r_{ua} > r_{ub}$). ## Equations recap | | | |---|---| | Posterior | $p(\Theta \| >_u) \propto p(>_u \| \Theta)\, p(\Theta)$ | | Likelihood | $p(i >_u j \| \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$ | | Prior | Normal: $\Theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ | | MAP objective | $\sum \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ | ## Open questions - How to choose negative-sampling distribution? (Uniform is the default; popularity-biased sampling helps in practice.) - Pairwise vs listwise (LambdaMART, ListNet)? Pairwise is simpler and usually sufficient.

---
name: BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking) loss
description: Pairwise ranking loss for implicit-feedback recommenders — observed items should rank above unobserved ones
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 09.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, ranking, loss-function]
---

# BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking) loss

A **pairwise ranking loss** for recommenders trained on implicit feedback (Rendle et al. 2009). The principled response to the [[negative-sampling-reco|negative-sampling problem]] — instead of forcing unobserved items to score 0, only require that observed items score *higher than* unobserved ones.

## The setup

For each user $u$:

- $I^+_u$ = observed items
- $I^-_u$ = unobserved items
- Triples $(u, i, j)$ where $i \in I^+_u$ and $j \in I^-_u$.

The model produces a score $\hat r_{u,\cdot}$ per (user, item). BPR trains:

$$\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$$

for as many triples as possible.

## The loss

$$p(i >_u j \mid \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$$

$$\text{BPR-OPT} = -\sum_{(u, i, j)} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) + \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$$

Maximising the log-likelihood = minimising negative log-sigmoid of score differences. The derivation is a MAP estimate with a Gaussian prior on $\Theta$, hence the name "Bayesian".

## Why pairwise beats pointwise on implicit data

| | Pointwise | Pairwise (BPR) |
|---|---|---|
| What it learns | $\hat r_{ui} \to 1$ for observed, $\to 0$ for unobserved | $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$ |
| Treats missing positives as | Negatives → drives them to 0 (wrong) | Just unranked relative to observed → no false signal |
| Aligned with ranking metric? | No (regression flavour) | Yes — directly optimises ordering |
| Train ≈ test distribution? | No | Yes — both are about ordering |

## SGD training

For each minibatch:

1. Sample a user $u$.
2. Sample observed item $i \in I^+_u$.
3. Sample unobserved item $j \in I^-_u$.
4. Update $\Theta$ to push $\hat r_{ui}$ up and $\hat r_{uj}$ down.

Cheap, scalable, model-agnostic.

## Pluggable into any scoring model

BPR is a **loss**, not a model. Any model producing $\hat r_{ui}$ can use BPR:

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] (BPR-MF — the canonical paper)
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[graph-neural-networks]] (LightGCN uses BPR)
- Sequential recommenders, GNNs, transformers

## Generalises to explicit feedback

If $u$ rated $a$ higher than $b$, you can express that as a pairwise preference $a >_u b$. BPR works in the explicit setting too.

## Related

- [[negative-sampling-reco]] — what BPR is the principled fix for
- [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]] — the contrast
- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — what BPR is aligned with

content.newText:
---
name: Pointwise vs pairwise loss (recommenders)
description: Two ways to train ranking models — predict per-item score (pointwise) vs predict per-pair preference (pairwise)
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 09.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, ranking, loss-function]
---

# Pointwise vs pairwise loss (recommenders)

Two paradigms for training ranking models. Choosing the right one is especially consequential for [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback|implicit feedback]] data.

## Pointwise

The model is trained to predict an **absolute score** per item — usually with binary cross-entropy or MSE.

$$\mathcal L_\text{point} = \sum_{(u,i)} \ell\big(\hat r_{ui},\, y_{ui}\big)$$

For implicit feedback, $y_{ui} = 1$ if observed, $0$ otherwise.

**Problem on implicit data:** unobserved entries get label 0, but most are *missing positives* (items the user might love but hasn't seen). The model is trained to push their predictions to 0 → can't recommend them.

## Pairwise

The model is trained on **pairs** $(u, i, j)$ where the user prefers $i$ over $j$ (e.g. observed $i$, unobserved $j$). Loss only requires correct ordering, not absolute values.

$$\mathcal L_\text{pair} = \sum_{(u, i, j)} -\ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) + \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$$

This is **[[bpr-loss|BPR loss]]** in compact form.

## Comparison

| | Pointwise | Pairwise |
|---|---|---|
| Trains on | (user, item, label) | (user, pos item, neg item) |
| Implicit feedback | Treats missing as negative — biased | Treats missing as unranked relative to observed — correct |
| Aligned with ranking metrics? | No | Yes |
| Model output | Calibrated probability | Just an ordering score |
| Use when | Calibrated probabilities matter (CTR prediction) | Top-K ranking is the goal (most reco) |

## Listwise (a third option)

Listwise losses (e.g. LambdaMART, ListNet) optimise the full ranked list at once. More aligned with metrics like NDCG, but more complex. For most reco settings, pairwise is enough.

## Related

- [[bpr-loss]] — the canonical pairwise loss
- [[negative-sampling-reco]] — what's broken about pointwise
- [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — what pairwise is aligned with

# Lecture 09 — BPR (flashcards)

What does BPR stand for and what does it optimise?
?
**Bayesian Personalized Ranking** — it optimises a **pairwise ranking loss**: observed items should rank higher than unobserved ones for each user.

What's the headline problem with pointwise loss on implicit feedback?
?
Unobserved items get label 0, but many are **missing positives** (items the user might love but hasn't seen). Pointwise loss pushes those predictions to 0 → the model can't recommend them.

How does BPR avoid the missing-positives problem?
?
It only requires $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$ for observed $i$ vs unobserved $j$. The model never has to commit to absolute scores — only correct ordering.

Write the BPR likelihood that user $u$ prefers $i$ over $j$.
?
$p(i >_u j \mid \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$, where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid.

Write the BPR-OPT objective.
?
$\sum_{(u,i,j) \in D_S} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ — maximise log-sigmoid of score differences with L2 regularisation.

Why is "Bayesian" in the name BPR?
?
The objective is derived as a **MAP estimate** with a Gaussian prior on $\Theta$ — i.e. maximising $p(>_u \mid \Theta) \cdot p(\Theta)$.

Can BPR be applied to explicit feedback?
?
**Yes** — set user preferences as pairs ($u$ rated $a$ higher than $b$ → $a >_u b$).

Is BPR a model or a loss?
?
A **loss function**. It can be plugged into any model that produces $\hat r_{ui}$ — matrix factorization, neural CF, GNNs (LightGCN), sequential recommenders.

Why is the train and test distribution aligned in pairwise training?
?
Both train and test are about **ranking** — at training the model learns to order observed > unobserved; at test time we rank candidates. The objective and the metric agree.

What does the SGD step for BPR look like?
?
Sample (user $u$, observed item $i$, unobserved item $j$); update $\Theta$ to push $\hat r_{ui}$ up and $\hat r_{uj}$ down.

# Lecture 10a — Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF)

## Overview

[[matrix-factorization-reco|Matrix factorization]] uses an inner product to combine user and item factors — a *linear* combination. **Neural Collaborative Filtering** (He et al., WWW 2017) replaces that with a neural network so the user-item interaction can be **non-linear** and more expressive. The lecture covers two NCF variants:

- **GMF** (Generalized Matrix Factorization) — element-wise product of user and item embeddings, then a learned weighted sum.
- **MLP** — concatenate user and item embeddings, push through a feedforward network.

NCF can fuse both (GMF + MLP) for even more expressivity.

## Key concepts

- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — GMF + MLP architectures
- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — what NCF generalises
- [[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco]] — NCF's loss function
- [[negative-sampling-reco]] — NCF samples negatives during training

## GMF — Generalised Matrix Factorization

A neural CF layer is added to classic MF:

$$\hat y_{ui} = a\big(\mathbf{h}^T (\mathbf{p}_u \odot \mathbf{q}_i)\big)$$

where $\odot$ is element-wise product, $\mathbf h$ are output-layer weights, and $a$ is an activation function.

**Special case:** if $a = \text{identity}$ and $\mathbf h$ = uniform 1, you recover the original MF $\hat y_{ui} = \mathbf p_u^T \mathbf q_i$.

The added expressivity:

- **Edge weights are learned** (not fixed to 1).
- **Non-linear activation** (sigmoid, ReLU) — the model can capture non-linear preference patterns.

## MLP

For higher flexibility, **concatenate** user and item embeddings (rather than element-wise multiply) and push through a feedforward network:

$$\mathbf z_1 = [\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i]$$

For each hidden layer $L$:

$$\mathbf z_{L+1} = a_L(\mathbf W_L \mathbf z_L + \mathbf b_L)$$

Output layer with sigmoid:

$$\hat y_{ui} = \sigma(\mathbf w_\text{out}^T \mathbf z_\text{last})$$

This can model **arbitrary** non-linear interactions. GMF can only do element-wise — MLP can do concatenation-then-mix.

## NCF (fusion)

Combine GMF and MLP for even higher flexibility — they learn **separate embeddings**, which are concatenated in the last hidden layer before the output:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User one-hot] --> EG[Embed: GMF]
    U --> EM1[Embed: MLP]
    I[Item one-hot] --> EG2[Embed: GMF]
    I --> EM2[Embed: MLP]
    EG --> Mul[u ⊙ i]
    EG2 --> Mul
    EM1 --> Concat[Concat]
    EM2 --> Concat
    Concat --> H1[Hidden] --> H2[Hidden]
    Mul --> Final[Concat with MLP last]
    H2 --> Final
    Final --> Out["σ output"]
    classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd
    class Mul,Concat,Final reco
```

## Loss

**Binary cross-entropy:**

$$\mathcal L = -\sum_{(u, i)} y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui}) \log (1 - \hat y_{ui})$$

with $y_{ui} = 1$ for observed pairs and $0$ for sampled [[negative-sampling-reco|negatives]]. Could equivalently use [[bpr-loss|BPR]] for ranking-aligned training.

## NCF implementation (slide 5)

- TensorFlow original.
- Built-in **negative sampling** for training.
- Built-in **leave-one-out evaluation protocol** (hold out one item per user).
- Hyperparameters: number of factors (latent dim), layer sizes, number of epochs.

## Open questions

- Does NCF outperform MF? Yes (per the paper) on MovieLens / Pinterest. Some replication studies have questioned the magnitude under proper hyperparameter tuning.
- Is the gain from non-linearity or just having more parameters? Open in the literature.

# Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF)

Generalises [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]] (He et al., WWW 2017) by **replacing the inner product with a neural network**. The user-item interaction can now be **non-linear** and far more expressive.

## Three model variants

### GMF — Generalized Matrix Factorization

$$\hat y_{ui} = a\big(\mathbf{h}^T (\mathbf{p}_u \odot \mathbf{q}_i)\big)$$

- $\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i$ — element-wise product of user and item embeddings (size $f$).
- $\mathbf h$ — learned output weights.
- $a$ — non-linear activation (sigmoid, ReLU).

Special case: $a = \text{identity}$ and $\mathbf h$ all 1s → recover MF $\mathbf p_u^T \mathbf q_i$.

### MLP

$$\hat y_{ui} = \sigma\big(\text{MLP}([\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i])\big)$$

Concatenate user and item embeddings, push through a feedforward network with hidden layers, end in a sigmoid for probability.

### NeuMF (fusion)

Combine GMF and MLP — each learns its own embeddings. Concatenate the two outputs in the final hidden layer before predicting.

## Loss

[[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco|Binary cross-entropy]] with [[negative-sampling-reco|negative sampling]]:

$$\mathcal L = -\sum_{(u, i)} y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui})\log(1 - \hat y_{ui})$$

## Why "more expressive" than MF

MF is *linear* in the latent dimensions — the score is a single inner product. NCF's MLP can express non-linear interactions like "user $u$ likes item $i$ only if dimension 3 of $\mathbf p_u$ AND dimension 5 of $\mathbf q_i$ are both above threshold" — patterns MF can never capture.

## Practical notes

- Originally implemented in TensorFlow.
- Built-in **negative sampling** during training.
- Standard evaluation: **leave-one-out** per user.
- Hyperparameters: number of latent factors, MLP layer sizes, number of epochs.

## Trade-offs vs MF

| | MF | NCF |
|---|---|---|
| Expressivity | Linear | Non-linear |
| Parameters | $O((|U| + |I|) \cdot f)$ | More (extra MLP weights) |
| Training | SGD or ALS | SGD only |
| Cold-start | Same as MF | Same — no help here |

## Related

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — the special case
- [[wide-and-deep]] — combines memorisation (wide) and generalisation (deep) — NCF lineage
- [[bpr-loss]] — alternative ranking-aligned loss
- [[graph-neural-networks]] — message-passing alternative for CF

# Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF)

Generalises [[matrix-factorization-reco|matrix factorization]] (He et al., WWW 2017) by **replacing the inner product with a neural network**. The user-item interaction can now be **non-linear** and far more expressive.

## Three model variants

### GMF — Generalized Matrix Factorization

$$\hat y_{ui} = a\big(\mathbf{h}^T (\mathbf{p}_u \odot \mathbf{q}_i)\big)$$

- $\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i$ — element-wise product of user and item embeddings (size $f$).
- $\mathbf h$ — learned output weights.
- $a$ — non-linear activation (sigmoid, ReLU).

Special case: $a = \text{identity}$ and $\mathbf h$ all 1s → recover MF $\mathbf p_u^T \mathbf q_i$.

### MLP

$$\hat y_{ui} = \sigma\big(\text{MLP}([\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i])\big)$$

Concatenate user and item embeddings, push through a feedforward network with hidden layers, end in a sigmoid for probability.

### NeuMF (fusion)

Combine GMF and MLP — each learns its own embeddings. Concatenate the two outputs in the final hidden layer before predicting.

## Loss

[[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco|Binary cross-entropy]] with [[negative-sampling-reco|negative sampling]]:

$$\mathcal L = -\sum_{(u, i)} y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui})\log(1 - \hat y_{ui})$$

## Why "more expressive" than MF

MF is *linear* in the latent dimensions — the score is a single inner product. NCF's MLP can express non-linear interactions like "user $u$ likes item $i$ only if dimension 3 of $\mathbf p_u$ AND dimension 5 of $\mathbf q_i$ are both above threshold" — patterns MF can never capture.

## Practical notes

- Originally implemented in TensorFlow.
- Built-in **negative sampling** during training.
- Standard evaluation: **leave-one-out** per user.
- Hyperparameters: number of latent factors, MLP layer sizes, number of epochs.

## Trade-offs vs MF

| | MF | NCF |
|---|---|---|
| Expressivity | Linear | Non-linear |
| Parameters | $O((|U| + |I|) \cdot f)$ | More (extra MLP weights) |
| Training | SGD or ALS | SGD only |
| Cold-start | Same as MF | Same — no help here |

## Related

- [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — the special case
- [[wide-and-deep]] — combines memorisation (wide) and generalisation (deep) — NCF lineage
- [[bpr-loss]] — alternative ranking-aligned loss
- [[graph-neural-networks]] — message-passing alternative for CF

content.newText:
---
name: Binary cross-entropy loss (recommenders)
description: Pointwise loss for binary preference modelling — used by NCF, DeepFM, Wide & Deep
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 10a.pdf
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 10b.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, loss-function]
---

# Binary cross-entropy loss (recommenders)

The standard pointwise loss for binary preference / CTR-style prediction.

$$\mathcal L = -\sum_{(u, i)} y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui}) \log (1 - \hat y_{ui})$$

with:

- $y_{ui} \in \{0, 1\}$ — observed = 1, sampled negative = 0.
- $\hat y_{ui} \in (0, 1)$ — model's predicted probability (usually after sigmoid).

## Where it shows up

- **[[neural-collaborative-filtering|NCF]]** — implicit feedback, sigmoid output.
- **[[wide-and-deep|Wide & Deep]]** — binary outputs.
- **CTR prediction** — ad clicks, recommended-item clicks.

## Pros

- Outputs **calibrated probabilities** — meaningful for downstream business logic (expected revenue calculations).
- Easy to combine with sampled negatives.
- Fast and well-understood.

## Cons

- **Pointwise** — same problem as discussed in [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]]: treating sampled negatives as label 0 hurts ranking when many "negatives" are missing positives.
- For pure top-K ranking, **[[bpr-loss|BPR]]** is usually better aligned.

## Numerical stability

In practice you don't compute $-\log(\sigma(x))$ — you use the **`logsigmoid`** or `binary_cross_entropy_with_logits` to avoid overflow when $x$ is large.

## Related

- [[bpr-loss]] — pairwise alternative
- [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]]
- [[neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[wide-and-deep]]

---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 10a
source: Chatbots and Reco 10a.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 10a — Neural Collaborative Filtering (flashcards)

What does NCF stand for?
?
**Neural Collaborative Filtering** — He et al., WWW 2017. Replaces the inner product in matrix factorization with a neural network for non-linear interactions.

Write the GMF (Generalized MF) equation.
?
$\hat y_{ui} = a(\mathbf h^T (\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i))$ — element-wise product of user/item embeddings, learned output weights $\mathbf h$, activation $a$.

What special case of GMF recovers original MF?
?
Set $a$ to the identity and $\mathbf h$ to the all-ones vector — then $\hat y_{ui} = \mathbf p_u^T \mathbf q_i$.

What's the difference between GMF and the MLP variant of NCF?
?
**GMF** uses element-wise product of embeddings. **MLP** *concatenates* user and item embeddings and pushes them through a feedforward network — strictly more flexible.

What does NeuMF (the fusion model) do?
?
Combines GMF and MLP — each learns separate embeddings, and the two outputs are concatenated in the final hidden layer before the output.

What loss function does NCF use?
?
**Binary cross-entropy** with sampled negatives: $\mathcal L = -\sum y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1-y_{ui})\log(1-\hat y_{ui})$.

Why is NCF "more expressive" than MF?
?
MF combines latent dims linearly via inner product. NCF's MLP can model **non-linear** interactions like "user $u$ likes item $i$ only when dim 3 of $\mathbf p_u$ AND dim 5 of $\mathbf q_i$ are both above threshold."

What evaluation protocol is built into NCF?
?
**Leave-one-out** — hold out one item per user as the test positive; rank it against random unobserved negatives.

What's the role of negative sampling in NCF training?
?
Implicit feedback is positive-only. NCF samples unobserved (user, item) pairs as label 0 to enable binary classification. Risk: missing positives sampled as negatives (see BPR for the principled fix).

--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 10b.pdf lecture: 10b slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 10b.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 10b — Wide and Deep ## Overview **Wide & Deep** (Cheng et al. 2016, Google Play Store) jointly trains two complementary models — a **wide linear model** (good at *memorisation* of feature crosses) and a **deep MLP** (good at *generalisation* via embeddings) — through a shared output. Designed for app recommendation; the canonical hybrid architecture and the conceptual ancestor of DeepFM, DCN, and most modern CTR models. ## Key concepts - [[wide-and-deep]] — the joint architecture - [[memorization-vs-generalization]] — the design rationale - [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — sibling architecture (deep without wide) ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Input[Sparse features
+ embeddings] --> Wide[Wide model:
generalised linear
+ feature crosses] Input --> Deep[Deep model:
embedded → MLP
+ ReLU] Wide --> Sum["Σ + sigmoid"] Deep --> Sum Sum --> Out["P(click)"] classDef wide fill:#e3f2fd classDef deep fill:#f3e5f5 class Wide wide class Deep deep ``` ## The two halves ### Wide (memorisation) Generalised linear model: $$y_\text{wide} = \mathbf w^T \mathbf x + b$$ $\mathbf x$ contains raw features and **cross-product transformations** (manually engineered first-order conjunctions): "user installed gmail AND impression is youtube". These crosses **memorise** strong co-occurrence patterns directly from data — perfect for "users like X also like Y" signals. ### Deep (generalisation) A standard MLP with ReLU activations: $$\mathbf a^{(L+1)} = f(\mathbf W^{(L)} \mathbf a^{(L)} + \mathbf b^{(L)})$$ Inputs to the first hidden layer are the **embedding vectors** of categorical features. The deep network *generalises* — embedding similarity carries over to unseen feature combinations. ## Joint training **Both halves are trained jointly** (not as an ensemble). For binary outputs (click / no click): $$P(Y = 1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma\big(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b\big)$$ Optimised with [[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco|binary cross-entropy]]. > "Wide for memorisation, deep for generalisation, joint for both." ## Memorisation vs generalisation (slide 1) | Wide | Deep | |---|---| | Memorises **frequent co-occurrences** | Generalises via **transitivity of correlation** | | Specific (item-A-then-item-B is a known pattern) | Diverse (similar embeddings → similar recs) | | Weak on long tail | Strong on long tail | | Engineered feature crosses | Learned embeddings | The combination handles both dimensions: hot common patterns AND novel/rare combinations. ## Implementation note (slide 4) Genres are fed in via **multi-hot encoding** with scikit-learn's `MultiLabelBinarizer`. E.g. movie 2355 has Animation, Children's, Comedy → `[0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, ...]`. ## Why it became the template Wide & Deep started a wave: **DeepFM** (FM as the wide part), **DCN** (Deep & Cross with explicit cross layers), **xDeepFM** (FM × deep with vector-wise interactions). All share the "wide for memorisation + deep for generalisation, jointly trained" recipe. ## Open questions - Wide vs FM as the memorisation half — when does each win? FM scales to bigger feature crosses, wide is simpler. - Why jointly trained vs ensembled? Joint training lets each half compensate for the other's errors *during training*; ensembles stack post-hoc.

--- name: Wide and Deep description: Hybrid recommender — jointly trained linear "wide" model (memorisation) + MLP "deep" model (generalisation) type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 10b.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, recommender-systems, deep-learning] --- # Wide and Deep A hybrid architecture (Cheng et al. 2016, Google Play Store) that **jointly trains** two complementary halves: - **Wide** — a generalised linear model with engineered cross-features. Good at **memorising** frequent co-occurrences. - **Deep** — an MLP over learned embeddings. Good at **generalising** to unseen feature combinations. > **Slogan:** Wide for memorisation, Deep for generalisation, Joint for both. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Sparse[Sparse features
+ embeddings] --> W[Wide:
x + cross(x)] Sparse --> D[Deep:
embed → MLP + ReLU] W --> Add[+] D --> Add Add --> Out["σ → P(click)"] classDef wide fill:#e3f2fd classDef deep fill:#f3e5f5 class W wide class D deep ``` ## Wide half Generalised linear model: $$y_\text{wide} = \mathbf w^T \mathbf x + b$$ $\mathbf x$ contains raw features and **manually engineered cross-product transformations** (`installed_app=gmail × impression_app=youtube`). These crosses memorise specific co-occurrence patterns directly. ## Deep half Standard MLP over **embedded** categorical features: $$\mathbf a^{(L+1)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf W^{(L)} \mathbf a^{(L)} + \mathbf b^{(L)})$$ Embeddings let the model generalise: similar items have similar vectors → similar recommendations even for unseen pairs. ## Joint training **Trained together**, not stacked / ensembled: $$P(Y = 1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma\big(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b\big)$$ Optimised with [[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco|binary cross-entropy]]. Joint training lets each half compensate for the other's errors during gradient updates. ## Why this combination Wide-only fails on long tail (no embeddings → can't generalise). Deep-only struggles with rare-but-important hot conjunctions (the embedding doesn't have enough signal). Combining them covers both. ## Lineage Wide & Deep started a wave of hybrid CTR architectures: - **DeepFM** (FM as the wide half) - **DCN** (Deep & Cross — explicit cross layers) - **xDeepFM** (vector-wise FM × deep) - **AutoInt** (attention as feature interaction) ## Use case CTR prediction, ad ranking, app store recommendation, news / video / product recommendation. Anywhere with sparse categorical features and abundant interaction data. ## Related - [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — deep-only sibling - [[factorization-machines]] — what DeepFM uses for the wide half - [[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco]] — the loss - [[content-based-filtering]] — wide is content-style; deep is CF-style

content.newText:
---
name: Memorization vs Generalization (recommenders)
description: The design tension behind hybrid recommenders — wide models memorise frequent patterns, deep models generalise via embeddings
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 10b.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, deep-learning]
---

# Memorization vs Generalization (recommenders)

The design tension behind [[wide-and-deep|Wide & Deep]] and its descendants. Two different jobs that recommenders need to do simultaneously, and which call for opposite tools.

## Memorization

> Learn frequent **co-occurrence** patterns directly from data.

- Implementation: **wide linear model with cross-product features** (`feature_A AND feature_B`).
- Strength: captures specific, hot, high-confidence patterns ("users who installed Gmail click on YouTube ads").
- Weakness: **brittle on the long tail** — a feature combination that's never been seen has weight zero.

## Generalization

> Recommend things that are *similar* to what worked before, even when the exact combination is new.

- Implementation: **deep MLP over learned embeddings**.
- Strength: similar items have similar embeddings → recommendations transfer to unseen pairs.
- Weakness: **noisy on rare specifics** — embedding similarity can over-generalise (the model recommends action movies to a user whose only action movie was a fluke).

## Why you want both

| | Memorization wins | Generalization wins |
|---|---|---|
| Hot common pattern | ✅ | ⚠️ over-smooths |
| Long tail | ❌ no signal | ✅ embedding fills in |
| Cold-start | ❌ | ⚠️ if features overlap |
| Diversity | ❌ same recs | ✅ explores |

Pure memorisation = popularity recommender. Pure generalisation = blurry, diverse recommendations that miss known-good signals.

## The Wide & Deep recipe

**Joint training** of both, not ensemble. Each half learns a complementary signal under a single shared loss. The wide half catches the hot specifics; the deep half handles everything else.

## Outside reco

The same memorisation/generalisation duality shows up:

- **Knowledge graphs vs language models** — KGs memorise explicit facts; LMs generalise via embedding similarity. RAG combines them.
- **Decision trees vs neural networks** — trees memorise (axis-aligned splits); NNs generalise (smooth function approximation).

## Related

- [[wide-and-deep]] — the canonical instantiation
- [[content-based-filtering]] — generalisation-style on features
- [[collaborative-filtering]] — memorisation-style on interactions

tags: [concept, recommender-systems, deep-learning]
---

# Memorization vs Generalization (recommenders)

The design tension behind [[wide-and-deep|Wide & Deep]] and its descendants. Two different jobs that recommenders need to do simultaneously, and which call for opposite tools.

## Memorization

> Learn frequent **co-occurrence** patterns directly from data.

- Implementation: **wide linear model with cross-product features** (`feature_A AND feature_B`).
- Strength: captures specific, hot, high-confidence patterns ("users who installed Gmail click on YouTube ads").
- Weakness: **brittle on the long tail** — a feature combination that's never been seen has weight zero.

## Generalization

> Recommend things that are *similar* to what worked before, even when the exact combination is new.

- Implementation: **deep MLP over learned embeddings**.
- Strength: similar items have similar embeddings → recommendations transfer to unseen pairs.
- Weakness: **noisy on rare specifics** — embedding similarity can over-generalise (the model recommends action movies to a user whose only action movie was a fluke).

## Why you want both

| | Memorization wins | Generalization wins |
|---|---|---|
| Hot common pattern | ✅ | ⚠️ over-smooths |
| Long tail | ❌ no signal | ✅ embedding fills in |
| Cold-start | ❌ | ⚠️ if features overlap |
| Diversity | ❌ same recs | ✅ explores |

Pure memorisation = popularity recommender. Pure generalisation = blurry, diverse recommendations that miss known-good signals.

## The Wide & Deep recipe

**Joint training** of both, not ensemble. Each half learns a complementary signal under a single shared loss. The wide half catches the hot specifics; the deep half handles everything else.

## Outside reco

The same memorisation/generalisation duality shows up:

- **Knowledge graphs vs language models** — KGs memorise explicit facts; LMs generalise via embedding similarity. RAG combines them.
- **Decision trees vs neural networks** — trees memorise (axis-aligned splits); NNs generalise (smooth function approximation).

## Related

- [[wide-and-deep]] — the canonical instantiation
- [[content-based-filtering]] — generalisation-style on features
- [[collaborative-filtering]] — memorisation-style on interactions

# Lecture 10b — Wide & Deep (flashcards)

What does Wide & Deep combine, and what does each half do?
?
A **wide linear model** (memorises frequent co-occurrences via cross-feature engineering) and a **deep MLP** (generalises via learned embeddings). Both are **trained jointly**, not ensembled.

What does the "wide" part learn?
?
**Memorisation** — frequent feature co-occurrences via engineered cross-product transformations like `installed_app=gmail × impression_app=youtube`.

What does the "deep" part learn?
?
**Generalisation** — embedding-based similarity that transfers to unseen feature combinations.

Are wide and deep ensembled or trained jointly?
?
**Jointly trained.** Joint training lets each half compensate for the other's errors *during gradient updates*; ensembles stack post-hoc.

Write the joint Wide & Deep prediction.
?
$P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma\big(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b\big)$, with cross-features $\phi(\mathbf x)$.

What problem does memorisation alone fail to handle?
?
**The long tail / cold combinations** — patterns never seen in the training data have weight zero in the wide model.

What problem does generalisation alone struggle with?
?
**Hot common patterns** — embedding similarity can over-smooth, missing strong specific co-occurrences that a memorisation model would catch directly.

Where does Wide & Deep come from, and what was its original use case?
?
**Cheng et al. 2016, Google Play Store** — recommending apps for the Google Play Store.

What two types of features feed the deep half?
?
**Embedded categorical features** (e.g. embeddings of users, items, app categories) — the embeddings provide the generalisation signal.

What loss function does Wide & Deep use for binary outputs?
?
**Binary cross-entropy** with sigmoid output: $\mathcal L = -\sum y \log \hat y + (1-y)\log(1-\hat y)$.

What encoding is used for movie genres in the lecture's implementation?
?
**Multi-hot encoding** via scikit-learn's `MultiLabelBinarizer`. E.g. movie 2355 (Animation, Children's, Comedy) → `[0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, ...]`.

--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf lecture: 11 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 11 — Sequential and session-based recommenders ## Overview Many recommendations happen **in sequence** — e-commerce browse → cart → checkout, music next-track, news feed scroll. Two related but distinct problems: - **Sequential recommendation** — each user has identity; combine the user's **historical preferences** with their **recent actions**. Examples: Markov chains, RNNs, CNNs, Transformers. - **Session-based recommendation** — **no user identity**; each session is handled independently. Examples: neighborhood methods, LSTMs, Transformers. The lecture surveys four neural architectures applied to these tasks: **GRU4Rec**, **LSTM-based**, **CNN/dilated-CNN-based** ("Caser", "NextItNet"), and **Transformer-based** (SSE-PT, SASRec). ## Key concepts - [[sequential-vs-session-based]] — the distinction - [[gru4rec]] — first major neural session-based recommender - [[sequential-cnn-reco]] — Caser, NextItNet (dilated) - [[transformer-encoder]] — used by SASRec, SSE-PT - [[multi-head-attention]] — the attention mechanism transformers use ## Sequential vs session-based (slide 1) | | Sequential | Session-based | |---|---|---| | **User identity?** | Yes | No | | **What it combines** | Long-term history + recent actions | Just within-session actions | | **Examples** | Markov chains, RNNs, CNNs, Transformers | Neighborhood methods, LSTMs, Transformers | | **Use case** | Logged-in e-commerce | Anonymous browsing, cold-user fallback | ## Sequential data layout (slide 2) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Session 1: i1, i2, i3, i4] --> Batch[Session-parallel
mini-batches] S2[Session 2: i1, i2, i3] --> Batch S3[Session 3: i1, ..., i6] --> Batch Batch --> Model[Recurrent
model] classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd class Batch reco ``` - **Session-parallel mini-batches** — each batch row carries one session. - Sessions are **independent** — when one ends, the next available session takes its slot. Hidden state is reset. - Variable-length sequences handled by replacement, not padding. ## GRU-based (GRU4Rec, slide 3) Hidasi et al. ICLR 2016 — first major neural session-based recommender. ```mermaid flowchart LR Input[One-hot item] --> Emb[Embedding] Emb --> G1[GRU layer] G1 --> Gm[More GRU layers] Gm --> FF[Feedforward] FF --> Out["Scores over items"] ``` - Input = current item (one-hot encoded, often with embedding). - Output = predicted next item in the session. - Items earlier in the session are weighted with a **discount**. ## LSTM-based (slide 4) LSTMs handle longer sequences than GRUs but are limited by sequence length. Three useful modifications: - **Time-aware gating** — gating logic varies with the time interval between events. - **Attention** — filter irrelevant items, learn varying influence. - **Matrix factorisation hybrid** — long-term behaviour via [[matrix-factorization-reco|MF]] + short-term via LSTM. ## CNN-based (Caser, slide 5) Tang & Wang, WSDM 2018. **Embed a window of recent items as an "image"** in (time, latent) space. - **Vertical filters** — encode how single items influence the next item. - **Horizontal filters** — group the influence of items. - Use the previous $L$ actions (e.g. $L=4$) to predict the next $T$ items (e.g. $T=2$). ## Dilated CNN (NextItNet, slide 6) Yuan et al. WSDM 2019. **Dilated convolutions** model long-range dependencies efficiently: - Increase the **receptive field without adding parameters**. - Filter applied over a field larger than its original length by dilating with zeros (also called "holed" or "sparse" filter). - Captures features at multiple scales — analogous to wavelet decomposition. ## Transformer-based (SSE-PT / SASRec, slide 7) Wu et al. 2020 (SSE-PT). Transformer + [[multi-head-attention|self-attention]] applied to user action sequences. Generally **outperforms LSTMs and CNNs for sequential recommendation** — same lesson as in NLP. - Self-attention focuses on items relevant to the next action. - Often combined with [[bpr-loss|BPR]] for ranking-aligned training. ## Open questions - When does session-based beat sequential? When users are anonymous, or when long-term history is noisy/stale. - Are transformers always best? Yes for accuracy, often loses on serving latency vs GRU.

---
name: Sequential vs session-based recommendation
description: Two related-but-distinct recommendation problems — sequential uses long-term user history, session-based uses only within-session events
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems]
---

# Sequential vs session-based recommendation

Two related but distinct problems within sequence-aware recommendation.

## Sequential recommendation

**User identity is known.** Combine the user's **historical preferences** (long-term) with their **recent actions** (short-term).

Use case: logged-in e-commerce, music streaming with user accounts, video recommendation.

Models: Markov chains, RNNs, CNNs, Transformers (e.g. SASRec, BERT4Rec).

## Session-based recommendation

**No user identity.** Each session is handled independently — even if the same user comes back tomorrow, the model treats it as a fresh session.

Use case: anonymous browsing, cold-user fallback, privacy-restricted contexts.

Models: neighborhood methods, [[gru4rec|GRU4Rec]], LSTMs, Transformers.

## Side-by-side

| | Sequential | Session-based |
|---|---|---|
| User identity | Yes | No |
| Combines | Long-term + short-term | Within-session only |
| Memory of past sessions | Yes | No |
| Cold-start friendly | No (needs history) | **Yes** (no user model needed) |
| Typical model classes | RNN, CNN, Transformer with user embeddings | RNN, Transformer without user embeddings |

## When to use each

- **Sequential** if you have user IDs and a meaningful history.
- **Session-based** when users are anonymous, when you want to handle cold-start gracefully, or when long-term history is noisy/stale.
- Hybrids: a sequential model that gracefully falls back to session-based for cold users.

## Related

- [[gru4rec]] — first major neural session-based recommender
- [[sequential-cnn-reco]] — CNN-style sequence models
- [[transformer-encoder]] — used by SASRec, SSE-PT, BERT4Rec
- [[multi-head-attention]]

content.newText:
--- name: GRU4Rec description: GRU-based session-parallel recurrent model — first major neural session-based recommender type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, recommender-systems, deep-learning, sequential] --- # GRU4Rec The first major neural [[sequential-vs-session-based|session-based recommender]] (Hidasi, Karatzoglou, Baltrunas, Tikk — ICLR 2016). Uses **GRU networks** with **session-parallel mini-batches** to predict the next item in a session. ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[Item one-hot encoded] --> Emb[Embedding lookup] Emb --> G1[GRU layer 1] G1 --> G2[GRU layer 2] G2 --> FF[Feedforward] FF --> Out["Scores over all items
(softmax / sampled)"] ``` - Input: actual current state of the session (one-hot of current item). - Output: predicted next item. - Items earlier in the session get a **discount weight** (recency boost). ## Session-parallel mini-batches The clever data layout: ``` Session 1: i1.1 → i1.2 → i1.3 → i1.4 Session 2: i2.1 → i2.2 → i2.3 Session 3: i3.1 → i3.2 → i3.3 → i3.4 → i3.5 → i3.6 Session 4: i4.1 → i4.2 Session 5: i5.1 → i5.2 → i5.3 ``` Each batch row carries one session in time. When a session ends, the next available session takes its slot in the batch — and the **hidden state for that row is reset** so behaviour from the previous session doesn't leak. ## Why GRU and not LSTM? GRUs have fewer parameters and train faster than LSTMs. For session-based reco, sessions are usually short (10s of items) so the LSTM's extra long-memory capacity is wasted. ## Loss GRU4Rec introduced **TOP1** and **BPR** losses for sampled-item ranking — pairwise objectives that align with [[reco-ranking-metrics|ranking metrics]] and avoid the negative-sampling pitfall of pointwise loss. ## Why it mattered - First neural network to substantially beat session-based memory baselines. - Established the "session as sequence, predict next item" framing. - Sparked LSTM-Reco, NARM (attention), Caser (CNN), SASRec (Transformer) — each replacing the GRU with a different sequence model. ## Related - [[sequential-vs-session-based]] - [[bpr-loss]] — used by GRU4Rec - [[transformer-encoder]] — modern successor (SASRec, BERT4Rec)

content.newText:
---
name: Sequential CNN recommenders (Caser, NextItNet)
description: CNN-based sequential recommenders that treat the sequence of recent items as a 2D image in (time, latent) space
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, deep-learning, cnn, sequential]
---

# Sequential CNN recommenders (Caser, NextItNet)

CNNs applied to sequential recommendation. Two key papers in the lecture:

- **Caser** (Tang & Wang, WSDM 2018) — *Personalized Top-N Sequential Recommendation via Convolutional Sequence Embedding*.
- **NextItNet** (Yuan et al., WSDM 2019) — uses **dilated** convolutions for long-range dependencies.

## Caser — sequence as "image"

Embed the previous $L$ items into an $L \times d$ matrix (time × latent). Apply 2D convolutions:

| Filter shape | What it captures |
|---|---|
| **Vertical** ($h \times d$) | How **single items** influence the next item — slides over time |
| **Horizontal** ($L \times 1$) | Groups the **influence of items** across the full window |

For each user, learn item embeddings $\mathbf{E}^{(u, t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ + a separate user embedding $\mathbf P_u$. Concatenate convolution outputs with $\mathbf P_u$, fully-connect to predicted item scores.

Predict the next $T$ items (e.g. $T = 2$) from the previous $L$ actions (e.g. $L = 4$).

## NextItNet — dilated convolutions

Standard 1D convolutions have a small receptive field — to capture long-range dependencies (e.g. an item from 30 steps ago) you need many layers, and parameters grow.

**Dilated convolutions** apply the filter over a field larger than its original length by **inserting zeros** ("holes") — increasing the receptive field **without adding parameters**:

| Layer | Kernel | Dilation | Receptive field |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1×3 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 1×3 | 2 | 7 |
| 3 | 1×3 | 4 | 15 |
| 4 | 1×3 | 8 | 31 |

Each layer doubles the receptive field — exponential growth, linear parameters.

Captures features at multiple temporal scales — like a wavelet decomposition of the sequence.

## Comparison

| | Caser | NextItNet | Transformer (SASRec) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receptive field | Small (window of $L$) | Large (dilated) | Full sequence (attention) |
| Parameters | Few | Few | More |
| Speed | Fast | Fast | Slower |
| Best on | Short sessions | Long sessions, fixed-vocab | Most settings (SOTA) |

## Why CNNs over RNNs for sequences?

- **Parallelisable** — RNNs are sequential.
- **Faster training**.
- **Receptive field** can be precisely controlled (dilation).

But transformers eventually beat both for accuracy.

## Related

- [[gru4rec]] — RNN alternative
- [[transformer-encoder]] — modern successor (SASRec, BERT4Rec)
- [[sequential-vs-session-based]]

## NextItNet — dilated convolutions

Standard 1D convolutions have a small receptive field — to capture long-range dependencies (e.g. an item from 30 steps ago) you need many layers, and parameters grow.

**Dilated convolutions** apply the filter over a field larger than its original length by **inserting zeros** ("holes") — increasing the receptive field **without adding parameters**:

| Layer | Kernel | Dilation | Receptive field |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1×3 | 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 1×3 | 2 | 7 |
| 3 | 1×3 | 4 | 15 |
| 4 | 1×3 | 8 | 31 |

Each layer doubles the receptive field — exponential growth, linear parameters.

Captures features at multiple temporal scales — like a wavelet decomposition of the sequence.

## Comparison

| | Caser | NextItNet | Transformer (SASRec) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receptive field | Small (window of $L$) | Large (dilated) | Full sequence (attention) |
| Parameters | Few | Few | More |
| Speed | Fast | Fast | Slower |
| Best on | Short sessions | Long sessions, fixed-vocab | Most settings (SOTA) |

## Why CNNs over RNNs for sequences?

- **Parallelisable** — RNNs are sequential.
- **Faster training**.
- **Receptive field** can be precisely controlled (dilation).

But transformers eventually beat both for accuracy.

## Related

- [[gru4rec]] — RNN alternative
- [[transformer-encoder]] — modern successor (SASRec, BERT4Rec)
- [[sequential-vs-session-based]]

lecture: 11
source: Chatbots and Reco 11.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 11 — Sequential and session-based recommenders (flashcards)

What's the difference between sequential and session-based recommendation?
?
**Sequential** uses **user identity** + history + recent actions. **Session-based** has **no user identity** — each session is treated independently.

Name three model classes used for sequential recommendation.
?
**Markov chains**, **RNNs**, **CNNs**, **Transformers** (any three).

Why are session-parallel mini-batches the standard data layout for GRU4Rec?
?
Each batch row carries one session in time; sessions are **independent**. When a session ends, the next session takes its slot in the batch and the **hidden state is reset** so behaviour from the previous session doesn't leak.

What is GRU4Rec?
?
The first major neural session-based recommender (Hidasi et al., ICLR 2016). GRU network with one-hot item input and softmax output over items, trained with **TOP1** or **BPR** loss.

What does Caser do that's distinctive?
?
**Embeds the previous L items as a 2D image** in (time, latent) space, then applies vertical (single-item influence) and horizontal (groups of items) convolutions.

What are dilated convolutions and why use them in NextItNet?
?
Apply the convolutional filter over a field larger than its original length by inserting **zeros** between weights. Increases the **receptive field exponentially** without adding parameters — captures long-range dependencies efficiently.

In Caser, what do vertical filters encode?
?
**How single items influence the next item** — the filter slides across time, focusing on individual item contributions.

In Caser, what do horizontal filters encode?
?
The **influence of groups of items** — the filter spans the full window length, mixing across multiple recent items.

Which neural architecture currently dominates sequential recommendation?
?
**Transformer-based models** (SSE-PT, SASRec, BERT4Rec) — same lesson as in NLP, attention beats RNN/CNN at sequence modelling.

When would you choose a session-based model over a sequential one?
?
When **users are anonymous** (no IDs), when handling cold users gracefully matters, or when long-term history is noisy/stale.

---

source: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
lecture: 12
slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 12 — Graph Neural Networks for recommendation

## Overview

GNNs are neural networks for **graph-structured data** — nodes (entities) connected by edges (relationships). They scale linearly $O(N + E)$, leverage locality, support multiple edge types, and generalise inductively to new nodes. Three deep dives:

1. **Message passing** — the universal GNN computation pattern.
2. **Why GNNs fit reco** — user-item interactions form a bipartite graph; message passing surfaces **high-order connectivity** that flat MF can't reach.
3. Two reco architectures: **NGCF** (Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering, Wang et al. 2019) and its simplified variant **LightGCN** (He et al. 2020). Closes with **knowledge-graph extensions** like **DKN** for news recommendation.

## Key concepts

- [[graph-neural-networks]] — message passing, aggregate, update
- [[ngcf]] — Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering
- [[lightgcn]] — LightGCN, simplified NGCF
- [[knowledge-graph-reco]] — DKN
- [[bpr-loss]] — used as the loss for both NGCF and LightGCN

## What is a GNN

A neural network that operates on graphs:

- **Nodes** carry feature vectors (embeddings).
- **Edges** carry the structure (and optionally features themselves).
- **Aggregate-update message passing**: nodes exchange info with neighbours, then transform.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A((A))
    B((B)) --> A
    C((C)) --> A
    D((D)) --> A
    A --> Aprime["A' = f(h_A, AGG{h_B, h_C, h_D})"]
    classDef gnn fill:#e3f2fd
    class Aprime gnn
```

**Three steps per layer:**

1. **Message** — each neighbor sends its current embedding.
2. **Aggregate** — combine all incoming messages (sum, mean, max).
3. **Update** — apply a neural network to compute the new node embedding:

$$\mathbf h'_A = f_\theta\big(\mathbf h_A,\ \text{AGG}(\{\mathbf h_B, \mathbf h_C, \mathbf h_D\})\big)$$

After $K$ layers, each node sees information from its **K-hop neighborhood**.

## Why GNNs fit reco

User-item interactions form a bipartite graph. Standard collaborative filtering captures:

- User-user similarity (user-based CF).
- Item-item similarity (item-based CF).
- User-item interaction (model-based CF, MF).

But **missing**: high-order connectivity. Example — recommend item $i_4$ to user $u_2$ because:

- $u_2$ interacted with $i_3$
- $u_3$ also interacted with $i_3$
- $u_3$ interacted with $i_4$

That's a 3-hop user-item-user-item chain. A GNN with 3 layers captures it natively; flat MF doesn't.

## NGCF — Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering (Wang et al., SIGIR 2019)

Define an **embedding propagation layer**: a user's embedding is refined with embeddings of items they've connected to, and item embeddings are refined with their users' embeddings. **Stacking $K$ layers captures $K$-hop signal.**

Recursive update:

$$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}\Big(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} (\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i))\Big)$$

with **trainable matrices** $\mathbf W_1, \mathbf W_2$ and a degree-normalisation discount factor.

Final user embedding = concatenation of $\mathbf e^{(0)}_u, \mathbf e^{(1)}_u, \ldots, \mathbf e^{(K)}_u$. Predicted score = dot product of user and item final embeddings. Trained with [[bpr-loss|BPR]].

## LightGCN (He et al. 2020)

A simplified NGCF — strip out the trainable matrices and non-linearity and you get a strictly better model:

- **Only embedding propagation** — no $\mathbf W_1, \mathbf W_2$.
- **No non-linear activation** — just sum.
- **The only trainable parameters are the original embeddings** $\mathbf e^{(0)}$.

Update simplifies to:

$$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}}\, \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$$

Final user embedding = **weighted sum (not concat)** of all layer outputs. Predicted score = dot product. Loss = BPR.

LightGCN consistently outperforms NGCF on benchmarks → the trainable matrices and non-linearity were *hurting*, not helping. Lesson: in collaborative filtering on bipartite graphs, **propagation alone is the signal**.

## Knowledge-graph extensions (slide 9)

A knowledge graph captures relationships between entities (e.g. movie–director–actor–genre). Use cases: news, fashion, food, gaming. **DKN** (Deep Knowledge-Aware Network, Wang et al. WWW 2018) combines:

- Knowledge graph (entity embeddings).
- Attention to encode user history.
- Convolutions for text features.

For news CTR prediction.

## Equations recap

| | |
|---|---|
| GNN message-passing | $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})$ |
| NGCF propagation | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}(\sum_i \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u| |\mathcal N_i|}} (\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)))$ |
| LightGCN propagation | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_i \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u| |\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ |

## Open questions

- How many layers? Usually 2-3 for reco — going deeper causes **over-smoothing** (all node embeddings converge).
- Inductive vs transductive — LightGCN is transductive (one embedding per known node). For new nodes, need GraphSAGE-style aggregation.

Unlike CNNs (grid-structured) or RNNs (sequence-structured), GNNs handle arbitrary connectivity. ## Why GNNs - **Leverage locality** — a node's neighborhood structures the computation. - **Multiple edge types** — heterogeneous graphs (user-item-tag-author networks). - **Computationally efficient** — $O(N + E)$ per layer (linear in nodes plus edges). - **Inductive** — generalise to unseen nodes if you use neighborhood-based aggregation. ## Message passing — the universal pattern Every GNN layer computes: ```mermaid flowchart TD Hb[h_B] --> Send Hc[h_C] --> Send Hd[h_D] --> Send Send[1. MESSAGE
Each neighbor sends its embedding] --> Agg Agg[2. AGGREGATE
Combine via SUM / MEAN / MAX] --> mA["m_A"] Ha[h_A] --> Update mA --> Update Update[3. UPDATE
NN computes new embedding] --> Hap["h'_A"] ``` Mathematically: $$\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta\big(\mathbf h_v,\ \text{AGG}(\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})\big)$$ After $K$ layers, each node sees its **K-hop neighborhood**. ## Common GNN variants | Variant | What's distinctive | |---|---| | **GCN** (Kipf & Welling) | Symmetric normalisation, fixed weights | | **GraphSAGE** | Inductive, supports unseen nodes via neighbour sampling | | **GAT** | Attention weights over neighbours | | **Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNN)** | Generic framework, edge-feature aware | ## Use cases - **Recommender systems** — user-item bipartite graph (NGCF, [[lightgcn|LightGCN]], PinSage) - **Drug discovery** — molecules as graphs (MIT's Halicin antibiotic) - **Traffic ETA** — road networks (Google Maps) - **Social network recommendation** — friend graphs - **Knowledge graphs** — entity-relation reasoning ## Limitation: over-smoothing Stacking too many GNN layers makes all node embeddings **converge** to similar values — "everyone looks like the average of the graph". Practical depth: 2-4 layers. ## Related - [[lightgcn]] — minimal GNN for collaborative filtering - [[ngcf]] — full NGCF with trainable propagation - [[knowledge-graph-reco]] — DKN - [[bpr-loss]] — common loss for GNN-based recommenders

content.newText:
---
name: NGCF (Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering)
description: Stacks embedding-propagation layers over the user-item bipartite graph to capture high-order connectivity
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, graph, deep-learning]
---

# NGCF (Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering)

A [[graph-neural-networks|GNN]]-based collaborative filter (Wang et al., SIGIR 2019). Operates on the bipartite user-item interaction graph; stacks $K$ embedding-propagation layers to capture **high-order connectivity** that flat MF can't reach.

## Embedding propagation

Recursive update for user $u$:

$$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}\Big(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \big(\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)\big)\Big)$$

Symmetric for items. Components:

| Term | Role |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal N_u$ | items the user has interacted with |
| $1/\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}$ | symmetric degree normalisation (discount factor) |
| $\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ | item's contribution |
| $\mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)$ | "interaction" between user and item embeddings |
| LeakyReLU | non-linearity |

## Final embedding and prediction

Final user embedding = **concatenation** of all layer outputs:

$$\mathbf e^*_u = \mathbf e^{(0)}_u \,\|\, \mathbf e^{(1)}_u \,\|\, \cdots \,\|\, \mathbf e^{(K)}_u$$

Predicted score = dot product:

$$\hat y_{ui} = \mathbf e^{*\top}_u\, \mathbf e^*_i$$

Trained with [[bpr-loss|BPR]].

## What it captures over flat MF

Standard CF sees user-user, item-item, and user-item similarity. NGCF additionally surfaces **multi-hop chains**:

> Recommend $i_4$ to $u_2$ because $u_2$ liked $i_3$, $u_3$ also liked $i_3$, and $u_3$ liked $i_4$.

A 3-hop chain. Captured by 3 layers of NGCF; invisible to flat MF.

## Limitations

- Trainable $\mathbf W$ matrices and non-linearity were later shown to be **harmful**, not helpful, on these tasks → **[[lightgcn|LightGCN]]** strips them out and outperforms NGCF.
- Transductive — needs to know the full graph at training time.

## Related

- [[graph-neural-networks]]
- [[lightgcn]] — successor / simplification
- [[collaborative-filtering]]
- [[bpr-loss]]

$$\hat y_{ui} = \mathbf e^{*\top}_u\, \mathbf e^*_i$$

Trained with [[bpr-loss|BPR]].

## What it captures over flat MF

Standard CF sees user-user, item-item, and user-item similarity. NGCF additionally surfaces **multi-hop chains**:

> Recommend $i_4$ to $u_2$ because $u_2$ liked $i_3$, $u_3$ also liked $i_3$, and $u_3$ liked $i_4$.

A 3-hop chain. Captured by 3 layers of NGCF; invisible to flat MF.

## Limitations

- Trainable $\mathbf W$ matrices and non-linearity were later shown to be **harmful**, not helpful, on these tasks → **[[lightgcn|LightGCN]]** strips them out and outperforms NGCF.
- Transductive — needs to know the full graph at training time.

## Related

- [[graph-neural-networks]]
- [[lightgcn]] — successor / simplification
- [[collaborative-filtering]]
- [[bpr-loss]]

---
name: LightGCN
description: Simplified NGCF — no trainable matrices, no non-linearity, just embedding propagation. Outperforms NGCF.
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, recommender-systems, graph, deep-learning]
---

# LightGCN

A radical simplification of [[ngcf|NGCF]] (He et al. 2020). Strips out the trainable matrices and non-linearity → strictly outperforms NGCF.

## What gets removed

| Component in NGCF | LightGCN |
|---|---|
| Trainable $\mathbf W_1, \mathbf W_2$ matrices | **Removed** |
| LeakyReLU non-linearity | **Removed** |
| Concatenation of layers | Replaced by **weighted sum** |

Result: the only trainable parameters are the **original embeddings** $\mathbf e^{(0)}_u, \mathbf e^{(0)}_i$.

## The propagation rule

$$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}}\, \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$$

Symmetric for items. Pure neighbour-averaging with degree normalisation. **No learned transformation.**

## Final embedding and prediction

Final embedding = weighted sum of layer outputs:

$$\mathbf e^*_u = \sum_{l=0}^{K} \alpha_l\, \mathbf e^{(l)}_u$$

with $\alpha_l$ either learned or fixed (e.g. $\alpha_l = 1/(K+1)$).

Predicted score = dot product. Loss = [[bpr-loss|BPR]].

## Why "less is more"

The lecture (and the LightGCN paper) shows empirically:

| Component | Effect on quality |
|---|---|
| Add $\mathbf W$ matrices | Hurts |
| Add LeakyReLU | Hurts |
| Concatenate vs sum | Sum slightly better |

The intuition: in collaborative filtering, the **structural signal of the graph** is what matters. Trainable weights overfit to noise; non-linearities aren't needed because aggregation is already enough to mix signals.

## Practical impact

LightGCN became the dominant GNN baseline for collaborative filtering after 2020. Most subsequent GNN-Reco papers compare against LightGCN as their baseline.

## Related

- [[ngcf]] — the predecessor
- [[graph-neural-networks]]
- [[bpr-loss]]
- [[collaborative-filtering]]
