--- name: Knowledge-graph recommendation (DKN) description: Inject external knowledge-graph relationships into a recommender — DKN combines KG embeddings, attention, and CNNs for news CTR prediction type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, recommender-systems, knowledge-graph] --- # Knowledge-graph recommendation (DKN) Use cases for knowledge graphs in reco: **news**, **fashion**, **food**, **gaming**. A knowledge graph captures relationships between entities (movie → director → actor → genre, or news article → topic → public figure → event). Injecting these relationships gives the recommender external context the [[reco-data-terminology|interaction matrix]] alone can't provide. ## Why KG helps - **Explainability** — "you watched movies directed by Spielberg, here are more Spielberg movies". - **Cold-start** — a brand-new article still has entity links. - **Cross-domain transfer** — a user who reads tech news has a profile in the KG that maps to other domains. ## DKN — Deep Knowledge-aware Network (Wang et al., WWW 2018) A news-recommendation model that combines three signals: ```mermaid flowchart LR News[News article] -->|word embeddings| KCNN News -->|entity links| KCNN[Knowledge-aware CNN] KCNN --> NEW[News embedding] Hist[User's clicked articles] --> Att[Attention
over history] NEW --> CTR Att --> CTR["CTR prediction"] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class CTR chatbot ``` | Component | Role | |---|---| | **Knowledge-aware CNN (KCNN)** | Combines word embeddings + entity embeddings (from the KG) + entity-context embeddings into a single news representation | | **Attention over user history** | Different past articles weigh differently for different candidate articles — attention learns the weights | | **CTR prediction** | Final binary classification: will the user click this article? | ## Joint learning DKN does **joint learning of semantic and knowledge representations** — so the news representation captures both linguistic content AND entity relationships from the KG. ## Use cases beyond news - **Movie recommendation** — director, actor, genre relationships. - **Fashion** — brand, designer, style relationships. - **Food** — cuisine, ingredient, allergen relationships. - **Gaming** — developer, genre, mechanic relationships. ## Related - [[graph-neural-networks]] — KGs are graphs; GNNs naturally extend to KGs (KGAT, RippleNet) - [[content-based-filtering]] — entity-as-content - [[cold-start-problem]] — KG helps mitigate

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 12
source: Chatbots and Reco 12.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 12 — GNNs for recommendation (flashcards)

What is a Graph Neural Network in one sentence?
?
A neural network designed for **graph-structured data** — nodes (entities) with edges (relationships), processed via message passing between neighbours.

What is the computational complexity of a GNN layer?
?
$O(N + E)$ — linear in the number of nodes plus edges. Efficient on sparse graphs.

State the three steps in GNN message passing.
?
**1. Message** — each neighbour sends its embedding. **2. Aggregate** — combine via SUM / MEAN / MAX. **3. Update** — apply a neural network to compute the new node embedding: $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u\})$.

After K GNN layers, what does each node "see"?
?
Its **K-hop neighborhood** — information from nodes up to $K$ steps away in the graph.

What's the high-order connectivity that GNNs capture but flat MF can't?
?
Multi-hop chains. Example: recommend $i_4$ to $u_2$ because $u_2$ liked $i_3$, $u_3$ also liked $i_3$, and $u_3$ liked $i_4$ — a 3-hop user-item-user-item chain.

What does NGCF stand for, and what's its key idea?
?
**Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering** (Wang et al. 2019). Defines an **embedding-propagation layer** with trainable matrices and LeakyReLU; stacking $K$ layers captures $K$-hop connectivity.

How does LightGCN simplify NGCF?
?
**Removes** trainable $\mathbf W$ matrices, **removes** LeakyReLU, replaces concatenation with sum. Only the original embeddings are trainable. Outperforms NGCF on benchmarks.

Write the LightGCN propagation rule.
?
$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u| |\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ — pure neighbour-averaging with symmetric degree normalisation.

What loss does LightGCN use for training?
?
**BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking)** — pairwise ranking loss over (user, observed item, unobserved item) triples.

What is "over-smoothing" in GNNs?
?
Stacking too many layers makes all node embeddings converge to similar values. Practical depth: 2-4 layers.

Name three use cases of GNNs outside recommendation.
?
**Drug / molecule discovery** (e.g. Halicin antibiotic), **traffic ETA estimation** (Google Maps), **social network friend recommendation**.

What does DKN stand for, and what's it for?
?
**Deep Knowledge-aware Network** — news CTR prediction. Combines a knowledge graph, attention over user history, and CNN-based text encoding.

Why is high-order connectivity hard for traditional MF?
?
MF only models direct user-item interactions and pairwise similarity. Reaching beyond direct neighbours requires the graph structure that GNNs explicitly use.

# Lecture 13 — NLP approaches and the Transformer

## Overview

Three families of modern NLP architectures, all built on the transformer:

| Family | Use case | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence-to-sequence** (encoder-decoder) | Translation, summarisation | Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART |
| **Autoencoders** (encoder-only) | Bidirectional embeddings, classification, NER | BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa |
| **Autoregressive** (decoder-only) | Generation, completion | GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral |

The lecture walks through the **transformer architecture** in detail — tokenization, attention, self-attention process, query/key/value, softmax, multi-head attention, positional encoding, residual connections, layer normalisation. Closes with the encoder/decoder/seq2seq family tree and a preview of [[lecture-14-transfer-learning|transfer learning]].

## Key concepts

- [[transformer-encoder]] — the universal building block
- [[multi-head-attention]] — the core operation
- [[self-attention]] — query-key-value mechanism
- [[positional-encoding]] — restore sequence order to permutation-equivariant attention
- [[tokenization]] — turning text into model inputs
- [[residual-connection]]
- [[layer-normalization]]

## The Transformer (slide 3)

**First architecture based solely on attention** (Vaswani et al. 2017). Key claims:

- Good at learning **global word relationships**.
- **Parallelisable** (unlike RNNs).
- Beats LSTMs and CNNs.
- The encoder generates a hidden state fed to the decoder.
- Key component: **multi-head self-attention**.

## Tokenization (slide 4)

| Type | Granularity |
|---|---|
| Character | Each character is a token |
| Word | Each word is a token (large vocabulary) |
| **Subword** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) | Combination — unknown words decomposed into known sub-pieces |

Subword tokenization is the modern default — it handles unknown words, keeps vocabulary manageable, and supports multilingual models.

## Attention (slide 5)

The original encoder-decoder LSTM produced a single hidden state for the whole input — a bottleneck. **Attention** lets the decoder access **a hidden state at every encoder step**. Different weights for different states → "where to attend".

## Self-attention (slides 6-8)

Same idea, *within* one sequence. As the model processes each token, it looks at other positions in the input for relevant context.

> **Permutation-equivariant** — attention has no notion of token order on its own. Position must be supplied via [[positional-encoding|positional encoding]].

The main idea: **instead of using fixed embeddings for each token, use the whole sequence to compute a weighted average of each embedding.**

## Scaled dot-product attention (slide 9)

The standard implementation:

1. Project each token embedding into three matrices: **Query** $Q$, **Key** $K$, **Value** $V$.
2. Compute similarity = dot product $QK^T$. Similar queries and keys give high dot products.
3. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ and apply softmax to get attention weights.
4. Update token embeddings by weighting the value vectors: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}) V$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input embeddings] --> Q[Linear → Q]
    X --> K[Linear → K]
    X --> V[Linear → V]
    Q --> Sim[Q K^T]
    K --> Sim
    Sim --> Scale[÷ √d_k]
    Scale --> SM[softmax]
    SM --> Wt[× V]
    V --> Wt
    Wt --> Out[Attention output]
    classDef nlp fill:#f3e5f5
    class SM,Wt nlp
```

## Why softmax (slide 10)

- Normalises any vector into a **probability distribution** — makes scores comparable.
- Acts like a **soft argmax**: if one value dominates, the output near-one-hots; if several are close, all stay highly probable.
- **Differentiable** — works cleanly with backpropagation.

## Multi-head self-attention (slide 12)

Repeat the attention mechanism multiple times in **parallel**, each with its own learned $Q, K, V$ projections.

- Each head learns to attend to **different representation subspaces**.
- Heads operate in parallel → cheap.
- The encoder-decoder attention head connects the encoder input sequence with the decoder input.

## Positional encoding (slide 13)

Self-attention is permutation-equivariant — to give the model order, **add a positional encoding to the token embeddings before the first transformer block**:

$$\mathrm{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d}), \quad \mathrm{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$$

Different frequencies along the embedding dim let the model resolve both adjacency and long-range distance.

## Residual connections + LayerNorm (slides 14-15)

Standard transformer block sublayer:

```
sublayer_output = x + Sublayer(LayerNorm(x))    # pre-norm
```

- **Residual** — gradients have an identity shortcut → trains deep networks (24, 48, 96+ layers).
- **LayerNorm** — normalise across feature dim within a token (not across batch like BatchNorm). More stable for variable-length sequences with small batches.

## Three architecture families (slide 21)

| Family | Encoder | Decoder | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder-Decoder** (seq2seq) | Yes | Yes | Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird |
| **Encoder-only** (autoencoder) | Yes | No | BERT, RoBERTa, XLM, ALBERT, ELECTRA, DeBERTa |
| **Decoder-only** (autoregressive) | No | Yes | GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral |

- **Autoencoders** train by adding noise (e.g. masking) to text and reconstructing it. Used downstream for classification, NER, etc.
- **Autoregressive** models predict the next token given previous tokens. Used for generation.

## Open questions

- Why pre-norm over post-norm? Pre-norm trains more stably for very deep transformers.
- BPE vs SentencePiece? SentencePiece handles whitespace as a regular character (better for languages without word boundaries like Chinese).

name: Multi-head attention
description: Run h parallel self-attentions with smaller dimensions, concatenate, and project — lets each head ask a different question

content.newText:
---
name: Tokenization
description: Splitting text into tokens for input to a language model — character, word, or subword granularity
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, nlp, llm]
---

# Tokenization

Splitting text into discrete tokens for input to a language model. The first step in any NLP pipeline.

## Granularity

| Type | Granularity | Vocab size | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Character** | Each character | Tiny (~100s) | Long sequences, hard to learn semantics |
| **Word** | Each word | Huge (millions) | Short sequences, but **OOV** problems for unknown words |
| **Subword** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) | Combination — frequent words stay whole, rare words split | Manageable (~30K-50K) | **The modern default** |

## How tokenization is done

| Method | Approach |
|---|---|
| **Space-based** | Split at whitespace |
| **Punctuation-based** | Split at punctuation (commas, periods, ?) |
| **Rule-based** | Pre-defined patterns (regex, language-specific rules) |
| **Neural-network-based** | Train a model to predict word boundaries — flexible, adaptive |

## Subword tokenization (the modern default)

Algorithms: **BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding)**, **WordPiece** (BERT), **SentencePiece** (T5, LLaMA).

The idea: start from characters; iteratively merge the most-frequent adjacent pair into a new token. After many merges, common words become single tokens, rare words decompose into known sub-pieces. Vocabulary stays manageable.

**Why it wins:**

- Handles unknown words (decompose into sub-pieces).
- Compact vocabulary.
- Works across languages (especially SentencePiece, which treats whitespace as a regular character).

## Why this matters for LLMs

- **Context length** is measured in tokens, not characters or words.
- **Cost** at inference scales with token count.
- **Quality** can shift on heavily-tokenized inputs (code, math, non-English).

## Example

`"unhappiness"` → BPE might tokenize as `["un", "happiness"]` or `["un", "happi", "ness"]` depending on training.

## Related

- [[transformer-encoder]] — what tokens feed into
- [[positional-encoding]] — added to token embeddings

content.newText:
---
name: Layer normalization
description: Normalise across the feature dim within a single token — replaces BatchNorm in transformers
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, deep-learning, transformer, normalization]
---

# Layer normalization

Normalises each input vector to **zero mean and unit variance** across the **feature dimension**, within a single token.

## Definition

For each token vector $\mathbf x \in \mathbb{R}^d$:

$$\hat x_j = \frac{x_j - \mu(\mathbf x)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2(\mathbf x) + \epsilon}}, \qquad y_j = \gamma_j \hat x_j + \beta_j$$

where $\mu, \sigma^2$ are computed over the $d$ feature dims of *this single token* and $\gamma, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ are learned.

## Why not BatchNorm?

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalises across | Batch + (sometimes seq) dims, per channel | Feature dim, per token |
| Depends on batch | Yes | No |
| Sequence-friendly | Variable lengths break it | Invariant to length |
| Inference batch=1 | Fragile | Identical to training |

For sequences with variable length and small inference batches, **LayerNorm wins**.

## Advantages

- **Improved training stability** — gradients well-behaved across deep stacks.
- **Improved generalisation** — acts like a regulariser.
- **Reduced sensitivity to initialisation**.
- **Faster convergence**.

## Pre-norm vs post-norm

Modern transformers use **pre-norm** (LayerNorm applied **before** the sublayer):

```
output = x + Sublayer(LayerNorm(x))
```

Pre-norm trains more stably for very deep transformers (>12 layers) and needs less learning-rate warmup.

## Related

- [[transformer-encoder]] — wraps each sublayer with LayerNorm
- [[batch-normalization]] — what it replaces
- [[residual-connection]] — paired with LayerNorm in transformer sublayer wrapping

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 13
source: Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 13 — NLP approaches and the Transformer (flashcards)

Name the three NLP architecture families based on the transformer.
?
**Sequence-to-sequence** (encoder-decoder, e.g. T5, BART), **Autoencoders** (encoder-only, e.g. BERT), and **Autoregressive** (decoder-only, e.g. GPT).

What's the headline claim of the original Transformer paper?
?
First architecture **based solely on attention** — no recurrence, no convolution. Parallelisable, captures global word relationships, beats LSTMs and CNNs.

Name three subword tokenization algorithms.
?
**BPE** (Byte-Pair Encoding), **WordPiece** (BERT), **SentencePiece** (T5, LLaMA).

Why is subword tokenization the modern default?
?
Handles **unknown words** by decomposing into known sub-pieces, keeps **vocabulary manageable**, and works across languages.

What's the main idea of self-attention?
?
Instead of using fixed embeddings, **use the whole sequence to compute a weighted average of each token's embedding** — letting each token attend to all others.

Self-attention is permutation-equivariant. What's the consequence?
?
The model has no notion of token order — must add **positional encoding** to token embeddings before the first transformer block.

State the scaled dot-product attention formula.
?
$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\big(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\big) V$.

Walk through the four steps of computing scaled dot-product attention.
?
1. Project each token into Q, K, V matrices via learned linear projections. 2. Similarity = $QK^T$ (dot product). 3. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ and apply softmax → attention weights. 4. Multiply weights by V to get the output.

Why softmax over raw scores?
?
Three reasons: **normalises any vector to a probability distribution**, acts as a **soft argmax** (preserves close runners-up), and is **differentiable** for backprop.

What does multi-head attention buy you over single-head?
?
Multiple heads run in parallel, each with its own learned $Q, K, V$ projections — they learn to attend to **different representation subspaces** simultaneously.

Why use LayerNorm in transformers instead of BatchNorm?
?
LayerNorm normalises **across the feature dim within a single token**, so it doesn't depend on batch size or sequence length. BatchNorm fragments on variable-length sequences with small batches.

What's the role of residual connections in transformers?
?
**Reduce vanishing gradients** (gradients have an identity shortcut), allow later layers to learn from initial features, and keep the loss surface smoother — making deep transformers (24, 48, 96+ layers) trainable.

Give an example of an encoder-only model and what it's used for.
?
**BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa** — used for downstream tasks like classification, NER, Q&A. Trained as denoising autoencoders.

Give an example of a decoder-only model and what it's used for.
?
**GPT (1-5), LLaMA, Mistral** — used for autoregressive generation (chat, completion, code).

Give an example of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) model.
?
**Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird** — used for translation, summarisation.

content.newText:
--- tags: [lecture] course: Chatbots source: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf lecture: 14 slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf|Open slides ↗]]" created: 2026-04-26 --- # Lecture 14 — Transfer learning, BERT and GPT ## Overview The lecturer's claim: **transfer learning is the single biggest reason modern deep learning works**. First widely used in computer vision (ImageNet pretraining), now ubiquitous in NLP (pretrain a language model, finetune on the task). The lecture walks through: 1. Transfer learning patterns: pretrain → finetune, freeze vs finetune, zero-shot. 2. CV pretraining/finetuning history (ImageNet, ResNet timing). 3. NLP pretraining objectives: **next-token prediction** (autoregressive) vs **masked-token prediction** (denoising autoencoder). 4. NLP finetuning: supervised, reward-model, **RLHF** (used in ChatGPT). 5. **BERT** in detail (encoder-only autoencoder). 6. **GPT generations 1-4** (decoder-only autoregressive). ## Key concepts - [[transfer-learning]] (extends CV note) - [[zero-shot-learning]] (extends CV note) - [[few-shot-learning]] (extends CV note) - [[bert-model]] — bidirectional encoder - [[gpt-models]] — autoregressive decoders - [[masked-language-model]] — BERT's pretraining objective - [[next-token-prediction]] — GPT's pretraining objective - [[rlhf]] ## Transfer learning pattern (slide 2) Train on one task, **adapt** to a new task by reusing the original weights as initialization for the new task: - **Body** = pretrained backbone (frozen or finetuned). - **Head** = task-specific output layer (always retrained). ```mermaid flowchart LR Pre[Pretrained model
e.g. BERT on Wikipedia] --> Body[Body: keep weights] Body --> Head[Add task-specific head:
classifier / token tagger / Q&A] Head --> Train[Train on small task dataset] classDef nlp fill:#f3e5f5 class Body,Head nlp ``` ## Freezing vs finetuning (slide 6) | Strategy | When | |---|---| | **Freeze body, train head only** | Target and base domain similar — fewer params, less data, less compute | | **Finetune all weights** | Domains differ — let the body adapt | ## Zero-shot learning (slide 3) A pretrained model is asked to do a task it wasn't explicitly trained on, with no further training. GPT-3-style "in-context" prompting popularised this — **the model performs the task purely from the prompt's instructions and examples**. ## CV pretraining cost (slide 5) - ResNet18 from scratch on ImageNet: **3 days** on 4 NVIDIA K80 GPUs (30 epochs). - ResNet152 for 120 epochs on 4 K80s: **4 months**. → Transfer learning isn't a nice-to-have, it's the only way a normal team can get a working CV model. ## NLP pretraining (slide 8) | Type | Objective | Models | |---|---|---| | **Supervised** | Translation pairs (En→De, En→Fr) | Older seq2seq | | **Unsupervised — autoregressive** | Predict next token given previous tokens | GPT family | | **Unsupervised — masked LM (denoising)** | Predict a masked token given surrounding tokens | BERT family | ## NLP finetuning (slide 9) | Method | What it does | |---|---| | **Supervised finetuning** | Standard supervised learning on the task — input → label | | **Reward-model finetuning** | Train a reward function, finetune the LLM with RL to maximise it | | **RLHF** | Reward function based on **human feedback** — used in ChatGPT | ## BERT (slides 10-13) **Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers** (Devlin et al. 2018). - **Encoder structure** of the Transformer. - Pretrained as both a **masked-language model** (denoising autoencoder) AND a **next-sentence prediction** task. - **BERT-large**: 340M params, 24 encoder blocks, 1024-dim embeddings, 16 attention heads. - Pretrained on BooksCorpus (800M words) + English Wikipedia (2,500M words). - Deployed in **Google Search in 2019**. - Many successors: RoBERTa, DeBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT. ### Masked LM (slide 11) Mask 15% of tokens, predict them given the rest. **Why mask instead of left-to-right?** Truly bidirectional models would otherwise see the target word through the other direction. Masking lets the model use both left and right context for the prediction. The 15% are split: - **80%** replaced with `[MASK]` — but `[MASK]` doesn't appear during finetuning → mismatch. - **10%** replaced with a random token — keeps the model robust. - **10%** left unchanged — keeps the model honest. ### BERT inputs (slide 12) - `[CLS]` — first token of every sentence; used for classification. - `[SEP]` — separator between sentences (Q&A, entailment). - **Segment embeddings** — A or B sentence indicator. - **Positional embeddings** — token position. ### BERT tasks (slides 13-16) | Task | Dataset | Example | |---|---|---| | Sentence-pair classification (entailment) | MNLI | "Pennsylvania Avenue tour" → entailment | | Single-sentence classification (sentiment) | SST-2 | "The Rock is destined to be…" → label 3 | | Question answering | SQuAD v1.1 | "What causes precipitation to fall?" → "gravity" | | Single-sentence tagging (NER) | CoNLL-2003 | EU → B-ORG, German → B-MISC, etc. | ## BERT vs GPT (slide 17) | | BERT | GPT | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Encoder-only | Decoder-only | | Pretraining | Masked LM + NSP | Next-token prediction | | Bidirectional? | Yes | No (left-to-right only) | | Best at | Understanding (classification, NER, Q&A) | Generation | | Released | 2018 | 2018 (GPT-1), continuing | ## GPT generations (slides 18-22) | | GPT-1 (2018) | GPT-2 (2019) | GPT-3 (2020) | GPT-4 (2023) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Parameters | 117M | 1.5B | 175B | undisclosed | | Layers | 12 | 48 | 96 | undisclosed | | Max tokens | 512 | 1024 | 2048 | 32768 | | Hidden | 768 | 1600 | 12288 | undisclosed | | Highlight | First decoder-only LM | Multi-task learner, **zero-shot transfer** | **Few-shot learner**, in-context learning | Multimodal, human-level on professional exams | GPT-1 was trained on BookCorpus (985M words, 11k books). GPT-3 on CommonCrawl (1T words). GPT-4 is multimodal (text + image), still hallucinates. ## Open questions - BERT-style or GPT-style for any given task? GPT for generation, BERT for understanding — though GPT-style models with finetuning now do everything. - When to RLHF vs just SFT? RLHF when subjective preferences matter (helpfulness, tone). SFT alone for well-defined tasks.

Masking lets the model use both left and right context for the prediction. The 15% are split: - **80%** replaced with `[MASK]` — but `[MASK]` doesn't appear during finetuning → mismatch. - **10%** replaced with a random token — keeps the model robust. - **10%** left unchanged — keeps the model honest. ### BERT inputs (slide 12) - `[CLS]` — first token of every sentence; used for classification. - `[SEP]` — separator between sentences (Q&A, entailment). - **Segment embeddings** — A or B sentence indicator. - **Positional embeddings** — token position. ### BERT tasks (slides 13-16) | Task | Dataset | Example | |---|---|---| | Sentence-pair classification (entailment) | MNLI | "Pennsylvania Avenue tour" → entailment | | Single-sentence classification (sentiment) | SST-2 | "The Rock is destined to be…" → label 3 | | Question answering | SQuAD v1.1 | "What causes precipitation to fall?" → "gravity" | | Single-sentence tagging (NER) | CoNLL-2003 | EU → B-ORG, German → B-MISC, etc. | ## BERT vs GPT (slide 17) | | BERT | GPT | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Encoder-only | Decoder-only | | Pretraining | Masked LM + NSP | Next-token prediction | | Bidirectional? | Yes | No (left-to-right only) | | Best at | Understanding (classification, NER, Q&A) | Generation | | Released | 2018 | 2018 (GPT-1), continuing | ## GPT generations (slides 18-22) | | GPT-1 (2018) | GPT-2 (2019) | GPT-3 (2020) | GPT-4 (2023) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Parameters | 117M | 1.5B | 175B | undisclosed | | Layers | 12 | 48 | 96 | undisclosed | | Max tokens | 512 | 1024 | 2048 | 32768 | | Hidden | 768 | 1600 | 12288 | undisclosed | | Highlight | First decoder-only LM | Multi-task learner, **zero-shot transfer** | **Few-shot learner**, in-context learning | Multimodal, human-level on professional exams | GPT-1 was trained on BookCorpus (985M words, 11k books). GPT-3 on CommonCrawl (1T words). GPT-4 is multimodal (text + image), still hallucinates. ## Open questions - BERT-style or GPT-style for any given task? GPT for generation, BERT for understanding — though GPT-style models with finetuning now do everything. - When to RLHF vs just SFT? RLHF when subjective preferences matter (helpfulness, tone). SFT alone for well-defined tasks.

content.newText:
For very small target datasets, a self-supervised backbone (or a [[clip]]-style multimodal one) often beats an ImageNet-supervised backbone of the same size.

## In NLP (Chatbots course extension)

The same pretrain → finetune pattern dominates NLP since BERT (2018):

- **Supervised pretraining** — translation pairs (En→De, En→Fr). Old.
- **Unsupervised — autoregressive** — next-token prediction. **GPT family**.
- **Unsupervised — masked LM (denoising)** — predict masked tokens given context. **BERT family**.

Finetuning options:

- **Supervised finetuning (SFT)** — standard input → label.
- **Reward-model finetuning** — define reward, finetune via RL.
- **[[rlhf|RLHF]]** — reward = human feedback; the recipe behind ChatGPT.

The lecturer's framing: **transfer learning is the single biggest reason modern deep learning works.**

## Related

- [[data-augmentation]] — critical when fine-tuning on small datasets
- [[loss-functions]] — same losses; what changes is the head, not the loss
- [[cnn-architecture]] — typical CV backbones
- [[vision-transformer]] — increasingly common as a fine-tuning starting point
- [[self-supervised-learning]] — alternative pretraining source
- [[few-shot-learning]] — extreme case (frozen encoder + kNN, no fine-tuning)
- [[bert-model]] — masked-LM pretraining, the NLP version
- [[gpt-models]] — autoregressive pretraining
- [[rlhf]] — alignment finetuning

---
name: BERT
description: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — encoder-only model pretrained as a denoising autoencoder
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, nlp, llm, transformer]
---

# BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)

Devlin et al. 2018. The encoder-only architecture that defined the **pretrain-then-finetune** paradigm in NLP.

## Architecture

- **Encoder structure** of the [[transformer-encoder|Transformer]] — no decoder.
- **BERT-large**: 340M params, 24 encoder blocks, 1024-dim embeddings, 16 attention heads.
- BERT-base: 110M params, 12 blocks, 768-dim, 12 heads.

## Pretraining tasks

1. **[[masked-language-model|Masked Language Model (MLM)]]** — mask 15% of tokens, predict them given the rest.
2. **Next-Sentence Prediction (NSP)** — given two sentences, predict whether the second follows the first.

Trained on:

- **BooksCorpus** (800M words)
- **English Wikipedia** (2,500M words)

## Why bidirectional matters

Left-to-right or right-to-left LMs can't be truly bidirectional — running both directions and concatenating "leaks" the answer (each word would see itself when processed from the other side). MLM solves this: by **masking** the target token, the model can use both left and right context safely.

## Special tokens

| Token | Use |
|---|---|
| `[CLS]` | First token of every sentence; embedding used for classification |
| `[SEP]` | Separator between sentences (Q&A, entailment) |
| `[MASK]` | Mask token for MLM training |

Plus segment embeddings (A vs B) and positional embeddings.

## Finetuning tasks

| Task | Dataset | Setup |
|---|---|---|
| Sentence-pair classification (entailment) | MNLI | `[CLS] s1 [SEP] s2 → CLS embedding → linear → label` |
| Single-sentence classification (sentiment) | SST-2 | `[CLS] s → CLS embedding → linear → label` |
| Question answering | SQuAD v1.1 | Predict span start/end indices |
| Single-sentence tagging (NER) | CoNLL-2003 | Per-token classification (B-PER, I-PER, B-ORG, …) |

## Successors

RoBERTa (better training), DeBERTa (disentangled attention), DistilBERT (smaller), ALBERT (parameter sharing), ELECTRA (replaced-token detection).

## Production impact

**Deployed in Google Search in 2019** — substantially improved query understanding for natural-language searches.

## BERT vs GPT

| | BERT | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Encoder | Decoder |
| Pretraining | MLM + NSP | Next-token prediction |
| Bidirectional | Yes | No |
| Best at | Understanding (classification, NER, Q&A) | Generation |

## Related

- [[transformer-encoder]] — what BERT is built from
- [[masked-language-model]] — pretraining task
- [[gpt-models]] — the autoregressive counterpart
- [[transfer-learning]] — the paradigm BERT proves out for NLP

content.newText:
---
name: GPT models (1-4)
description: OpenAI's autoregressive decoder-only transformer family — GPT-1 through GPT-4 and their scaling story
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, nlp, llm, transformer]
---

# GPT models (1-4)

The **autoregressive decoder-only** transformer family from OpenAI. Each generation kept the architecture roughly the same and **scaled compute, data, and parameters** — establishing the "scaling laws" era.

## The lineage

| Model | Year | Params | Layers | Max tokens | Hidden | Highlight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPT-1** | 2018 | 117M | 12 | 512 | 768 | First decoder-only LM; SFT-pretrain template |
| **GPT-2** | 2019 | 1.5B | 48 | 1024 | 1600 | **Multi-task learner**; zero-shot transfer |
| **GPT-3** | 2020 | 175B | 96 | 2048 | 12288 | **Few-shot in-context learner**; the inflection point |
| **GPT-4** | 2023 | undisclosed | undisclosed | 32768 | undisclosed | Multimodal (text + image), human-level on professional exams |

Plus GPT-3.5 (2022, ChatGPT base) and GPT-5 (2025).

## Architecture

- **Decoder structure** of the transformer (causal masking — each token only attends to previous tokens).
- Pretrained on **next-token prediction**: given $x_1, \ldots, x_t$, predict $x_{t+1}$.
- The same architecture across generations — the breakthroughs are scale.

## Training data scale

| | Words |
|---|---|
| GPT-1 | BookCorpus, ~985M (11k books) |
| GPT-2 | 8M web documents (WebText) |
| GPT-3 | CommonCrawl, 1T+ words |
| GPT-4 | Undisclosed; multimodal corpora |

## What each generation showed

- **GPT-1** — pretraining a decoder LM on lots of text + lightweight finetuning beats supervised SOTA on 9/12 tasks.
- **GPT-2** — **task conditioning + zero-shot transfer**. Same input + different prompt → different output. SOTA on 7/8 zero-shot LM benchmarks.
- **GPT-3** — **few-shot learning works**. Show the model 1-5 examples in the prompt; it generalises without finetuning. Building bigger LMs gives more.
- **GPT-4** — **multimodality**, human-level on bar exam, USMLE, etc. Hallucinates less but still hallucinates. Mostly pretraining (not finetuning) drives capability.

## ChatGPT

GPT-3.5 finetuned with **[[rlhf|RLHF]]** + safety training. Released Nov 2022. Reached:

- **5 days** to 1M MAU.
- **2 months** to 100M MAU — fastest-growing tech in history.

## Limitations

- Hallucinates (confidently states wrong facts).
- Knowledge cutoff ([[rag-pipeline|RAG]] mitigates).
- No persistent memory across sessions.
- Closed-source, expensive at scale.

## Related

- [[transformer-encoder]] — sibling architecture (BERT uses this; GPT uses decoder)
- [[bert-model]] — encoder-only contrast
- [[next-token-prediction]] — GPT's pretraining objective
- [[rlhf]] — alignment finetuning used for ChatGPT
- [[transfer-learning]] — the paradigm GPT operationalises

content.newText:
---
name: Masked Language Model (MLM)
description: BERT's pretraining objective — mask 15% of tokens and predict them given surrounding context (denoising autoencoder)
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, nlp, llm, pretraining]
---

# Masked Language Model (MLM)

The pretraining objective behind [[bert-model|BERT]] and the **denoising autoencoder** family (BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa). **Mask 15% of tokens, train the model to predict them given the rest of the sentence.**

## Why mask?

Truly bidirectional language modelling (using both left and right context) is hard:

- **Left-to-right LM** (GPT-style) — model can't see the right context.
- **Right-to-left LM** — same problem mirrored.
- **Concatenate the two** — each word "sees itself" via the other direction. Information leakage.

**Masking solves it:** the target token is replaced with `[MASK]`, so the model can use both left and right context safely without leaking the answer.

## The 15% recipe

The 15% of tokens chosen for masking are split:

| Replaced with | Frequency |
|---|---|
| `[MASK]` token | **80%** |
| Random token | **10%** |
| Unchanged (still labelled as "predict") | **10%** |

**Why this split?** `[MASK]` doesn't appear during finetuning (real downstream tasks don't have `[MASK]` tokens). To prevent the model from over-relying on the special token's presence, 20% of the time the masked positions look like normal tokens but the model still has to predict them — keeping it honest.

## What the model learns

Bidirectional contextual representations. Each token's hidden state encodes the meaning of that token *given its full context*. These representations transfer well to downstream tasks (classification, NER, Q&A).

## Comparison to next-token prediction

| | MLM (BERT) | Next-token prediction (GPT) |
|---|---|---|
| Context | Bidirectional | Left-only |
| Generates? | No | Yes |
| Best at | Understanding | Generation |
| Pretraining objective | Predict masked tokens | Predict next token |

## Modern variants

- **Span masking** (SpanBERT) — mask contiguous spans, not random single tokens. Better for span-prediction tasks.
- **Replaced Token Detection** (ELECTRA) — replace tokens with plausible alternatives, train discriminator to detect.
- **Whole-word masking** — mask whole BPE words, not individual subword pieces.

## Related

- [[bert-model]] — uses MLM
- [[next-token-prediction]] — the autoregressive alternative
- [[transformer-encoder]] — the architecture
- [[transfer-learning]] — what MLM enables in NLP

To prevent the model from over-relying on the special token's presence, 20% of the time the masked positions look like normal tokens but the model still has to predict them — keeping it honest.

## What the model learns

Bidirectional contextual representations. Each token's hidden state encodes the meaning of that token *given its full context*. These representations transfer well to downstream tasks (classification, NER, Q&A).

## Comparison to next-token prediction

| | MLM (BERT) | Next-token prediction (GPT) |
|---|---|---|
| Context | Bidirectional | Left-only |
| Generates? | No | Yes |
| Best at | Understanding | Generation |
| Pretraining objective | Predict masked tokens | Predict next token |

## Modern variants

- **Span masking** (SpanBERT) — mask contiguous spans, not random single tokens. Better for span-prediction tasks.
- **Replaced Token Detection** (ELECTRA) — replace tokens with plausible alternatives, train discriminator to detect.
- **Whole-word masking** — mask whole BPE words, not individual subword pieces.

## Related

- [[bert-model]] — uses MLM
- [[next-token-prediction]] — the autoregressive alternative
- [[transformer-encoder]] — the architecture
- [[transfer-learning]] — what MLM enables in NLP

--- name: Next-token prediction (autoregressive LM) description: GPT's pretraining objective — predict the next token given previous tokens, trained left-to-right with causal masking type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, nlp, llm, pretraining] --- # Next-token prediction (autoregressive LM) The pretraining objective behind [[gpt-models|GPT]], LLaMA, Mistral, and every other autoregressive language model. **Given tokens $x_1, \ldots, x_t$, predict $x_{t+1}$.** ## Loss Cross-entropy over the vocabulary: $$\mathcal L = -\sum_t \log P(x_{t+1} \mid x_1, \ldots, x_t; \theta)$$ Equivalent to maximising the likelihood of the training corpus under the model. ## Architecture requirement: causal masking The model can only attend to *past* tokens, not future ones (otherwise it cheats — it sees the answer it's supposed to predict). Implemented by **masking** the upper triangle of the attention matrix to $-\infty$ before softmax. ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Input: x_1 ... x_t] --> Pos[+ positional encoding] Pos --> Att["Self-attention
(causal masked)"] Att --> MLP[MLP] MLP --> Out[Logits over vocab] Out --> Pred["Predict x_{t+1}"] classDef nlp fill:#f3e5f5 class Att nlp ``` ## Why it works as pretraining To predict the next token well, the model must internalise: - **Syntax** — predict closing brackets, agreement. - **Semantics** — predict topically-coherent words. - **Facts** — predict completions of "The capital of France is __". - **Reasoning** — predict the result of arithmetic, logic chains. A model that's good at next-token prediction has implicitly learned all of the above. ## Generation At inference: sample from the model's output distribution (or greedily pick argmax), append the chosen token, repeat. This is **autoregressive generation**. | Sampling strategy | What it does | |---|---| | Greedy | Always pick argmax — boring, repetitive | | Top-k | Sample from top $k$ tokens | | Nucleus / top-p | Sample from smallest set with cumulative prob ≥ $p$ | | Temperature | Sharpen ($T < 1$) or flatten ($T > 1$) the distribution | ## Comparison to MLM | | Next-token (GPT) | MLM (BERT) | |---|---|---| | Context | Left-only (causal) | Bidirectional | | Can generate? | **Yes** | Not naturally | | Pretraining-finetuning gap | None (always predicting tokens) | `[MASK]` token doesn't appear at finetuning | | Best at | Generation, in-context learning | Understanding, classification | ## Related - [[gpt-models]] — uses next-token prediction - [[masked-language-model]] — the bidirectional alternative - [[transformer-encoder]] — sibling architecture (decoder vs encoder) - [[rlhf]] — typical post-training step after autoregressive pretraining

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 14
source: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 14 — Transfer learning, BERT, GPT (flashcards)

What does BERT stand for?
?
**Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers**.

What's BERT's architecture in one sentence?
?
**Encoder-only** transformer pretrained as a denoising autoencoder (masked LM + next-sentence prediction).

What's BERT's pretraining objective?
?
**Masked Language Model (MLM)** — mask 15% of tokens, predict them given the rest. Plus **Next-Sentence Prediction**.

Why mask tokens instead of just doing left-to-right LM in BERT?
?
Truly bidirectional models with concatenation **leak the answer** (each word would see itself from the other direction). Masking lets the model use both sides safely.

What's the BERT 15% masking recipe split?
?
**80%** replaced with `[MASK]`, **10%** replaced with a **random token**, **10%** left unchanged. The 20% of non-`[MASK]` cases reduces the pretrain-finetune mismatch.

What does GPT stand for, and what's its architecture?
?
**Generative Pretrained Transformer** — **decoder-only** transformer pretrained on **next-token prediction**.

Differences between BERT and GPT?
?
**BERT** = encoder-only, bidirectional (MLM), best at understanding (classification, NER, Q&A). **GPT** = decoder-only, left-to-right (next-token), best at generation.

How many parameters does GPT-3 have?
?
**175B** parameters (96 layers, 12288 hidden, 2048 max tokens).

What did GPT-3 demonstrate that GPT-2 did not?
?
**Few-shot in-context learning** — show 1-5 examples in the prompt and the model generalises without any finetuning.

How long would training ResNet152 from scratch on ImageNet take?
?
**~4 months** on 4 NVIDIA K80 GPUs (120 epochs). This is why transfer learning matters — most teams can't afford pretraining.

When should you freeze the body vs finetune all weights?
?
**Freeze + retrain head** when the target and base domains are similar. **Finetune all** when the domains differ.

Name the three NLP finetuning options.
?
**Supervised finetuning (SFT)**, **reward-model finetuning** (define a reward, RL on it), and **RLHF** (reward = human feedback; used in ChatGPT).

What does zero-shot learning mean for an LLM?
?
The pretrained model performs a task **without any training examples** — purely from the prompt's instructions.

Where was BERT first deployed in production?
?
**Google Search in 2019** — substantially improved query understanding.

What three datasets is BERT pretrained on?
?
**BooksCorpus** (800M words) and **English Wikipedia** (2,500M words).

What special tokens does BERT use?
?
**`[CLS]`** (first token, used for classification), **`[SEP]`** (separator between sentences), **`[MASK]`** (mask token for MLM training).

---
tags: [lecture]
course: Chatbots
source: Chatbots and Reco 14b.pdf
lecture: 14b
slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 14b.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 14b — RLHF and LLM memory

## Overview

A short add-on to lecture 14 covering two topics in modern LLM systems:

1. **RLHF — what makes ChatGPT "ChatGPT"** beyond GPT-3.5. Distribution-matching (cross-entropy) → mode-seeking (RL on human-rated preferences). The trade-off: gain reliability, lose diversity.
2. **Memory in LLMs** — five strategies for giving an LLM long-term recall beyond its context window: buffer, summary, entity, knowledge graph, and vector-store memory.

## Key concepts

- [[rlhf]] — RL with human feedback
- [[llm-memory]] — strategies for long-term recall

## RLHF "secret sauce" (slide 1)

| | GPT base (cross-entropy) | RLHF |
|---|---|---|
| Loss | Cross-entropy on next token | Reward signal from human preferences |
| Behaviour | **Distribution matching** — mimics text statistics | **Mode seeking** — biases toward most-rated outputs |
| Trade-off | Diversity preserved, can hallucinate | Reliability up, **diversity lost** |
| Good for | Creative generation, novelty | Q&A, instructions, helpful assistance |
| Bad for | Following instructions, safety | Generating disruptive new ideas |

The lecturer's analogy: **RLHF is Page Rank for LLMs** — it captures relevance / quality the way Page Rank captured website authority. By biasing toward what humans rated highly, you lose the long-tail diversity that the base model carried.

## LLM memory (slide 2)

LLMs have a **default short-term memory** (the context window). For long-term, persistent memory — recalling facts across sessions — five strategies:

| Strategy | What it stores | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Buffer memory** | All prior messages verbatim | Simple; **fills context fast** |
| **Conversation summary** | A running summary updated over time | Compact; loses detail |
| **Entity memory** | Facts about specific entities (names, places, products) | Structured; needs entity extraction |
| **Knowledge graph memory** | Relationships between entities | Most expressive; complex to build |
| **Vector-stored memory** | Past conversations as vectors; query top-K most relevant | **The default for production agents** ([[rag-pipeline|RAG]] over chat history) |

## Open questions

- When does RLHF hurt? When you want creative output (poetry, novel ideas). Use the base model.
- Can you combine memory types? Yes — most production systems blend buffer (recent) + vector-store (older).

content.newText:
--- name: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) description: Finetune an LLM with RL using a reward signal trained on human preferences — the recipe behind ChatGPT type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 14.pdf - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 14b.pdf - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, alignment, training] --- # RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) The **alignment recipe** that turned GPT-3.5 into ChatGPT (Ouyang et al., OpenAI 2022). Use a reward function trained on **human preferences** to RL-finetune a language model toward outputs humans prefer. ## The three-step pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD Base[Pretrained LLM
e.g. GPT-3.5] --> SFT[Step 1: SFT
Finetune on human-written demonstrations] SFT --> Pref[Step 2: Reward Model
Train RM on preference pairs
RM predicts human preference] Pref --> RL[Step 3: PPO
Optimise SFT model w/ RL
Maximise RM reward] RL --> Out[Aligned LLM
e.g. ChatGPT] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class Out chatbot ``` | Step | Input | Output | |---|---|---| | **1 — SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)** | Human-written demonstrations of good responses | An instruction-following model | | **2 — Reward Model** | Preference pairs (humans pick the better of two completions) | An RM that predicts human preferences | | **3 — PPO** | The SFT model + RM | Fine-tuned model that maximises RM reward | ## Why it's complex Step 3 (PPO) requires **four models in memory simultaneously**: | Model | Role | |---|---| | **Policy** | The LLM being trained | | **Reference model** | A frozen copy of the SFT model (KL-divergence anchor) | | **Reward model** | Frozen, scores responses | | **Value model (critic)** | Predicts expected reward — needed for PPO | Sensitive to hyperparameters and prone to **reward hacking** (model exploits the RM rather than genuinely improving). ## Distribution matching vs mode seeking | | Cross-entropy pretraining | RLHF | |---|---|---| | Loss | Cross-entropy on next token | Reward signal from human preferences | | Behaviour | **Distribution matching** | **Mode seeking** | | Diversity | Preserved | **Lost** — concentrated on most-rated outputs | | Reliability | Hallucinates | More reliable Q&A | | Trade-off | Creative but unsafe | Safe but conservative | The lecturer's analogy: **RLHF is the LLM equivalent of Page Rank** — captures human-judged quality at the cost of long-tail diversity. ## Modern alternatives - **[[dpo|DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)]]** — derive a closed-form policy update from preference pairs. **No reward model, no RL loop.** Just supervised learning on preferences. - **GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)** — eliminates the critic by using group-baseline normalisation. DeepSeek R1 used GRPO. - **RLVR (RL with Verifiable Rewards)** — replace the human reward model with **deterministic verifiers** (compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math). State of the art for reasoning models. ## Trade-offs - **Pros**: aligned, follows instructions, refuses unsafe queries. - **Cons**: less creative, expensive (4 models in memory), reward hacking. ## Related - [[gpt-models]] — what RLHF is applied to - [[transfer-learning]] — RLHF is one form of finetuning - [[llm-evaluation]] — RLHF is itself an answer to "how do you align LLM outputs with what humans want" - [[llm-guardrails]] — companion safety layer

content.newText:
---
name: LLM memory strategies
description: Five ways to give an LLM persistent recall beyond its context window — buffer, summary, entity, knowledge graph, vector
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 14b.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, chatbots, memory]
---

# LLM memory strategies

LLMs have a default short-term memory — the **context window**. For long-term, persistent recall (across sessions or beyond context length), use one of five strategies:

| Strategy | What it stores | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Buffer memory** | All prior messages verbatim | Short conversations, simplicity |
| **Conversation summary memory** | A running summary updated over time | Long conversations, compact storage |
| **Entity memory** | Facts about specific entities (names, places, products) | Need structured recall ("what's the user's email?") |
| **Knowledge graph memory** | Relationships between entities | Multi-hop reasoning, structured domain |
| **Vector-stored memory** | Past conversations as embeddings; query top-K most relevant | **Production default** — RAG over chat history |

## Comparison

| | Buffer | Summary | Entity | KG | Vector |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Faithfulness | Highest (verbatim) | Loses detail | Loses detail | Structured detail | Retrieved verbatim |
| Context cost | Linear in conversation length | Constant | Small | Variable | Constant (top-K) |
| Setup | Trivial | LLM call to summarise | Entity extraction needed | KG construction needed | Vector DB needed |
| Best for | Toy / short chats | Long support sessions | CRM-style chatbots | Domain reasoning | **Production agents** |

## Why vector-stored memory dominates production

- Scales to unlimited history.
- Cheap to query (top-K cosine similarity).
- Reuses [[rag-pipeline|RAG infrastructure]] you probably already have.
- Combines naturally with retrieval over external documents.

## Hybrid setups

Most production agents combine:

- **Buffer** for the last few exchanges (immediate context).
- **Summary** for medium-term coherence.
- **Vector** for long-term recall.

## Related

- [[rag-pipeline]] — vector-stored memory is RAG over chat history
- [[agentic-workflow]] — agents need memory to be useful across sessions
- [[knowledge-graph-reco]] — KG memory shares its tech

# Lecture 14b — RLHF + LLM memory (flashcards)

What's the standard loss in GPT pretraining, and what does RLHF replace it with?
?
GPT pretrains with **cross-entropy** (distribution matching). RLHF replaces / augments this with a **reward signal from human preferences** → mode seeking.

Distribution matching vs mode seeking — what's the difference?
?
**Distribution matching** (cross-entropy) — the model mimics the statistics of the training text, preserving diversity. **Mode seeking** (RLHF) — the model concentrates probability on outputs humans rated highly, losing diversity.

What's the lecturer's analogy for what RLHF does?
?
**Page Rank for LLMs** — captures the relevance / quality of outputs the way Page Rank captured website authority.

What does RLHF lose?
?
**Diversity** — the model is biased toward common, well-rated answers. Bad for novelty / disruptive ideas; good for reliability.

Why is RLHF "not the best solution" for novelty according to the lecture?
?
Because it constrains the model to output **common knowledge** — what humans rated highly is by definition close to existing canonical answers. Disruptive new ideas live in the long tail RLHF discards.

Name the five LLM memory strategies.
?
**Buffer** (verbatim history), **Conversation summary**, **Entity memory** (facts about entities), **Knowledge graph memory**, and **Vector-stored memory**.

What's vector-stored memory?
?
Past conversations stored in a **vector database**; on each new query, retrieve the top-K most semantically similar past conversations to provide context.

Which memory strategy is the production default and why?
?
**Vector-stored memory** — scales to unlimited history, cheap query (top-K cosine), and reuses the same RAG infrastructure used for document retrieval.

Why combine multiple memory strategies in practice?
?
**Buffer** for immediate context (last few exchanges), **Summary** for medium-term coherence, **Vector** for long-term recall — each handles a different time scale.

What does buffer memory store?
?
All prior messages **verbatim** — simplest, but fills context fast.

---
tags: [lecture]
course: Chatbots
source: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
lecture: 15
slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 15 — Evolution of chatbots, ChatGPT, and the major LLM products

## Overview

A timeline of chatbots from Turing (1950) to ChatGPT (2022) and beyond, then a tour of the modern LLM landscape: **OpenAI's APIs** (Chat Completions, Assistant, Responses, Functions, Images, Agents) and the **major LLM families** (Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, xAI Grok, Anthropic Claude, Meta LLaMA). Closes with three Anthropic concepts: **CLAUDE.md, Skills, Agents**.

## Key concepts

- [[chatbot-history-timeline]] — Turing → ELIZA → ALICE → Siri → ChatGPT
- [[openai-apis]] — Chat Completions, Assistant, Responses, Functions
- [[llm-product-landscape]] — Copilot, Gemini, Grok, Claude, LLaMA
- [[finite-state-machine-chatbot]] — the pre-LLM era
- [[rlhf]] — what made ChatGPT
- [[agentic-workflow]] — handoffs, guardrails

## Chatbot timeline (slide 1)

| Year | Milestone | Tech |
|---|---|---|
| 1950 | **Alan Turing** — *Computing Machinery and Intelligence* (Turing test) | Concept, no implementation |
| 1966 | **MIT's ELIZA** — first chatbot | Keyword matching + scripted responses |
| 1995 | **A.L.I.C.E.** | Heuristic pattern matching, AIML rules |
| 2010 | **Apple Siri** — first chatbot worldwide | Speech recognition, intent classification, knowledge graphs |
| 2012 | **Google Now** | Speech recognition + behavioural / contextual signals |
| 2014 | **Cortana, Alexa** | Microsoft and Amazon's voice assistants |
| 2016 | **Facebook Messenger chatbots** | Business-focused, automated conversations, API-integrated |
| 2022 | **ChatGPT** (Nov) | Pretrained GPT-3.5 + RLHF |

ChatGPT became the **fastest-growing technology in history**: 5 days to 1M MAU, 2 months to 100M MAU.

## ChatGPT (slide 2)

- Released by OpenAI Nov 2022.
- Implementation: **pretrained GPT-3.5 finetuned with [[rlhf|RLHF]]**.
- Revenue: $200M (end of 2022) → $1.6B (2023, 700% YoY) → $3.7B (2024).

## OpenAI APIs (slides 3–9)

### Chat Completions (slide 3)

```python
client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Who won the world series in 2020?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "The Los Angeles Dodgers."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Where was it played?"}
    ]
)
```

Three roles:

- **system** — sets assistant behaviour.
- **user** — the prompt.
- **assistant** — model's response (or stored prior responses for conversation continuity).

### Assistant API (slide 5) — *Deprecated 2026*

Persistent **threads** that store message history per conversation. Auto-truncate when over context length. **Deprecated in 2026.**

### Responses API (slide 6–7)

```python
client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    input="Write a one-sentence bedtime story about a unicorn."
)
```

Simplified API replacing the old Completions / Chat for general requests. Roles updated to **developer / user / assistant**.

### Reasoning models (slide 8)

```python
client.responses.create(
    model="o3-mini",
    reasoning={"effort": "medium"},  # low / medium / high
    input=[...]
)
```

**Reasoning models think before they answer** (chain of thought). Effort options trade speed against reasoning depth.

### Function calling (slide 9)

```python
tools=[{"type": "function",
  "function": {
    "name": "getCurrentWeather",
    "parameters": {"location": ..., "unit": ...},
    "required": ["location"]
  }
}]
```

Lets the assistant **execute callbacks** and call external APIs.

### Images & vision (slide 10)

- `gpt-image-1` for text → image generation.
- GPT-4o for image analysis (vision).

### Agents (slide 11)

```python
booking_agent = Agent(name="Booking agent")
refund_agent = Agent(name="Refund agent")
guardrail_agent = Agent(name="Guardrail agent")
triage_agent = Agent(
    name="Triage", model="gpt-5",
    instructions="...",
    handoffs=[booking_agent, refund_agent],
    input_guardrails=[guardrail_agent]
)
```

- **Agents** = LLMs equipped with instructions and tools.
- **Handoffs** delegate to other agents for specific tasks.
- **Guardrails** validate inputs.

## LLM product landscape (slides 12–15)

| Product | Company | Architecture | Notable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft Copilot** | Microsoft | Prometheus tech | 3 phases: Bing orchestrator queries → grounding (fresh data) → answer with citations |
| **Google Gemini** | Google | Transformer decoder | Multimodal (text, image, sound, video). Paper has 1300+ authors. |
| **xAI Grok** | xAI | Transformer decoder | Integrated in X; multimodal (text + images); **trained to maximise truth seeking** |
| **Anthropic Claude** | Anthropic | Transformer decoder | Trained with **Constitutional AI** (embed ethical principles); multimodal |
| **Meta LLaMA** | Meta | Transformer decoder | **Open-weight** (close to OSS but not fully); multimodal |

## Anthropic-specific (slides 17–19)

- **CLAUDE.md** — markdown doc at the root of a project that gives Claude **persistent context** about the codebase. Read at start of every conversation. Sections: project overview, code style, common workflows, testing, known pitfalls.
- **Skills** — reusable, versioned instruction sets invoked via slash commands (`/skill-name`). Bundle instructions + routing + workflow + assets.
- **Agents** — autonomous AI workflows that plan, execute multi-step tasks, use tools (computer use, bash, file ops, MCP servers).

## Open questions

- Is Constitutional AI better than RLHF? Different, not strictly better — different alignment philosophy.
- Open-weight vs open-source distinction? Open-weight = weights released, training code/data may be private. LLaMA is open-weight.

---
name: Chatbot history timeline
description: Major chatbot milestones from Turing (1950) to ChatGPT (2022) — key models, dates, technologies
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, chatbots, history]
---

# Chatbot history timeline

| Year | Milestone | What it did | Tech |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1950** | **Alan Turing** — *Computing Machinery and Intelligence* | Proposed the **Turing test** | Concept only |
| **1966** | **MIT's ELIZA** | First chatbot — Rogerian therapy responses | Keyword matching, scripted responses |
| **1995** | **A.L.I.C.E.** | Heuristic pattern matching, won awards but **didn't pass Turing test** | AIML rules |
| **2010** | **Apple Siri** | First chatbot **available worldwide** | Speech recognition + intent classification + knowledge graphs |
| **2012** | **Google Now** | Predictive contextual recommendations | Speech recognition, behavioural / contextual signals |
| **2014** | **Cortana, Alexa** | Microsoft's PC assistant + Amazon's voice device | Voice + cloud APIs |
| **2016** | **Facebook Messenger chatbots** | Business-focused, automated, API-integrated | Rule + ML combo |
| **Nov 2022** | **ChatGPT** | Conversational LLM, mainstream breakthrough | Pretrained GPT-3.5 + [[rlhf|RLHF]] |

## ChatGPT's adoption record

- **5 days** to 1M MAU.
- **2 months** to 100M MAU — **fastest-growing technology in history**.
- Revenue: $200M (end 2022) → $1.6B (2023, +700% YoY) → $3.7B (2024).

## Turing → ELIZA → ChatGPT — what changed

| Era | State model | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-LLM (ELIZA, ALICE, Siri) | Hand-coded FSM + scripted intents | Brittle to phrasing, no nuance |
| LLM-based (ChatGPT, Claude) | Context window of n tokens | Hallucination, knowledge cutoff |

The paradigm shift: **state went from a database variable to "the n tokens currently in the context window"** — no more pre-defined dialogue graphs.

## Why ChatGPT was the inflection point

- GPT-3.5 + **RLHF** made the model *helpful, safe, instruction-following*.
- Free public access via a chat UI.
- Right time: enterprise NLP was struggling with task-specific fine-tuning; ChatGPT made one model do many jobs zero-shot.

## Related

- [[finite-state-machine-chatbot]] — the pre-LLM era
- [[rlhf]] — what made ChatGPT
- [[gpt-models]]
- [[rag-pipeline]] — modern grounding for hallucination

---
name: OpenAI APIs (Chat Completions, Assistant, Responses, Functions, Agents)
description: Tour of OpenAI's main programming interfaces and their evolution
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, openai, api]
---

# OpenAI APIs

A tour of OpenAI's main programming interfaces.

## Chat Completions

The classic chat API.

```python
client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system",   "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user",     "content": "Who won the world series in 2020?"},
        {"role": "assistant","content": "The Los Angeles Dodgers."},
        {"role": "user",     "content": "Where was it played?"}
    ]
)
```

| Role | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `system` | Set assistant behaviour / persona |
| `user` | The prompt |
| `assistant` | Model's response — also store prior responses for multi-turn |

## Assistant API (deprecated 2026)

Persistent **threads** that auto-store conversation history per user. Auto-truncate when over the model's context length. **Deprecated in 2026** in favour of Responses API.

## Responses API

Simplified, current default for general requests.

```python
client.responses.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    input="Write a one-sentence bedtime story about a unicorn."
)
```

Roles updated to `developer / user / assistant`.

## Reasoning models (o-series)

```python
client.responses.create(
    model="o3-mini",
    reasoning={"effort": "medium"},  # low / medium / high
    input=[...]
)
```

**Reasoning models think before they answer** (chain-of-thought is internal). Effort options trade speed against reasoning depth and token use.

## Function calling

```python
tools=[{
  "type": "function",
  "function": {
    "name": "getCurrentWeather",
    "parameters": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "location": {"type": "string"},
        "unit": {"type": "string", "enum": ["c", "f"]}
      },
      "required": ["location"]
    }
  }
}]
```

LLM identifies when it needs the tool and outputs structured JSON to invoke it.

## Images and Vision

- **`gpt-image-1`** — text → image generation, editing, variations.
- **GPT-4o vision** — analyse images via URL or base64 input.

## Agents

```python
booking_agent = Agent(name="Booking agent")
refund_agent = Agent(name="Refund agent")
guardrail_agent = Agent(name="Guardrail agent")
triage_agent = Agent(
    name="Triage", model="gpt-5",
    instructions="...",
    handoffs=[booking_agent, refund_agent],
    input_guardrails=[guardrail_agent]
)
```

| Concept | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Agent** | LLM equipped with instructions and tools |
| **Handoff** | Delegate to another agent for a specific task |
| **Guardrail** | Validate inputs / outputs before / after agent runs |

## Related

- [[agentic-workflow]] — the design pattern Agents implement
- [[function-calling]] — see also general concept
- [[llm-guardrails]]
- [[rag-pipeline]] — common companion to Agents

| **Agent** | LLM equipped with instructions and tools |
| **Handoff** | Delegate to another agent for a specific task |
| **Guardrail** | Validate inputs / outputs before / after agent runs |

## Related

- [[agentic-workflow]] — the design pattern Agents implement
- [[function-calling]] — see also general concept
- [[llm-guardrails]]
- [[rag-pipeline]] — common companion to Agents

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 15
source: Chatbots and Reco 15.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 15 — Evolution of chatbots, ChatGPT, products (flashcards)

In what year did Alan Turing publish "Computing Machinery and Intelligence"?
?
**1950** — proposed the Turing test as an experiment to assess whether an agent is intelligent.

What was the first chatbot, and when?
?
**MIT's ELIZA (1966)** — matched keywords to scripted Rogerian-therapy responses. ("My mother loves flowers" → "Tell me about your mother.")

What was A.L.I.C.E. and when?
?
**1995** chatbot based on ELIZA. Applied **heuristic pattern matching** to user input. Won numerous awards but **didn't pass the Turing test**.

What was the first chatbot available worldwide, and when?
?
**Apple Siri (2010)** — used speech recognition, intent recognition, Q&A, and knowledge graphs.

When was ChatGPT released and what was distinctive about it?
?
**November 2022** — first mainstream conversational LLM. Implemented as **pretrained GPT-3.5 + RLHF**. Reached 100M MAU in 2 months — fastest-growing tech ever.

What's Microsoft Copilot's underlying technology called?
?
**Prometheus** — three phases: Bing orchestrator generates related queries → grounding (fresh data, news, contextual signals + safety filters) → answer generation with citations.

What's distinctive about Anthropic's Claude training?
?
Trained with **Constitutional AI** — embed ethical principles directly into the model rather than only in external guardrails.

What's the difference between open-source and open-weight?
?
**Open-source** = code, weights, training data, full reproducibility. **Open-weight** = weights released, training pipeline/data private. **LLaMA is open-weight, not open-source.**

Name the three roles in OpenAI Chat Completions API.
?
**system** (sets assistant behaviour), **user** (the prompt), **assistant** (model response or stored prior responses).

What does the OpenAI Responses API do that's different?
?
A **simplified** API for general requests. Replaced the older Completions / Chat APIs as the default. Updated roles: developer / user / assistant.

What are reasoning models (o3-mini, o-series)?
?
LLMs that **think before they answer** (chain of thought is internal). Effort options (low, medium, high) trade speed against reasoning depth.

What does an OpenAI Agent consist of?
?
An LLM equipped with **instructions and tools**, optionally with **handoffs** to other agents and **guardrails** to validate inputs/outputs.

What is a CLAUDE.md file?
?
A markdown doc at the root of a project that gives Claude **persistent context** about the codebase — read automatically at the start of every conversation. Sections: project overview, code style, common workflows, testing, known pitfalls.

What is a Claude Skill?
?
A reusable, versioned **instruction set** invoked via slash commands (`/skill-name`). Bundles instructions, routing logic, workflow files, and binary assets.

How fast did ChatGPT reach 100M monthly active users?
?
**2 months** — fastest-growing technology in history. Reached 1M MAU in 5 days.
