content.newText:
---
tags: [lecture]
course: Chatbots
source: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
lecture: 16
slides: "[[30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 16 — Advanced LLM techniques

## Overview

A taxonomy of the engineering tricks behind modern frontier LLMs. **A modern frontier LLM uses nearly all of these simultaneously.** Seven categories:

1. **Architecture** — [[mixture-of-experts|MoE]]
2. **Infrastructure** — [[kv-cache|KV caching]], **paged attention**, [[rope|RoPE]], **kernel fusion**
3. **Attention optimisation** — [[flash-attention|Flash attention]], [[mqa-gqa-mla|MQA/GQA/MLA]], **sparse attention**
4. **Training efficiency** — [[zero-optimizer|ZeRO optimizer]] (1/2/3), [[mixed-precision|Mixed precision]] (BF16, FP8)
5. **Parameter-efficient finetuning** — [[lora|LoRA]], **QLoRA**
6. **Inference optimisation** — [[quantization|Quantization]] (INT8, INT4), [[speculative-decoding|Speculative decoding]]
7. **Alignment** — PPO → DPO → GRPO + [[rlvr|RLVR]]

## Key concepts

- [[mixture-of-experts]]
- [[kv-cache]]
- [[rope]]
- [[flash-attention]]
- [[mqa-gqa-mla]]
- [[zero-optimizer]]
- [[mixed-precision]]
- [[lora]]
- [[quantization]]
- [[speculative-decoding]]
- [[dpo]]
- [[grpo-rlvr]]

## 1. Mixture of Experts (slide 2)

Many parameters total, **only a fraction activated per token**. A learned **gating network** routes each token to a subset of "experts" (specialised feed-forward blocks).

- **DeepSeek V3:** 671B total params, **only 37B active per token**.
- Shared experts handle common patterns; routed experts specialise.
- Used by nearly every frontier model after 2025: DeepSeek V3/R1, LLaMA 4, Gemini, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral 3.

> **Slogan:** "big brain at a small brain price" during inference.

Key challenges: **load balancing across experts** (so some experts don't dominate) + **expert parallelism** in distributed training.

## 2. Infrastructure (slides 3-5)

### KV caching

During autoregressive generation, each new token attends to all previous tokens. Without caching, you recompute K and V projections for every prior token at every step → **O(n²) redundant work**. KV cache stores computed K/V tensors and appends new entries each step → **O(n²) → O(n) per token**.

### Paged Attention (vLLM, Kwon et al. 2023)

Naive KV caching pre-allocates contiguous blocks for max sequence length, wasting memory on short sequences. Paged Attention borrows OS virtual-memory concepts: KV cache split into **fixed-size pages, allocated on demand, non-contiguous in GPU memory**. Result: **2-4× higher serving throughput** — used by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang.

### RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding, Su et al. 2021)

Solves the limitation of fixed positional embeddings (can't extrapolate beyond training context length).

- Instead of *adding* position at input, **rotates Q and K vectors by an angle proportional to position at every attention layer**.
- Dimensions paired, each rotated at a different frequency (like harmonics).
- Key property: $Q \cdot K^T$ depends only on **relative distance** $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.
- **Continuous rotations enable context extension via frequency scaling** — how LLaMA 3 went from 8K → 128K context.

Used by virtually every modern LLM: LLaMA 3/4, DeepSeek V3/R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral, Gemma.

### Kernel fusion

Each GPU operation (matmul, softmax, add) is a separate kernel. Each kernel reads from slow HBM, computes, writes back. Chain many small ops = more time on memory traffic than compute.

**Solution:** combine multiple operations into a **single kernel** — intermediate results stay in fast on-chip SRAM and never round-trip to HBM. Examples: RoPE fused into attention; **Flash Attention is fundamentally a fusion strategy**.

DeepSeek V3's low training cost was partly due to aggressive kernel fusion.

## 3. Attention optimisation (slides 6-8)

### The O(n²) bottleneck

- Attention matrix is $n \times n$ — doubling context quadruples cost.
- Memory for the attention matrix is $O(n^2)$ — at 128K tokens, hundreds of GB.
- KV cache grows linearly with length × heads × head_dim.

For a 70B model with 128K context, the **KV cache alone can consume tens of GB per sequence**.

Three orthogonal axes of optimisation:

| Axis | Approach | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Reduce KV cache size** | Share K/V across heads | MQA, GQA, MLA |
| **(b) Hardware-efficient computation** | Tile + fuse | Flash Attention |
| **(c) Reduce participating tokens** | Sparse patterns | Sliding window, BigBird |

### MQA → GQA → MLA (slide 7)

| Method | Idea | Trade-off | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MQA** (Shazeer 2019) | All query heads share **one** KV pair | Dramatic cache savings (32 KV → 1), small quality loss | PaLM, Falcon, Gemini |
| **GQA** (Ainslie 2023) | Query heads grouped (e.g. 32 Q / 8 KV groups) | Balance of cache + quality | LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral |
| **MLA** (DeepSeek V2, 2024) | Compress K/V into a small **latent vector** before caching | **57× KV cache reduction** vs MHA, claims to outperform | DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3 |

KV cache progression: MHA → MQA (minimal but lossy) → GQA (balanced) → MLA (compressed, best quality).

### Flash Attention + Sparse Attention (slide 8)

> Flash Attention changes **HOW** you compute. Sparse changes **WHAT** you compute. Use both.

- **Flash Attention** (Dao et al. 2022) — tile Q, K, V into blocks; compute attention entirely in fast SRAM; **never materialise the full $n \times n$ matrix in HBM**. **2-4× speedup + significant memory reduction.** Default backend for all transformer training/inference.
- **Sparse Attention** — each token attends to a subset, not the full sequence. Reduces $O(n^2) \to O(n)$.
  - **Sliding Window** (Mistral) — each token attends to a local window (128 or 4096 tokens).
  - **GPT-OSS** alternates full-GQA layers with 128-token sliding window layers.

## 4. Training efficiency (slides 9-10)

### ZeRO (Rajbhandari et al. 2020, Microsoft)

Training stores 3 things per parameter: weights, gradients, optimizer states (Adam = 2× extra). Standard data parallelism stores all three on every GPU.

ZeRO **partitions** them progressively across GPUs:

| Stage | Partitions | Memory | Communication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ZeRO-1** | Optimizer states | ~4× reduction | Minimal |
| **ZeRO-2** | + gradients | More reduction | More |
| **ZeRO-3** | + model weights | Maximum savings — no GPU holds the full model; gathered on the fly | Most |

Available via **DeepSpeed (Microsoft)** and **FSDP (PyTorch native)**. ZeRO-3 lets you train models much larger than a single GPU's memory.

### Mixed precision (slide 10)

| Format | Bits | Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 32 | $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ | Master weights, gradient accumulation, optimizer states |
| **FP16** | 16 | $10^{-4}$ to $65k$ | 2× faster but overflow-prone |
| **BF16** | 16 | Same range as FP32 | Modern default for compute |
| **FP8** | 8 | Smaller range | DeepSeek V3 pioneered for training, **4× memory reduction vs FP32** |

**BF16 mixed precision is universal in modern LLM training.** FP8 emerging.

## 5. LoRA + QLoRA (slide 11)

### LoRA (Hu et al. 2021, Microsoft)

Instead of updating the full weight matrix $W$, freeze it and add a **low-rank decomposition**:

$$\Delta W = A \times B,\ \ A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r},\ B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d},\ r \ll d$$

Typically $r = 8\text{-}64$. Only train $A$ and $B$ — usually 0.1-1% of total params.

**Why it works:** fine-tuning updates have **low intrinsic rank**. At inference, merge $A \times B$ back into $W$ → **zero extra latency**.

### QLoRA (Dettmers et al. 2023)

Base model loaded in **4-bit precision (NF4 format)**, LoRA adapters trained in BF16 on top. **Fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU.**

LoRA / QLoRA **democratised fine-tuning** — any engineer with a single GPU can adapt frontier models.

## 6. Inference optimisation (slides 12-13)

### Quantization

Mixed precision is *training*; quantization is *inference*. Reduce trained model precision:

| Format | Memory savings | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **INT8** | 2× vs FP16 | Minimal loss; widely supported |
| **INT4** | 4× | Noticeable but acceptable; QLoRA & local deployment |

| Technique | When |
|---|---|
| **PTQ** (Post-Training) | Fast, no retraining |
| **QAT** (Quantization-Aware Training) | Higher quality, needs retraining |

**Quantization makes it possible to run a 70B model on consumer hardware.** LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.

### Speculative decoding

Autoregressive generation is sequential — one token at a time through the full model. GPU is **underutilised** because each forward pass doesn't saturate the hardware.

**Idea:** use a small fast **draft model** to propose 4-8 tokens ahead. Then run the large target model on all proposed tokens **in parallel in a single forward pass**. Accepted tokens kept; first rejection → fallback to target model's prediction.

If draft matches target 70-90% of the time → **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical outputs**.

Variants:

- **Separate draft model** (1B drafting for 70B).
- **Self-speculative** (model uses its own early layers).
- **Multi-Token Prediction heads** (DeepSeek V3: 85-90% acceptance).

## 7. Alignment (slides 14-15)

### PPO + RLHF (Ouyang et al. 2022)

The classic [[rlhf|RLHF]] pipeline:

1. **SFT** — Fine-tune on human-written demonstrations.
2. **Reward Model** — Train RM on preference pairs.
3. **PPO** — Use RM to RL-finetune the SFT model.

Requires **4 models simultaneously** (policy, reference, reward, value/critic). Sensitive, expensive, prone to reward hacking.

### DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023, Stanford)

> You can derive a **closed-form expression for the optimal policy** directly from preference data.

- **No reward model. No RL loop.** Just policy + frozen reference + preference pairs.
- Loss directly increases $P(\text{preferred})$ and decreases $P(\text{rejected})$.
- **Essentially supervised learning on preference data.**

Trade-off: DPO is **offline** (fixed dataset), so it can't easily incorporate verifiable rewards or on-policy exploration. Limits ceiling for reasoning tasks.

Used by LLaMA 2/3 and the open-source community as the default starting point.

### GRPO + RLVR (DeepSeek 2024)

**GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)**:

- **Eliminates the critic** from PPO.
- Generate $N$ responses per prompt, score them all, use group **mean as baseline**.
- Each response's advantage = its reward minus group mean.
- ~33% memory reduction, dramatically simpler.

**RLVR (RL with Verifiable Rewards)**:

- Not an algorithm — a **training framework**.
- Replace human preferences with **deterministic verifiers**: compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math, calculators for arithmetic.
- **No reward model needed.** Generate many attempts, check which are correct, reinforce the correct ones.

**GRPO + RLVR** is the recipe behind the reasoning model revolution (DeepSeek R1 etc.).

### Alignment evolution

| Generation | What it removed |
|---|---|
| **PPO** (4 models, complex) | — |
| **DPO** (2 models, offline) | RM + value model |
| **GRPO** (no critic, on-policy) | Value model |
| **GRPO + RLVR** (no RM) | Reward model — replaced by deterministic verifier |

## How it all fits (slide 16)

| Layer | Techniques |
|---|---|
| **Architecture** | MoE |
| **Infrastructure** | KV Caching • Paged Attention • RoPE • Kernel Fusion |
| **Attention** | Flash Attention • MQA / GQA / MLA • Sparse / Sliding Window |
| **Training** | ZeRO • Mixed Precision (BF16 / FP8) |
| **Adaptation** | LoRA • QLoRA |
| **Deployment** | Quantization (INT8 / INT4) • Speculative Decoding |
| **Alignment** | PPO → DPO → GRPO / RLVR |

> **A modern frontier LLM uses nearly all of these simultaneously.** Understanding the full stack is what separates an AI engineer from someone who just calls an API.

## Open questions

- When is sparse attention worth the quality cost? Long-context tasks where most attention is local.
- Is MoE the future for everyone, or just frontier labs? Production deployment is harder due to expert parallelism — smaller teams stick with dense.

- Key property: $Q \cdot K^T$ depends only on **relative distance** $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.
- **Continuous rotations enable context extension via frequency scaling** — how LLaMA 3 went from 8K → 128K context.

Used by virtually every modern LLM: LLaMA 3/4, DeepSeek V3/R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral, Gemma.

### Kernel fusion

Each GPU operation (matmul, softmax, add) is a separate kernel. Each kernel reads from slow HBM, computes, writes back. Chain many small ops = more time on memory traffic than compute.

**Solution:** combine multiple operations into a **single kernel** — intermediate results stay in fast on-chip SRAM and never round-trip to HBM. Examples: RoPE fused into attention; **Flash Attention is fundamentally a fusion strategy**.

DeepSeek V3's low training cost was partly due to aggressive kernel fusion.

## 3. Attention optimisation (slides 6-8)

### The O(n²) bottleneck

- Attention matrix is $n \times n$ — doubling context quadruples cost.
- Memory for the attention matrix is $O(n^2)$ — at 128K tokens, hundreds of GB.
- KV cache grows linearly with length × heads × head_dim.

For a 70B model with 128K context, the **KV cache alone can consume tens of GB per sequence**.

Three orthogonal axes of optimisation:

| Axis | Approach | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Reduce KV cache size** | Share K/V across heads | MQA, GQA, MLA |
| **(b) Hardware-efficient computation** | Tile + fuse | Flash Attention |
| **(c) Reduce participating tokens** | Sparse patterns | Sliding window, BigBird |

### MQA → GQA → MLA (slide 7)

| Method | Idea | Trade-off | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MQA** (Shazeer 2019) | All query heads share **one** KV pair | Dramatic cache savings (32 KV → 1), small quality loss | PaLM, Falcon, Gemini |
| **GQA** (Ainslie 2023) | Query heads grouped (e.g. 32 Q / 8 KV groups) | Balance of cache + quality | LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral |
| **MLA** (DeepSeek V2, 2024) | Compress K/V into a small **latent vector** before caching | **57× KV cache reduction** vs MHA, claims to outperform | DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3 |

KV cache progression: MHA → MQA (minimal but lossy) → GQA (balanced) → MLA (compressed, best quality).

### Flash Attention + Sparse Attention (slide 8)

> Flash Attention changes **HOW** you compute. Sparse changes **WHAT** you compute. Use both.

- **Flash Attention** (Dao et al. 2022) — tile Q, K, V into blocks; compute attention entirely in fast SRAM; **never materialise the full $n \times n$ matrix in HBM**. **2-4× speedup + significant memory reduction.** Default backend for all transformer training/inference.
- **Sparse Attention** — each token attends to a subset, not the full sequence. Reduces $O(n^2) \to O(n)$.
  - **Sliding Window** (Mistral) — each token attends to a local window (128 or 4096 tokens).
  - **GPT-OSS** alternates full-GQA layers with 128-token sliding window layers.

## 4. Training efficiency (slides 9-10)

### ZeRO (Rajbhandari et al. 2020, Microsoft)

Training stores 3 things per parameter: weights, gradients, optimizer states (Adam = 2× extra). Standard data parallelism stores all three on every GPU.

ZeRO **partitions** them progressively across GPUs:

| Stage | Partitions | Memory | Communication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ZeRO-1** | Optimizer states | ~4× reduction | Minimal |
| **ZeRO-2** | + gradients | More reduction | More |
| **ZeRO-3** | + model weights | Maximum savings — no GPU holds the full model; gathered on the fly | Most |

Available via **DeepSpeed (Microsoft)** and **FSDP (PyTorch native)**. ZeRO-3 lets you train models much larger than a single GPU's memory.

### Mixed precision (slide 10)

| Format | Bits | Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 32 | $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ | Master weights, gradient accumulation, optimizer states |
| **FP16** | 16 | $10^{-4}$ to $65k$ | 2× faster but overflow-prone |
| **BF16** | 16 | Same range as FP32 | Modern default for compute |
| **FP8** | 8 | Smaller range | DeepSeek V3 pioneered for training, **4× memory reduction vs FP32** |

**BF16 mixed precision is universal in modern LLM training.** FP8 emerging.

## 5. LoRA + QLoRA (slide 11)

### LoRA (Hu et al. 2021, Microsoft)

Instead of updating the full weight matrix $W$, freeze it and add a **low-rank decomposition**:

$$\Delta W = A \times B,\ \ A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r},\ B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d},\ r \ll d$$

Typically $r = 8\text{-}64$. Only train $A$ and $B$ — usually 0.1-1% of total params.

**Why it works:** fine-tuning updates have **low intrinsic rank**. At inference, merge $A \times B$ back into $W$ → **zero extra latency**.

### QLoRA (Dettmers et al. 2023)

Base model loaded in **4-bit precision (NF4 format)**, LoRA adapters trained in BF16 on top. **Fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU.**

LoRA / QLoRA **democratised fine-tuning** — any engineer with a single GPU can adapt frontier models.

## 6. Inference optimisation (slides 12-13)

### Quantization

Mixed precision is *training*; quantization is *inference*. Reduce trained model precision:

| Format | Memory savings | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **INT8** | 2× vs FP16 | Minimal loss; widely supported |
| **INT4** | 4× | Noticeable but acceptable; QLoRA & local deployment |

| Technique | When |
|---|---|
| **PTQ** (Post-Training) | Fast, no retraining |
| **QAT** (Quantization-Aware Training) | Higher quality, needs retraining |

**Quantization makes it possible to run a 70B model on consumer hardware.** LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.

### Speculative decoding

Autoregressive generation is sequential — one token at a time through the full model. GPU is **underutilised** because each forward pass doesn't saturate the hardware.

**Idea:** use a small fast **draft model** to propose 4-8 tokens ahead. Then run the large target model on all proposed tokens **in parallel in a single forward pass**. Accepted tokens kept; first rejection → fallback to target model's prediction.

If draft matches target 70-90% of the time → **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical outputs**.

Variants:

- **Separate draft model** (1B drafting for 70B).
- **Self-speculative** (model uses its own early layers).
- **Multi-Token Prediction heads** (DeepSeek V3: 85-90% acceptance).

## 7. Alignment (slides 14-15)

### PPO + RLHF (Ouyang et al. 2022)

The classic [[rlhf|RLHF]] pipeline:

1. **SFT** — Fine-tune on human-written demonstrations.
2. **Reward Model** — Train RM on preference pairs.
3. **PPO** — Use RM to RL-finetune the SFT model.

Requires **4 models simultaneously** (policy, reference, reward, value/critic). Sensitive, expensive, prone to reward hacking.

### DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023, Stanford)

> You can derive a **closed-form expression for the optimal policy** directly from preference data.

- **No reward model. No RL loop.** Just policy + frozen reference + preference pairs.
- Loss directly increases $P(\text{preferred})$ and decreases $P(\text{rejected})$.
- **Essentially supervised learning on preference data.**

Trade-off: DPO is **offline** (fixed dataset), so it can't easily incorporate verifiable rewards or on-policy exploration. Limits ceiling for reasoning tasks.

Used by LLaMA 2/3 and the open-source community as the default starting point.

### GRPO + RLVR (DeepSeek 2024)

**GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)**:

- **Eliminates the critic** from PPO.
- Generate $N$ responses per prompt, score them all, use group **mean as baseline**.
- Each response's advantage = its reward minus group mean.
- ~33% memory reduction, dramatically simpler.

**RLVR (RL with Verifiable Rewards)**:

- Not an algorithm — a **training framework**.
- Replace human preferences with **deterministic verifiers**: compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math, calculators for arithmetic.
- **No reward model needed.** Generate many attempts, check which are correct, reinforce the correct ones.

**GRPO + RLVR** is the recipe behind the reasoning model revolution (DeepSeek R1 etc.).

### Alignment evolution

| Generation | What it removed |
|---|---|
| **PPO** (4 models, complex) | — |
| **DPO** (2 models, offline) | RM + value model |
| **GRPO** (no critic, on-policy) | Value model |
| **GRPO + RLVR** (no RM) | Reward model — replaced by deterministic verifier |

## How it all fits (slide 16)

| Layer | Techniques |
|---|---|
| **Architecture** | MoE |
| **Infrastructure** | KV Caching • Paged Attention • RoPE • Kernel Fusion |
| **Attention** | Flash Attention • MQA / GQA / MLA • Sparse / Sliding Window |
| **Training** | ZeRO • Mixed Precision (BF16 / FP8) |
| **Adaptation** | LoRA • QLoRA |
| **Deployment** | Quantization (INT8 / INT4) • Speculative Decoding |
| **Alignment** | PPO → DPO → GRPO / RLVR |

> **A modern frontier LLM uses nearly all of these simultaneously.** Understanding the full stack is what separates an AI engineer from someone who just calls an API.

## Open questions

- When is sparse attention worth the quality cost? Long-context tasks where most attention is local.
- Is MoE the future for everyone, or just frontier labs? Production deployment is harder due to expert parallelism — smaller teams stick with dense.

---
name: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
description: Many parameters, only a fraction activated per token — gating network routes tokens to specialised "expert" sub-networks
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, architecture]
---

# Mixture of Experts (MoE)

A model architecture with **many parameters total but only a fraction activated per token**. A learned **gating network** routes each token to a subset of "experts" — specialised feed-forward blocks.

## The idea

Replace each transformer FFN block with $E$ parallel FFN blocks (the experts) plus a tiny **gating network** that, for each token, selects the top-$k$ experts to use (commonly $k = 2$).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Tok[Token embedding] --> Gate[Gating network]
    Gate -->|top-k routing| Sel[Select k experts]
    Sel --> E1[Expert 1]
    Sel --> E2[Expert 2]
    Sel --> Ek[Expert k]
    E1 --> Sum[Weighted sum]
    E2 --> Sum
    Ek --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Output]
    classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0
    class Gate,Sel chatbot
```

## Headline number

**DeepSeek V3:** 671B total params, **only 37B active per token**.

The model has the *capacity* of a 671B dense model but the *inference cost* of a 37B dense model. **"Big brain at a small brain price."**

## Two kinds of experts

- **Shared experts** — process every token; handle common patterns.
- **Routed experts** — selected per token by the gating network; specialise on niches.

## Key engineering challenges

- **Load balancing** — without a balancing loss, the gating network may overuse a few experts and starve others.
- **Expert parallelism** — distributing experts across GPUs/nodes adds communication overhead.
- **Routing stability** — gating decisions can be noisy early in training.

## Adoption

Nearly every frontier model after 2025: **DeepSeek V3/R1, LLaMA 4, Gemini, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral 3**.

## Why it works

Different tokens require different "kinds of thinking" — code tokens benefit from one specialisation, prose tokens from another. MoE lets the model **dynamically allocate capacity** to where it's needed instead of forcing every token through the same dense FFN.

## Trade-offs

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Massive capacity scaling | More memory than active-params suggests (you still need to store all experts) |
| Inference cost grows with active params, not total | Distributed serving is harder |
| Per-token specialisation | Load balancing requires care |

## Related

- [[transformer-encoder]] — what MoE replaces the FFN of
- [[zero-optimizer]] — distributed training infrastructure

content.newText:
---
name: KV cache (and Paged Attention)
description: Cache K and V projections during autoregressive generation — turns $O(n^2)$ work into $O(n)$ per token. Paged Attention manages it efficiently.
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, inference]
---

# KV cache (and Paged Attention)

A foundational inference optimisation for autoregressive LLMs.

## The problem

During autoregressive generation, each new token's attention needs the K and V projections of **all previous tokens**.

Without caching, you recompute K and V for every prior token at every step → **$O(n^2)$ redundant work** per generated sequence.

## KV cache

Store computed K and V tensors. Each new step only computes K and V for the **single new token** and appends to the cache.

| | Naive | With KV cache |
|---|---|---|
| Per-token work | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Memory | $O(1)$ extra | $O(n \cdot \text{heads} \cdot d_k)$ |

KV cache trades **memory for speed**. **Universal in all autoregressive LLMs.**

## The memory problem

- Cache size grows linearly with sequence length × heads × head_dim.
- For a 70B model with 128K context: **tens of GB per sequence**.
- Multiply by a batch of users → exhaust GPU memory fast.

This drives the family of optimisations: [[mqa-gqa-mla|MQA/GQA/MLA]] reduce per-token cache size; **Paged Attention** reduces memory waste from over-allocation.

## Paged Attention (vLLM, Kwon et al. 2023)

Naive KV caching pre-allocates **contiguous blocks** for max sequence length — wastes memory on short sequences (most users don't fill 128K context).

Paged Attention borrows from **OS virtual memory**:

- KV cache split into **fixed-size pages**.
- Pages **allocated on demand**.
- Pages can be **non-contiguous in GPU memory**.

Result: **2-4× higher serving throughput**.

## Used by

KV caching: every autoregressive LLM (universal). Paged Attention: **vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang** (every modern serving stack).

## Related

- [[mqa-gqa-mla]] — reduce cache size per token
- [[flash-attention]] — orthogonal — speeds up the actual attention matmul
- [[transformer-encoder]] — what's being attended over

content.newText:
---
name: RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)
description: Position encoded by rotating Q and K vectors at every attention layer — dot product depends on relative distance, enables context extension
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, transformer, positional-encoding]
---

# RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)

A positional encoding for transformers (Su et al. 2021) that **rotates Q and K vectors** by an angle proportional to position at every attention layer. The dominant scheme in modern LLMs (LLaMA 3/4, DeepSeek V3/R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral, Gemma).

## The problem with previous position encodings

- **Sinusoidal absolute** (Vaswani 2017) — added at input only; doesn't generalise beyond training context length cleanly.
- **Learned absolute** (BERT/ViT) — needs a learned vector per position; no extrapolation at all.

## How RoPE works

For each (Q, K) head dimension pair, **rotate by an angle proportional to position**:

$$Q_m \to R(m \theta)\, Q_m, \quad K_n \to R(n \theta)\, K_n$$

where $R(\phi)$ is a 2D rotation matrix and $\theta$ is a frequency that varies per dimension pair (like sinusoidal harmonics).

## The key property

After rotation, the dot product becomes:

$$Q_m^T K_n = (R(m\theta) Q)^T (R(n\theta) K) = Q^T R((n - m)\theta) K$$

→ **Dot product depends only on the relative distance $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.**

This is a **relative** positional encoding even though it's applied as an absolute rotation per token.

## Context extension

Because rotations are continuous, you can scale frequencies to handle **longer contexts at inference time than during training**. This is how:

- **LLaMA 3** went from 8K → 128K context.
- Modern models support 128K, 1M, even 2M tokens with frequency-scaling tricks (NTK scaling, YaRN, LongRoPE).

## Why it dominates

| | Sinusoidal | Learned absolute | RoPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Where applied | Input embedding | Input embedding | **Every attention layer** |
| Relative distance? | Approximates | No | **Yes** (exact) |
| Extrapolates? | Some | No | **Yes**, with frequency scaling |
| Used in modern LLMs? | Rare | Rare | **Universal** |

## Related

- [[positional-encoding]] — broader concept, includes sinusoidal/learned/rotary
- [[multi-head-attention]] — RoPE applied per head
- [[transformer-encoder]]

--- name: Flash Attention description: Compute attention in tiled blocks within fast on-chip SRAM, never materialising the full $n \times n$ matrix in HBM type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, attention, gpu] --- # Flash Attention A hardware-aware reformulation of attention (Dao et al. 2022). **Changes HOW you compute attention, not WHAT you compute** — output is mathematically identical to vanilla attention, but **2-4× faster** with **significant memory reduction**. ## The problem Standard attention computes the full $n \times n$ attention matrix in HBM (slow GPU memory): ``` Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) V ``` For $n = 32$K tokens, that matrix is 1B entries → tens of GB. Most of the runtime is **memory traffic**, not arithmetic. ## The Flash Attention trick **Tile** Q, K, V into blocks. For each block: 1. Load Q-block, K-block, V-block from HBM into fast on-chip SRAM (much smaller, much faster). 2. Compute that piece of the attention matrix entirely in SRAM. 3. Update running softmax statistics (online softmax algorithm). 4. Write only the output block back to HBM. **The full $n \times n$ matrix is never materialised.** Memory complexity drops from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HBM[HBM - slow] QH[Q] --- KH[K] --- VH[V] --- OH[Output] end subgraph SRAM[On-chip SRAM - fast] Tile[Q-block × K-block × V-block
compute attention here] end HBM -->|tile load| SRAM SRAM -->|output block| HBM classDef fast fill:#e3f2fd classDef slow fill:#ffebee class SRAM fast class HBM slow ``` ## Result - **2-4× speedup** on training and inference. - Memory complexity $O(n)$ instead of $O(n^2)$. - **Mathematically identical output** to vanilla attention — drop-in replacement. ## A kernel-fusion strategy Flash Attention is fundamentally a **kernel fusion** strategy: combine matmul + softmax + matmul into a single GPU kernel that runs entirely in SRAM. Same idea as fusing RoPE into attention. ## Adoption **Default backend for all transformer training and inference.** Available in PyTorch's `scaled_dot_product_attention`, FlashAttention library, vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, etc. ## Variants - **Flash Attention 2** (Dao 2023) — better parallelism, ~2× faster than v1. - **Flash Attention 3** (2024) — Hopper-optimised, asynchronous compute, FP8 support. ## Combine with sparse attention Flash Attention changes *how* you compute. **Sparse attention** changes *what* you compute (which tokens participate). They compose — use both for long-context efficiency. ## Related - [[kv-cache]] — orthogonal — reduces KV memory, not compute - [[mqa-gqa-mla]] — orthogonal — reduces KV size per token - [[transformer-encoder]] — what's being optimised

--- name: Flash Attention description: Compute attention in tiled blocks within fast on-chip SRAM, never materialising the full $n \times n$ matrix in HBM type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, attention, gpu] --- # Flash Attention A hardware-aware reformulation of attention (Dao et al. 2022). **Changes HOW you compute attention, not WHAT you compute** — output is mathematically identical to vanilla attention, but **2-4× faster** with **significant memory reduction**. ## The problem Standard attention computes the full $n \times n$ attention matrix in HBM (slow GPU memory): ``` Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) V ``` For $n = 32$K tokens, that matrix is 1B entries → tens of GB. Most of the runtime is **memory traffic**, not arithmetic. ## The Flash Attention trick **Tile** Q, K, V into blocks. For each block: 1. Load Q-block, K-block, V-block from HBM into fast on-chip SRAM (much smaller, much faster). 2. Compute that piece of the attention matrix entirely in SRAM. 3. Update running softmax statistics (online softmax algorithm). 4. Write only the output block back to HBM. **The full $n \times n$ matrix is never materialised.** Memory complexity drops from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HBM[HBM - slow] QH[Q] --- KH[K] --- VH[V] --- OH[Output] end subgraph SRAM[On-chip SRAM - fast] Tile[Q-block × K-block × V-block
compute attention here] end HBM -->|tile load| SRAM SRAM -->|output block| HBM classDef fast fill:#e3f2fd classDef slow fill:#ffebee class SRAM fast class HBM slow ``` ## Result - **2-4× speedup** on training and inference. - Memory complexity $O(n)$ instead of $O(n^2)$. - **Mathematically identical output** to vanilla attention — drop-in replacement. ## A kernel-fusion strategy Flash Attention is fundamentally a **kernel fusion** strategy: combine matmul + softmax + matmul into a single GPU kernel that runs entirely in SRAM. Same idea as fusing RoPE into attention. ## Adoption **Default backend for all transformer training and inference.** Available in PyTorch's `scaled_dot_product_attention`, FlashAttention library, vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, etc. ## Variants - **Flash Attention 2** (Dao 2023) — better parallelism, ~2× faster than v1. - **Flash Attention 3** (2024) — Hopper-optimised, asynchronous compute, FP8 support. ## Combine with sparse attention Flash Attention changes *how* you compute. **Sparse attention** changes *what* you compute (which tokens participate). They compose — use both for long-context efficiency. ## Related - [[kv-cache]] — orthogonal — reduces KV memory, not compute - [[mqa-gqa-mla]] — orthogonal — reduces KV size per token - [[transformer-encoder]] — what's being optimised

---
name: MQA / GQA / MLA — KV-cache reduction families
description: Three strategies to shrink the KV cache by sharing or compressing K/V across attention heads — Multi-Query, Grouped-Query, Multi-Head Latent Attention
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, attention]
---

# MQA / GQA / MLA — KV-cache reduction families

Three strategies to shrink the per-token [[kv-cache|KV cache]] by sharing or compressing K and V across attention heads. The KV cache dominates memory in long-context LLMs, so cache-reduction is critical.

## Standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

Each of $H$ heads has its own $K, V$ pair. KV cache size = $n \cdot H \cdot d_k \cdot 2$ (the 2 = K + V).

## MQA — Multi-Query Attention (Shazeer 2019)

**All query heads share a single K/V pair.**

- Cache savings: dramatic — 32 KV heads → 1 KV pair → **32× reduction**.
- Quality cost: small but measurable.
- Used by **PaLM, Falcon, Gemini**.

## GQA — Grouped-Query Attention (Ainslie et al. 2023)

A middle ground. Query heads grouped; **each group shares one KV pair**.

- E.g. 32 query heads / 8 KV groups = 4 queries per KV pair → **4× cache reduction**.
- Quality cost: minimal.
- **Most popular today** — used by **LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral**.

## MLA — Multi-Head Latent Attention (DeepSeek V2, 2024)

**Compress** K and V into a small **latent vector** before caching, **decompress** at attention time.

- **57× KV cache reduction** vs MHA.
- Claims to **outperform** MHA, not just match it.
- Used by **DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3**.

## Side-by-side

| Method | KV cache | Quality | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MHA** (standard) | Full | Best (baseline) | Older models |
| **MQA** | Minimal | Slightly worse | PaLM, Falcon, Gemini |
| **GQA** | Balanced | Near-MHA | **LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral** (modern default) |
| **MLA** | Compressed | Equal or better than MHA | DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3 |

## Progression

```
MHA (full) → MQA (minimal but lossy) → GQA (balanced) → MLA (compressed, best quality)
```

## Why these matter

For a 70B model with 128K context, the KV cache alone can consume tens of GB per sequence. Multiply by a batch of users → exhaust GPU memory fast. **Cache-reduction is the only way to serve long-context LLMs at scale.**

## Related

- [[kv-cache]] — what's being reduced
- [[multi-head-attention]] — what's being modified
- [[flash-attention]] — orthogonal — reduces compute, not cache

--- name: ZeRO optimizer (1, 2, 3) description: Progressively partition optimizer states, gradients, and weights across GPUs — train models far larger than a single GPU can hold type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, training, distributed] --- # ZeRO optimizer (1, 2, 3) A distributed-training technique (Rajbhandari et al. 2020, Microsoft) that **progressively partitions** the components of training state across GPUs. Three stages, each adding more partitioning. ## The problem Training stores 3 things per parameter: - **Weights** (FP32 master + BF16 working). - **Gradients**. - **Optimizer states** — for Adam: 1st and 2nd moments (2× extra). In **standard data parallelism**, **every GPU stores all three**. For a 70B model in BF16 + Adam, that's hundreds of GB per GPU. ## ZeRO progressive partitioning | Stage | What it partitions across $N$ GPUs | Memory savings | Comm overhead | |---|---|---|---| | **ZeRO-1** | Optimizer states | ~4× reduction | Minimal | | **ZeRO-2** | + gradients | More savings | More | | **ZeRO-3** | + model weights | Maximum — no GPU holds the full model; weights gathered on demand | Most | ```mermaid flowchart TD Vanilla["Standard DP
each GPU: weights + grads + optimizer state"] Z1["ZeRO-1
partition: optimizer state"] Z2["ZeRO-2
partition: optimizer + gradients"] Z3["ZeRO-3
partition: optimizer + gradients + weights"] Vanilla --> Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 ``` ## ZeRO-3 The most aggressive — no single GPU holds the full model. Weights are **gathered just-in-time** for each forward/backward pass, then re-partitioned. Lets you train models far larger than a single GPU's memory. Cost: more communication between GPUs per step. ## Implementations | Library | Maintainer | |---|---| | **DeepSpeed** | Microsoft (the original) | | **FSDP** (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) | PyTorch native (later, similar idea) | ## When to use which - **ZeRO-1 / ZeRO-2** — model fits per-GPU but optimizer states blow it up. - **ZeRO-3 / FSDP** — model itself doesn't fit per GPU. ## Related - [[mixed-precision]] — what BF16/FP32 master weights look like - [[lora]] — alternative path to fitting big-model finetuning on small hardware - [[mixture-of-experts]] — needs expert parallelism, often combined with ZeRO

content.newText:
---
name: Mixed precision training
description: Train in lower precision (FP16, BF16, FP8) for speed/memory but keep master state in FP32 for stability
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, training]
---

# Mixed precision training

Use **lower precision** (FP16, BF16, FP8) for fast computation **and** **higher precision** (FP32) for the parts that need numerical stability — the best of both worlds.

## The four formats

| Format | Bits | Range | Speed | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 32 | $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ | Baseline | Master weights, gradient accumulation, optimizer states |
| **FP16** | 16 | $10^{-4}$ to $65k$ | 2× faster than FP32 | Forward/backward — but **overflow-prone** |
| **BF16** (Brain Float) | 16 | Same range as FP32 | 2× faster | **Modern default for compute** — same range as FP32, lower fraction precision |
| **FP8** | 8 | Smaller range | 4× faster than FP32 | Pioneered by **DeepSeek V3** for training |

## What lives where

| Component | Precision |
|---|---|
| Master weights | **FP32** |
| Gradient accumulation | **FP32** |
| Optimizer states (Adam moments) | **FP32** |
| Forward matmul + activations | **BF16** |
| Backward gradient computation | **BF16** |
| Input embeddings | **BF16** |

## Why BF16 over FP16

| | FP16 | BF16 |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $10^{-4}$ to $65k$ — narrow | Same as FP32 |
| Fraction precision | More bits | Fewer bits |
| Overflow risk | High | Low |
| Underflow risk | High | Low |

BF16 trades **fraction precision** for **range**. For LLM training, range matters more than precision (gradients can be tiny or huge); BF16 is the **modern universal default**.

## FP8 (the new frontier)

Pioneered by **DeepSeek V3** for training. **4× memory reduction vs FP32, 2× vs BF16.** Requires hardware support (Hopper, Blackwell). Not universal yet but emerging.

## Mixed precision vs quantization

| | Mixed precision | [[quantization|Quantization]] |
|---|---|---|
| When | Training | Inference |
| Goal | Faster training, less memory | Smaller deployed model |
| Output | A trained model in BF16 | A compressed inference model in INT8/INT4 |

## Related

- [[zero-optimizer]] — partitions FP32 master weights / gradients / states across GPUs
- [[quantization]] — inference-time analog

content.newText:
---
name: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and QLoRA
description: Fine-tune by adding small low-rank update matrices instead of changing the full weights — democratised LLM finetuning
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, finetuning, parameter-efficient]
---

# LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and QLoRA

A **parameter-efficient finetuning** method (Hu et al. 2021, Microsoft). Instead of updating the full weight matrix $W$, **freeze it and add a small low-rank decomposition**. **QLoRA** combines LoRA with 4-bit quantization for even more efficiency.

## LoRA mechanics

For each weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ in a transformer, freeze $W$ and add:

$$\Delta W = A \times B, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}, \quad r \ll d$$

Effective weight at inference: $W + \Delta W = W + A B$.

Train **only $A$ and $B$**. Typical rank $r = 8$ to $64$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input] --> Wfrozen["W (frozen)"]
    X --> A["A (trainable, d×r)"]
    A --> B["B (trainable, r×d)"]
    Wfrozen --> Add[+]
    B --> Add
    Add --> Out[Output]
    classDef frozen fill:#ffebee
    classDef train fill:#e3f2fd
    class Wfrozen frozen
    class A,B train
```

## What you save

- **Trainable params** — typically **0.1-1%** of total params.
- **Memory** — only optimizer states for $A, B$, not for $W$.
- **Storage** — checkpoint only the LoRA weights, not the full model.

## Why it works

Empirical: **fine-tuning updates have low intrinsic rank**. The model doesn't need to change every weight — most of the useful adjustment lives in a low-rank subspace.

## Inference cost

After training, **merge $A B$ back into $W$**:

$$W' = W + A B$$

→ **Zero extra latency** at inference. The model looks exactly like a regular fine-tuned model.

## QLoRA (Dettmers et al. 2023)

Combine LoRA with **4-bit quantization**:

- Base model loaded in **4-bit precision** (NF4 — Normal Float 4 format, optimised for normally-distributed weights).
- LoRA adapters trained in BF16 on top.
- **Fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU.**

## Why these democratised LLM finetuning

Before LoRA: adapting a frontier model required massive compute (tens of GPUs, full-model gradients, full optimizer states). After LoRA: a single hobbyist GPU can fine-tune large models. The open-source LLM community runs on LoRA / QLoRA.

## Related

- [[mixed-precision]] — same precision-trade philosophy
- [[zero-optimizer]] — alternative path to fitting big-model training
- [[quantization]] — what QLoRA uses for the base model
- [[transfer-learning]] — LoRA is a flavour of finetuning

content.newText:
---
name: Quantization (INT8 / INT4)
description: Reduce trained model precision for inference — 2-4× memory savings to run large models on consumer hardware
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, inference]
---

# Quantization (INT8 / INT4)

**Inference optimisation** — reduce trained model precision (BF16 → INT8 / INT4) to make it smaller and faster to run.

> **Mixed precision is for training. Quantization is for inference.**

## Two techniques

| Technique | When | Cost | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PTQ** (Post-Training Quantization) | After training, on the trained weights | No retraining | Good for INT8, sometimes degrades at INT4 |
| **QAT** (Quantization-Aware Training) | Simulate quantization **during training** so model learns to be robust | Requires retraining | Higher quality, especially at low bits |

## Two formats

| Format | Memory savings | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **INT8** | ~2× vs FP16 | Minimal loss; widely supported |
| **INT4** | ~4× vs FP16 | Noticeable but acceptable; QLoRA, local deployment |

## Why this matters

Quantization is **what makes it possible to run a 70B model on consumer hardware**. Without it, the model wouldn't fit. With INT4 quantization, a 70B model takes ~35 GB — fits on a single high-end GPU or two consumer cards.

**LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.**

## Numerical mechanics (sketch)

Map a floating-point weight $w$ to an integer representation via per-channel or per-tensor scaling:

$$w \approx s \cdot q,\quad q \in \{-2^{b-1}, \ldots, 2^{b-1} - 1\}$$

Critical to choose $s$ (scale) per channel/group/tensor to preserve dynamic range. **Outlier-aware** quantization (handling extreme weight values specially) is the headline research direction post-2023.

## Mixed-precision quantization

In practice, use **mixed quantization**: keep sensitive layers (attention, embeddings) at higher precision (BF16/INT8) and less sensitive layers (FFN) at lower (INT4). Tools like **GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant** automate this.

## Quantization vs Mixed Precision

| | [[mixed-precision|Mixed Precision]] | Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Training | Inference |
| Goal | Fast, low-memory training | Small, fast deployed model |
| Bits | 16 (BF16) | 8 or 4 (INT) |
| Output | A trained model in BF16 | A compressed model in INT |

## Related

- [[lora]] / **QLoRA** — combines LoRA with INT4 quantization
- [[mixed-precision]] — training-time analog
- [[speculative-decoding]] — orthogonal inference speedup

| **INT4** | ~4× vs FP16 | Noticeable but acceptable; QLoRA, local deployment |

## Why this matters

Quantization is **what makes it possible to run a 70B model on consumer hardware**. Without it, the model wouldn't fit. With INT4 quantization, a 70B model takes ~35 GB — fits on a single high-end GPU or two consumer cards.

**LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.**

## Numerical mechanics (sketch)

Map a floating-point weight $w$ to an integer representation via per-channel or per-tensor scaling:

$$w \approx s \cdot q,\quad q \in \{-2^{b-1}, \ldots, 2^{b-1} - 1\}$$

Critical to choose $s$ (scale) per channel/group/tensor to preserve dynamic range. **Outlier-aware** quantization (handling extreme weight values specially) is the headline research direction post-2023.

## Mixed-precision quantization

In practice, use **mixed quantization**: keep sensitive layers (attention, embeddings) at higher precision (BF16/INT8) and less sensitive layers (FFN) at lower (INT4). Tools like **GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant** automate this.

## Quantization vs Mixed Precision

| | [[mixed-precision|Mixed Precision]] | Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Training | Inference |
| Goal | Fast, low-memory training | Small, fast deployed model |
| Bits | 16 (BF16) | 8 or 4 (INT) |
| Output | A trained model in BF16 | A compressed model in INT |

## Related

- [[lora]] / **QLoRA** — combines LoRA with INT4 quantization
- [[mixed-precision]] — training-time analog
- [[speculative-decoding]] — orthogonal inference speedup

--- name: Speculative decoding description: Use a small fast draft model to propose tokens; verify many at once with the big target model — 2-3× speedup with identical outputs type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, inference] --- # Speculative decoding An inference speedup that uses a **small fast draft model** to propose tokens, then verifies many at once with the **big target model** in a single forward pass. **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical outputs.** ## The problem Autoregressive generation is sequential — one token at a time through the full model. **GPU is underutilised** because each single-token forward pass doesn't saturate the hardware (the model is bottlenecked by memory bandwidth, not compute). ## The trick ```mermaid flowchart LR Draft["Small draft model
(e.g. 1B)"] -->|"propose 4-8 tokens"| Prop[Proposed tokens] Prop -->|"verify in parallel"| Target["Large target model
(e.g. 70B)"] Target -->|"accept all that match"| Out[Accepted tokens] Target -->|"first reject → fallback"| Fall[Use target's prediction at first mismatch] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class Target chatbot ``` 1. Use a small **draft** model (e.g. 1B params) to generate the next 4-8 tokens autoregressively. Cheap. 2. Run the **target** (large) model on **all proposed tokens in parallel** — single forward pass. 3. For each position, check if the target model's distribution agrees with the draft's choice. **Keep accepted tokens; at first rejection, fall back to the target's prediction at that position.** 4. Repeat. ## Why it speeds up - The big model can verify $N$ tokens in roughly the **time of generating 1** (because the verification is one parallel forward pass). - If draft matches target **70-90% of the time** (common for "easy" tokens like punctuation, common words), you get **2-3× speedup**. - **Output is mathematically identical** to greedy/sampling from the target model alone — no quality loss. ## Variants | Variant | Approach | |---|---| | **Separate draft model** | A 1B model drafting for a 70B model | | **Self-speculative** | Use the model's own early layers as the "draft" | | **Multi-Token Prediction heads** | Add MTP heads during training (DeepSeek V3: **85-90% acceptance rate**) | ## Limitations - Need a draft model whose predictions correlate with the target — distillation helps. - Speedup falls if acceptance rate is low (e.g. on hard or unusual contexts). - Memory cost — both models loaded simultaneously. ## When to use Anywhere autoregressive generation latency matters: chat, code completion, batch inference. **Default in modern serving stacks** (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang). ## Related - [[kv-cache]] — orthogonal — what speculative decoding speeds up - [[quantization]] — orthogonal — different inference optimisation

content.newText:
---
name: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)
description: Closed-form supervised loss for preference alignment — no reward model, no RL loop. Replaces RLHF with simpler training.
type: concept
courses: [Chatbots]
sources:
  - course: Chatbots
    file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
tags: [concept, llm, alignment, training]
---

# DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)

A simpler alternative to [[rlhf|RLHF]] (Rafailov et al. 2023, Stanford). The insight: **you can derive a closed-form expression for the optimal policy directly from preference data — no reward model, no RL loop needed.**

## Compared to RLHF (PPO)

| | RLHF (PPO) | DPO |
|---|---|---|
| Models in memory | 4 (policy, ref, reward, value) | 2 (policy, frozen ref) |
| Training | RL loop with sampled rollouts | Supervised gradient steps on preference pairs |
| Reward model needed? | Yes | **No** |
| Sensitive to hyperparameters | Yes | Less |
| Can incorporate verifiable rewards? | Yes (custom RM) | **No** (offline only) |

## How DPO works

Given preference pairs $(x, y_w, y_l)$ where $y_w$ is preferred over $y_l$ for prompt $x$:

The DPO loss directly **increases $P(y_w)$ and decreases $P(y_l)$** relative to a frozen reference model:

$$\mathcal L_\text{DPO} = -\mathbb E_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\Big[\log \sigma\Big(\beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_l \mid x)}\Big)\Big]$$

Essentially **supervised learning on preference data** with a Bradley-Terry-style sigmoid loss.

## The trade-off — DPO is offline

- DPO trains on a **fixed preference dataset** rather than generating new responses.
- Limits its ceiling for tasks where on-policy exploration matters (reasoning, code).
- **Can't easily incorporate verifiable rewards** — you'd need to grow the preference set.

## Who uses it

- **LLaMA 2/3** preference alignment.
- **Open-source community default** for "I want to align my model without a massive RL infrastructure."

## When to use which

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Preference alignment, simple, no big infrastructure | **DPO** |
| Need on-policy exploration / verifiable rewards | **PPO / GRPO** |
| Reasoning, math, code with auto-verifiers | **GRPO + RLVR** |

## Related

- [[rlhf]] — the more complex predecessor
- [[grpo-rlvr]] — newer, even simpler
- [[transfer-learning]] — DPO is a finetuning method

content.newText:
--- name: GRPO + RLVR description: GRPO eliminates the critic from PPO by using group-mean baseline; RLVR replaces reward model with deterministic verifiers. The recipe behind reasoning models. type: concept courses: [Chatbots] sources: - course: Chatbots file: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf created: 2026-04-26 tags: [concept, llm, alignment, reasoning] --- # GRPO + RLVR The state-of-the-art alignment recipe for **reasoning models**. **GRPO** simplifies PPO by eliminating the critic; **RLVR** replaces the reward model with deterministic verifiers. Combined, they're the recipe behind DeepSeek R1 and the post-2024 reasoning model wave. ## GRPO — Group Relative Policy Optimization (DeepSeek 2024) Eliminates the **critic (value model)** from PPO by using group statistics as a baseline. ```mermaid flowchart TD Prompt["Prompt"] Prompt --> R1[Response 1] Prompt --> R2[Response 2] Prompt --> Rn[Response N] R1 --> S1[Score 1] R2 --> S2[Score 2] Rn --> Sn[Score N] S1 --> Mean[Group mean] S2 --> Mean Sn --> Mean Mean --> Adv["Advantage =
response score - group mean"] Adv --> Update[Policy update] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class Mean,Adv chatbot ``` For each prompt: 1. Generate a group of $N$ responses. 2. Score them all (with a reward model, or with RLVR — see below). 3. Use the **group mean as baseline**. 4. Each response's **advantage** = its reward minus group mean. 5. Update the policy. | | PPO | GRPO | |---|---|---| | Critic (value model) | Required | **Removed** | | Memory | 4 models | 3 models | | Memory savings | — | **~33% reduction** | DeepSeek R1 was trained with GRPO. ## RLVR — Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards A **training framework**, not an algorithm. Replace the human-trained reward model with **deterministic verifiers**: | Domain | Verifier | |---|---| | Code | Compiler + test runner (does the code compile, do the tests pass?) | | Math | Symbolic checker (is the algebraic answer correct?) | | Arithmetic | Calculator (is the number right?) | | Logic puzzles | Solver / proof checker | **No reward model needed.** Generate many attempts, check which are correct, reinforce the correct ones. ## The combination GRPO + RLVR pipeline: 1. Generate $N$ responses to a verifiable prompt. 2. Run the verifier on each — produces 0/1 reward. 3. GRPO uses group mean as baseline → advantage per response. 4. Update policy. **No reward model, no critic, no human-in-the-loop annotation.** ## Why this is the reasoning model breakthrough Pre-RLVR alignment was bottlenecked on **expensive human preference annotation**. RLVR replaces it with **infinite, free, automatic reward signal** — limited only by compute. You can now scale RL training to millions of math/code problems. > If you're building reasoning capabilities, GRPO + RLVR is the state of the art. ## The alignment evolution | Generation | What it removed | |---|---| | **PPO** (4 models, complex) | — | | **DPO** (2 models, offline) | RM + value model | | **GRPO** (no critic, on-policy) | Value model | | **GRPO + RLVR** (no RM) | Reward model — replaced by deterministic verifier | ## Limitations - **Only works for verifiable domains.** Open-ended creative writing, dialogue helpfulness, etc. still need RM-based alignment (DPO or PPO). - Hybrid systems use RLVR for math/code + DPO for chat. ## Related - [[rlhf]] — the predecessor - [[dpo]] — sibling simplification - [[gpt-models]] — what alignment is applied to

content.newText:
---
tags: [flashcards, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
lecture: 16
source: Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Lecture 16 — Advanced LLM techniques (flashcards)

What is Mixture of Experts in one sentence?
?
A model with **many parameters total but only a fraction activated per token** — a learned gating network routes each token to a subset of "expert" sub-networks.

How many parameters does DeepSeek V3 have, and how many are active per token?
?
**671B total, only 37B active per token** — capacity of 671B at the inference cost of 37B.

What problem does KV caching solve?
?
Naively, autoregressive generation recomputes K and V for every prior token at every step → **O(n²)** redundant work. KV caching stores K/V tensors and only computes new ones for the new token → **O(n) per token**.

What is Paged Attention?
?
A KV-cache memory-management technique (vLLM) borrowing OS virtual-memory concepts — KV cache split into **fixed-size pages allocated on demand**, non-contiguous in GPU memory. **2-4× higher serving throughput.**

What does RoPE stand for and what does it do?
?
**Rotary Position Embedding.** Rotates Q and K vectors by an angle proportional to position at every attention layer. The dot product depends only on **relative distance** $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.

How did LLaMA 3 extend its context from 8K to 128K?
?
**Frequency scaling of RoPE** — because rotations are continuous, you can scale frequencies to handle longer contexts than seen during training.

What does Flash Attention change about the computation?
?
**HOW** you compute, not WHAT. Tiles Q, K, V into blocks and computes attention entirely in fast SRAM, **never materialising the full n×n matrix in HBM**. Output is mathematically identical; **2-4× speedup**.

Why is KV cache the bottleneck for long-context LLMs?
?
Cache size grows linearly with $n \times \text{heads} \times d_k$. For a 70B model with 128K context, the cache alone consumes tens of GB per sequence. Serving many users → exhausts GPU memory.

Compare MQA, GQA, and MLA in one line each.
?
**MQA** — all query heads share **one** KV pair (huge cache savings, slight quality loss). **GQA** — query heads grouped, each group shares KV (balanced, modern default). **MLA** — compresses K/V into a **latent vector** (57× cache reduction, can outperform MHA).

What is ZeRO and what does each stage partition?
?
**ZeRO-1**: optimizer states. **ZeRO-2**: + gradients. **ZeRO-3**: + model weights — no GPU holds the full model. From DeepSpeed (Microsoft); FSDP is the PyTorch equivalent.

What's the difference between BF16 and FP16?
?
Both 16-bit, but **BF16 (Brain Float)** has the **same range as FP32** (more bits to exponent, fewer to fraction) — much less prone to overflow/underflow. **BF16 is the modern universal default for LLM training.**

What is LoRA?
?
**Low-Rank Adaptation.** Freeze the full weight matrix $W$ and add a learned low-rank update $\Delta W = AB$ where $A \in \mathbb R^{d \times r}, B \in \mathbb R^{r \times d}$ with $r \ll d$. Trains 0.1-1% of params; merge $AB$ back into $W$ at inference for **zero extra latency**.

What is QLoRA?
?
LoRA + **4-bit quantization** of the base model (NF4 format). Lets you fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48 GB GPU.

What's the difference between mixed precision and quantization?
?
**Mixed precision** = training-time, uses BF16/FP8 for compute and FP32 for master state. **Quantization** = inference-time, reduces a trained model to INT8 / INT4 for smaller, faster deployment.

What is speculative decoding?
?
Use a small fast **draft model** to propose 4-8 tokens, then verify them all in parallel with the big **target model**. Accepted tokens kept; first rejection → fall back. **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical output.**

Compare the alignment evolution: PPO → DPO → GRPO → GRPO+RLVR. What does each remove?
?
**PPO** — 4 models (policy, reference, reward, value). **DPO** — eliminates reward model + value model (just policy + frozen reference, supervised). **GRPO** — eliminates value model (uses group mean as baseline). **GRPO + RLVR** — eliminates reward model too (uses deterministic verifiers like compilers).

What does RLVR stand for and what does it replace?
?
**RL with Verifiable Rewards.** Replaces the human-preference reward model with **deterministic verifiers** — compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math, calculators. No human annotation needed.

Why is GRPO + RLVR the recipe behind reasoning models?
?
Pre-RLVR alignment was bottlenecked on expensive human preference annotation. RLVR provides **infinite, free, automatic reward signal** for verifiable domains (math, code), letting RL training scale to millions of problems.

# Chatbots and Recommender Systems — Study Hub

## Exam-day refs

- [[exam-blueprint]] — what's tested, where
- [[exam-style-guide]] — MCQ patterns, distractor types, time budget

## Study artifacts

- [[clusters/README|Topic clusters →]] — atoms, cheat sheets per topic
- [[flashcards/README|Flashcards by lecture →]] — Spaced Repetition decks

# Chatbots and Recommender Systems — Exam Blueprint

**Format:** MCQ (per [[../../30-Sources/Chatbots/_course|course manifest]]).
**Coverage:** Lectures 02-16 (Miguel Fierro).

> **No mock exam released for this course.** This blueprint estimates topic coverage based on lecture weight (slide count, depth, recency). Recalibrate when a real practice exam exists.

## Topic-coverage estimate

Weighting heuristic = (slide count) × (recency / centrality bias). Higher rank = more likely to be tested heavily.

| Rank | Topic cluster | Lectures | Slides | Why high |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Modern LLMs & advanced techniques** (MoE, KV cache, RoPE, LoRA, Quantization, RLHF/DPO/GRPO) | 16 | 17 | Densest deck, most current, most likely the prof's "what's hot now" focus |
| 2 | **NLP & Transformer architecture** (attention, multi-head, positional encoding, residual, LayerNorm, BERT, GPT) | 13, 14 | 20 + 23 | Foundation — everything later builds on these |
| 3 | **Decision trees, RF, GBDT** (Gini, entropy, leaf-wise, exact vs histogram, LightGBM, XGBoost) | 08 | 17 | Self-contained, lots of testable detail |
| 4 | **Reco data + metrics** (terminology, splits, RMSE / NDCG / MAP, CTR / LTV, A/B test, architectures) | 02, 04 | 18 + 14 | Definition-rich; foundational vocabulary |
| 5 | **Matrix factorization & SAR** (latent factors, SGD vs ALS, similarity metrics, FM/FFM) | 06, 07 | 17 + 5 | Equation-heavy; classic exam material |
| 6 | **Neural reco** (NCF, Wide & Deep, BPR loss) | 09, 10a, 10b | 5 + 5 + 6 | Paradigm-defining — likely featured |
| 7 | **GNNs for reco** (NGCF, LightGCN, knowledge graphs) | 12 | 17 | Recent and dense; pairwise loss connection |
| 8 | **Sequential/session-based** (GRU4Rec, Caser, dilated CNN, SASRec) | 11 | 7 | Smaller deck but distinct paradigm |
| 9 | **Evolution of state + chatbot history** (FSMs, ELIZA, Siri, ChatGPT timeline, OpenAI APIs) | 05, 15 | 5 + 19 | Heavy on memorisation of dates/names |
| 10 | **Collaborative vs Content-based** | 03 | 5 | Foundational concept; small deck |

## Likely question formats

Without a mock, I infer from the slide content:

- **Definition recall** — "What is X?" / "Define Y." Heavy in foundational lectures (02-04, 13).
- **Algorithm comparison** — "Which is faster: Gini or Entropy?" / "Difference between MQA and GQA?" Common in lectures 06-08, 16.
- **Equation identification** — "Which formula expresses BPR?" / "What does LightGCN simplify in NGCF?" Common in 06, 09, 12.
- **Use-case matching** — "Which architecture for cold-start?" / "Which loss for implicit feedback?" Common in 02-04, 09-12.
- **Historical sequence** — "When was BERT released?" / "What did GPT-2 demonstrate?" Common in 14-15.

## High-leverage atoms

These atomic facts are likely to appear repeatedly across MCQs. **Memorise these word-for-word.**

| Fact | From |
|---|---|
| User, item, interaction = 3 reco components | L02 |
| Cold start fix = content-based filtering | L03 |
| Implicit feedback's negative-sampling problem | L02, L09 |
| RMSE penalises outliers more than MAE | L04 |
| NDCG accounts for **rank position** of hits | L04 |
| LTV ≥ 3× CAC = healthy benchmark | L04 |
| Software 1.0 (FSM) → Software 2.0 (Transformer) shift | L05 |
| RAG = Ingestion → Retrieval → Augmentation → Generation | L05 |
| SAR scoring: $\sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$ | L06 |
| MF: $\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$; SVD adds biases $\mu + b_u + b_i$ | L06 |
| ALS = alternate, each subproblem convex with closed form | L06 |
| FM = MF generalised; pairwise interactions $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle x_i x_j$, computed in $O(nk)$ | L07 |
| Gini = $1 - \sum p_k^2$; Entropy = $-\sum p_k \log p_k$ | L08 |
| LightGBM = leaf-wise growth; XGBoost = level-wise default | L08 |
| BPR loss: $\sum \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ | L09 |
| NCF = MLP over concatenated user+item embeddings | L10a |
| Wide&Deep = wide (memorise crosses) + deep (generalise via embeddings), **jointly trained** | L10b |
| GRU4Rec uses session-parallel mini-batches; hidden state resets per session | L11 |
| LightGCN = NGCF minus W matrices and ReLU; outperforms NGCF | L12 |
| Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** → needs positional encoding | L13 |
| Scaled dot-product attention: $\text{softmax}(QK^T / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ | L13 |
| BERT mask 15%: 80%/10%/10% (`[MASK]` / random / unchanged) | L14 |
| BERT = encoder-only (MLM); GPT = decoder-only (next-token) | L14 |
| ChatGPT = GPT-3.5 + RLHF; 2 months to 100M MAU | L14b, L15 |
| RLHF = mode seeking (loses diversity); base = distribution matching | L14b |
| MoE: many params, only fraction active (DeepSeek V3: 671B/37B) | L16 |
| KV cache turns O(n²) into O(n) per token | L16 |
| RoPE rotates Q, K → dot product depends on relative distance | L16 |
| Flash Attention = tile in SRAM, never materialise n×n in HBM | L16 |
| LoRA: $\Delta W = AB$ low-rank; merges back, zero extra latency | L16 |
| GRPO + RLVR = recipe for reasoning models (no critic, no RM) | L16 |

## Bang-for-buck ranking

### Priority 1 — modern LLM material (Lecture 16)

Most lectures are paragraph-density definitions; deck 16 is **acronym-density** (MoE, KV, RoPE, MQA, GQA, MLA, ZeRO, BF16, FP8, LoRA, QLoRA, INT4, PPO, DPO, GRPO, RLVR) — and current, so almost certainly tested. Drill the table in the Cluster 01 cheat sheet.

### Priority 2 — Transformer mechanics (Lectures 13, 14)

The exam will almost certainly include some "what does softmax do in attention?" / "BERT pretraining objective?" / "BERT vs GPT?" recall. These are foundational and easily testable.

### Priority 3 — Tree ensembles (Lecture 08)

Self-contained, lots of distinguishing details (Gini vs entropy, level vs leaf, exact vs histogram, LightGBM vs XGBoost). Easy to write 4-5 MCQs from this deck.

### Priority 4 — Reco metrics & data (Lectures 02, 04)

Definition recall heavy. NDCG, RMSE, MAE, MAP, CTR, LTV, cold start, splits — pure vocabulary. Easy points if memorised.

### Priority 5 — Reco algorithms (Lectures 06-12)

MF, ALS, BPR, NCF, Wide&Deep, GRU4Rec, LightGCN — likely 1-2 questions each, so don't over-invest in any single one. Master the **comparisons** (BPR vs pointwise, NGCF vs LightGCN, MQA vs GQA vs MLA) — those are the easiest distractor traps.

### Priority 6 — Chatbot history (Lecture 15)

Date-ordering questions are easy if memorised, painful otherwise. Memorise the timeline.

## Concept gaps in 20-Concepts/

After ingestion, **all major Chatbots concepts are stocked**. CV-overlap concepts (transformer-encoder, multi-head-attention, positional-encoding, transfer-learning, zero-shot-learning, few-shot-learning) were extended with a `Chatbots` source rather than duplicated.

## Next steps

1. Take [[practice-mocks/mock-1-questions|Mock 1]] under exam conditions.
2. Mark against [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key|the answer key]].
3. Re-rank priorities based on **your** weak spots, not this average estimate.

# Chatbots — Exam Style Guide (MCQ)

A study aid for the **MCQ-only** exam format declared in [[../../30-Sources/Chatbots/_course|the course manifest]]. **No mock exam released yet** — this guide is grounded in the lecture wording from Miguel Fierro's slides, not in real graded papers. Recalibrate when a real practice exam exists.

## What we know

- **Format:** MCQ.
- Other parameters (count, weighting, time, allowed materials, penalty for wrong) — **not specified** in current manifest. Confirm with the prof or update the manifest when known.

## How to study for an MCQ-only exam

The big difference vs an exam with open-ended questions: **rote recall and distinguishing similar concepts wins**. There is no partial credit; you either pick the right option or you don't.

| Tactic | Why |
|---|---|
| **Drill atomic facts** with flashcards | One MCQ = one fact |
| **Memorise comparisons** (Gini vs Entropy, MQA vs GQA, BERT vs GPT) | The juiciest distractors are "swap the two terms" |
| **Memorise verbatim slide phrasings** | Distractors paraphrase; correct answers often reuse exact lecture wording |
| **Skip nothing** — broad coverage > deep dives on small topics | MCQ exams sample widely |

## Prof's vocabulary (use the exact terms in your head)

Reuse these terms when reading questions; recognising the exact phrasing helps you spot the correct answer.

| Term | Lecture |
|---|---|
| user / item / interaction | L02 |
| explicit / implicit feedback | L02 |
| cold start (cold user / cold item) | L02 |
| stratified split / chronological split | L02 |
| collaborative vs content-based filtering | L03 |
| memorisation vs generalisation | L10b |
| RMSE / MAE / NDCG / MAP / Precision@K / Recall@K | L04 |
| novelty / diversity / serendipity / coverage | L04 |
| CTR / conversion / AOV / MAU / LTV | L04 |
| LTV ≥ 3 × CAC | L04 |
| affinity matrix / co-occurrence / similarity matrix | L06 |
| count / lift / Jaccard / mutual information / cosine | L06 |
| latent factors / SGD vs ALS | L06 |
| factorization machines / field-aware FM | L07 |
| Gini index / entropy | L08 |
| bagging / boosting / level-wise / leaf-wise | L08 |
| pointwise vs pairwise loss | L09 |
| Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) | L09 |
| GMF / MLP / NCF | L10a |
| wide and deep / jointly trained | L10b |
| sequential vs session-based | L11 |
| GRU4Rec / Caser / SASRec | L11 |
| message passing / aggregate / update | L12 |
| NGCF / LightGCN | L12 |
| permutation-equivariant | L13 |
| query / key / value | L13 |
| scaled dot-product attention | L13 |
| multi-head attention | L13 |
| positional encoding | L13 |
| residual connection / LayerNorm | L13 |
| tokenization (BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece) | L13 |
| autoencoder / autoregressive / sequence-to-sequence | L13 |
| transfer learning / pretraining / finetuning | L14 |
| masked language model / next-token prediction | L14 |
| BERT / RoBERTa / DeBERTa | L14 |
| GPT 1-5 / generations | L14 |
| RLHF / SFT / reward model / PPO | L14, L14b |
| distribution matching vs mode seeking | L14b |
| LLM memory: buffer / summary / entity / KG / vector | L14b |
| RAG / chunking / embedding / vector store | L05 |
| function calling / agent loop / ReAct | L05, L15 |
| Mixture of Experts (MoE) | L16 |
| KV cache / paged attention | L16 |
| RoPE | L16 |
| Flash Attention / sparse attention | L16 |
| MQA / GQA / MLA | L16 |
| ZeRO-1 / ZeRO-2 / ZeRO-3 | L16 |
| BF16 / FP16 / FP8 / mixed precision | L16 |
| LoRA / QLoRA | L16 |
| INT8 / INT4 / quantization / PTQ / QAT | L16 |
| speculative decoding / draft model | L16 |
| DPO / GRPO / RLVR | L16 |

## Likely distractor patterns (inferred)

Based on lecture content, expect these MCQ trap families:

### (a) Swap the two terms in a comparison

| Question topic | Likely trap |
|---|---|
| RMSE vs MAE | Swap which is more sensitive to outliers (RMSE is) |
| Gini vs Entropy | Swap which is faster (Gini is) |
| Level-wise vs Leaf-wise | Swap LightGBM (leaf-wise) and XGBoost (level-wise default) |
| BERT vs GPT | Swap encoder/decoder, MLM/next-token, bidirectional/left-to-right |
| MQA vs GQA vs MLA | Swap who shares K/V (MQA = all, GQA = groups, MLA = compressed) |
| ZeRO-1/2/3 | Swap which stage adds gradients vs weights |
| PPO vs DPO vs GRPO | Swap which removes which model |

### (b) Same fact, opposite direction

| Right answer | Trap |
|---|---|
| Stratified split avoids cold-start in eval | "Stratified split causes cold-start" |
| BPR optimises ordering, not absolute values | "BPR is pointwise classification" |
| LightGCN has fewer parameters than NGCF | "LightGCN has more parameters" |
| RoPE encodes relative position | "RoPE encodes absolute position only" |
| RLHF loses diversity | "RLHF preserves diversity" |

### (c) Plausible-but-incomplete

A real property of the concept that isn't *the defining* property.

| Concept | Right answer | Plausible distractor |
|---|---|---|
| LightGBM | Leaf-wise growth | "Uses bagging" (true of RF, not LGBM) |
| GBDT | Sequential additive trees | "Trains in parallel" (true of RF, not GBDT) |
| Wide & Deep | Jointly trained | "Stacked ensemble" |
| BERT | Masked LM + bidirectional | "Trained left-to-right like GPT" |
| MoE | Only fraction active per token | "All experts active for every token" |

### (d) Computational / numerical

- Cache complexity: KV cache turns O(n²) into **O(n) per token**.
- ZeRO-3 → no GPU holds the full model.
- LoRA params: **0.1-1% of total**.
- INT4 → **4× memory reduction vs FP16**.
- Flash Attention → 2-4× speedup, identical output.
- Speculative decoding → 2-3× speedup, identical output.

## Time budget tactics (MCQ)

Without a known total count, plan generally:

- **First pass (60% of time):** answer everything you know quickly. Skip and mark anything that needs thought.
- **Second pass (30%):** return to marked questions and reason through.
- **Final pass (10%):** never leave blank if there's no penalty (confirm in exam instructions). Educated guess > zero.

## Quick-decision heuristic for MCQ

When two options seem equally correct under time pressure:

- The **more technically specific** answer is usually right.
- The answer using the **exact lecture term** beats the paraphrase.
- **Avoid superlatives** ("always", "completely", "perfectly") — those almost always appear in distractors.

## Hard rules

- Stay grounded — vague hand-waves fine, fabricated specifics aren't.
- If a question seems ambiguous, pick the most technically correct interpretation (not the most charitable one).
- Trust the exact lecture phrasing.

## Recalibrate when

- A real mock exam is released → rewrite this guide grounded in the prof's actual distractor design.
- Exam format details (count, weighting, duration) are confirmed → update the manifest and time-budget section.

## Cross-references

- [[exam-blueprint]] — topic coverage estimate
- [[clusters/README|Topic clusters]] — atoms + cheat sheets per topic
- [[flashcards/README|Flashcards]] — drill the recall layer
