| **Skip nothing** — broad coverage > deep dives on small topics | MCQ exams sample widely |

## Prof's vocabulary (use the exact terms in your head)

Reuse these terms when reading questions; recognising the exact phrasing helps you spot the correct answer.

| Term | Lecture |
|---|---|
| user / item / interaction | L02 |
| explicit / implicit feedback | L02 |
| cold start (cold user / cold item) | L02 |
| stratified split / chronological split | L02 |
| collaborative vs content-based filtering | L03 |
| memorisation vs generalisation | L10b |
| RMSE / MAE / NDCG / MAP / Precision@K / Recall@K | L04 |
| novelty / diversity / serendipity / coverage | L04 |
| CTR / conversion / AOV / MAU / LTV | L04 |
| LTV ≥ 3 × CAC | L04 |
| affinity matrix / co-occurrence / similarity matrix | L06 |
| count / lift / Jaccard / mutual information / cosine | L06 |
| latent factors / SGD vs ALS | L06 |
| factorization machines / field-aware FM | L07 |
| Gini index / entropy | L08 |
| bagging / boosting / level-wise / leaf-wise | L08 |
| pointwise vs pairwise loss | L09 |
| Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) | L09 |
| GMF / MLP / NCF | L10a |
| wide and deep / jointly trained | L10b |
| sequential vs session-based | L11 |
| GRU4Rec / Caser / SASRec | L11 |
| message passing / aggregate / update | L12 |
| NGCF / LightGCN | L12 |
| permutation-equivariant | L13 |
| query / key / value | L13 |
| scaled dot-product attention | L13 |
| multi-head attention | L13 |
| positional encoding | L13 |
| residual connection / LayerNorm | L13 |
| tokenization (BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece) | L13 |
| autoencoder / autoregressive / sequence-to-sequence | L13 |
| transfer learning / pretraining / finetuning | L14 |
| masked language model / next-token prediction | L14 |
| BERT / RoBERTa / DeBERTa | L14 |
| GPT 1-5 / generations | L14 |
| RLHF / SFT / reward model / PPO | L14, L14b |
| distribution matching vs mode seeking | L14b |
| LLM memory: buffer / summary / entity / KG / vector | L14b |
| RAG / chunking / embedding / vector store | L05 |
| function calling / agent loop / ReAct | L05, L15 |
| Mixture of Experts (MoE) | L16 |
| KV cache / paged attention | L16 |
| RoPE | L16 |
| Flash Attention / sparse attention | L16 |
| MQA / GQA / MLA | L16 |
| ZeRO-1 / ZeRO-2 / ZeRO-3 | L16 |
| BF16 / FP16 / FP8 / mixed precision | L16 |
| LoRA / QLoRA | L16 |
| INT8 / INT4 / quantization / PTQ / QAT | L16 |
| speculative decoding / draft model | L16 |
| DPO / GRPO / RLVR | L16 |

## Likely distractor patterns (inferred)

Based on lecture content, expect these MCQ trap families:

### (a) Swap the two terms in a comparison

| Question topic | Likely trap |
|---|---|
| RMSE vs MAE | Swap which is more sensitive to outliers (RMSE is) |
| Gini vs Entropy | Swap which is faster (Gini is) |
| Level-wise vs Leaf-wise | Swap LightGBM (leaf-wise) and XGBoost (level-wise default) |
| BERT vs GPT | Swap encoder/decoder, MLM/next-token, bidirectional/left-to-right |
| MQA vs GQA vs MLA | Swap who shares K/V (MQA = all, GQA = groups, MLA = compressed) |
| ZeRO-1/2/3 | Swap which stage adds gradients vs weights |
| PPO vs DPO vs GRPO | Swap which removes which model |

### (b) Same fact, opposite direction

| Right answer | Trap |
|---|---|
| Stratified split avoids cold-start in eval | "Stratified split causes cold-start" |
| BPR optimises ordering, not absolute values | "BPR is pointwise classification" |
| LightGCN has fewer parameters than NGCF | "LightGCN has more parameters" |
| RoPE encodes relative position | "RoPE encodes absolute position only" |
| RLHF loses diversity | "RLHF preserves diversity" |

### (c) Plausible-but-incomplete

A real property of the concept that isn't *the defining* property.

| Concept | Right answer | Plausible distractor |
|---|---|---|
| LightGBM | Leaf-wise growth | "Uses bagging" (true of RF, not LGBM) |
| GBDT | Sequential additive trees | "Trains in parallel" (true of RF, not GBDT) |
| Wide & Deep | Jointly trained | "Stacked ensemble" |
| BERT | Masked LM + bidirectional | "Trained left-to-right like GPT" |
| MoE | Only fraction active per token | "All experts active for every token" |

### (d) Computational / numerical

- Cache complexity: KV cache turns O(n²) into **O(n) per token**.
- ZeRO-3 → no GPU holds the full model.
- LoRA params: **0.1-1% of total**.
- INT4 → **4× memory reduction vs FP16**.
- Flash Attention → 2-4× speedup, identical output.
- Speculative decoding → 2-3× speedup, identical output.

## Time budget tactics (MCQ)

Without a known total count, plan generally:

- **First pass (60% of time):** answer everything you know quickly. Skip and mark anything that needs thought.
- **Second pass (30%):** return to marked questions and reason through.
- **Final pass (10%):** never leave blank if there's no penalty (confirm in exam instructions). Educated guess > zero.

## Quick-decision heuristic for MCQ

When two options seem equally correct under time pressure:

- The **more technically specific** answer is usually right.
- The answer using the **exact lecture term** beats the paraphrase.
- **Avoid superlatives** ("always", "completely", "perfectly") — those almost always appear in distractors.

## Hard rules

- Stay grounded — vague hand-waves fine, fabricated specifics aren't.
- If a question seems ambiguous, pick the most technically correct interpretation (not the most charitable one).
- Trust the exact lecture phrasing.

## Recalibrate when

- A real mock exam is released → rewrite this guide grounded in the prof's actual distractor design.
- Exam format details (count, weighting, duration) are confirmed → update the manifest and time-budget section.

## Cross-references

- [[exam-blueprint]] — topic coverage estimate
- [[clusters/README|Topic clusters]] — atoms + cheat sheets per topic
- [[flashcards/README|Flashcards]] — drill the recall layer

# Cluster 01 — Modern LLM techniques

**Highest priority for the exam.** Lecture 16 is the densest, most current deck (17 slides packed with acronyms) and almost certainly the prof's "what matters now" focus.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-16-advanced-llm-techniques]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/mixture-of-experts]] — MoE: many params, only a fraction active per token
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/kv-cache]] — KV cache + Paged Attention
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/rope]] — Rotary Position Embedding
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/flash-attention]] — tiled attention in SRAM
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/mqa-gqa-mla]] — KV-cache reduction families
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/zero-optimizer]] — distributed training partitioning
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/mixed-precision]] — BF16, FP8 training
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/lora]] — Low-Rank Adaptation
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/quantization]] — INT8/INT4 inference
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/speculative-decoding]] — draft + verify
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/dpo]] — Direct Preference Optimization
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/grpo-rlvr]] — GRPO + RLVR for reasoning models

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-16]]

## Source slides

[Slides ↗](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf)

## What this cluster is about

A modern frontier LLM is a stack of engineering tricks layered on top of the basic transformer. Lecture 16 organises them into seven families that map cleanly to the inference and training pipeline.

**Architecture: Mixture of Experts (MoE)** — instead of one dense FFN per layer, $E$ parallel "experts" with a gating network routing each token to a subset. DeepSeek V3 has 671B total params but only 37B active per token, getting the *capacity* of a 671B model at the *inference cost* of a 37B model. Used by every frontier model after 2025.

**Infrastructure: KV cache, Paged Attention, RoPE, kernel fusion.** KV caching is the universal optimisation that turns autoregressive generation from O(n²) to O(n) per token. Paged Attention manages the cache via OS-style virtual memory pages for 2-4× higher serving throughput. **RoPE** rotates Q and K vectors at every layer so attention depends on *relative* distance — the trick that lets LLaMA 3 extend from 8K to 128K context. **Kernel fusion** combines GPU operations into single kernels that stay in fast SRAM.

**Attention optimisation: Flash Attention + MQA/GQA/MLA + sparse attention.** Three orthogonal axes. Flash Attention changes *how* you compute (tile in SRAM, never materialise the n×n matrix) — 2-4× speedup with mathematically identical output. MQA/GQA/MLA shrink the per-token KV cache by sharing or compressing K/V across heads (32 KV heads → 1 in MQA; 8 in GQA; compressed latent in MLA). **Sparse attention** changes *what* you compute (sliding window, BigBird).

**Training: ZeRO + mixed precision.** ZeRO progressively partitions optimizer states, gradients, then weights across GPUs (stages 1, 2, 3). ZeRO-3 means no GPU holds the full model. Mixed precision uses BF16 for compute and FP32 for master state — BF16 wins over FP16 because it has FP32's range (no overflow). FP8 emerging on Hopper.

**Adaptation: LoRA + QLoRA.** Freeze the full weight matrix and add a learned low-rank update $\Delta W = AB$ with $r \ll d$ — only 0.1-1% of params trainable, merge back at inference for zero extra latency. QLoRA loads the base model in 4-bit (NF4) and trains LoRA in BF16 → fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU. Together they democratised LLM finetuning.

**Deployment: quantization + speculative decoding.** Quantization (INT8 / INT4) is what makes 70B models runnable on consumer hardware. Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to propose tokens and verifies them in parallel with the big target model — 2-3× speedup with mathematically identical output.

**Alignment: PPO → DPO → GRPO + RLVR.** The simplification arc. PPO needs 4 models (policy, reference, reward, value). DPO derives a closed-form policy update from preference pairs — no reward model, no RL. GRPO removes the value model by using group-mean as baseline. **RLVR** removes the reward model by replacing it with deterministic verifiers (compilers for code, calculators for math). GRPO + RLVR is the recipe behind DeepSeek R1 and the reasoning-model wave.

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-01]
cluster: 01
---

# Cluster 01 — Atoms (Modern LLM techniques)

## Mixture of Experts (MoE)

1. **Definition.** Many parameters total, only a fraction activated per token. A learned **gating network** routes each token to a subset of "experts" (specialised FFN blocks) [[mixture-of-experts]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=2).
2. **DeepSeek V3 numbers.** 671B total params, only **37B active per token**.
3. **Two expert types.** *Shared experts* handle common patterns; *routed experts* specialise.
4. **Slogan.** "Big brain at a small brain price during inference."
5. **Key challenges.** Load balancing across experts + expert parallelism in distributed training.
6. **Adoption.** Nearly every frontier model after 2025: DeepSeek V3/R1, LLaMA 4, Gemini, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral 3.

## KV cache + Paged Attention

7. **KV cache complexity.** Without caching: O(n²) per token. With caching: **O(n) per token** [[kv-cache]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=3).
8. **Memory cost.** For a 70B model with 128K context, KV cache alone consumes **tens of GB per sequence**.
9. **Paged Attention.** Borrows OS virtual-memory concepts: KV cache split into fixed-size pages, allocated on demand, **non-contiguous in GPU memory**. **2-4× higher serving throughput.**
10. **Adoption.** Universal in autoregressive LLMs. Paged Attention used by **vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang**.

## RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)

11. **The problem.** Original positional encodings (sinusoidal added at input) **can't generalise beyond training context length** [[rope]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=4).
12. **Mechanism.** Instead of adding position at input, **rotate Q and K vectors by angle proportional to position at every attention layer**. Dimensions paired, each rotated at a different frequency.
13. **Key property.** $Q \cdot K^T$ depends only on **relative distance** $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.
14. **Context extension.** Continuous rotations enable scaling — **how LLaMA 3 went from 8K → 128K**.
15. **Adoption.** LLaMA 3/4, DeepSeek V3/R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral, Gemma — virtually every modern LLM.

## Kernel fusion

16. **The problem.** Each GPU operation (matmul, softmax, add) is a separate kernel. Each reads/writes HBM. Chain many small ops = **more time on memory traffic than compute** — [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=5).
17. **The fix.** Combine multiple operations into a **single kernel** — intermediate results stay in fast on-chip SRAM, never round-trip to HBM.
18. **Examples.** RoPE fused into attention; **Flash Attention is fundamentally a fusion strategy**. DeepSeek V3's low training cost was partly aggressive kernel fusion.

## Attention optimisation overview

19. **The O(n²) bottleneck.** Standard attention computes a full $n \times n$ matrix. Doubling context **quadruples** cost — [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=6).
20. **Three orthogonal axes.** (a) Reduce KV cache size per token → MQA/GQA/MLA. (b) Make computation hardware-efficient → Flash Attention. (c) Reduce which tokens participate → sparse attention.

## MQA / GQA / MLA

21. **MQA** (Shazeer 2019). All query heads share **one** KV pair. **32× cache reduction**, slight quality loss. Used by PaLM, Falcon, Gemini [[mqa-gqa-mla]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=7).
22. **GQA** (Ainslie 2023). Query heads grouped, each group shares one KV. E.g. 32 Q / 8 KV groups = 4 queries per KV → **4× cache reduction**, near-MHA quality. **Modern default** — used by LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral.
23. **MLA** (DeepSeek V2, 2024). Compresses K/V into a **latent vector** before caching, decompresses at attention time. **57× KV cache reduction vs MHA**, claims to outperform.
24. **Progression.** MHA → MQA (minimal but lossy) → GQA (balanced) → MLA (compressed, best quality).

## Flash Attention

25. **Idea.** Tile Q, K, V into blocks; compute attention entirely in **fast SRAM**, **never materialise the full n×n matrix in HBM** [[flash-attention]] — [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=8).
26. **Result.** **2-4× speedup** + significant memory reduction. **Mathematically identical output** to vanilla attention.
27. **Status.** Default backend for all transformer training and inference.
28. **Versus sparse attention.** Flash Attention changes **HOW** you compute. Sparse changes **WHAT** you compute. Use both.

## Sparse Attention

29. **Sliding Window** (Mistral). Each token attends to a **local window** (128 or 4096 tokens). Reduces $O(n^2) \to O(n)$.
30. **GPT-OSS pattern.** Alternates full-GQA layers with 128-token sliding window layers.

## ZeRO Optimizer

31. **The problem.** Training stores 3 things per param (weights, gradients, optimizer states). Standard data parallelism stores all on every GPU [[zero-optimizer]] — [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=9).
32. **ZeRO-1.** Partition **optimizer states** across GPUs. ~4× memory reduction, minimal communication.
33. **ZeRO-2.** Partition optimizer states **+ gradients**.
34. **ZeRO-3.** Partition optimizer states + gradients **+ model weights**. **No GPU holds the full model** — weights gathered on demand. Maximum savings, more communication.
35. **Implementations.** **DeepSpeed** (Microsoft, original) and **FSDP** (PyTorch native).

## Mixed precision

36. **FP32.** 32-bit, range $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$. Master weights, gradient accumulation, optimizer states [[mixed-precision]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=10).
37. **FP16.** 16-bit, narrow range ($10^{-4}$ to $65k$). 2× faster but **overflow-prone**.
38. **BF16.** 16-bit with **FP32 range** (more bits to exponent, fewer to fraction). **Modern universal default** for compute.
39. **FP8.** 8-bit, **4× memory reduction vs FP32**. Pioneered by **DeepSeek V3** for training.
40. **Usage.** FP32 for what persists; BF16 for forward matmul, activations, gradient computation, embeddings.

## LoRA / QLoRA

41. **LoRA mechanics.** Freeze full $W$; add $\Delta W = AB$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}, r \ll d$ (typically $r = 8\text{-}64$) [[lora]] — [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=11).
42. **Trainable params.** Only $A$ and $B$ — typically **0.1-1% of total params**.
43. **Why it works.** Fine-tuning updates have **low intrinsic rank**.
44. **Inference cost.** Merge $A B$ back into $W$ → **zero extra latency**.
45. **QLoRA** (Dettmers 2023). Base model in **4-bit (NF4)**, LoRA adapters in BF16. **Fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU.**
46. **Impact.** Democratised LLM finetuning — any engineer with one GPU.

## Quantization

47. **Mixed precision = training; quantization = inference** [[quantization]] — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=12).
48. **PTQ** (Post-Training). Quantize after training — fast, no retraining.
49. **QAT** (Quantization-Aware Training). Simulate quantization during training so model learns to be robust — higher quality, requires retraining.
50. **INT8.** ~2× memory reduction vs FP16, minimal quality loss, widely supported.
51. **INT4.** ~4× reduction, noticeable but acceptable quality loss. Used by QLoRA and local deployment.
52. **Headline.** Quantization is what makes it possible to run a 70B model on **consumer hardware**. LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.

## Speculative decoding

53. **The problem.** Autoregressive generation is sequential — one token at a time. **GPU underutilised** because each forward pass doesn't saturate the hardware [[speculative-decoding]] — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=13).
54. **Idea.** Use a small fast **draft model** to propose 4-8 tokens. Then run the **target model** on all proposed tokens **in parallel in a single forward pass**.
55. **Result.** If draft matches target 70-90% of the time → **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical outputs**.
56. **Variants.** Separate draft model (1B for 70B); self-speculative; **Multi-Token Prediction heads** (DeepSeek V3: 85-90% acceptance rate).

## Alignment evolution

57. **PPO + RLHF.** SFT → reward model on preferences → PPO. Requires **4 models** simultaneously (policy, reference, reward, value/critic). Sensitive, prone to reward hacking [[rlhf]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=14).
58. **DPO.** **Closed-form policy update** from preference pairs. **No reward model, no RL loop.** Just policy + frozen reference + preference pairs. Essentially supervised on preferences. Used by LLaMA 2/3 [[dpo]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=14).
59. **GRPO** (DeepSeek 2024). Eliminates the **critic** by using group-mean as baseline. ~33% memory reduction. DeepSeek R1 trained with GRPO [[grpo-rlvr]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=15).
60. **RLVR.** RL with **Verifiable Rewards** — replace human reward model with deterministic verifiers (compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math, calculators). No reward model needed.
61. **Evolution.** PPO (4 models) → DPO (2 models, offline) → GRPO (no critic, on-policy) → **GRPO + RLVR (no RM, recipe behind reasoning models)**.

## How it all fits

62. **The stack** ([slide 16](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=16)):
    - **Architecture:** MoE
    - **Infrastructure:** KV Caching, Paged Attention, RoPE, Kernel Fusion
    - **Attention:** Flash Attention, MQA/GQA/MLA, Sparse / Sliding Window
    - **Training:** ZeRO, Mixed Precision (BF16/FP8)
    - **Adaptation:** LoRA, QLoRA
    - **Deployment:** Quantization (INT8/INT4), Speculative Decoding
    - **Alignment:** PPO → DPO → GRPO/RLVR
63. **A modern frontier LLM uses nearly all of these simultaneously.**

# Cluster 01 — Atoms (Modern LLM techniques)

## Mixture of Experts (MoE)

1. **Definition.** Many parameters total, only a fraction activated per token. A learned **gating network** routes each token to a subset of "experts" (specialised FFN blocks) [[mixture-of-experts]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=2).
2. **DeepSeek V3 numbers.** 671B total params, only **37B active per token**.
3. **Two expert types.** *Shared experts* handle common patterns; *routed experts* specialise.
4. **Slogan.** "Big brain at a small brain price during inference."
5. **Key challenges.** Load balancing across experts + expert parallelism in distributed training.
6. **Adoption.** Nearly every frontier model after 2025: DeepSeek V3/R1, LLaMA 4, Gemini, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral 3.

## KV cache + Paged Attention

7. **KV cache complexity.** Without caching: O(n²) per token. With caching: **O(n) per token** [[kv-cache]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=3).
8. **Memory cost.** For a 70B model with 128K context, KV cache alone consumes **tens of GB per sequence**.
9. **Paged Attention.** Borrows OS virtual-memory concepts: KV cache split into fixed-size pages, allocated on demand, **non-contiguous in GPU memory**. **2-4× higher serving throughput.**
10. **Adoption.** Universal in autoregressive LLMs. Paged Attention used by **vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang**.

## RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)

11. **The problem.** Original positional encodings (sinusoidal added at input) **can't generalise beyond training context length** [[rope]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=4).
12. **Mechanism.** Instead of adding position at input, **rotate Q and K vectors by angle proportional to position at every attention layer**. Dimensions paired, each rotated at a different frequency.
13. **Key property.** $Q \cdot K^T$ depends only on **relative distance** $(m - n)$, not absolute positions.
14. **Context extension.** Continuous rotations enable scaling — **how LLaMA 3 went from 8K → 128K**.
15. **Adoption.** LLaMA 3/4, DeepSeek V3/R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral, Gemma — virtually every modern LLM.

## Kernel fusion

16. **The problem.** Each GPU operation (matmul, softmax, add) is a separate kernel. Each reads/writes HBM. Chain many small ops = **more time on memory traffic than compute** — [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=5).
17. **The fix.** Combine multiple operations into a **single kernel** — intermediate results stay in fast on-chip SRAM, never round-trip to HBM.
18. **Examples.** RoPE fused into attention; **Flash Attention is fundamentally a fusion strategy**. DeepSeek V3's low training cost was partly aggressive kernel fusion.

## Attention optimisation overview

19. **The O(n²) bottleneck.** Standard attention computes a full $n \times n$ matrix. Doubling context **quadruples** cost — [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=6).
20. **Three orthogonal axes.** (a) Reduce KV cache size per token → MQA/GQA/MLA. (b) Make computation hardware-efficient → Flash Attention. (c) Reduce which tokens participate → sparse attention.

## MQA / GQA / MLA

21. **MQA** (Shazeer 2019). All query heads share **one** KV pair. **32× cache reduction**, slight quality loss. Used by PaLM, Falcon, Gemini [[mqa-gqa-mla]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=7).
22. **GQA** (Ainslie 2023). Query heads grouped, each group shares one KV. E.g. 32 Q / 8 KV groups = 4 queries per KV → **4× cache reduction**, near-MHA quality. **Modern default** — used by LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral.
23. **MLA** (DeepSeek V2, 2024). Compresses K/V into a **latent vector** before caching, decompresses at attention time. **57× KV cache reduction vs MHA**, claims to outperform.
24. **Progression.** MHA → MQA (minimal but lossy) → GQA (balanced) → MLA (compressed, best quality).

## Flash Attention

25. **Idea.** Tile Q, K, V into blocks; compute attention entirely in **fast SRAM**, **never materialise the full n×n matrix in HBM** [[flash-attention]] — [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=8).
26. **Result.** **2-4× speedup** + significant memory reduction. **Mathematically identical output** to vanilla attention.
27. **Status.** Default backend for all transformer training and inference.
28. **Versus sparse attention.** Flash Attention changes **HOW** you compute. Sparse changes **WHAT** you compute. Use both.

## Sparse Attention

29. **Sliding Window** (Mistral). Each token attends to a **local window** (128 or 4096 tokens). Reduces $O(n^2) \to O(n)$.
30. **GPT-OSS pattern.** Alternates full-GQA layers with 128-token sliding window layers.

## ZeRO Optimizer

31. **The problem.** Training stores 3 things per param (weights, gradients, optimizer states). Standard data parallelism stores all on every GPU [[zero-optimizer]] — [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=9).
32. **ZeRO-1.** Partition **optimizer states** across GPUs. ~4× memory reduction, minimal communication.
33. **ZeRO-2.** Partition optimizer states **+ gradients**.
34. **ZeRO-3.** Partition optimizer states + gradients **+ model weights**. **No GPU holds the full model** — weights gathered on demand. Maximum savings, more communication.
35. **Implementations.** **DeepSpeed** (Microsoft, original) and **FSDP** (PyTorch native).

## Mixed precision

36. **FP32.** 32-bit, range $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$. Master weights, gradient accumulation, optimizer states [[mixed-precision]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=10).
37. **FP16.** 16-bit, narrow range ($10^{-4}$ to $65k$). 2× faster but **overflow-prone**.
38. **BF16.** 16-bit with **FP32 range** (more bits to exponent, fewer to fraction). **Modern universal default** for compute.
39. **FP8.** 8-bit, **4× memory reduction vs FP32**. Pioneered by **DeepSeek V3** for training.
40. **Usage.** FP32 for what persists; BF16 for forward matmul, activations, gradient computation, embeddings.

## LoRA / QLoRA

41. **LoRA mechanics.** Freeze full $W$; add $\Delta W = AB$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}, r \ll d$ (typically $r = 8\text{-}64$) [[lora]] — [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=11).
42. **Trainable params.** Only $A$ and $B$ — typically **0.1-1% of total params**.
43. **Why it works.** Fine-tuning updates have **low intrinsic rank**.
44. **Inference cost.** Merge $A B$ back into $W$ → **zero extra latency**.
45. **QLoRA** (Dettmers 2023). Base model in **4-bit (NF4)**, LoRA adapters in BF16. **Fine-tune a 70B model on a single 48GB GPU.**
46. **Impact.** Democratised LLM finetuning — any engineer with one GPU.

## Quantization

47. **Mixed precision = training; quantization = inference** [[quantization]] — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=12).
48. **PTQ** (Post-Training). Quantize after training — fast, no retraining.
49. **QAT** (Quantization-Aware Training). Simulate quantization during training so model learns to be robust — higher quality, requires retraining.
50. **INT8.** ~2× memory reduction vs FP16, minimal quality loss, widely supported.
51. **INT4.** ~4× reduction, noticeable but acceptable quality loss. Used by QLoRA and local deployment.
52. **Headline.** Quantization is what makes it possible to run a 70B model on **consumer hardware**. LLaMA 4 Scout was designed for single-server deployment via 4-bit quantization.

## Speculative decoding

53. **The problem.** Autoregressive generation is sequential — one token at a time. **GPU underutilised** because each forward pass doesn't saturate the hardware [[speculative-decoding]] — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=13).
54. **Idea.** Use a small fast **draft model** to propose 4-8 tokens. Then run the **target model** on all proposed tokens **in parallel in a single forward pass**.
55. **Result.** If draft matches target 70-90% of the time → **2-3× speedup with mathematically identical outputs**.
56. **Variants.** Separate draft model (1B for 70B); self-speculative; **Multi-Token Prediction heads** (DeepSeek V3: 85-90% acceptance rate).

## Alignment evolution

57. **PPO + RLHF.** SFT → reward model on preferences → PPO. Requires **4 models** simultaneously (policy, reference, reward, value/critic). Sensitive, prone to reward hacking [[rlhf]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=14).
58. **DPO.** **Closed-form policy update** from preference pairs. **No reward model, no RL loop.** Just policy + frozen reference + preference pairs. Essentially supervised on preferences. Used by LLaMA 2/3 [[dpo]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=14).
59. **GRPO** (DeepSeek 2024). Eliminates the **critic** by using group-mean as baseline. ~33% memory reduction. DeepSeek R1 trained with GRPO [[grpo-rlvr]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=15).
60. **RLVR.** RL with **Verifiable Rewards** — replace human reward model with deterministic verifiers (compilers for code, symbolic checkers for math, calculators). No reward model needed.
61. **Evolution.** PPO (4 models) → DPO (2 models, offline) → GRPO (no critic, on-policy) → **GRPO + RLVR (no RM, recipe behind reasoning models)**.

## How it all fits

62. **The stack** ([slide 16](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 16.pdf#page=16)):
    - **Architecture:** MoE
    - **Infrastructure:** KV Caching, Paged Attention, RoPE, Kernel Fusion
    - **Attention:** Flash Attention, MQA/GQA/MLA, Sparse / Sliding Window
    - **Training:** ZeRO, Mixed Precision (BF16/FP8)
    - **Adaptation:** LoRA, QLoRA
    - **Deployment:** Quantization (INT8/INT4), Speculative Decoding
    - **Alignment:** PPO → DPO → GRPO/RLVR
63. **A modern frontier LLM uses nearly all of these simultaneously.**

---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-01]
cluster: 01

# Cluster 01 — Cheat sheet (Modern LLM techniques)

## The seven layers

| Layer | Techniques |
|---|---|
| **Architecture** | MoE |
| **Infrastructure** | KV Cache • Paged Attention • RoPE • Kernel Fusion |
| **Attention** | Flash Attention • MQA / GQA / MLA • Sparse / Sliding Window |
| **Training** | ZeRO (1/2/3) • Mixed Precision (BF16/FP8) |
| **Adaptation** | LoRA • QLoRA |
| **Deployment** | Quantization (INT8/INT4) • Speculative Decoding |
| **Alignment** | PPO → DPO → GRPO + RLVR |

## Headline numbers

| Number | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **671B / 37B** | DeepSeek V3 total params / active per token (MoE) |
| **57×** | KV cache reduction for MLA vs MHA |
| **32×** | KV cache reduction for MQA vs MHA |
| **4×** | KV cache reduction for GQA vs MHA (typical 32 Q / 8 KV) |
| **2-4×** | Paged Attention serving throughput improvement |
| **2-4×** | Flash Attention speedup |
| **2-3×** | Speculative decoding speedup |
| **0.1-1%** | LoRA trainable params as % of total |
| **70B on 48GB** | QLoRA finetuning |
| **2× / 4×** | INT8 / INT4 memory reduction vs FP16 |
| **8K → 128K** | LLaMA 3 context extension via RoPE frequency scaling |
| **85-90%** | Multi-Token Prediction acceptance rate (DeepSeek V3) |

## MoE — Mixture of Experts

| | |
|---|---|
| Idea | Many params, fraction activated per token |
| Routing | Gating network selects top-k experts (often k=2) |
| Two types | Shared (always on) + routed (specialised) |
| Challenge | Load balancing + expert parallelism |
| Used by | DeepSeek V3/R1, LLaMA 4, Gemini, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Mistral 3 |

## KV cache + Paged Attention

| | |
|---|---|
| KV cache complexity | O(n²) → **O(n) per token** |
| Cache size | $n \times \text{heads} \times d_k \times 2$ |
| Paged Attention | OS-style virtual memory pages, allocated on demand |
| Used by | All autoregressive LLMs (KV); vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang (Paged) |

## RoPE

| | |
|---|---|
| What it does | Rotates Q, K by angle ∝ position at every attention layer |
| Key property | Dot product depends on **relative distance** $(m-n)$ |
| Context extension | Frequency scaling — LLaMA 3 went from 8K to 128K |
| Used by | Virtually every modern LLM (LLaMA, DeepSeek, Qwen, Mistral, Gemma) |

## MQA / GQA / MLA — KV-cache reduction

| Method | KV per query | Quality | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MHA** (baseline) | 1 KV per head | Best | Older models |
| **MQA** | 1 KV total | Slight loss | PaLM, Falcon, Gemini |
| **GQA** | 1 KV per group of heads | Near-MHA | **LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral** (default) |
| **MLA** | Compressed latent | Equal/better | DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3 |

## Flash Attention

| | |
|---|---|
| Trick | Tile Q/K/V into blocks, compute in SRAM |
| Memory | $O(n^2) \to O(n)$ |
| Speed | 2-4× |
| Output | **Mathematically identical** to vanilla |
| Status | Default backend everywhere |

## ZeRO

| Stage | Partitions | Memory | Comm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ZeRO-1** | Optimizer states | ~4× | Minimal |
| **ZeRO-2** | + Gradients | More | More |
| **ZeRO-3** | + Weights (no GPU has full model) | Maximum | Most |

Implementations: **DeepSpeed** (Microsoft, original), **FSDP** (PyTorch native).

## Mixed precision

| Format | Bits | Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 32 | $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ | Master state, optimizer |
| **FP16** | 16 | Narrow ($10^{-4}$ to 65k) | Overflow-prone, deprecated for training |
| **BF16** | 16 | **Same as FP32** | **Modern default for compute** |
| **FP8** | 8 | Smaller | Pioneered by DeepSeek V3 |

Master state always FP32; computations BF16 (or FP8).

## LoRA / QLoRA

| | LoRA | QLoRA |
|---|---|---|
| Base model | Frozen, full precision | Frozen, **4-bit (NF4)** |
| Adapters | $\Delta W = AB$, rank r=8-64 | Same, BF16 |
| Trainable | 0.1-1% of params | Same |
| Inference latency | Zero extra (merge AB back) | Same |
| Headline | Cheap finetuning | **70B on a single 48GB GPU** |

## Quantization

| Format | Memory | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **INT8** | 2× vs FP16 | Minimal loss |
| **INT4** | 4× vs FP16 | Acceptable |

PTQ (post-training, fast) vs QAT (during training, higher quality).

## Speculative decoding

| | |
|---|---|
| Idea | Small draft model proposes 4-8 tokens; big target verifies in parallel |
| Speedup | 2-3× if draft acceptance is 70-90% |
| Output | **Mathematically identical** |
| Variants | Separate draft / Self-speculative / Multi-Token Prediction (DeepSeek V3) |

## Alignment evolution

| Step | What it removes | Models in memory |
|---|---|---|
| **PPO** (RLHF) | — | 4 (policy, ref, reward, value) |
| **DPO** | RM + value | 2 (policy, ref) |
| **GRPO** | Value only | 3 |
| **GRPO + RLVR** | RM (replace with verifier) | 2 (policy, ref) |

DPO offline; GRPO on-policy. RLVR works for **verifiable** domains (code, math).

## Common traps

- **Mixed precision ≠ quantization.** Mixed precision = training; quantization = inference.
- **Flash Attention does NOT change the output.** Same math, faster compute. Don't confuse with sparse attention.
- **MoE total params ≠ active params.** "DeepSeek V3 has 671B params" is true but misleading — only 37B are active per token.
- **LoRA does NOT add inference latency.** $A B$ merges back into $W$.
- **DPO is offline.** Can't easily incorporate verifiable rewards or new model rollouts.
- **RLVR works only for verifiable domains.** Open-ended chat needs DPO or PPO.
- **ZeRO-3 ≠ tensor parallelism.** ZeRO partitions; TP shards individual operations. Often combined.
- **RoPE encodes RELATIVE, not absolute, position** even though applied as an absolute rotation.

## Mental model: the LLM stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Arch["Architecture: MoE"]
    Infra["Infra: KV Cache • Paged Attn • RoPE • Kernel Fusion"]
    Attn["Attention: Flash • MQA/GQA/MLA • Sparse"]
    Train["Training: ZeRO • Mixed Precision"]
    Adapt["Adaptation: LoRA / QLoRA"]
    Deploy["Deploy: Quantization • Speculative Decoding"]
    Align["Alignment: PPO → DPO → GRPO/RLVR"]

    Arch --> Infra --> Attn --> Train --> Adapt --> Deploy --> Align

    classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0
    class Arch,Infra,Attn,Train,Adapt,Deploy,Align chatbot
```

## MoE — Mixture of Experts

## KV cache + Paged Attention

| Cache size | $n \times \text{heads} \times d_k \times 2$ |
| Paged Attention | OS-style virtual memory pages, allocated on demand |
| Used by | All autoregressive LLMs (KV); vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang (Paged) |

## RoPE

| | |
|---|---|
| What it does | Rotates Q, K by angle ∝ position at every attention layer |
| Key property | Dot product depends on **relative distance** $(m-n)$ |
| Context extension | Frequency scaling — LLaMA 3 went from 8K to 128K |
| Used by | Virtually every modern LLM (LLaMA, DeepSeek, Qwen, Mistral, Gemma) |

## MQA / GQA / MLA — KV-cache reduction

| Method | KV per query | Quality | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MHA** (baseline) | 1 KV per head | Best | Older models |
| **MQA** | 1 KV total | Slight loss | PaLM, Falcon, Gemini |
| **GQA** | 1 KV per group of heads | Near-MHA | **LLaMA 3, GPT-OSS, Mistral** (default) |
| **MLA** | Compressed latent | Equal/better | DeepSeek V2/V3/R1, Kimi K2, Mistral 3 |

## Flash Attention

| | |
|---|---|
| Trick | Tile Q/K/V into blocks, compute in SRAM |
| Memory | $O(n^2) \to O(n)$ |
| Speed | 2-4× |
| Output | **Mathematically identical** to vanilla |
| Status | Default backend everywhere |

## ZeRO

| Stage | Partitions | Memory | Comm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ZeRO-1** | Optimizer states | ~4× | Minimal |
| **ZeRO-2** | + Gradients | More | More |
| **ZeRO-3** | + Weights (no GPU has full model) | Maximum | Most |

Implementations: **DeepSpeed** (Microsoft, original), **FSDP** (PyTorch native).

## Mixed precision

| Format | Bits | Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 32 | $10^{-38}$ to $10^{38}$ | Master state, optimizer |
| **FP16** | 16 | Narrow ($10^{-4}$ to 65k) | Overflow-prone, deprecated for training |
| **BF16** | 16 | **Same as FP32** | **Modern default for compute** |
| **FP8** | 8 | Smaller | Pioneered by DeepSeek V3 |

Master state always FP32; computations BF16 (or FP8).

## LoRA / QLoRA

| | LoRA | QLoRA |
|---|---|---|
| Base model | Frozen, full precision | Frozen, **4-bit (NF4)** |
| Adapters | $\Delta W = AB$, rank r=8-64 | Same, BF16 |
| Trainable | 0.1-1% of params | Same |
| Inference latency | Zero extra (merge AB back) | Same |
| Headline | Cheap finetuning | **70B on a single 48GB GPU** |

## Quantization

| Format | Memory | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **INT8** | 2× vs FP16 | Minimal loss |
| **INT4** | 4× vs FP16 | Acceptable |

PTQ (post-training, fast) vs QAT (during training, higher quality).

## Speculative decoding

| | |
|---|---|
| Idea | Small draft model proposes 4-8 tokens; big target verifies in parallel |
| Speedup | 2-3× if draft acceptance is 70-90% |
| Output | **Mathematically identical** |
| Variants | Separate draft / Self-speculative / Multi-Token Prediction (DeepSeek V3) |

## Alignment evolution

| Step | What it removes | Models in memory |
|---|---|---|
| **PPO** (RLHF) | — | 4 (policy, ref, reward, value) |
| **DPO** | RM + value | 2 (policy, ref) |
| **GRPO** | Value only | 3 |
| **GRPO + RLVR** | RM (replace with verifier) | 2 (policy, ref) |

DPO offline; GRPO on-policy. RLVR works for **verifiable** domains (code, math).

## Common traps

- **Mixed precision ≠ quantization.** Mixed precision = training; quantization = inference.
- **Flash Attention does NOT change the output.** Same math, faster compute. Don't confuse with sparse attention.
- **MoE total params ≠ active params.** "DeepSeek V3 has 671B params" is true but misleading — only 37B are active per token.
- **LoRA does NOT add inference latency.** $A B$ merges back into $W$.
- **DPO is offline.** Can't easily incorporate verifiable rewards or new model rollouts.
- **RLVR works only for verifiable domains.** Open-ended chat needs DPO or PPO.
- **ZeRO-3 ≠ tensor parallelism.** ZeRO partitions; TP shards individual operations. Often combined.
- **RoPE encodes RELATIVE, not absolute, position** even though applied as an absolute rotation.

## Mental model: the LLM stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Arch["Architecture: MoE"]
    Infra["Infra: KV Cache • Paged Attn • RoPE • Kernel Fusion"]
    Attn["Attention: Flash • MQA/GQA/MLA • Sparse"]
    Train["Training: ZeRO • Mixed Precision"]
    Adapt["Adaptation: LoRA / QLoRA"]
    Deploy["Deploy: Quantization • Speculative Decoding"]
    Align["Alignment: PPO → DPO → GRPO/RLVR"]

    Arch --> Infra --> Attn --> Train --> Adapt --> Deploy --> Align

    classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0
    class Arch,Infra,Attn,Train,Adapt,Deploy,Align chatbot
```

# Cluster 02 — Transformer and attention

The architectural foundation of every modern LLM. Master this and the rest of the course makes sense; skip it and the rest is opaque.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-13-nlp-approaches]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]] — the building block
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]] — query/key/value mechanism
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]] — parallel heads, different subspaces
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/positional-encoding]] — restoring order
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/tokenization]] — turning text into tokens
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/layer-normalization]] — feature-dim normalisation
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/residual-connection]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-13]]

## Source slides

[Slides ↗](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf)

## What this cluster is about

The transformer (Vaswani 2017) was the first architecture **based solely on attention** — no recurrence, no convolution. It achieves three things RNNs and CNNs can't all do simultaneously: capture **global** dependencies (any token can attend to any other), train **in parallel** (no sequential dependency in the forward pass), and stack to enormous depth.

The core operation is **scaled dot-product self-attention**. For each token, project to three vectors: **Query** (what am I asking about?), **Key** (what do I offer?), and **Value** (what do I contribute?). The attention output is a weighted average of values, with weights = $\text{softmax}(QK^T / \sqrt{d_k})$. *Similar queries and keys produce high dot products → that token contributes more.* The big idea is that **instead of using fixed embeddings for each token, the whole sequence is used to compute a weighted average per token** — context-aware representations from the first layer.

Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**: it treats input as a set, not a sequence. Position must be supplied via **positional encoding** (sinusoidal in the original; RoPE in modern LLMs — see [[../01-modern-llm-techniques/overview|Cluster 01]]). **Multi-head attention** runs $h$ self-attentions in parallel with smaller per-head dim, each with its own $Q, K, V$ projections — letting the model attend to **different representation subspaces** at the same time.

Each transformer block wraps two sublayers (attention + MLP) with **residual connections** and **LayerNorm**. Residuals provide identity gradients for stable training of deep stacks. **LayerNorm** normalises across the feature dim within a single token (vs BatchNorm across batch+seq) — invariant to batch size and sequence length, which matters for variable-length inputs and inference at batch size 1.

Three architecture families derive from the transformer: **encoder-only** (BERT — bidirectional MLM, best at understanding), **decoder-only** (GPT — autoregressive next-token, best at generation), and **encoder-decoder seq2seq** (T5 — translation, summarisation). Each is the same building block in a different configuration. (See [[../09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/overview|Cluster 09]].)

# Cluster 02 — Atoms (Transformer and attention)

## The Transformer

1. **Headline.** First architecture **based solely on attention** — no recurrence, no convolution (Vaswani et al. 2017) [[transformer-encoder]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=3).
2. **Strengths.** Captures global word relationships; **parallelisable**; beats LSTMs and CNNs.
3. **Encoder-decoder structure.** Encoder produces hidden states; decoder consumes them via cross-attention.
4. **Key component.** Multi-head self-attention.

## Tokenization

5. **Three granularities.** Character / word / **subword** [[tokenization]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=4).
6. **Subword algorithms.** **BPE** (Byte-Pair Encoding), **WordPiece** (BERT), **SentencePiece** (T5, LLaMA).
7. **Why subword wins.** Handles unknown words by decomposing; manageable vocabulary; multilingual-friendly.
8. **Other methods.** Space-based, punctuation-based, rule-based, neural-network-based.

## Attention (background)

9. **Original attention.** Encoder-decoder LSTMs produced one hidden state for the whole input — bottleneck. Attention lets the decoder access **a hidden state at every encoder step**, with learned weights — [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=5).

## Self-attention

10. **The main idea.** Instead of fixed embeddings, **use the whole sequence to compute a weighted average of each token's embedding** [[self-attention]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=7).
11. **Permutation-equivariant.** Self-attention has **no notion of token order** — position must be added via positional encoding.
12. **Captures long-term dependencies.** Any token can attend to any other regardless of distance.

## Scaled dot-product attention

13. **Three projections per token.** Project embedding into **Query**, **Key**, **Value** matrices [[multi-head-attention]] — [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=9).
14. **Similarity = dot product** $Q K^T$. Similar queries and keys give high values.
15. **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to keep gradients stable, then **softmax** to get attention weights.
16. **Output formula.** $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}) V$.
17. **Four steps.** Project Q/K/V → compute $QK^T$ → scale + softmax → weight V.

## Why softmax

18. **Three reasons** [[self-attention]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=10):
    - Normalises any vector into a **probability distribution**.
    - Acts as a **soft argmax** — exaggerates dominance, preserves close ties.
    - **Differentiable** for backprop.

## Multi-head attention

19. **Parallel heads.** Repeat scaled dot-product attention $h$ times in parallel, each with its own learned $Q, K, V$ projections [[multi-head-attention]] — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=12).
20. **Why multiple heads.** Each head learns to attend to **different representation subspaces** simultaneously.
21. **Cross-attention.** The encoder-decoder MHA module connects encoder input + decoder input.
22. **Parallel computation.** All heads run at once.

## Positional encoding

23. **Need.** Self-attention is permutation-equivariant; we **add positional encoding to token embeddings before the first transformer block** [[positional-encoding]] — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=13).
24. **Sinusoidal scheme.** Different frequencies along the embedding dim let the model resolve both adjacency and long-range distance: $\text{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$, $\text{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$.
25. **Modern alternative.** **RoPE** rotates Q and K at every layer — see [[../01-modern-llm-techniques/atoms|Cluster 01]] (atoms 11-15).

## Residual connections

26. **Three benefits** [[residual-connection]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=14):
    - **Reduce vanishing gradients** (identity shortcut for gradient flow).
    - Let later layers learn from initial features (without skip, that info would be lost).
    - **Smooth loss surface** — fewer saddle points, more robust optimisation.

## Layer normalization

27. **What it does.** Normalise each token's embedding to **zero mean and unit variance across the feature dim** [[layer-normalization]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=15).
28. **Why not BatchNorm.** Sequences are short, variable-length; inference is often batch=1. BatchNorm fragments. LayerNorm doesn't depend on batch.
29. **Advantages.** Improved training stability + generalisation, reduced sensitivity to initialisation, faster convergence.

## Three architecture families

30. **Encoder-decoder (seq2seq).** Both encoder and decoder. Translation, summarisation. **Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird** [[transformer-encoder]] — [slide 21](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=21).
31. **Encoder-only (autoencoder).** No decoder. Pretrain by adding noise (masking) and reconstructing → bidirectional embeddings. Used downstream for classification, NER, Q&A. **BERT, RoBERTa, XLM, ALBERT, ELECTRA, DeBERTa.**
32. **Decoder-only (autoregressive).** No encoder. Predict next token given previous tokens. Used for generation. **GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral.**

6. **Subword algorithms.** **BPE** (Byte-Pair Encoding), **WordPiece** (BERT), **SentencePiece** (T5, LLaMA).
7. **Why subword wins.** Handles unknown words by decomposing; manageable vocabulary; multilingual-friendly.

## Attention (background)

9. **Original attention.** Encoder-decoder LSTMs produced one hidden state for the whole input — bottleneck. Attention lets the decoder access **a hidden state at every encoder step**, with learned weights — [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=5).

10. **The main idea.** Instead of fixed embeddings, **use the whole sequence to compute a weighted average of each token's embedding** [[self-attention]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=7).
11. **Permutation-equivariant.** Self-attention has **no notion of token order** — position must be added via positional encoding.
12. **Captures long-term dependencies.** Any token can attend to any other regardless of distance.

13. **Three projections per token.** Project embedding into **Query**, **Key**, **Value** matrices [[multi-head-attention]] — [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=9).

15. **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to keep gradients stable, then **softmax** to get attention weights.
16. **Output formula.** $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}) V$.
17. **Four steps.** Project Q/K/V → compute $QK^T$ → scale + softmax → weight V.

## Why softmax

18. **Three reasons** [[self-attention]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=10):
    - Normalises any vector into a **probability distribution**.
    - Acts as a **soft argmax** — exaggerates dominance, preserves close ties.
    - **Differentiable** for backprop.

## Multi-head attention

19. **Parallel heads.** Repeat scaled dot-product attention $h$ times in parallel, each with its own learned $Q, K, V$ projections [[multi-head-attention]] — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=12).
20. **Why multiple heads.** Each head learns to attend to **different representation subspaces** simultaneously.
21. **Cross-attention.** The encoder-decoder MHA module connects encoder input + decoder input.
22. **Parallel computation.** All heads run at once.

## Positional encoding

23. **Need.** Self-attention is permutation-equivariant; we **add positional encoding to token embeddings before the first transformer block** [[positional-encoding]] — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=13).
24. **Sinusoidal scheme.** Different frequencies along the embedding dim let the model resolve both adjacency and long-range distance: $\text{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$, $\text{PE}_{(\text{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$.
25. **Modern alternative.** **RoPE** rotates Q and K at every layer — see [[../01-modern-llm-techniques/atoms|Cluster 01]] (atoms 11-15).

## Residual connections

26. **Three benefits** [[residual-connection]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=14):
    - **Reduce vanishing gradients** (identity shortcut for gradient flow).
    - Let later layers learn from initial features (without skip, that info would be lost).
    - **Smooth loss surface** — fewer saddle points, more robust optimisation.

## Layer normalization

27. **What it does.** Normalise each token's embedding to **zero mean and unit variance across the feature dim** [[layer-normalization]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=15).
28. **Why not BatchNorm.** Sequences are short, variable-length; inference is often batch=1. BatchNorm fragments. LayerNorm doesn't depend on batch.
29. **Advantages.** Improved training stability + generalisation, reduced sensitivity to initialisation, faster convergence.

## Three architecture families

30. **Encoder-decoder (seq2seq).** Both encoder and decoder. Translation, summarisation. **Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird** [[transformer-encoder]] — [slide 21](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 13.pdf#page=21).
31. **Encoder-only (autoencoder).** No decoder. Pretrain by adding noise (masking) and reconstructing → bidirectional embeddings. Used downstream for classification, NER, Q&A. **BERT, RoBERTa, XLM, ALBERT, ELECTRA, DeBERTa.**
32. **Decoder-only (autoregressive).** No encoder. Predict next token given previous tokens. Used for generation. **GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral.**

---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-02]
cluster: 02
---

# Cluster 02 — Cheat sheet (Transformer and attention)

## Definitions

| Term | One-liner |
|---|---|
| Transformer | Attention-only architecture; no recurrence, no convolution |
| Self-attention | Each token attends to all tokens in its own sequence |
| **Permutation-equivariant** | Self-attention has no notion of token order without positional encoding |
| Q / K / V | Learned projections of token embeddings — Query asks, Key offers, Value contributes |
| Scaled dot-product | Attention weights = $\text{softmax}(QK^T / \sqrt{d_k})$ |
| Multi-head attention | $h$ parallel attentions, each with own Q/K/V projections, attending to different subspaces |
| Positional encoding | Vector added to token embeddings before first transformer block to inject order |
| LayerNorm | Normalise across feature dim **within a single token** |
| Residual connection | $\text{out} = x + \text{Sublayer}(x)$ — identity shortcut for gradient flow |

## Key formula

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients stable when dim is large.

## Sinusoidal positional encoding

$$\mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\mathrm{pos}/10000^{2i/d}), \qquad \mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$$

## Three architecture families

| Family | Used for | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder-only** (autoencoder) | Understanding | BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, ALBERT, ELECTRA |
| **Decoder-only** (autoregressive) | Generation | GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral |
| **Encoder-decoder** (seq2seq) | Translation, summarisation | Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird |

## Self-attention process (4 steps)

1. Project each token to $Q, K, V$.
2. Compute $QK^T$ (similarity).
3. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$, apply softmax → attention weights.
4. Weight V → output.

## Tokenization

| Type | Granularity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Character | Each character | `["u", "n", "h", ...]` |
| Word | Each word | `["unhappiness"]` (or `<UNK>`) |
| **Subword** | Mix | `["un", "happiness"]` (BPE) |

Modern default: subword (BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece).

## Why softmax in attention

- Normalises any vector to a probability distribution.
- Soft argmax — preserves close runners-up.
- Differentiable.

## Why LayerNorm not BatchNorm

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalises across | Batch dim | Feature dim within token |
| Depends on batch size | Yes | No |
| Variable seq length | Fragile | Invariant |
| Inference batch=1 | Wrong stats | Identical to training |

## Why residuals

- Reduce vanishing gradient (identity shortcut).
- Let later layers learn from initial features.
- Smooth loss surface (fewer saddle points).

## Common traps

- **"Self-attention captures order"** — false. Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**; positional encoding adds order.
- **"Multi-head attention is faster"** — not the point. The point is **different representation subspaces** in parallel.
- **"Softmax is just normalisation"** — no, it's a soft argmax that exaggerates winners.
- **"BatchNorm and LayerNorm are interchangeable"** — no, they normalise across different dims and behave very differently for variable-length sequences.
- **"BERT and GPT have the same architecture"** — no. BERT is **encoder-only** (bidirectional); GPT is **decoder-only** (left-to-right).

## Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Tok[Tokenize text] --> Emb[Token embeddings + positional encoding]
    Emb --> Block[Transformer block × N]
    subgraph Block_internals[Block]
        L1[LayerNorm] --> Att[Multi-head self-attention]
        Att --> R1["+ residual"]
        R1 --> L2[LayerNorm]
        L2 --> MLP[MLP: d → 4d → d]
        MLP --> R2["+ residual"]
    end
    Block --> Out[Hidden states]
    classDef nlp fill:#f3e5f5
    class Att,MLP nlp
```

Scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients stable when dim is large.

## Sinusoidal positional encoding

$$\mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i)} = \sin(\mathrm{pos}/10000^{2i/d}), \qquad \mathrm{PE}_{(\mathrm{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$$

## Three architecture families

| Family | Used for | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder-only** (autoencoder) | Understanding | BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, ALBERT, ELECTRA |
| **Decoder-only** (autoregressive) | Generation | GPT 1-5, LLaMA, Mistral |
| **Encoder-decoder** (seq2seq) | Translation, summarisation | Vanilla Transformer, T5, BART, M2M, BigBird |

## Self-attention process (4 steps)

1. Project each token to $Q, K, V$.
2. Compute $QK^T$ (similarity).
3. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$, apply softmax → attention weights.
4. Weight V → output.

## Tokenization

| Type | Granularity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Character | Each character | `["u", "n", "h", ...]` |
| Word | Each word | `["unhappiness"]` (or `<UNK>`) |
| **Subword** | Mix | `["un", "happiness"]` (BPE) |

Modern default: subword (BPE / WordPiece / SentencePiece).

## Why softmax in attention

- Normalises any vector to a probability distribution.
- Soft argmax — preserves close runners-up.
- Differentiable.

## Why LayerNorm not BatchNorm

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalises across | Batch dim | Feature dim within token |
| Depends on batch size | Yes | No |
| Variable seq length | Fragile | Invariant |
| Inference batch=1 | Wrong stats | Identical to training |

## Why residuals

- Reduce vanishing gradient (identity shortcut).
- Let later layers learn from initial features.
- Smooth loss surface (fewer saddle points).

## Common traps

- **"Self-attention captures order"** — false. Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant**; positional encoding adds order.
- **"Multi-head attention is faster"** — not the point. The point is **different representation subspaces** in parallel.
- **"Softmax is just normalisation"** — no, it's a soft argmax that exaggerates winners.
- **"BatchNorm and LayerNorm are interchangeable"** — no, they normalise across different dims and behave very differently for variable-length sequences.
- **"BERT and GPT have the same architecture"** — no. BERT is **encoder-only** (bidirectional); GPT is **decoder-only** (left-to-right).

## Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Tok[Tokenize text] --> Emb[Token embeddings + positional encoding]
    Emb --> Block[Transformer block × N]
    subgraph Block_internals[Block]
        L1[LayerNorm] --> Att[Multi-head self-attention]
        Att --> R1["+ residual"]
        R1 --> L2[LayerNorm]
        L2 --> MLP[MLP: d → 4d → d]
        MLP --> R2["+ residual"]
    end
    Block --> Out[Hidden states]
    classDef nlp fill:#f3e5f5
    class Att,MLP nlp
```

# Cluster 03 — Decision trees, Random Forest, GBDT

Self-contained, lots of testable distinctions (Gini vs Entropy, level- vs leaf-wise, exact vs histogram). High exam yield.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-08-decision-trees]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/decision-tree]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/gini-vs-entropy]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/random-forest]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/gradient-boosting-decision-trees]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-08]]

## Source slides

[Slides ↗](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf)

## What this cluster is about

Tree-based models dominate **tabular** machine learning. Decision trees recursively split feature space into rectangles to **minimise impurity** at each leaf — measured by Gini index or Entropy. Trees are intuitive, handle categorical variables natively, need no normalisation — but **overfit easily**.

Two complementary fixes:

**Random Forest (bagging).** Train $B$ decision trees on **bootstrapped subsets** (sampling with replacement) of the data, in parallel. Final prediction = average / vote. Bagging reduces variance because the trees overfit to *different* random idiosyncrasies; averaging cancels them out. RF also samples random feature subsets at each split to decorrelate trees further.

**Gradient-Boosting Decision Trees — GBDT (boosting).** Train trees **sequentially**, each new tree fit to the **residual error** of the previous ensemble. Modern GBDT (XGBoost, LightGBM) uses a 2nd-order Taylor expansion of the loss to find optimal splits in closed form. Two important orthogonal choices: **growth strategy** (level-wise — XGBoost default, balanced; vs leaf-wise — LightGBM default, faster on big data, can overfit) and **split-finding** (exact — try every value, slow; vs histogram — bin features, fast, equal accuracy on large data).

**XGBoost vs LightGBM** is the headline GBDT comparison: XGBoost from DMLC, level-wise default, supports both exact and histogram. LightGBM from Microsoft, leaf-wise, histogram-only, **10-30% faster on Spark with 15% higher AUC** on the Higgs dataset. Both have GPU, distributed training, and 100+ hyperparameters.

The question to anchor everything: **GBDT > Random Forest on most tabular benchmarks**, because sequential error correction makes more aggressive use of each tree. RF still wins on training-time parallelism.

tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-03]
cluster: 03
---

# Cluster 03 — Atoms (Decision Trees, RF, GBDT)

## Decision Trees

1. **Definition.** Recursively split feature space into rectangles to minimise impurity at each leaf [[decision-tree]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=1).
2. **Pure node.** All data points in the node belong to the **same class** (impurity = 0).
3. **Pros.** Intuitive / explainable, cuts feature space in (hyper)cuboids, **handles categorical variables natively**, **no feature normalisation needed**, handles missing values.
4. **Con.** **Overfit easily** — fixed by Random Forest (bagging) or GBDT (boosting).

## Gini vs Entropy

5. **Gini index.** $G = \sum_k p_k(1 - p_k) = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$. Variance-flavoured. **Faster** to compute (no log) [[gini-vs-entropy]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=3).
6. **Entropy.** $H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$. Information-theoretic. Slower (log).
7. **Both zero when** the node is **pure**.
8. **Both maximal when** classes are evenly split (binary: Gini = 0.5, Entropy = 1).
9. **In production.** **Gini wins for speed.** Splits chosen by either are usually similar.

## Random Forest

10. **Bagging definition.** **Bootstrap aggregation** — sample $B$ subsets with **replacement**, train one tree per subset, then average (regression) or majority-vote (classification) [[random-forest]] — [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=6).
11. **Why with replacement.** Each bag is a representative subsample (same point can appear multiple times); ensures we don't run out of data.
12. **Random feature subset at each split.** Typically $\sqrt{n}$ features for classification — decorrelates trees.
13. **Trains in parallel** — each tree is independent.
14. **Bagging is algorithm-agnostic** — applies to any base learner, not just trees.

## GBDT

15. **Definition.** Sequential ensemble of weak decision trees, each fit to the **residual error** of the previous ensemble [[gradient-boosting-decision-trees]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=7).
16. **Additive update.** $\hat y_i^{(t)} = \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)$.
17. **Loss with regularisation.** $\mathcal L^{(t)} = \sum_i \ell(y_i, \hat y_i^{(t-1)} + f_t(x_i)) + \Omega(f_t)$. $\Omega(f_t)$ penalises tree complexity.
18. **Taylor expansion (XGBoost / LightGBM).** $\mathcal L^{(t)} \approx \sum_i [g_i f_t(x_i) + \tfrac{1}{2} h_i f_t(x_i)^2] + \Omega(f_t)$ with gradient $g_i$ and Hessian $h_i$. Makes optimal split fast to find.

## Tree-growth strategies

19. **Level-wise.** Grow the tree level by level. Balanced. **Better for smaller datasets.** [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=10).
20. **Leaf-wise.** Split the leaf with the **highest loss change**. **Better for larger datasets, faster, can overfit without tuning.**
21. **XGBoost default.** Level-wise.
22. **LightGBM default.** Leaf-wise.

## Split-finding

23. **Exact.** Try every feature value. Slow, marginally more accurate [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=11).
24. **Histogram.** Bin features into a histogram, evaluate bin boundaries. **Fast, equal accuracy on large datasets.**
25. **LightGBM.** Histogram-only.
26. **XGBoost.** Supports both.

## XGBoost vs LightGBM

27. **XGBoost.** DMLC, level-wise default, exact + histogram, ~100 params [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=13).
28. **LightGBM.** Microsoft, leaf-wise default, histogram-only, **130+ params**.
29. **Spark headline.** LightGBM on Spark (SynapseML) is **10-30% faster** than SparkML on the Higgs dataset and achieves **15% higher AUC**.
30. **Distributed LightGBM strategy** ([slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 08.pdf#page=14)): partition data across Spark workers; build local histograms; merge histograms for non-overlapping feature subsets; find local best splits on merged histograms; sync up the global best.

## When to use which

31. **Tabular data.** GBDT (LightGBM, XGBoost) — usually best.
32. **Need explainability + small data.** Single decision tree.
33. **Variance reduction without much tuning.** Random Forest.
34. **Massive data + needs to fit in memory.** LightGBM.
35. **Robust default.** XGBoost.

# Cluster 04 — Reco data and metrics

Foundational vocabulary for everything that follows. Pure recall, easy points if memorised.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-02-data-terminology]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-03-collaborative-vs-content-based]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-04-offline-vs-online-metrics]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-terminology]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/cold-start-problem]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-splits]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-transformations]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/negative-sampling-reco]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/collaborative-filtering]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/content-based-filtering]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-rating-metrics]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-ranking-metrics]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-diversity-metrics]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-online-metrics]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/ab-test]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-architectures]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-02]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-03]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-04]]

## What this cluster is about

Three foundational lectures, each defining vocabulary you'll use everywhere later.

**Data terminology (Lecture 02).** Every recommender has three components: **user, item, interaction**. Interactions come in two flavours — **explicit** (user actively rates, surveys, reviews — informative but costly and biased) and **implicit** (user behaves: clicks, purchases, dwell — abundant but **negatives are hard to define**). The fundamental implicit-feedback problem ("did the user not like this, or just not see it?") motivates negative sampling and pairwise ranking losses (Cluster 06). Cold start — too little data for a user (cold user) or item (cold item) — is collaborative filtering's headline weakness; content-based filtering is the textbook fix. Data splits matter for evaluation: **random** (risks artificial cold users), **stratified** (same users in train + test → standard for movie reco), **chronological** (split by timestamp → required when tastes drift).

**CF vs CB (Lecture 03).** **Collaborative filtering** uses only the user-item matrix — "users with similar behaviors get similar recommendations". **Content-based** uses user/item features — works on day one for cold items because features exist before interactions do. Most production systems blend both ([[../06-neural-reco/overview|Cluster 06]] — Wide & Deep is the canonical hybrid).

**Metrics (Lecture 04).** Two evaluation worlds. **Offline** measures model quality on held-out data — rating metrics (RMSE, MAE — RMSE penalises outliers more), ranking metrics (Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP@K, NDCG@K — NDCG accounts for *rank position* of hits), and diversity metrics (novelty, diversity, serendipity, coverage). **Online** measures business impact — CTR, conversion, AOV, MAU, LTV (with the heuristic LTV ≥ 3× CAC), evaluated via **A/B tests**. Long-tail items are profitable but hurt by cold-start; ALS-like accuracy-optimised models concentrate on the popular head and tank diversity. Three deployment **architectures**: batch (cheap, stale), real-time (fresh, expensive), **2-step / hybrid** (batch generates candidates, real-time reranks — industry default).

# Cluster 04 — Reco data and metrics

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/ab-test]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-02]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-03]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-04]]

## What this cluster is about

Three foundational lectures, each defining vocabulary you'll use everywhere later.

**Data terminology (Lecture 02).** Every recommender has three components: **user, item, interaction**. Interactions come in two flavours — **explicit** (user actively rates, surveys, reviews — informative but costly and biased) and **implicit** (user behaves: clicks, purchases, dwell — abundant but **negatives are hard to define**). The fundamental implicit-feedback problem ("did the user not like this, or just not see it?") motivates negative sampling and pairwise ranking losses (Cluster 06). Cold start — too little data for a user (cold user) or item (cold item) — is collaborative filtering's headline weakness; content-based filtering is the textbook fix. Data splits matter for evaluation: **random** (risks artificial cold users), **stratified** (same users in train + test → standard for movie reco), **chronological** (split by timestamp → required when tastes drift).

**CF vs CB (Lecture 03).** **Collaborative filtering** uses only the user-item matrix — "users with similar behaviors get similar recommendations". **Content-based** uses user/item features — works on day one for cold items because features exist before interactions do. Most production systems blend both ([[../06-neural-reco/overview|Cluster 06]] — Wide & Deep is the canonical hybrid).

**Metrics (Lecture 04).** Two evaluation worlds. **Offline** measures model quality on held-out data — rating metrics (RMSE, MAE — RMSE penalises outliers more), ranking metrics (Precision@K, Recall@K, MAP@K, NDCG@K — NDCG accounts for *rank position* of hits), and diversity metrics (novelty, diversity, serendipity, coverage). **Online** measures business impact — CTR, conversion, AOV, MAU, LTV (with the heuristic LTV ≥ 3× CAC), evaluated via **A/B tests**. Long-tail items are profitable but hurt by cold-start; ALS-like accuracy-optimised models concentrate on the popular head and tank diversity. Three deployment **architectures**: batch (cheap, stale), real-time (fresh, expensive), **2-step / hybrid** (batch generates candidates, real-time reranks — industry default).

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-04]
cluster: 04
---

# Cluster 04 — Atoms (Reco data and metrics)

## Three components

1. **Every reco system has three components.** User, item, interaction (feedback) [[reco-data-terminology]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=1).
2. **User** = customer who interacted and to whom the system recommends.
3. **Item** = product that can be recommended.
4. **Interaction / feedback** = a type of interaction between a user and an item.

## Explicit vs implicit feedback

5. **Explicit pros.** More informative, easier for algos, **can express negatives** [[explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=3).
6. **Explicit cons.** Costly, biased toward willing raters, may need calibration.
7. **Implicit pros.** Plentiful from logs, less statistical bias, more data points.
8. **Implicit cons.** Less graded, **negatives are hard to define** ("no click" = "didn't see" or "saw and rejected"?).

## Cold start

9. **Cold user.** Too little interaction history to recommend (new user, infrequent buyer) [[cold-start-problem]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=7).
10. **Cold item.** Too few interactions to learn the item's profile (new releases, unique products).
11. **Textbook fix.** Content-based filtering — features exist from day one even when interactions don't.

## Data transformations

12. **Count.** Affinity = interaction count [[reco-data-transformations]] — [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=8).
13. **Weighted count.** By interaction type (view < click < buy).
14. **Time decay.** $w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$ — half-life $T$.
15. **One-hot.** Categorical → indicator vector. **Increases sparsity.**
16. **Binarize.** Numerical → ordinal (e.g. rating ≥ 4 → 1).

## Negative sampling

17. **Need.** Implicit feedback is positive-only. Sample unobserved (user, item) pairs as negatives [[negative-sampling-reco]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=14).
18. **Risk.** A sampled "negative" might be a missing positive. The principled fix: **pairwise loss (BPR)** instead of pointwise classification.

## Data splits

19. **Random split.** Risks creating cold users in test [[reco-data-splits]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 02.pdf#page=15).
20. **Stratified split.** Same set of users in both train and test → avoids artificial coldness. **Default for movie recommendation.**
21. **Chronological split.** Split by timestamp. **Required when tastes drift** (fashion, news).

## Collaborative vs content-based

22. **Collaborative filtering.** Correlate behaviors across users. "Users with similar behaviors get similar recs" [[collaborative-filtering]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf#page=1).
23. **Content-based.** Use user/item features. Match on profile similarity [[content-based-filtering]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 03.pdf#page=4).
24. **CF strength.** Captures latent structure no one labelled.
25. **CF weakness.** Cold-start.
26. **CB strength.** Handles cold-start.
27. **CB content** = user features, item features, review text, knowledge graph, multi-domain (image/video), context.

## Rating metrics

28. **RMSE.** $\sqrt{\frac{1}{|K|} \sum (\hat r - r)^2}$. **More affected by outliers** [[reco-rating-metrics]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=3).
29. **MAE.** $\frac{1}{|K|} \sum |\hat r - r|$. Better estimator of true average error.
30. **AUC.** Discriminate positives from negatives.
31. **Logloss / cross-entropy.** Heavily penalises confident wrong predictions.

## Ranking metrics

32. **Precision@K.** (relevant in top-K) / K — fraction of recommendations correct [[reco-ranking-metrics]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=4).
33. **Recall@K.** (relevant in top-K) / (total relevant) — fraction of relevant items surfaced.
34. **MAP@K.** Mean Average Precision — position-weighted, averaged across users.
35. **NDCG@K.** Both **hit** and **rank position** matter — discount $\log_2(k+1)$ for further-down hits.

## Diversity metrics

36. **Novelty.** How rare are recommended items? [[reco-diversity-metrics]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=7).
37. **Diversity.** How different are items in the list from each other?
38. **Serendipity.** How surprising are recs vs the user's history?
39. **Coverage.** What fraction of the catalog gets recommended?
40. **Long tail.** Item distribution — few popular, many rare. **Profitable but hurt by cold-start.** Accuracy-optimised models like ALS over-recommend the head.

## Online metrics

41. **CTR.** Clicks ÷ visits — engagement [[reco-online-metrics]] — [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=9).
42. **Conversion rate.** Conversions ÷ visits.
43. **AOV.** Average Order Value = total revenue ÷ orders.
44. **MAU.** Monthly Active Users.
45. **LTV.** Lifetime Value. Healthy benchmark: **LTV ≥ 3 × CAC** (Customer Acquisition Cost).

## A/B test

46. **Definition.** Randomise traffic into two arms; A gets one variant, B gets another. Compare metrics [[ab-test]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=10).
47. **Multi-variate test.** A/B with more than two arms.

## Reco architectures

48. **Batch.** Score everything offline → cache → serve. Cheap, stale [[reco-architectures]] — [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=11).
49. **Real-time.** Score at request time. Fresh, expensive.
50. **2-step (hybrid).** Batch generates candidates → real-time reranks 100-1000 items. **Industry default for large catalogs.**
