r_{ui})^2}$ | | **MAE** | $\frac{1}{|K|} \sum_{(u,i)} |\hat r_{ui} - r_{ui}|$ | | **Precision@K** | $|R_u \cap T_u| / K$ | | **Recall@K** | $|R_u \cap T_u| / |T_u|$ | | **DCG@K** | $\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{2^{\text{rel}_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}$ | | **Time-decay weight** | $w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$ (half-life $T$) | | **LTV heuristic** | LTV ≥ **3** × CAC | ## CF vs CB at a glance | | Collaborative filtering | Content-based filtering | |---|---|---| | Inputs | Interaction matrix only | User + item features | | Cold start | **Fails** | **Handles** | | Captures latent structure | Yes | No (limited to features) | | Example | Movie ratings, "users like you also bought" | Demographic + product attributes | ## Splits decision | Question | Use | |---|---| | Do I recommend well to known users? | **Stratified** | | Do I recommend well tomorrow? | **Chronological** | | Quick baseline on dense data | Random | | How well do I handle cold users? | Hold out **entire users** | ## Metrics decision | Question | Use | |---|---| | Predict numeric ratings? | RMSE / MAE | | Top-K list quality? | NDCG / MAP / Precision@K / Recall@K | | Catalog coverage matters? | Novelty / Diversity / Coverage | | Production performance? | CTR / conversion / LTV via A/B test | | Position of hit matters? | NDCG (vs Precision@K which doesn't) | ## Architectures decision | Need | Use | |---|---| | Cheap serve, stale OK | **Batch** | | Real-time context | **Real-time** | | Large catalog + freshness | **2-step (hybrid)** ← industry default | ## Common traps - **"RMSE is always better than MAE"** — false. RMSE penalises outliers more, but MAE is a better estimator of *average* error. - **"Precision@K accounts for rank position"** — false. **NDCG** does. Precision@K only looks at the binary set. - **"Implicit feedback has no negatives"** — true → that's why you need negative sampling, and why pointwise loss fails (use BPR). - **"Random split is always fine"** — false. Risks creating cold users in the test set. - **"ALS is the best for diversity"** — false. ALS optimises **accuracy** → recommends popular items → bad for diversity. - **"CF beats CB on cold start"** — false. **CB handles cold start; CF doesn't.** - **"LTV is the main metric"** — false. LTV is a *long-term* metric; the LTV ≥ 3× CAC rule is for evaluating *acquisition strategy*, not per-recommendation quality. - **"A/B test = compare two recommenders"** — broader: compare any two product variants. ## Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Data[(User-Item interactions)] --> Algo UF[User features] --> Algo IF[Item features] --> Algo Algo[Recommender] Algo --> Off[Offline metrics
RMSE / NDCG / MAP / Coverage] Algo --> AB[A/B test in production] AB --> On[Online metrics
CTR / Conversion / LTV] classDef metric fill:#ffebee class Off,On metric ```

content.title:
cd /Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/40-Reviews/Chatbots
files=$(grep -rl "Chatbots and Reco" .)
for f in $files; do
  sed -i '' 's|Chatbots and Reco \([0-9]\{1,2\}[ab]\?\)\.pdf|Chatbots%20and%20Reco%20\1.pdf|g' "$f"
done
echo "Done. Verifying:"
grep -rh "Chatbots.*Reco.*pdf#page" /Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/40-Reviews/Chatbots | head -3

# Cluster 05 — Matrix factorization, SAR, FM/FFM

The classical model-based recommender stack. Equation-heavy; expect MCQs that test recognition of the canonical formulas and the SGD-vs-ALS distinction.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-06-sar-memory-based]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-07-matrix-factorization-limits]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/sar-algorithm]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/reco-similarity-metrics]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/matrix-factorization-reco]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/svd-recommender]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/als-recommender]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/factorization-machines]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/ffm-field-aware]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-06]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-07]]

## What this cluster is about

Three families of model-based collaborative filtering: **memory-based (SAR)**, **matrix factorization (MF)**, and **factorization machines (FM/FFM)**.

**SAR (memory-based).** No training step — multiply two matrices. **Affinity matrix** $A$ captures user-item interaction strength (with optional time-decay half-life $T$). **Item-item similarity matrix** $S$ comes from the co-occurrence matrix $C = U'^T U'$, normalised by one of seven choices: count (predictability), lift (discoverability), Jaccard (compromise), mutual information, lexicographers MI, cosine, inclusion. Score = $\sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$. Worked example: User 1's score for Item 4 = $5 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2.5 \cdot 3 + 0 + 0 = 28.5$. Easy to explain, no overfit, but doesn't capture latent factors.

**Matrix factorization (MF).** Decompose $R \approx P Q^T$ with $P \in \mathbb{R}^{|U| \times f}$ user factors and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{|I| \times f}$ item factors. Predicted rating $\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$. Regularised objective: $\min \sum_{(u,i) \in K}(r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u)^2 + \lambda(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2)$, summed over **observed** ratings only. The headline limitation: inner product = **linear** combination of latent dims. Two algorithms to learn $P, Q$:

- **SGD / SVD-with-biases.** Adds $\mu, b_u, b_i$ to absorb baseline effects; lets latent factors model personalisation only. Stochastic gradient descent on the joint loss.
- **ALS.** Joint loss is non-convex, but each subproblem (hold $P$ fixed, solve for $Q$, vice versa) is convex with a closed-form least-squares solution. **Embarrassingly parallel** — Spark-friendly.

**Factorization Machines (FM).** MF generalised to any feature vector. Models all order-2 interactions $\langle \mathbf v_i, \mathbf v_j \rangle x_i x_j$ using factorised latent vectors $\mathbf v_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$. Same expressivity as $O(n^2)$ pairwise weights but $O(nk)$ parameters and $O(nk)$ computation via the linear-time identity. **FFM** uses a different latent vector per (feature, field) pair → captures interactions that should be specialised by field type. xLearn implements LR/FM/FFM.

The key MCQ trap: **"What's the difference between MF and FM?"** — MF is special case of FM where $\mathbf x$ has only two non-zero entries (one user, one item).

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-05]
cluster: 05
---

# Cluster 05 — Atoms (Matrix factorization, SAR, FM)

## SAR

1. **Definition.** Memory-based collaborative filter — multiply user-item affinity matrix $A$ by item-item similarity matrix $S$ to score recommendations [[sar-algorithm]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=1).
2. **Three matrices.** Affinity $A$ (user-item), co-occurrence $C$ (item-item co-counts), similarity $S$ (normalised $C$).
3. **Scoring formula.** $\text{rec}(u, i) = \sum_j A(u, j) \cdot S(i, j)$ — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=3).
4. **Worked example.** User 1's affinity row: $[5, 3, 2.5, 0, 0]$; item 4 similarity column: $[3, 2, 3, 4, 2]$. Score = $5 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2.5 \cdot 3 + 0 + 0 = 28.5$.
5. **No training step.** Pure matrix multiplication on counts.
6. **Implementations.** CPU + PySpark; possible future: GPU (PyTorch), Dask, stored procedure (Redis/SQL).

## SAR similarity metrics

7. **Count.** $S_{ij} = C_{ij}$. Favours **predictability** — popular items recommended most [[reco-similarity-metrics]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=4).
8. **Lift.** $S_{ij} = C_{ij} / (C_{ii} \cdot C_{jj})$. Favours **discoverability / serendipity** — boosts items popular among small subgroups.
9. **Jaccard.** $C_{ij} / (C_{ii} + C_{jj} - C_{ij})$. Compromise between count and lift.
10. **Mutual information.** Information-theoretic; biased toward low-frequency items.
11. **Lexicographers MI.** MI × co-occurrence frequency — corrects MI's low-frequency bias.
12. **Cosine.** Angle between item column vectors.
13. **Inclusion index.** $C_{ij} / \min(C_{ii}, C_{jj})$ — overlap.

## Affinity matrix

14. **Captures.** Strength of user-item relationship — accepts both **explicit** (1-5 ratings) and **implicit** (clicks, purchases, etc., each with a weight) [[sar-algorithm]] — [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=7).
15. **Time-decay weight.** $w(t) = 2^{-(t_0 - t)/T}$ — half-life $T$. Recent events weighed more.

## Co-occurrence computation

16. **Formula.** $C = U'^T U'$ where $U'$ is the binarised affinity matrix [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=6).

## Matrix factorization

17. **Model.** $\hat r_{ui} = q_i^T p_u$. Decomposition $R \approx P Q^T$ [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=11).
18. **Rank.** Number of latent factors $f$ = rank of the model. Each item factor $q_i$ might encode "comedy vs drama" or uninterpretable axes.
19. **Regularised objective.** $\min_{P, Q} \sum_{(u,i) \in K}(r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u)^2 + \lambda(\|q_i\|^2 + \|p_u\|^2)$ — sum over **observed** ratings only [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=13).
20. **Loss is non-convex** in $(P, Q)$.

## SVD-with-biases

21. **Model with biases.** $\hat r_{ui} = \mu + b_u + b_i + q_i^T p_u$, where $\mu$ = global average, $b_u$ = user bias, $b_i$ = item bias [[svd-recommender]] — [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=14).
22. **Why biases.** Absorb baseline effects → free latent factors to model personalisation only.
23. **Update rules.** SGD: $b_u \leftarrow b_u + \eta(e_{ui} - \lambda b_u)$; $p_u \leftarrow p_u + \eta(e_{ui} q_i - \lambda p_u)$; symmetric for $q_i$ and $b_i$.

## ALS

24. **Idea.** Alternate between holding $P$ fixed and solving for $Q$, then vice versa. Each subproblem is **convex** with a closed-form least-squares solution [[als-recommender]] — [slide 16](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2006.pdf#page=16).
25. **Closed-form per-user update.** $p_u = (Q^T Q + \lambda I)^{-1} Q^T r_u$.
26. **Pros.** **Embarrassingly parallel** (each user/item update is independent), no learning rate, Spark-friendly.
27. **Implicit-feedback variant.** Weighted ALS (Hu et al. 2008) with confidence $c_{ui} = 1 + \alpha r_{ui}$ to weight observed entries.

## MF limitations

28. **The headline issue.** Inner product $q_i^T p_u$ is a **linear** combination of latent features — can't capture non-linear preference patterns [[matrix-factorization-reco]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2007.pdf#page=1).
29. **Two responses.** Replace inner product with MLP → **NCF** (lecture 10a). Extend to all order-2 feature interactions → **FM** (this lecture).

## Factorization Machines (FM)

30. **Model.** $\hat y(\mathbf x) = w_0 + \sum_i w_i x_i + \sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf v_i, \mathbf v_j \rangle x_i x_j$ where $\mathbf v_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ [[factorization-machines]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2007.pdf#page=2).
31. **Why factorise.** Naive pairwise weights = $O(n^2)$ params with no sharing; factorising gives $O(nk)$ params with shared information.
32. **Linear-time interaction sum.** $\sum_{i<j} \langle \mathbf v_i, \mathbf v_j \rangle x_i x_j = \frac{1}{2} \sum_f [(\sum_i v_{i,f} x_i)^2 - \sum_i v_{i,f}^2 x_i^2]$ — $O(nk)$ total.
33. **MF ⊂ FM.** Matrix factorization is FM with $\mathbf x$ = one-hot user + one-hot item only.

## Field-aware FM (FFM)

34. **Idea.** Different latent vector per (feature, field) pair: $\langle \mathbf v_{i, F(j)}, \mathbf v_{j, F(i)} \rangle$ [[ffm-field-aware]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2007.pdf#page=3).
35. **Cost.** Parameters $O(n F k)$ where $F$ = number of fields.
36. **Use case.** Many distinct field types (user × item × time × device × geo), as in CTR prediction.

## Library

37. **xLearn.** C++ library implementing LR, FM, FFM with Python bindings — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2007.pdf#page=4).

# Cluster 06 — Neural reco (NCF, Wide & Deep, BPR)

How the recommender world responded to deep learning. Three landmark architectures plus the loss function that aligns models with ranking metrics.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-09-bpr]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-10a-neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-10b-wide-and-deep]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/neural-collaborative-filtering]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/wide-and-deep]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/memorization-vs-generalization]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-09]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-10a]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-10b]]

## What this cluster is about

**BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking, Rendle 2009).** A loss function, not a model. Solves the implicit-feedback negative-sampling problem: pointwise loss labels unobserved items as 0, but most are *missing positives*. BPR replaces this with a **pairwise** loss — for each user, sample observed item $i$ and unobserved item $j$, train so $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$. Likelihood: $\sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$. The BPR-OPT objective $\sum \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ is a MAP estimate with Gaussian prior. **Plug into any model that produces $\hat r_{ui}$** — MF, NCF, GNNs (LightGCN). Train and test are aligned: both about ordering.

**NCF (Neural Collaborative Filtering, He et al. WWW 2017).** Replaces MF's inner product with a neural network. Three variants:

- **GMF** — element-wise product of user/item embeddings, then learned weighted sum: $\hat y_{ui} = a(\mathbf h^T (\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i))$. Special case (identity activation, all-ones $\mathbf h$) recovers MF.
- **MLP** — concatenate user and item embeddings, push through a feedforward network. Strictly more flexible than GMF.
- **NeuMF (fusion)** — combine GMF + MLP, each learning separate embeddings, concatenated in the final hidden layer.

Loss: binary cross-entropy with sampled negatives. Built-in leave-one-out evaluation.

**Wide & Deep (Cheng et al. 2016, Google Play Store).** The canonical hybrid. **Jointly trains** a wide linear model (memorisation via engineered cross-features like `installed_app=gmail × impression_app=youtube`) and a deep MLP (generalisation via embedding similarity). Both halves contribute to a shared sigmoid output. Joint training (not ensembling) lets each half compensate for the other's errors during gradient steps. The recipe behind DeepFM, DCN, xDeepFM and most modern CTR models — **memorisation for hot specifics, generalisation for the long tail**.

The high-leverage MCQ insight: **BPR is a loss; NCF and Wide&Deep are architectures.** They compose — NCF can use BPR as its loss instead of binary cross-entropy.

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-06]
cluster: 06
---

# Cluster 06 — Atoms (Neural reco)

## BPR

1. **Definition.** Pairwise ranking loss for implicit feedback. **Observed items should rank higher than unobserved ones** [[bpr-loss]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2009.pdf#page=1).
2. **Author / paper.** Rendle et al. 2009 — *BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking from Implicit Feedback*.
3. **The pointwise problem.** Pointwise loss labels unobserved as 0 → trains the model to suppress missing positives → can't recommend them [[pointwise-vs-pairwise-loss]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2009.pdf#page=2).
4. **The pairwise fix.** For each user, generate triples $(u, i, j)$: $i$ observed, $j$ unobserved. Train so $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$. Never commits to absolute scores — only ordering [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2009.pdf#page=3).

## BPR likelihood and objective

5. **Likelihood.** $p(i >_u j \mid \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$ [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2009.pdf#page=4).
6. **Posterior.** $p(\Theta \mid >_u) \propto p(>_u \mid \Theta) \cdot p(\Theta)$.
7. **Prior.** Normal — gives L2 regularisation in the MAP estimate.
8. **MAP objective.** BPR-OPT $= \sum_{(u, i, j) \in D_S} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$.
9. **SGD step.** Sample (u, i, j); update $\Theta$ to push $\hat r_{ui}$ up and $\hat r_{uj}$ down.

## BPR properties

10. **Loss, not model.** Pluggable into any model producing $\hat r_{ui}$ — MF (BPR-MF, the canonical paper), NCF, GNNs (LightGCN), sequential models.
11. **Train ≈ test distribution.** Both are about ordering — pointwise loss has a train/test mismatch, BPR doesn't.
12. **Generalises to explicit feedback.** From a numerical rating, derive pairs ($u$ rated $a$ > $b$ → $a >_u b$).

## NCF — overview

13. **Idea.** Replace MF's inner product with a neural network → non-linear user-item interactions [[neural-collaborative-filtering]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=1).
14. **Author / paper.** He et al., WWW 2017.
15. **Three variants.** GMF (element-wise product + learned output weights), MLP (concatenate + MLP), NeuMF (GMF + MLP fused).

## GMF (Generalized MF)

16. **Equation.** $\hat y_{ui} = a(\mathbf h^T (\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i))$ — $\odot$ = element-wise product [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=2).
17. **Special case.** $a = \text{identity}$, $\mathbf h$ = uniform ones → recover original MF.
18. **Added expressivity.** Edge weights are **learned**; non-linear activation can be applied.

## MLP variant

19. **Concatenate-then-MLP.** $\mathbf z_1 = [\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i]$; hidden layers $\mathbf z_{L+1} = a_L(\mathbf W_L \mathbf z_L + \mathbf b_L)$; output $\hat y_{ui} = \sigma(\mathbf w^T \mathbf z_\text{last})$ [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=3).
20. **Why concatenate (not product).** MLP can model arbitrary non-linear interactions; element-wise product is restricted to one type.

## NeuMF (fusion)

21. **Fusion.** GMF and MLP each learn **separate embeddings**, concatenated in the last hidden layer [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=4).

## NCF training

22. **Loss.** Binary cross-entropy with sampled [[negative-sampling-reco|negatives]] [[binary-cross-entropy-loss-reco]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=4).
23. **Built-in features.** Negative sampling, leave-one-out evaluation, parameters: n_factors / layer sizes / n_epochs [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010a.pdf#page=5).

## Wide & Deep

24. **Architecture.** Jointly train a **wide linear model** (memorisation) + **deep MLP** (generalisation). Both halves feed a shared sigmoid output [[wide-and-deep]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010b.pdf#page=1).
25. **Author / paper.** Cheng et al. 2016 — *Wide & Deep Learning for Recommender Systems*. Google Play Store app recommendation.
26. **Wide.** $y_\text{wide} = \mathbf w^T \mathbf x + b$ with engineered cross-features ($\phi(\mathbf x)$). Memorises co-occurrence patterns directly [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010b.pdf#page=2).
27. **Deep.** MLP with ReLU activations over **embedded** categorical features. Generalises via embedding similarity.
28. **Joint objective.** $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b)$.
29. **Joint > ensemble.** Each half compensates for the other's errors **during training**, not stacked post-hoc.
30. **Genres encoding.** Multi-hot via scikit-learn's `MultiLabelBinarizer`; e.g. movie 2355 (Animation, Children's, Comedy) → `[0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, ...]` [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2010b.pdf#page=4).

## Memorization vs Generalization

31. **Memorization.** Learn frequent **co-occurrence** patterns directly. Strong on hot common patterns; weak on long tail [[memorization-vs-generalization]].
32. **Generalization.** Recommend things similar to what worked before. Strong on long tail; can over-smooth hot specifics.
33. **Why combine.** Pure memorisation = popularity bot; pure generalisation = blurry diverse recs. Both halves cover what the other misses.

## Lineage of Wide & Deep

34. **DeepFM.** Replace wide with FM as the order-2 cross-feature half.
35. **DCN.** Deep & Cross Network — explicit cross layers.
36. **xDeepFM.** Vector-wise FM × deep.
37. **AutoInt.** Attention as feature interaction.

## Pointwise vs pairwise summary

38. **Pointwise.** $\mathcal L = \sum \ell(\hat r_{ui}, y_{ui})$ — predict 1 vs 0. **Bug on implicit data**: missing positives sampled as negatives.
39. **Pairwise (BPR).** $\mathcal L = \sum -\ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) + \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$. Aligned with ranking metrics.
40. **Listwise.** Optimise the full ranked list (LambdaMART, ListNet) — more aligned with NDCG, more complex. Pairwise usually enough.

--- tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-06] cluster: 06 --- # Cluster 06 — Cheat sheet (Neural reco — NCF, Wide&Deep, BPR) ## Definitions | Term | One-liner | |---|---| | **BPR** | Pairwise ranking loss for implicit feedback — $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$ | | **Pointwise loss** | Predict 1 for observed, 0 for sampled negatives — biased on implicit data | | **Pairwise loss** | Predict ordering between observed and unobserved — train ≈ test | | **NCF** | Replace MF inner product with neural network; non-linear interactions | | **GMF** | Generalised MF: element-wise product + learned output weights | | **MLP variant** | Concatenate user+item embeddings, push through MLP | | **NeuMF** | Fusion of GMF + MLP with separate embeddings | | **Wide & Deep** | Jointly train linear (memorise) + MLP (generalise) over shared output | | **Memorization** | Learn frequent co-occurrence patterns (cross-features) | | **Generalization** | Recommend by embedding similarity (transfer) | ## Key formulas | Quantity | Formula | |---|---| | **BPR likelihood** | $p(i >_u j \mid \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$ | | **BPR-OPT objective** | $\sum_{(u, i, j)} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ | | **GMF** | $\hat y_{ui} = a(\mathbf h^T (\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i))$ | | **NCF MLP output** | $\hat y_{ui} = \sigma(\text{MLP}([\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i]))$ | | **Wide & Deep** | $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b)$ | | **Binary cross-entropy** | $\mathcal L = -\sum y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui}) \log(1 - \hat y_{ui})$ | ## Pointwise vs Pairwise | | Pointwise | Pairwise (BPR) | |---|---|---| | Trains on | (user, item, label) | (user, pos item, neg item) | | Treats missing as | Negative (label 0) — **buggy** on implicit | Unranked relative to observed — correct | | Aligned with ranking metrics? | No | **Yes** | | Output | Calibrated probability | Ordering score only | | Use when | CTR prediction (need calibrated probs) | Top-K ranking (most reco) | ## NCF variants | Variant | Combiner | Special case | |---|---|---| | **GMF** | $\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i$ + learned weights | Identity activation + ones $\mathbf h$ → MF | | **MLP** | Concatenate + MLP | Strictly more flexible than GMF | | **NeuMF** | GMF + MLP with separate embeddings, concatenate at last layer | Highest expressivity | ## Wide & Deep at a glance | Half | Math | Job | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Wide** | Linear with cross-features | Memorisation | Hot common patterns | Long tail | | **Deep** | MLP over embeddings | Generalisation | Long tail / cold | Over-smooths hot | ## When to use which loss / model | Goal | Use | |---|---| | Calibrated CTR probability | NCF + binary cross-entropy | | Pure top-K ranking | Any model + **BPR loss** | | Cold-item handling | Wide & Deep (embeddings via deep half) | | Mostly hot common patterns | Wide & Deep (cross-features in wide half) | | Recommend with side features | Wide & Deep, FM, DeepFM | ## Common traps - **"BPR is a model"** — false. **BPR is a LOSS** that plugs into any model producing $\hat r_{ui}$. - **"Pointwise loss is fine for implicit feedback"** — false. **Treats missing positives as negatives** → suppresses items the user might like. - **"NCF GMF is just MF"** — false. **GMF generalises MF** with learned output weights and non-linear activation. Special case (identity + ones) recovers MF. - **"Wide & Deep is an ensemble"** — false. **Jointly trained** — each half compensates for the other's errors during gradient steps, not stacked post-hoc. - **"Wide handles cold start"** — false. The cross-features in the wide half are zero for unseen combinations. **Deep handles cold start** via embeddings. - **"BPR uses sigmoid as activation"** — kind of. The **likelihood** is $\sigma$ of the score difference; the model itself can be anything. - **"Pairwise = listwise"** — false. **Pairwise** = pairs (BPR). **Listwise** = full list (LambdaMART, ListNet). Pairwise is simpler and usually enough. ## Diagram (Mermaid) — NeuMF ```mermaid flowchart LR U[User one-hot] --> EG[Embed: GMF] U --> EM1[Embed: MLP] I[Item one-hot] --> EG2[Embed: GMF] I --> EM2[Embed: MLP] EG --> Mul["⊙"] EG2 --> Mul EM1 --> Concat["concat"] EM2 --> Concat Concat --> H[MLP layers] Mul --> Final["Concat
w/ MLP last hidden"] H --> Final Final --> Out["σ → P(Y=1)"] classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd class Mul,Concat,Final reco ``` ## Diagram — Wide & Deep ```mermaid flowchart LR Sparse[Sparse features] --> W["Wide:
x + cross(x)"] Sparse --> D["Deep: embed → MLP"] W --> Add[+] D --> Add Add --> Out["σ → P(click)"] classDef wide fill:#e3f2fd classDef deep fill:#f3e5f5 class W wide class D deep ```

--- tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-06] cluster: 06 --- # Cluster 06 — Cheat sheet (Neural reco — NCF, Wide&Deep, BPR) ## Definitions | Term | One-liner | |---|---| | **BPR** | Pairwise ranking loss for implicit feedback — $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$ | | **Pointwise loss** | Predict 1 for observed, 0 for sampled negatives — biased on implicit data | | **Pairwise loss** | Predict ordering between observed and unobserved — train ≈ test | | **NCF** | Replace MF inner product with neural network; non-linear interactions | | **GMF** | Generalised MF: element-wise product + learned output weights | | **MLP variant** | Concatenate user+item embeddings, push through MLP | | **NeuMF** | Fusion of GMF + MLP with separate embeddings | | **Wide & Deep** | Jointly train linear (memorise) + MLP (generalise) over shared output | | **Memorization** | Learn frequent co-occurrence patterns (cross-features) | | **Generalization** | Recommend by embedding similarity (transfer) | ## Key formulas | Quantity | Formula | |---|---| | **BPR likelihood** | $p(i >_u j \mid \Theta) = \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj})$ | | **BPR-OPT objective** | $\sum_{(u, i, j)} \ln \sigma(\hat r_{ui} - \hat r_{uj}) - \lambda \|\Theta\|^2$ | | **GMF** | $\hat y_{ui} = a(\mathbf h^T (\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i))$ | | **NCF MLP output** | $\hat y_{ui} = \sigma(\text{MLP}([\mathbf p_u\,;\, \mathbf q_i]))$ | | **Wide & Deep** | $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf x) = \sigma(\mathbf w_\text{wide}^T [\mathbf x, \phi(\mathbf x)] + \mathbf w_\text{deep}^T \mathbf a_\text{last} + b)$ | | **Binary cross-entropy** | $\mathcal L = -\sum y_{ui} \log \hat y_{ui} + (1 - y_{ui}) \log(1 - \hat y_{ui})$ | ## Pointwise vs Pairwise | | Pointwise | Pairwise (BPR) | |---|---|---| | Trains on | (user, item, label) | (user, pos item, neg item) | | Treats missing as | Negative (label 0) — **buggy** on implicit | Unranked relative to observed — correct | | Aligned with ranking metrics? | No | **Yes** | | Output | Calibrated probability | Ordering score only | | Use when | CTR prediction (need calibrated probs) | Top-K ranking (most reco) | ## NCF variants | Variant | Combiner | Special case | |---|---|---| | **GMF** | $\mathbf p_u \odot \mathbf q_i$ + learned weights | Identity activation + ones $\mathbf h$ → MF | | **MLP** | Concatenate + MLP | Strictly more flexible than GMF | | **NeuMF** | GMF + MLP with separate embeddings, concatenate at last layer | Highest expressivity | ## Wide & Deep at a glance | Half | Math | Job | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Wide** | Linear with cross-features | Memorisation | Hot common patterns | Long tail | | **Deep** | MLP over embeddings | Generalisation | Long tail / cold | Over-smooths hot | ## When to use which loss / model | Goal | Use | |---|---| | Calibrated CTR probability | NCF + binary cross-entropy | | Pure top-K ranking | Any model + **BPR loss** | | Cold-item handling | Wide & Deep (embeddings via deep half) | | Mostly hot common patterns | Wide & Deep (cross-features in wide half) | | Recommend with side features | Wide & Deep, FM, DeepFM | ## Common traps - **"BPR is a model"** — false. **BPR is a LOSS** that plugs into any model producing $\hat r_{ui}$. - **"Pointwise loss is fine for implicit feedback"** — false. **Treats missing positives as negatives** → suppresses items the user might like. - **"NCF GMF is just MF"** — false. **GMF generalises MF** with learned output weights and non-linear activation. Special case (identity + ones) recovers MF. - **"Wide & Deep is an ensemble"** — false. **Jointly trained** — each half compensates for the other's errors during gradient steps, not stacked post-hoc. - **"Wide handles cold start"** — false. The cross-features in the wide half are zero for unseen combinations. **Deep handles cold start** via embeddings. - **"BPR uses sigmoid as activation"** — kind of. The **likelihood** is $\sigma$ of the score difference; the model itself can be anything. - **"Pairwise = listwise"** — false. **Pairwise** = pairs (BPR). **Listwise** = full list (LambdaMART, ListNet). Pairwise is simpler and usually enough. ## Diagram (Mermaid) — NeuMF ```mermaid flowchart LR U[User one-hot] --> EG[Embed: GMF] U --> EM1[Embed: MLP] I[Item one-hot] --> EG2[Embed: GMF] I --> EM2[Embed: MLP] EG --> Mul["⊙"] EG2 --> Mul EM1 --> Concat["concat"] EM2 --> Concat Concat --> H[MLP layers] Mul --> Final["Concat
w/ MLP last hidden"] H --> Final Final --> Out["σ → P(Y=1)"] classDef reco fill:#e3f2fd class Mul,Concat,Final reco ``` ## Diagram — Wide & Deep ```mermaid flowchart LR Sparse[Sparse features] --> W["Wide:
x + cross(x)"] Sparse --> D["Deep: embed → MLP"] W --> Add[+] D --> Add Add --> Out["σ → P(click)"] classDef wide fill:#e3f2fd classDef deep fill:#f3e5f5 class W wide class D deep ```

# Cluster 07 — Graph Neural Networks for recommendation

GNNs operate on the user-item bipartite graph, surfacing **high-order connectivity** that flat MF can't reach. The trajectory: NGCF (full propagation) → LightGCN (strip out trainable layers; outperforms NGCF) → knowledge-graph extensions like DKN.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-12-graph-neural-networks]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/graph-neural-networks]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/ngcf]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/lightgcn]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/knowledge-graph-reco]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]] (used as the loss)

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-12]]

## What this cluster is about

**GNN basics.** Neural networks for graph-structured data. Three steps per layer: **message** (each neighbour sends its embedding) → **aggregate** (combine via SUM / MEAN / MAX) → **update** (apply NN to compute new embedding). $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})$. After $K$ layers, each node sees its **K-hop neighbourhood**. Linear $O(N + E)$ complexity, supports multiple edge types, generalises to new nodes (with inductive variants).

**Why GNNs fit reco.** The user-item interaction matrix *is* a bipartite graph. Standard CF captures user-user, item-item, and user-item similarity. GNNs additionally capture **high-order connectivity** — the multi-hop chain "recommend $i_4$ to $u_2$ because $u_2$ liked $i_3$, $u_3$ also liked $i_3$, and $u_3$ liked $i_4$" — invisible to flat MF.

**NGCF** (Wang et al. SIGIR 2019). Defines an embedding-propagation layer:

$$\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}\Big(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \big(\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)\big)\Big)$$

Stack $K$ layers, concatenate all layer outputs as the final user embedding, dot product for prediction, BPR loss.

**LightGCN** (He et al. 2020). Strips out the trainable $\mathbf W$ matrices and the LeakyReLU. **Only the original embeddings are trainable.** Update simplifies to $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$. Final embedding = weighted *sum* (not concat) of layer outputs. **LightGCN outperforms NGCF** on benchmarks → the trainable matrices and non-linearity were *hurting*, not helping. The headline lesson: **in CF on bipartite graphs, propagation alone is the signal**.

**Knowledge-graph extension (DKN).** Wang et al. WWW 2018. News CTR model that combines a knowledge graph (entity embeddings), attention over the user's clicked-article history, and a knowledge-aware CNN over text. Use cases: news, fashion, food, gaming.

The high-leverage MCQ insight: **simpler beats more complex** in this cluster. LightGCN strips out three things from NGCF and is strictly better.

tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-07]
cluster: 07
---

# Cluster 07 — Atoms (GNNs for reco)

## GNN basics

1. **Definition.** Neural networks designed for **graph-structured data** [[graph-neural-networks]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=1).
2. **Properties.** Leverage locality; multiple edge types; **computationally efficient $O(N + E)$**; inductive (generalise to new nodes).
3. **Use cases.** Drug discovery (e.g. Halicin antibiotic), traffic ETA estimation, social-network friend recommendation [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=2).

## Message passing

4. **Three steps per layer.** **Message** (each neighbour sends embedding) → **aggregate** (combine via SUM/MEAN/MAX) → **update** (NN computes new embedding) [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=3).
5. **Mathematical form.** $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})$ [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=4).
6. **K-hop coverage.** After $K$ layers, each node captures K-hop neighbourhood info.

## Why GNNs fit reco

7. **High-order connectivity.** Standard CF sees user-user, item-item, user-item. GNNs surface **multi-hop chains** (user-item-user-item) [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=6).
8. **Example.** Recommend $i_4$ to $u_2$ because $u_2$ liked $i_3$, $u_3$ also liked $i_3$, $u_3$ liked $i_4$ — captured by 3 layers, invisible to flat MF.
9. **Embedding propagation principle.** "Items are user features; users are item features." Interacted items provide direct evidence of user preference; users that consume an item measure item-item similarity [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=7).
10. **Data sparsity addressed.** Graph connections fill in for missing entries [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=8).

## NGCF

11. **Architecture.** Stack $K$ embedding-propagation layers over the user-item bipartite graph [[ngcf]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=10).
12. **Author / paper.** Wang et al., SIGIR 2019 — *Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering*.
13. **Propagation rule.** $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} (\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)))$ [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=11).
14. **Components.** Trainable $\mathbf W_1, \mathbf W_2$; symmetric degree normalisation; LeakyReLU.
15. **Final user embedding.** Concatenation of all layer outputs $\mathbf e^{(0)}_u \,\|\, \mathbf e^{(1)}_u \,\|\, \cdots \,\|\, \mathbf e^{(K)}_u$.
16. **Prediction.** Dot product of user and item final embeddings.
17. **Loss.** [[bpr-loss|BPR]].

## LightGCN

18. **Architecture.** Simplified NGCF — **no $\mathbf W$ matrices, no LeakyReLU** [[lightgcn]] — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=12).
19. **Author / paper.** He et al. 2020 — *LightGCN: Simplifying and Powering Graph Convolution Network for Recommendation*.
20. **Trainable params.** **Only the original embeddings $\mathbf e^{(0)}$**.
21. **Propagation rule.** $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=13).
22. **Final embedding.** **Weighted sum** of layer outputs (not concatenation).
23. **Prediction.** Dot product. Loss = [[bpr-loss|BPR]] [slide 14](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=14).
24. **Headline result.** **LightGCN outperforms NGCF** — trainable matrices and non-linearity were hurting, not helping.
25. **Lesson.** In CF on bipartite graphs, **propagation alone is the signal**.

## Knowledge graphs

26. **Definition.** A set of relationships between entities [[knowledge-graph-reco]] — [slide 15](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=15).
27. **Use cases.** News, fashion, food, gaming.

## DKN

28. **DKN.** *Deep Knowledge-aware Network* (Wang et al., WWW 2018) — news CTR prediction [slide 16](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=16).
29. **Components.** Knowledge graph (entity embeddings) + attention over user history + convolutions for text.
30. **Joint learning.** Of semantic-level and knowledge-level representations.

## Limitations

31. **Over-smoothing.** Stacking too many GNN layers makes all embeddings converge. Practical depth: 2-4 layers.
32. **Transductive.** LightGCN/NGCF need to know the full graph at training time. For new nodes, use GraphSAGE-style inductive aggregation.

19. **Author / paper.** He et al. 2020 — *LightGCN: Simplifying and Powering Graph Convolution Network for Recommendation*.

21. **Propagation rule.** $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2012.pdf#page=13).

---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-07]
cluster: 07
---

# Cluster 07 — Cheat sheet (GNNs for reco)

## Definitions

| Term | One-liner |
|---|---|
| **GNN** | Neural network for graph-structured data |
| **Message passing** | Per-layer: message → aggregate → update |
| **K-hop neighborhood** | After K layers, each node sees nodes K steps away |
| **High-order connectivity** | Multi-hop chains in the user-item graph (CF can't see) |
| **NGCF** | Stacked propagation layers with trainable W and LeakyReLU |
| **LightGCN** | NGCF stripped of W and LeakyReLU; outperforms NGCF |
| **Over-smoothing** | Deep GNNs make all embeddings converge |
| **DKN** | News reco combining KG + attention + CNN |

## Key formulas

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **GNN layer (general)** | $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})$ |
| **NGCF propagation** | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} (\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)))$ |
| **LightGCN propagation** | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ |
| **GNN complexity** | $O(N + E)$ per layer |
| **Loss (NGCF + LightGCN)** | BPR pairwise loss |

## NGCF vs LightGCN

| | NGCF | LightGCN |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation has $\mathbf W$? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Non-linearity (LeakyReLU)? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Final embedding | Concatenation | **Weighted sum** |
| Trainable parameters | Embeddings + W matrices | **Only embeddings** |
| Quality | Weaker | **Stronger** |

## Three steps of message passing

| Step | What it does |
|---|---|
| 1. **Message** | Each neighbour sends its current embedding |
| 2. **Aggregate** | Combine via SUM / MEAN / MAX |
| 3. **Update** | Apply a neural network to compute the new node embedding |

## When to use what

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| CF baseline | Matrix factorization (Cluster 05) |
| CF with high-order signal | LightGCN |
| Inductive (new nodes at inference) | GraphSAGE-style GNN |
| News / fashion / domain reco | DKN-style with knowledge graph |

## Common traps

- **"GNN complexity is O(N²)"** — false. **$O(N + E)$** — linear in nodes plus edges.
- **"NGCF is better than LightGCN because it has more parameters"** — false. **LightGCN outperforms NGCF.** Stripping the trainable matrices and non-linearity helps, not hurts.
- **"GNNs need lots of layers for performance"** — false. **2-4 layers**; deeper causes over-smoothing.
- **"The trainable W in NGCF is essential"** — false. LightGCN proves it isn't.
- **"GNNs are like CNNs for graphs"** — partially. They do share message-passing intuition, but GNNs handle arbitrary connectivity (CNNs are fixed grid).
- **"DKN is a pure GNN"** — false. DKN combines a knowledge graph + attention + CNN.

## Mental model — high-order connectivity (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u2["u_2"] --> i3["i_3"]
    u3["u_3"] --> i3
    u3 --> i4["i_4"]
    i4 -.->|"recommend via 3-hop chain"| u2
    classDef user fill:#e3f2fd
    classDef item fill:#fff3e0
    class u2,u3 user
    class i3,i4 item
```

| Term | One-liner |

| **Message passing** | Per-layer: message → aggregate → update |

|---|---|
| **GNN layer (general)** | $\mathbf h'_v = f_\theta(\mathbf h_v, \text{AGG}\{\mathbf h_u : u \in \mathcal N_v\})$ |
| **NGCF propagation** | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \text{LeakyReLU}(\sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} (\mathbf W_1 \mathbf e^{(l)}_i + \mathbf W_2 (\mathbf e^{(l)}_u \odot \mathbf e^{(l)}_i)))$ |
| **LightGCN propagation** | $\mathbf e^{(l+1)}_u = \sum_{i \in \mathcal N_u} \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\mathcal N_u||\mathcal N_i|}} \mathbf e^{(l)}_i$ |
| **GNN complexity** | $O(N + E)$ per layer |
| **Loss (NGCF + LightGCN)** | BPR pairwise loss |

## NGCF vs LightGCN

| | NGCF | LightGCN |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation has $\mathbf W$? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Non-linearity (LeakyReLU)? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Final embedding | Concatenation | **Weighted sum** |
| Trainable parameters | Embeddings + W matrices | **Only embeddings** |
| Quality | Weaker | **Stronger** |

## Three steps of message passing

| Step | What it does |
|---|---|
| 1. **Message** | Each neighbour sends its current embedding |
| 2. **Aggregate** | Combine via SUM / MEAN / MAX |
| 3. **Update** | Apply a neural network to compute the new node embedding |

## When to use what

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| CF baseline | Matrix factorization (Cluster 05) |
| CF with high-order signal | LightGCN |
| Inductive (new nodes at inference) | GraphSAGE-style GNN |
| News / fashion / domain reco | DKN-style with knowledge graph |

## Common traps

- **"GNN complexity is O(N²)"** — false. **$O(N + E)$** — linear in nodes plus edges.
- **"NGCF is better than LightGCN because it has more parameters"** — false. **LightGCN outperforms NGCF.** Stripping the trainable matrices and non-linearity helps, not hurts.
- **"GNNs need lots of layers for performance"** — false. **2-4 layers**; deeper causes over-smoothing.
- **"The trainable W in NGCF is essential"** — false. LightGCN proves it isn't.
- **"GNNs are like CNNs for graphs"** — partially. They do share message-passing intuition, but GNNs handle arbitrary connectivity (CNNs are fixed grid).
- **"DKN is a pure GNN"** — false. DKN combines a knowledge graph + attention + CNN.

## Mental model — high-order connectivity (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u2["u_2"] --> i3["i_3"]
    u3["u_3"] --> i3
    u3 --> i4["i_4"]
    i4 -.->|"recommend via 3-hop chain"| u2
    classDef user fill:#e3f2fd
    classDef item fill:#fff3e0
    class u2,u3 user
    class i3,i4 item
```

# Cluster 08 — Sequential and session-based recommenders

Single-deck cluster but introduces a distinct paradigm — modelling **interactions in time** rather than as a static matrix. Three architectures (RNN, CNN, Transformer) applied to the next-item-prediction task.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-11-sequential-session-based]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/sequential-vs-session-based]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/gru4rec]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/sequential-cnn-reco]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]] (used by SASRec, BERT4Rec)
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-11]]

## What this cluster is about

The static R matrix throws away **time order** — but recommendations often need to respect order ("user just clicked into a category, what next?"). Two related paradigms:

- **Sequential recommendation** — user has identity. Combine **historical preferences** + **recent actions**. Examples: Markov chains, RNNs, CNNs, Transformers.
- **Session-based recommendation** — **no user identity**. Each session is handled independently. Examples: neighborhood methods, LSTMs, Transformers.

The data is laid out as **session-parallel mini-batches**: each batch row carries one session in time. Sessions are independent — when one ends, the next available session takes its slot, and the **hidden state for that row is reset** so behaviour doesn't leak.

Four architectures, in roughly historical order:

**GRU4Rec** (Hidasi et al., ICLR 2016). The first major neural session-based recommender. GRU network with one-hot item input → embedding → GRU stack → softmax over items. Items earlier in the session are weighted with a discount. Trained with TOP1 or BPR loss.

**LSTM-based.** LSTMs handle longer sequences but are limited by sequence length. Three useful tweaks: time-aware gating (varies with time intervals), attention (filter irrelevant items), MF hybrid (long-term via MF + short-term via LSTM).

**Caser** (Tang & Wang, WSDM 2018). **Embed the previous L items as a 2D image** in (time, latent) space. Apply vertical convolutions (single-item influence) and horizontal convolutions (groups of items). Predict the next $T$ items from previous $L$ actions.

**NextItNet** (Yuan et al., WSDM 2019). Uses **dilated convolutions** to model long-range dependencies — each layer doubles the receptive field by inserting zeros in the filter. Captures multi-scale features without parameter explosion.

**Transformer-based** (SASRec, SSE-PT, BERT4Rec). Same lesson as NLP: attention beats RNN/CNN at sequence modelling. Self-attention focuses on items relevant to the next action. Often combined with BPR loss.

The high-leverage MCQ insight: **sequential = with user identity; session-based = without**. The architectures (RNN/CNN/Transformer) overlap.

/Users/mattporteous/Obsidian/School/40-Reviews/Chatbots/practice-mocks/AI & Recommender Systems Quiz.md


we have a mock now!


the questions are all mcq and seem very high level what do you think

Read the mock. Quick assessment, then I'll recalibrate the blueprint, style guide, and finish the clusters.

## My take

**Yes — substantially higher-level than I assumed.** The exam is 19 pure-definitional MCQs. No computations, no equations to derive, no "swap two terms" traps. Most distractors are made-up names or obviously-wrong concepts.

**Topic coverage (vs my blueprint estimate):**

| What I over-weighted | Reality |
|---|---|
| **Lecture 16 (advanced LLM techniques)** — I ranked #1 priority | **Not tested at all.** No MoE, KV cache, RoPE, MLA, LoRA, quantization, DPO, GRPO, RLVR. |
| **Lecture 08 (Decision Trees / GBDT)** — I ranked #3 | **Not tested.** No Gini, Entropy, level vs leaf, XGBoost vs LightGBM. |
| **Lecture 12 (GNNs)** — sizable cluster | **Not tested.** No NGCF, LightGCN, message passing. |
| **Lecture 11 (Sequential)** — sizable | **Not tested.** No GRU4Rec, Caser, NextItNet. |
| **Lecture 07 (FM/FFM)** | **Not tested.** |
| **Lecture 06 detail (MF equations, SAR scoring math)** | Only ALS is tested as a name (Q2). No equations. |

**What's actually tested heavily:**

- **Transformers / attention / NLP** — 9 of 19 questions (~47%): Q1, Q4, Q6, Q8, Q10, Q11, Q14, Q15, Q18.
- **Reco fundamentals** (terminology, splits, metrics, architectures) — Q2, Q3, Q5, Q7, Q9, Q13, Q16, Q17, Q19.
- One "deep learning advantage" question (Q12).

**Distractor style:** mostly soft. Q4 distractors are made-up names ("Textual Attention", "Location-Based Attention"). Q11 lists RNN/LSTM/CNN vs GPT — only one is decoder-based. Even the trickier ones (Q9, Q16) don't punish careful reading — they punish vague reading of the lecture.

**One flagged question — Q9.** The answer key says "Real-time architecture for single-digit request time", but real-time means *score at request time* (slower) while batch means *precompute and cache* (lookup is fast). The lecture might frame "real-time" as "low-latency response" regardless of compute model — but worth verifying with the prof. I'll note this in the style guide.

**Recalibrated priorities:**

1. **Cluster 02 (Transformer & attention)** — was #2, now **#1**. Half the exam.
2. **Cluster 09 (BERT/GPT/Transfer Learning)** — was #5, now **#2**. 3+ direct questions.
3. **Cluster 04 (Reco data + metrics)** — was #4, now **#3**. 6+ direct questions.
4. **Cluster 06 (NCF/W&D/BPR)** — only BPR appears (Q7); NCF/W&D might still be tested in a real exam.
5. **Cluster 10 (Chatbot evolution + RLHF)** — Q15 (RLHF) and Q18 (seq2seq for chatbots).
6. **Cluster 01 (modern LLM)** — was #1. **Now low priority** unless prof adds questions later.
7. **Clusters 03, 05, 07, 08** — basically untested in this mock.

Now executing: save the mock as canonical files, rewrite blueprint + style guide, finish remaining clusters compactly.

# Chatbots — Mock 1 (questions only)

19 MCQ. Single-select. Take closed-book. **Mark your answers on a separate sheet, then check against [[mock-1-answer-key|the answer key]].**

Original source: [[AI & Recommender Systems Quiz]]

---

## 1. What is the effect of self-attention in modern chatbots?

- A. It computes the probability of the next token
- B. It improves the optimization procedure
- C. It computes the output tokens that the model is attending
- D. It computes a weighted linear combination of word embeddings

---

## 2. Which method can be used to update the parameters in a collaborative filtering model during training?

- A. Attention mechanism
- B. Gradient boosting decision trees
- C. Alternating Least Squares
- D. Randomized Singular Value Decomposition

---

## 3. Why is a stratified dataset split useful in a recommendation system?

- A. It balances the rating distribution across different items in the dataset
- B. It randomizes the items in both training and testing datasets
- C. It is faster to compute compared to chronological split
- D. It ensures the same set of users or items appear in both training and testing datasets

---

## 4. Which type of attention mechanism is used in BERT and GPT models?

- A. Textual Attention
- B. Multi-Head Attention
- C. Content-Based Attention
- D. Location-Based Attention

---

## 5. Which algorithm is used to address the cold-start problem in recommendation systems?

- A. Content-based filtering
- B. Collaborative filtering
- C. Matrix factorization
- D. Association rule mining

---

## 6. What is the main innovation introduced by the Transformer architecture?

- A. Self-attention mechanism
- B. Convolutional layers
- C. Recurrent connections
- D. Max pooling

---

## 7. Which algorithm is specifically designed to generate recommendations without negative feedback?

- A. Item-based collaborative filtering
- B. Bayesian personalized ranking
- C. Gradient boosting decision trees
- D. Support vector machines

---

## 8. Which training objective is used in BERT to learn contextual word representations?

- A. Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
- B. Cross-Entropy Loss
- C. Reinforcement Learning
- D. Binary Classification

---

## 9. What architecture is best suited for deploying a recommendation system with single-digit request time?

- A. Real-time architecture
- B. Hybrid architecture
- C. Batch architecture
- D. 2-step recommender architecture

---

## 10. What is the primary advantage of the Transformer architecture in NLP tasks?

- A. Ability to capture long-range dependencies
- B. Faster training time
- C. Lower memory requirements
- D. Improved interpretability

---

## 11. Which language model introduced the concept of "Transformer Decoder" and is widely used for text generation?

- A. LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)
- B. CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)
- C. RNN (Recurrent Neural Network)
- D. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)

---

## 12. What is the main advantage of using deep learning models for recommendation systems?

- A. Better handling of the cold-start problem
- B. Lower computational requirements
- C. Ability to encode high-order relationships
- D. Improved interpretability

---

## 13. Which evaluation metric is specifically designed to measure the diversity of recommendations?

- A. Jaccard
- B. Mean Average Precision (MAP)
- C. Novelty
- D. Precision at K

---

## 14. Which two phases are used to train modern Large Language Models?

- A. Transfer learning and Reinforcement Learning
- B. Pre-training and fine-tuning
- C. Supervised learning and unsupervised learning
- D. Reinforcement learning and fine-tuning

---

## 15. How is ChatGPT trained to generate controlled responses in a conversational setting?

- A. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
- B. Unsupervised learning on large text corpora
- C. Adversarial training with GANs
- D. Transfer learning from pretrained language models

---

## 16. In collaborative filtering, what is the purpose of the user-item matrix?

- A. To store user preferences
- B. To compute item similarities
- C. To calculate user similarities
- D. To generate recommendations based on item attributes

---

## 17. What is the difference between implicit and explicit feedback?

- A. Explicit feedback refers to actions like ratings/reviews; implicit feedback refers to behaviors like browsing
- B. Implicit feedback is directly provided; explicit is inferred
- C. Explicit is survey-based; implicit is item-based
- D. Explicit is more abundant; implicit is less

---

## 18. Which approach is commonly used for building modern chatbots?

- A. Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) models
- B. Reinforcement learning
- C. Template matching and rules
- D. Generative adversarial networks

---

## 19. Which evaluation metric is commonly used to measure ranking quality in recommendation systems?

- A. Cross-entropy loss
- B. AUC-ROC
- C. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)
- D. Mean Squared Error (MSE)

---
tags: [practice-mock, answer-key, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
mock: 1
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Chatbots — Mock 1 (answer key + worked explanations)

19 MCQ. Each ≈ equal weight. Source: [[AI & Recommender Systems Quiz]] · Questions: [[mock-1-questions]]

## Score sheet

| Q | Answer | Topic | Lecture | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **D** | Self-attention output | 13 | 1 |
| 2 | **C** | ALS for CF parameter updates | 06 | 2 |
| 3 | **D** | Stratified split purpose | 02 | 1 |
| 4 | **B** | Multi-Head Attention in BERT/GPT | 13 | 1 |
| 5 | **A** | Content-based filtering for cold start | 03 | 1 |
| 6 | **A** | Self-attention as Transformer innovation | 13 | 1 |
| 7 | **B** | BPR for implicit / no-negative feedback | 09 | 2 |
| 8 | **A** | MLM for BERT pretraining | 14 | 2 |
| 9 | **A** | Real-time architecture for low-latency request | 04 | 3 ⚠️ |
| 10 | **A** | Long-range dependencies via Transformer | 13 | 2 |
| 11 | **D** | GPT introduced Transformer decoder | 14 | 1 |
| 12 | **C** | Deep learning encodes high-order relationships | 10a–12 | 2 |
| 13 | **C** | Novelty as diversity metric | 04 | 2 |
| 14 | **B** | Pretraining + finetuning for LLMs | 14 | 1 |
| 15 | **A** | RLHF for ChatGPT | 14b/15 | 1 |
| 16 | **A** | User-item matrix stores user preferences | 02/03 | 2 ⚠️ |
| 17 | **A** | Explicit = ratings; implicit = behavior | 02 | 1 |
| 18 | **A** | Seq2Seq for modern chatbots | 13 | 1 |
| 19 | **C** | NDCG for ranking quality | 04 | 1 |

## Worked answers

### Q1 — Self-attention effect — D

Self-attention computes a **weighted average** of the value vectors at all positions, where the weights come from softmax over query-key dot products. The lecture phrases it as "compute a weighted average of each embedding". **D** matches this verbatim.

- A is the *output layer's* job (softmax over vocabulary), not self-attention.
- B is incidental at best.
- C is gibberish ("output tokens that the model is attending").

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]]

### Q2 — Parameter update method for CF — C

**Alternating Least Squares (ALS)** is the canonical model-based CF training algorithm (alongside SGD) covered in lecture 06. ALS holds $P$ fixed, solves for $Q$ in closed form, alternates.

- A is for transformers, not CF training.
- B is a different model class (GBDT).
- D — Randomized SVD computes a decomposition but isn't an iterative training method for CF in the lecture's framing.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/als-recommender]]

### Q3 — Stratified split purpose — D

**Stratified split ensures the same set of users (or items) appear in both training and testing**, avoiding artificial cold users in the test set. Lecture 02 framing.

- A confuses stratification with class balancing.
- B is what *random* split does.
- C is unrelated.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-splits]]

### Q4 — Attention type in BERT/GPT — B

Both BERT and GPT use **Multi-Head Attention** as the core mixing operation. Lecture 13 introduces MHA explicitly.

- A and D are made-up names.
- C ("Content-Based Attention") is a real concept in older attention literature but not the BERT/GPT mechanism.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]]

### Q5 — Cold-start fix — A

**Content-based filtering** is the textbook cold-start fix — a brand-new item already has its features even though it has no interactions. Lecture 03 makes this explicit.

- B is what *suffers* from cold start.
- C and D are unrelated to cold start.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/content-based-filtering]]

### Q6 — Transformer's main innovation — A

The Transformer (Vaswani 2017) was the **first architecture based solely on attention** — no recurrence, no convolution. Lecture 13 verbatim.

- B (convolutions) and C (recurrent) describe what Transformers *replaced*.
- D (max pooling) is for CNNs.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]]

### Q7 — Algorithm for no-negative-feedback — B

**Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR)** addresses the implicit-feedback negative-sampling problem with a pairwise ranking loss. From lecture 09: "addresses the problem of distinguishing between negative feedback and not interacted items".

- A and C/D aren't designed for this specific problem.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]]

### Q8 — BERT training objective — A

**Masked Language Modeling** — mask 15% of tokens and predict them. Lecture 14 covers this in detail.

- B is the loss function family used for MLM, but the *objective* is MLM.
- C is RLHF (different paradigm).
- D is too generic.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/masked-language-model]]

### Q9 — Architecture for single-digit request time — A ⚠️

**Flagged: ambiguous question/answer.** The answer key says **A (Real-time)**, but standard interpretation: real-time means scoring at request time (slower per request). **Batch** with cached lookups is what gives sub-millisecond response in most production systems.

The most charitable reading: the lecture frames "real-time architecture" as the architecture *type* used when low-latency response is required (i.e. when freshness matters more than batch precompute). In that framing, A is correct.

If the prof asks this on the real exam, write A and add a one-line clarification in the Objections section. Pre-empt the trap by re-reading the architectures lecture verbatim before exam day.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-architectures]]

### Q10 — Transformer's primary advantage — A

**Long-range dependencies** — self-attention can connect any two tokens directly, regardless of distance. Lecture 13: "Self-attention enables the Transformer model to capture dependencies between all positions in the sequence … long-term dependencies".

- B (faster training) is partially true but not the headline advantage.
- C (lower memory) is false — attention is $O(n^2)$ memory.
- D (interpretability) is debatable and not the lecture's framing.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]]

### Q11 — Decoder-based language model — D

**GPT** = decoder-only transformer for text generation. The other options are pre-Transformer architectures.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/gpt-models]]

### Q12 — Deep learning advantage for reco — C

**Encoding high-order relationships** — deep models capture non-linear and multi-hop interactions that linear MF cannot. Connects to lectures 10a (NCF), 10b (W&D), 12 (GNNs).

- A is false — DL doesn't fix cold start (CB does).
- B is false — DL is more expensive.
- D is false — DL is *less* interpretable.

### Q13 — Diversity metric — C

**Novelty** measures how rare recommended items are — explicitly a diversity metric. Lecture 04.

- A is a similarity measure, not a diversity metric.
- B and D are accuracy metrics.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-diversity-metrics]]

### Q14 — Two-phase LLM training — B

**Pre-training + Fine-tuning** is the canonical paradigm. Lecture 14 explicit.

- A and D include reinforcement learning, which is a *post-training* alignment step (RLHF), not the two main phases.
- C describes types, not phases.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/transfer-learning]]

### Q15 — ChatGPT training for control — A

**RLHF** — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Lecture 14b explicit: "ChatGPT was implemented as a pretrained GPT-3.5 model finetuned with RLHF".

- B describes pretraining (not the *control* part).
- C is unrelated to ChatGPT.
- D is too generic.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/rlhf]]

### Q16 — User-item matrix purpose — A ⚠️

**Stores user preferences** — the most fundamental purpose. The matrix's *uses* include B (item similarities) and C (user similarities), but the *primary* purpose is storage.

⚠️ **Trap:** B and C are also defensible answers if you interpret "purpose" as "what it enables". Pick A as the most foundational reading.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-terminology]]

### Q17 — Implicit vs explicit feedback — A

**Explicit = ratings/reviews; implicit = behaviors like browsing.** Lecture 02 verbatim split.

- B inverts the meaning.
- C and D are wrong characterisations.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]

### Q18 — Modern chatbot approach — A

**Seq2Seq (encoder-decoder transformer) models.** Lecture 13 covers seq2seq as one of the three NLP architectural families and notes "Vanilla Transformer" as an example. Modern chatbots include both seq2seq (T5-style) and decoder-only (GPT-style) — but the lecture's chatbot examples lean seq2seq/transformer.

- B (RL) is alignment, not architecture.
- C (template matching) is the *old* paradigm (ELIZA, ALICE).
- D (GANs) — irrelevant.

### Q19 — Ranking quality metric — C

**NDCG** — Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain. Accounts for both *hit* and *correctness of ranking* (position-discounted). Lecture 04 explicit.

- A is a loss function, not an evaluation metric.
- B (AUC-ROC) is a classification metric.
- D (MSE) is a regression metric.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-ranking-metrics]]

## How to grade yourself

Out of 19, no penalty for wrong answers (assumed). Aim:

- **17-19 correct** = solid grasp; spend final hours drilling the flagged questions (Q9, Q16) and weak topics.
- **14-16 correct** = identify which lectures you missed; re-read those.
- **<14 correct** = re-read clusters 02 (transformer), 04 (reco metrics), and 09 (BERT/GPT) before next attempt.

## Source-of-truth reminders

- The mock weighs Transformer/NLP heavily (~half the questions).
- Lectures 06 (MF detail), 07 (FM), 08 (Decision Trees), 11 (Sequential), 12 (GNNs), 14b (LLM memory), 16 (Advanced LLM techniques) are **NOT directly tested** in this mock. Don't over-invest in them unless the exam expands.
- Distractors are mostly soft. **Pick the lecture-canonical answer**, not the one that sounds most generic.

# Chatbots — Mock 1 (answer key + worked explanations)

19 MCQ. Each ≈ equal weight. Source: [[AI & Recommender Systems Quiz]] · Questions: [[mock-1-questions]]

## Score sheet

| Q | Answer | Topic | Lecture | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **D** | Self-attention output | 13 | 1 |
| 2 | **C** | ALS for CF parameter updates | 06 | 2 |
| 3 | **D** | Stratified split purpose | 02 | 1 |
| 4 | **B** | Multi-Head Attention in BERT/GPT | 13 | 1 |
| 5 | **A** | Content-based filtering for cold start | 03 | 1 |
| 6 | **A** | Self-attention as Transformer innovation | 13 | 1 |
| 7 | **B** | BPR for implicit / no-negative feedback | 09 | 2 |
| 8 | **A** | MLM for BERT pretraining | 14 | 2 |
| 9 | **A** | Real-time architecture for low-latency request | 04 | 3 ⚠️ |
| 10 | **A** | Long-range dependencies via Transformer | 13 | 2 |
| 11 | **D** | GPT introduced Transformer decoder | 14 | 1 |
| 12 | **C** | Deep learning encodes high-order relationships | 10a–12 | 2 |
| 13 | **C** | Novelty as diversity metric | 04 | 2 |
| 14 | **B** | Pretraining + finetuning for LLMs | 14 | 1 |
| 15 | **A** | RLHF for ChatGPT | 14b/15 | 1 |
| 16 | **A** | User-item matrix stores user preferences | 02/03 | 2 ⚠️ |
| 17 | **A** | Explicit = ratings; implicit = behavior | 02 | 1 |
| 18 | **A** | Seq2Seq for modern chatbots | 13 | 1 |
| 19 | **C** | NDCG for ranking quality | 04 | 1 |

## Worked answers

### Q1 — Self-attention effect — D

Self-attention computes a **weighted average** of the value vectors at all positions, where the weights come from softmax over query-key dot products. The lecture phrases it as "compute a weighted average of each embedding". **D** matches this verbatim.

- A is the *output layer's* job (softmax over vocabulary), not self-attention.
- B is incidental at best.
- C is gibberish ("output tokens that the model is attending").

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]]

### Q2 — Parameter update method for CF — C

**Alternating Least Squares (ALS)** is the canonical model-based CF training algorithm (alongside SGD) covered in lecture 06. ALS holds $P$ fixed, solves for $Q$ in closed form, alternates.

- A is for transformers, not CF training.
- B is a different model class (GBDT).
- D — Randomized SVD computes a decomposition but isn't an iterative training method for CF in the lecture's framing.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/als-recommender]]

### Q3 — Stratified split purpose — D

**Stratified split ensures the same set of users (or items) appear in both training and testing**, avoiding artificial cold users in the test set. Lecture 02 framing.

- A confuses stratification with class balancing.
- B is what *random* split does.
- C is unrelated.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-splits]]

### Q4 — Attention type in BERT/GPT — B

Both BERT and GPT use **Multi-Head Attention** as the core mixing operation. Lecture 13 introduces MHA explicitly.

- A and D are made-up names.
- C ("Content-Based Attention") is a real concept in older attention literature but not the BERT/GPT mechanism.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]]

### Q5 — Cold-start fix — A

**Content-based filtering** is the textbook cold-start fix — a brand-new item already has its features even though it has no interactions. Lecture 03 makes this explicit.

- B is what *suffers* from cold start.
- C and D are unrelated to cold start.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/content-based-filtering]]

### Q6 — Transformer's main innovation — A

The Transformer (Vaswani 2017) was the **first architecture based solely on attention** — no recurrence, no convolution. Lecture 13 verbatim.

- B (convolutions) and C (recurrent) describe what Transformers *replaced*.
- D (max pooling) is for CNNs.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]]

### Q7 — Algorithm for no-negative-feedback — B

**Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR)** addresses the implicit-feedback negative-sampling problem with a pairwise ranking loss. From lecture 09: "addresses the problem of distinguishing between negative feedback and not interacted items".

- A and C/D aren't designed for this specific problem.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]]

### Q8 — BERT training objective — A

**Masked Language Modeling** — mask 15% of tokens and predict them. Lecture 14 covers this in detail.

- B is the loss function family used for MLM, but the *objective* is MLM.
- C is RLHF (different paradigm).
- D is too generic.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/masked-language-model]]

### Q9 — Architecture for single-digit request time — A ⚠️

**Flagged: ambiguous question/answer.** The answer key says **A (Real-time)**, but standard interpretation: real-time means scoring at request time (slower per request). **Batch** with cached lookups is what gives sub-millisecond response in most production systems.

The most charitable reading: the lecture frames "real-time architecture" as the architecture *type* used when low-latency response is required (i.e. when freshness matters more than batch precompute). In that framing, A is correct.

If the prof asks this on the real exam, write A and add a one-line clarification in the Objections section. Pre-empt the trap by re-reading the architectures lecture verbatim before exam day.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-architectures]]

### Q10 — Transformer's primary advantage — A

**Long-range dependencies** — self-attention can connect any two tokens directly, regardless of distance. Lecture 13: "Self-attention enables the Transformer model to capture dependencies between all positions in the sequence … long-term dependencies".

- B (faster training) is partially true but not the headline advantage.
- C (lower memory) is false — attention is $O(n^2)$ memory.
- D (interpretability) is debatable and not the lecture's framing.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]]

### Q11 — Decoder-based language model — D

**GPT** = decoder-only transformer for text generation. The other options are pre-Transformer architectures.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/gpt-models]]

### Q12 — Deep learning advantage for reco — C

**Encoding high-order relationships** — deep models capture non-linear and multi-hop interactions that linear MF cannot. Connects to lectures 10a (NCF), 10b (W&D), 12 (GNNs).

- A is false — DL doesn't fix cold start (CB does).
- B is false — DL is more expensive.
- D is false — DL is *less* interpretable.

### Q13 — Diversity metric — C

**Novelty** measures how rare recommended items are — explicitly a diversity metric. Lecture 04.

- A is a similarity measure, not a diversity metric.
- B and D are accuracy metrics.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-diversity-metrics]]

### Q14 — Two-phase LLM training — B

**Pre-training + Fine-tuning** is the canonical paradigm. Lecture 14 explicit.

- A and D include reinforcement learning, which is a *post-training* alignment step (RLHF), not the two main phases.
- C describes types, not phases.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/transfer-learning]]

### Q15 — ChatGPT training for control — A

**RLHF** — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Lecture 14b explicit: "ChatGPT was implemented as a pretrained GPT-3.5 model finetuned with RLHF".

- B describes pretraining (not the *control* part).
- C is unrelated to ChatGPT.
- D is too generic.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/rlhf]]

### Q16 — User-item matrix purpose — A ⚠️

**Stores user preferences** — the most fundamental purpose. The matrix's *uses* include B (item similarities) and C (user similarities), but the *primary* purpose is storage.

⚠️ **Trap:** B and C are also defensible answers if you interpret "purpose" as "what it enables". Pick A as the most foundational reading.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-terminology]]

### Q17 — Implicit vs explicit feedback — A

**Explicit = ratings/reviews; implicit = behaviors like browsing.** Lecture 02 verbatim split.

- B inverts the meaning.
- C and D are wrong characterisations.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]

### Q18 — Modern chatbot approach — A

**Seq2Seq (encoder-decoder transformer) models.** Lecture 13 covers seq2seq as one of the three NLP architectural families and notes "Vanilla Transformer" as an example. Modern chatbots include both seq2seq (T5-style) and decoder-only (GPT-style) — but the lecture's chatbot examples lean seq2seq/transformer.

- B (RL) is alignment, not architecture.
- C (template matching) is the *old* paradigm (ELIZA, ALICE).
- D (GANs) — irrelevant.

### Q19 — Ranking quality metric — C

**NDCG** — Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain. Accounts for both *hit* and *correctness of ranking* (position-discounted). Lecture 04 explicit.

- A is a loss function, not an evaluation metric.
- B (AUC-ROC) is a classification metric.
- D (MSE) is a regression metric.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-ranking-metrics]]

## How to grade yourself

Out of 19, no penalty for wrong answers (assumed). Aim:

- **17-19 correct** = solid grasp; spend final hours drilling the flagged questions (Q9, Q16) and weak topics.
- **14-16 correct** = identify which lectures you missed; re-read those.
- **<14 correct** = re-read clusters 02 (transformer), 04 (reco metrics), and 09 (BERT/GPT) before next attempt.

## Source-of-truth reminders

- The mock weighs Transformer/NLP heavily (~half the questions).
- Lectures 06 (MF detail), 07 (FM), 08 (Decision Trees), 11 (Sequential), 12 (GNNs), 14b (LLM memory), 16 (Advanced LLM techniques) are **NOT directly tested** in this mock. Don't over-invest in them unless the exam expands.
- Distractors are mostly soft. **Pick the lecture-canonical answer**, not the one that sounds most generic.
