# Chatbots and Recommender Systems — Exam Blueprint

**Format:** MCQ (per [[../../30-Sources/Chatbots/_course|course manifest]]).
**Source mock:** [[practice-mocks/AI & Recommender Systems Quiz]] — 19 questions, single-select, no penalty for wrong (assumed).
**Coverage:** Lectures 02–16 (Miguel Fierro). **The mock heavily under-samples lectures 06 detail, 07, 08, 11, 12, 14b, 16.**

## Per-question table

| Q | Answer | Topic | Lecture | Concept | Type | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **D** | Self-attention output (weighted average of embeddings) | 13 | [[../../20-Concepts/self-attention]] | definition | 1 |
| 2 | **C** | ALS for CF parameter updates | 06 | [[../../20-Concepts/als-recommender]] | definition | 2 |
| 3 | **D** | Stratified split = same users in train + test | 02 | [[../../20-Concepts/reco-data-splits]] | definition | 1 |
| 4 | **B** | Multi-Head Attention in BERT/GPT | 13 | [[../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]] | definition | 1 |
| 5 | **A** | Content-based filtering for cold start | 03 | [[../../20-Concepts/content-based-filtering]] | application | 1 |
| 6 | **A** | Self-attention as Transformer's main innovation | 13 | [[../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]] | definition | 1 |
| 7 | **B** | BPR for implicit / no-negative feedback | 09 | [[../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]] | application | 2 |
| 8 | **A** | MLM as BERT's pretraining objective | 14 | [[../../20-Concepts/masked-language-model]] | definition | 2 |
| 9 | **A** ⚠️ | Real-time architecture for low-latency request | 04 | [[../../20-Concepts/reco-architectures]] | application | 3 |
| 10 | **A** | Long-range dependencies via Transformer | 13 | [[../../20-Concepts/self-attention]] | definition | 2 |
| 11 | **D** | GPT introduced Transformer decoder | 14 | [[../../20-Concepts/gpt-models]] | definition | 1 |
| 12 | **C** | Deep learning encodes high-order relationships | 10a-12 | [[../../20-Concepts/neural-collaborative-filtering]], [[../../20-Concepts/graph-neural-networks]] | comparison | 2 |
| 13 | **C** | Novelty as diversity metric | 04 | [[../../20-Concepts/reco-diversity-metrics]] | definition | 2 |
| 14 | **B** | Pretraining + finetuning as the two LLM phases | 14 | [[../../20-Concepts/transfer-learning]] | definition | 1 |
| 15 | **A** | RLHF for ChatGPT | 14b/15 | [[../../20-Concepts/rlhf]] | definition | 1 |
| 16 | **A** ⚠️ | User-item matrix stores user preferences | 02-03 | [[../../20-Concepts/reco-data-terminology]] | definition | 2 |
| 17 | **A** | Explicit = ratings; implicit = behavior | 02 | [[../../20-Concepts/explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]] | definition | 1 |
| 18 | **A** | Seq2Seq for modern chatbots | 13 | [[../../20-Concepts/transformer-encoder]] | application | 2 |
| 19 | **C** | NDCG for ranking quality | 04 | [[../../20-Concepts/reco-ranking-metrics]] | definition | 1 |

⚠️ Q9 and Q16 are ambiguous — see [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key]] for discussion.

## Topic frequency — by question count

| Rank | Topic cluster | Questions | Lectures |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transformer & attention** (self-attention, MHA, long-range deps, transformer innovation) | **5** (Q1, Q4, Q6, Q10, Q18) | 13 |
| 2 | **BERT, GPT & transfer learning** (MLM, GPT, pretraining/finetuning) | **4** (Q8, Q11, Q14, Q15) | 14, 14b |
| 3 | **Reco metrics & evaluation** (NDCG, novelty, architecture, splits) | **4** (Q3, Q9, Q13, Q19) | 02, 04 |
| 4 | **Reco fundamentals** (CF/CB, cold start, feedback, R matrix) | **4** (Q5, Q12, Q16, Q17) | 02, 03 |
| 5 | **Classical reco algorithms** (ALS, BPR) | **2** (Q2, Q7) | 06, 09 |

**Coverage:** Transformers + BERT/GPT = **9 of 19 ≈ 47%**. Reco fundamentals + metrics = **8 of 19 ≈ 42%**. Classical algorithms (ALS, BPR) = **2 of 19 ≈ 11%**.

**What's NOT in the mock:**

- Lecture 06 detail (MF equations, SAR scoring, similarity metrics taxonomy)
- Lecture 07 (FM, FFM)
- Lecture 08 (Decision Trees, Random Forest, GBDT, Gini, entropy)
- Lecture 10a/10b detail (NCF GMF/MLP equations, Wide&Deep architecture)
- Lecture 11 (Sequential, GRU4Rec, Caser, NextItNet)
- Lecture 12 (GNNs, NGCF, LightGCN, knowledge graphs)
- Lecture 14b (LLM memory strategies)
- Lecture 16 (entire deck — MoE, KV cache, RoPE, Flash Attention, MQA/GQA/MLA, ZeRO, mixed precision, LoRA, quantization, speculative decoding, DPO, GRPO, RLVR)

→ **The mock is significantly more high-level than the lecture content suggests.** This radically changes the priority ranking from my pre-mock estimate.

## Bang-for-buck ranking

### Priority 1 — Transformer & attention (5 questions)

5 of 19. Anchor the cluster with these atoms:

- **Self-attention output = weighted average of embeddings** (Q1, Q10).
- **Transformer's innovation = self-attention** (no recurrence, no convolution) (Q6).
- **Multi-head attention** is what BERT and GPT both use (Q4).
- **Seq2Seq is the modern chatbot architecture** (Q18).

Drill: [[clusters/02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 2 — BERT, GPT, transfer learning (4 questions)

- **MLM is BERT's pretraining objective** (Q8).
- **GPT = decoder-only Transformer for generation** (Q11).
- **Pretraining + finetuning are the two LLM phases** (Q14).
- **RLHF made ChatGPT controllable** (Q15).

Drill: [[clusters/09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/cheat-sheet|Cluster 09 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 3 — Reco metrics & evaluation (4 questions)

- **NDCG = ranking quality with position discount** (Q19).
- **Novelty = diversity metric** (Q13).
- **Stratified split = same users in train + test** (Q3).
- **Real-time architecture = low-latency** (Q9, ⚠️ ambiguous).

Drill: [[clusters/04-reco-data-and-metrics/cheat-sheet|Cluster 04 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 4 — Reco fundamentals (4 questions)

- **Content-based filtering for cold start** (Q5).
- **Deep learning encodes high-order relationships** (Q12).
- **User-item matrix stores user preferences** (Q16, ⚠️ ambiguous).
- **Explicit = ratings/reviews; implicit = behavior** (Q17).

Drill: [[clusters/04-reco-data-and-metrics/cheat-sheet|Cluster 04 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 5 — Classical reco algorithms (2 questions)

- **ALS updates parameters in CF** (Q2) — name recall, no equations needed.
- **BPR for no-negative feedback** (Q7) — name recall, no equations needed.

Drill: [[clusters/05-matrix-factorization-and-sar/cheat-sheet|Cluster 05]] (just for ALS) and [[clusters/06-neural-reco/cheat-sheet|Cluster 06]] (just for BPR).

### Skip-candidate clusters (untested in this mock)

These are still useful to skim — the prof might add questions in the real exam — but **don't sink hours here**:

- **Cluster 01 (modern LLM)** — was my pre-mock #1. Not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 03 (Decision Trees)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 07 (GNNs)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 08 (Sequential)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 10 (Chatbot evolution + RLHF)** — only 1 question (Q15, RLHF). Skim only.

**Time savings:** if the real exam mirrors this mock, ~50% of the lecture content (decks 06 detail, 07, 08, 11, 12, 16) is testing-irrelevant. Don't grind those equations.

## Concept gaps in 20-Concepts/

After ingestion, **all concepts mentioned in the mock have a corresponding note** in `20-Concepts/`. No gaps.

## Next steps

1. **Re-take the mock closed-book on paper.** Time it (~2 min/question = ~40 min total).
2. Mark against [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key|the answer key]] (you, not me).
3. Identify weak topics → re-read the relevant cluster cheat sheet.
4. **Confirm Q9 with the prof if possible** — the "real-time = low-latency" framing isn't standard outside the lecture.

**Coverage:** Transformers + BERT/GPT = **9 of 19 ≈ 47%**. Reco fundamentals + metrics = **8 of 19 ≈ 42%**. Classical algorithms (ALS, BPR) = **2 of 19 ≈ 11%**.

**What's NOT in the mock:**

- Lecture 06 detail (MF equations, SAR scoring, similarity metrics taxonomy)
- Lecture 07 (FM, FFM)
- Lecture 08 (Decision Trees, Random Forest, GBDT, Gini, entropy)
- Lecture 10a/10b detail (NCF GMF/MLP equations, Wide&Deep architecture)
- Lecture 11 (Sequential, GRU4Rec, Caser, NextItNet)
- Lecture 12 (GNNs, NGCF, LightGCN, knowledge graphs)
- Lecture 14b (LLM memory strategies)
- Lecture 16 (entire deck — MoE, KV cache, RoPE, Flash Attention, MQA/GQA/MLA, ZeRO, mixed precision, LoRA, quantization, speculative decoding, DPO, GRPO, RLVR)

→ **The mock is significantly more high-level than the lecture content suggests.** This radically changes the priority ranking from my pre-mock estimate.

## Bang-for-buck ranking

### Priority 1 — Transformer & attention (5 questions)

5 of 19. Anchor the cluster with these atoms:

- **Self-attention output = weighted average of embeddings** (Q1, Q10).
- **Transformer's innovation = self-attention** (no recurrence, no convolution) (Q6).
- **Multi-head attention** is what BERT and GPT both use (Q4).
- **Seq2Seq is the modern chatbot architecture** (Q18).

Drill: [[clusters/02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 2 — BERT, GPT, transfer learning (4 questions)

- **MLM is BERT's pretraining objective** (Q8).
- **GPT = decoder-only Transformer for generation** (Q11).
- **Pretraining + finetuning are the two LLM phases** (Q14).
- **RLHF made ChatGPT controllable** (Q15).

Drill: [[clusters/09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/cheat-sheet|Cluster 09 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 3 — Reco metrics & evaluation (4 questions)

- **NDCG = ranking quality with position discount** (Q19).
- **Novelty = diversity metric** (Q13).
- **Stratified split = same users in train + test** (Q3).
- **Real-time architecture = low-latency** (Q9, ⚠️ ambiguous).

Drill: [[clusters/04-reco-data-and-metrics/cheat-sheet|Cluster 04 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 4 — Reco fundamentals (4 questions)

- **Content-based filtering for cold start** (Q5).
- **Deep learning encodes high-order relationships** (Q12).
- **User-item matrix stores user preferences** (Q16, ⚠️ ambiguous).
- **Explicit = ratings/reviews; implicit = behavior** (Q17).

Drill: [[clusters/04-reco-data-and-metrics/cheat-sheet|Cluster 04 cheat sheet]].

### Priority 5 — Classical reco algorithms (2 questions)

- **ALS updates parameters in CF** (Q2) — name recall, no equations needed.
- **BPR for no-negative feedback** (Q7) — name recall, no equations needed.

Drill: [[clusters/05-matrix-factorization-and-sar/cheat-sheet|Cluster 05]] (just for ALS) and [[clusters/06-neural-reco/cheat-sheet|Cluster 06]] (just for BPR).

### Skip-candidate clusters (untested in this mock)

These are still useful to skim — the prof might add questions in the real exam — but **don't sink hours here**:

- **Cluster 01 (modern LLM)** — was my pre-mock #1. Not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 03 (Decision Trees)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 07 (GNNs)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 08 (Sequential)** — not tested. **Skim only.**
- **Cluster 10 (Chatbot evolution + RLHF)** — only 1 question (Q15, RLHF). Skim only.

**Time savings:** if the real exam mirrors this mock, ~50% of the lecture content (decks 06 detail, 07, 08, 11, 12, 16) is testing-irrelevant. Don't grind those equations.

## Concept gaps in 20-Concepts/

After ingestion, **all concepts mentioned in the mock have a corresponding note** in `20-Concepts/`. No gaps.

## Next steps

1. **Re-take the mock closed-book on paper.** Time it (~2 min/question = ~40 min total).
2. Mark against [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key|the answer key]] (you, not me).
3. Identify weak topics → re-read the relevant cluster cheat sheet.
4. **Confirm Q9 with the prof if possible** — the "real-time = low-latency" framing isn't standard outside the lecture.

content.newText:
---
tags: [exam-prep, style-guide]
course: Chatbots
source: AI & Recommender Systems Quiz.md
created: 2026-04-26
---

# Chatbots — Exam Style Guide (MCQ)

Grounded in the prof's released **Mock 1** ([[practice-mocks/AI & Recommender Systems Quiz|original]] · [[practice-mocks/mock-1-questions|questions]] · [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key|answer key]]). Updated to reflect the actual question style observed.

## What we know

- **Format:** MCQ, single-select.
- **Mock 1 size:** 19 questions.
- **Likely real exam:** similar count and style.
- **Penalty:** assumed none for wrong answers.

## Headline observation

**The mock is much more high-level than the lecture content suggests.** Most questions are **definition recall**. There are no:

- Equation derivations
- Numerical computations
- "Swap two terms" traps (the CV course had these)
- Acronym-soup deep dives (deck 16 isn't tested at all)

This means **studying the lecture-canonical phrasing of foundational concepts beats grinding equations**.

## Distractor patterns observed

### (a) Made-up technical terms (most common)

Roughly half the questions have one or more distractors that sound plausible but are **invented terminology**. Examples:

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q4 (Attention in BERT/GPT) | "Multi-Head Attention" | "Textual Attention", "Location-Based Attention" — invented names |
| Q11 (Decoder LM) | "GPT" | "LSTM", "CNN", "RNN" — pre-Transformer architectures |

**How to handle:** if you don't recognise a term and another option is the lecture-canonical name, pick the canonical name.

### (b) Wrong-domain answers

The right answer comes from the lecture; distractors come from adjacent ML topics that don't apply.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q7 (no-negative feedback) | "Bayesian personalized ranking" | "Support vector machines", "GBDT" — different problem class |
| Q19 (ranking quality) | "NDCG" | "Cross-entropy loss" (a loss, not metric), "MSE" (regression metric), "AUC-ROC" (classification) |

### (c) Plausible-but-incomplete

The distractor is *true about the concept* but isn't the *primary* answer.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q10 (Transformer's primary advantage) | "Long-range dependencies" | "Faster training" (true but not the primary advantage) |
| Q15 (ChatGPT controlled responses) | "RLHF" | "Transfer learning from pretrained LMs" (true of pretraining, not of *control*) |
| Q16 (User-item matrix purpose) | "Store user preferences" | "Compute item similarities" / "Compute user similarities" (uses, not the primary purpose) |

**How to handle:** read the question's qualifier carefully — *primary*, *main*, *specifically*, *commonly*. Pick the most foundational answer.

### (d) Inverted / negated meanings

Less common but exists.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q17 (implicit vs explicit) | "Explicit = ratings; implicit = browsing" | "Implicit is directly provided; explicit is inferred" — inverts the definitions |

### (e) The genuinely ambiguous question

| Q | Issue |
|---|---|
| **Q9** | Says "real-time architecture for single-digit request time". Standard interpretation: real-time = score at request time (slower), batch + cache = sub-millisecond lookups. The lecture's framing is "real-time = low-latency response architecture" — pick A and move on. |
| **Q16** | "Purpose of user-item matrix" — store, similarity, both? The most foundational reading is **store**, but B and C are defensible. |

## Stem style

- Stems are **single sentences**, 10-25 words.
- Most stems start with **"What", "Which", "How"**.
- **Bracketed disambiguation** is rare in this mock (CV course had it; this one doesn't).
- No code, no equations, no tables in the stems.

## Pattern of correct-answer phrasing

Looking at the right answers as a corpus:

- They tend to use the **exact lecture term** (multi-head attention, NDCG, RLHF, BPR, MLM, ALS).
- They **avoid superlatives** ("always", "completely") that appear in distractors.
- They are **the most foundational of the four options**, not the most general.

**Quick heuristic for guessing under time pressure:** the correct option is usually the one that uses **the prof's exact terminology** rather than a generic paraphrase.

## Vocabulary the prof rewards

Use the exact terms in your head — they are the scoring keywords.

| Term | Where used |
|---|---|
| user / item / interaction | L02, Q16, Q17 |
| explicit / implicit feedback | L02, Q17 |
| cold start | L02-03, Q5 |
| stratified split | L02, Q3 |
| collaborative / content-based filtering | L03, Q5 |
| Alternating Least Squares (ALS) | L06, Q2 |
| Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) | L09, Q7 |
| Multi-Head Attention | L13, Q4 |
| self-attention | L13, Q1, Q6 |
| weighted average / linear combination | L13, Q1 |
| long-range dependencies | L13, Q10 |
| sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) | L13, Q18 |
| Masked Language Modeling (MLM) | L14, Q8 |
| pretraining + finetuning | L14, Q14 |
| GPT decoder | L14, Q11 |
| RLHF | L14b, L15, Q15 |
| novelty / diversity / serendipity / coverage | L04, Q13 |
| NDCG / MAP / Precision@K | L04, Q19 |
| real-time / batch / hybrid / 2-step | L04, Q9 |

## Topics with high return — drill these

12. **BPR for no-negative feedback** (Q7).

The mock has zero questions on:

- Decision Trees / GBDT / LightGBM / XGBoost (lecture 08)

- GNNs / NGCF / LightGCN (lecture 12)
- Sequential / GRU4Rec / SASRec (lecture 11)

- MF equations, SAR scoring math, similarity metric taxonomy (lecture 06 detail)
- NCF GMF/MLP equations, Wide & Deep architecture detail (lecture 10a/10b)

These may still appear if the real exam differs from the mock — but **don't grind these as priorities**.

## Time-budget tactics

For 19 questions with ~40 min budget (typical):

- **First pass (60% of time):** answer everything you know quickly. Skip and mark anything that needs thought.
- **Second pass (30%):** return to marked questions and reason through.
- **Final pass (10%):** never leave blank — educated guess if there's no penalty.

Per-question target: ~2 min average; budget less for definition recall (15-30 sec), more for the ambiguous ones.

## Quick-decision heuristic for MCQ

When two options seem equally correct under time pressure:

- The one that uses the **lecture-canonical term** (not the paraphrase) wins.
- The one that **answers the qualifier in the question** ("primary", "main", "specifically") wins.
- **Avoid superlatives** ("always", "completely", "perfectly") — usually distractors.

- Stay grounded — vague hand-waves fine, fabricated specifics aren't.

# Chatbots — Exam Style Guide (MCQ)

Grounded in the prof's released **Mock 1** ([[practice-mocks/AI & Recommender Systems Quiz|original]] · [[practice-mocks/mock-1-questions|questions]] · [[practice-mocks/mock-1-answer-key|answer key]]). Updated to reflect the actual question style observed.

## What we know

- **Format:** MCQ, single-select.
- **Mock 1 size:** 19 questions.
- **Likely real exam:** similar count and style.
- **Penalty:** assumed none for wrong answers.

## Headline observation

**The mock is much more high-level than the lecture content suggests.** Most questions are **definition recall**. There are no:

- Equation derivations
- Numerical computations
- "Swap two terms" traps (the CV course had these)
- Acronym-soup deep dives (deck 16 isn't tested at all)

This means **studying the lecture-canonical phrasing of foundational concepts beats grinding equations**.

## Distractor patterns observed

### (a) Made-up technical terms (most common)

Roughly half the questions have one or more distractors that sound plausible but are **invented terminology**. Examples:

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q4 (Attention in BERT/GPT) | "Multi-Head Attention" | "Textual Attention", "Location-Based Attention" — invented names |
| Q11 (Decoder LM) | "GPT" | "LSTM", "CNN", "RNN" — pre-Transformer architectures |

**How to handle:** if you don't recognise a term and another option is the lecture-canonical name, pick the canonical name.

### (b) Wrong-domain answers

The right answer comes from the lecture; distractors come from adjacent ML topics that don't apply.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q7 (no-negative feedback) | "Bayesian personalized ranking" | "Support vector machines", "GBDT" — different problem class |
| Q19 (ranking quality) | "NDCG" | "Cross-entropy loss" (a loss, not metric), "MSE" (regression metric), "AUC-ROC" (classification) |

### (c) Plausible-but-incomplete

The distractor is *true about the concept* but isn't the *primary* answer.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q10 (Transformer's primary advantage) | "Long-range dependencies" | "Faster training" (true but not the primary advantage) |
| Q15 (ChatGPT controlled responses) | "RLHF" | "Transfer learning from pretrained LMs" (true of pretraining, not of *control*) |
| Q16 (User-item matrix purpose) | "Store user preferences" | "Compute item similarities" / "Compute user similarities" (uses, not the primary purpose) |

**How to handle:** read the question's qualifier carefully — *primary*, *main*, *specifically*, *commonly*. Pick the most foundational answer.

### (d) Inverted / negated meanings

Less common but exists.

| Question | Right | Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Q17 (implicit vs explicit) | "Explicit = ratings; implicit = browsing" | "Implicit is directly provided; explicit is inferred" — inverts the definitions |

### (e) The genuinely ambiguous question

| Q | Issue |
|---|---|
| **Q9** | Says "real-time architecture for single-digit request time". Standard interpretation: real-time = score at request time (slower), batch + cache = sub-millisecond lookups. The lecture's framing is "real-time = low-latency response architecture" — pick A and move on. |
| **Q16** | "Purpose of user-item matrix" — store, similarity, both? The most foundational reading is **store**, but B and C are defensible. |

## Stem style

- Stems are **single sentences**, 10-25 words.
- Most stems start with **"What", "Which", "How"**.
- **Bracketed disambiguation** is rare in this mock (CV course had it; this one doesn't).
- No code, no equations, no tables in the stems.

## Pattern of correct-answer phrasing

Looking at the right answers as a corpus:

- They tend to use the **exact lecture term** (multi-head attention, NDCG, RLHF, BPR, MLM, ALS).
- They **avoid superlatives** ("always", "completely") that appear in distractors.
- They are **the most foundational of the four options**, not the most general.

**Quick heuristic for guessing under time pressure:** the correct option is usually the one that uses **the prof's exact terminology** rather than a generic paraphrase.

## Vocabulary the prof rewards

Use the exact terms in your head — they are the scoring keywords.

| Term | Where used |
|---|---|
| user / item / interaction | L02, Q16, Q17 |
| explicit / implicit feedback | L02, Q17 |
| cold start | L02-03, Q5 |
| stratified split | L02, Q3 |
| collaborative / content-based filtering | L03, Q5 |
| Alternating Least Squares (ALS) | L06, Q2 |
| Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) | L09, Q7 |
| Multi-Head Attention | L13, Q4 |
| self-attention | L13, Q1, Q6 |
| weighted average / linear combination | L13, Q1 |
| long-range dependencies | L13, Q10 |
| sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) | L13, Q18 |
| Masked Language Modeling (MLM) | L14, Q8 |
| pretraining + finetuning | L14, Q14 |
| GPT decoder | L14, Q11 |
| RLHF | L14b, L15, Q15 |
| novelty / diversity / serendipity / coverage | L04, Q13 |
| NDCG / MAP / Precision@K | L04, Q19 |
| real-time / batch / hybrid / 2-step | L04, Q9 |

# Cluster 08 — Atoms (Sequential and session-based)

> **Mock 1 coverage: 0 questions.** Skim only unless the real exam adds them.

## Sequential vs session-based

1. **Sequential.** User has identity. Combine **historical preferences + recent actions**. Markov chains, RNNs, CNNs, Transformers [[sequential-vs-session-based]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=1).
2. **Session-based.** **No user identity.** Each session handled independently. Neighborhood methods, LSTMs, Transformers.

## Sequential data layout

3. **Session-parallel mini-batches.** Each batch row = one session in time [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=2).
4. **Hidden-state reset.** When a session ends, the next session takes its slot and the **hidden state for that row is reset** so behaviour doesn't leak.

## GRU4Rec

5. **First major neural session-based recommender.** Hidasi et al., ICLR 2016 [[gru4rec]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=3).
6. **Architecture.** One-hot item input → embedding → GRU stack → softmax over items.
7. **Training loss.** TOP1 or BPR (pairwise). Items earlier in session weighted with discount.

## LSTM-based

8. **Three modifications** [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=4):
   - **Time-aware gating** — varies with time intervals between events.
   - **Attention** — filter irrelevant items, distinguish influence levels.
   - **MF hybrid** — long-term via MF + short-term via LSTM.

## Caser (Sequential CNN)

9. **Embed previous L items as 2D image.** (Time, latent) space [[sequential-cnn-reco]] — [slide 5](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=5).
10. **Vertical filters.** Encode how single items influence the next item.
11. **Horizontal filters.** Group the influence of items.
12. **Predict next T items** from previous L actions.

## NextItNet (Dilated CNN)

13. **Dilated convolutions.** Apply filter over field larger than original length by inserting zeros — increases **receptive field without adding parameters** [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=6).
14. **Long-range dependencies** captured via exponentially-growing receptive field.

## Transformer-based

15. **SASRec, SSE-PT, BERT4Rec.** Self-attention focuses on items relevant to next action [slide 7](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2011.pdf#page=7).
16. **Strongest current paradigm.** Same lesson as NLP.

# Cluster 08 — Cheat sheet (Sequential / session-based)

> **Mock 1 coverage: 0 questions.** Skim only.

## Sequential vs session-based

| | Sequential | Session-based |
|---|---|---|
| User identity | Yes | No |
| Combines | Long-term + short-term | Within-session only |
| Architectures | Markov, RNN, CNN, Transformer | LSTM, Transformer, neighborhood |
| Use case | Logged-in e-commerce | Anonymous browsing |

## Models in order of arrival

| Model | Architecture | Year |
|---|---|---|
| **GRU4Rec** | GRU | 2016 (Hidasi et al.) |
| **LSTM with attention** | LSTM | various |
| **Caser** | 2D CNN over (time, latent) | 2018 (Tang & Wang) |
| **NextItNet** | Dilated 1D CNN | 2019 (Yuan et al.) |
| **SASRec / SSE-PT / BERT4Rec** | Transformer | 2019-2020 |

## Key tricks

- **Session-parallel mini-batches** with **hidden-state reset** between sessions.
- **Caser**: vertical filters = single items; horizontal filters = groups.
- **NextItNet dilated convs**: receptive field grows exponentially without parameter explosion.
- **SASRec uses BPR** loss for ranking-aligned training.

## Common traps

- **"Sequential and session-based are the same"** — false. Sequential has user identity; session-based doesn't.
- **"Caser is for images"** — false. Caser embeds the *sequence* as a 2D matrix; it's a sequence model.
- **"GRU4Rec uses pointwise loss"** — false. Uses TOP1 / BPR (pairwise).

## Cross-references

- [[../06-neural-reco/cheat-sheet|Cluster 06]] — BPR (used by SASRec)
- [[../02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02]] — Transformer (used by SASRec/BERT4Rec)

# Cluster 09 — BERT, GPT, and transfer learning

**Mock 1 coverage: 4 questions** (Q8, Q11, Q14, Q15). Second-highest priority cluster.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-14-transfer-learning]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/transfer-learning]] (extends CV note)
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/bert-model]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/gpt-models]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/masked-language-model]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/next-token-prediction]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/zero-shot-learning]] (extends CV note)
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/few-shot-learning]] (extends CV note)
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/rlhf]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-14]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-14b]]

## What this cluster is about

The lecturer's claim: **transfer learning is the single biggest reason modern deep learning works.** First widely used in CV (ImageNet pretraining); now ubiquitous in NLP.

**Two-phase paradigm.** Pretraining (large unlabelled corpus) → finetuning (small labelled task dataset). Mock Q14 tests this directly. The body (pretrained backbone) is reused; the head is task-specific.

**Two NLP pretraining objectives**:

- **Masked Language Model (MLM)** — BERT family. Mask 15% of tokens, predict them given the rest. Bidirectional → strong for understanding tasks. Mock Q8 tests this directly. The 15% split: 80% `[MASK]`, 10% random token, 10% unchanged — to reduce the pretrain/finetune mismatch.
- **Next-token prediction** — GPT family. Predict the next token autoregressively. Decoder-only with causal masking → strong for generation. Mock Q11 tests this directly.

**BERT** (Devlin 2018). Encoder-only Transformer. **BERT-large**: 340M params, 24 encoder blocks, 1024-dim embeddings, 16 heads. Pretrained on BooksCorpus (800M words) + Wikipedia (2,500M words). Deployed in **Google Search 2019**. Successors: RoBERTa, DeBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, ELECTRA. Special tokens: `[CLS]` (classification), `[SEP]` (separator), `[MASK]` (training only).

**GPT** (1-5). Decoder-only Transformer with causal masking. Each generation kept the architecture roughly fixed and **scaled compute, data, parameters**:

- GPT-1 (2018, 117M): SFT-pretrain template.
- GPT-2 (2019, 1.5B): multi-task learner, **zero-shot transfer**.
- GPT-3 (2020, 175B): **few-shot in-context learning**, the inflection point.
- GPT-4 (2023, undisclosed): multimodal, human-level on professional exams.

**Three NLP finetuning options**: (1) **Supervised finetuning (SFT)** — standard input → label. (2) **Reward-model finetuning** — define a reward, RL on it. (3) **RLHF** — reward = human feedback. Used in ChatGPT (Mock Q15).

**Freeze vs finetune.** If target and base domains are similar → freeze body, retrain head only. If they differ → finetune all weights at low LR. CV pretraining cost: ResNet18 from scratch on ImageNet = 3 days on 4 K80 GPUs; ResNet152 for 120 epochs = 4 months. Transfer learning isn't a nice-to-have, it's the only way most teams ship.

**Three NLP finetuning options**: (1) **Supervised finetuning (SFT)** — standard input → label. (2) **Reward-model finetuning** — define a reward, RL on it. (3) **RLHF** — reward = human feedback. Used in ChatGPT (Mock Q15).

**Freeze vs finetune.** If target and base domains are similar → freeze body, retrain head only. If they differ → finetune all weights at low LR. CV pretraining cost: ResNet18 from scratch on ImageNet = 3 days on 4 K80 GPUs; ResNet152 for 120 epochs = 4 months. Transfer learning isn't a nice-to-have, it's the only way most teams ship.

---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-09]
cluster: 09
---

# Cluster 09 — Atoms (BERT, GPT, Transfer Learning)

> **Mock 1 directly tests:** Q8 (MLM), Q11 (GPT decoder), Q14 (pretrain+finetune), Q15 (RLHF).

## Transfer learning

1. **The lecturer's claim.** **Transfer learning is the single biggest reason modern deep learning works** [[transfer-learning]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=1).
2. **Pattern.** Train on one task → adapt to a new task. Original weights = body; task-specific output = head [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=2).
3. **First widely used in CV.** Then ubiquitous in NLP.

## Zero-shot

4. **Zero-shot learning.** Pretrained model adapts to a new task **without retraining** [[zero-shot-learning]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=3).

## CV pretraining numbers (slide 5)

5. **ResNet18 from scratch on ImageNet.** 30 epochs on 4 NVIDIA K80 GPUs = **3 days**.
6. **ResNet152 for 120 epochs on 4 K80 GPUs.** = **4 months**.

## Freeze vs finetune

7. **If target and base domains are similar.** Freeze body, retrain head only [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=6).
8. **If domains differ.** Finetune all weights at low LR.

## NLP pretraining objectives

9. **Supervised pretraining.** Translation pairs (En→De, En→Fr) — old, expensive [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=8).
10. **Unsupervised — autoregressive.** Next-token prediction. **GPT family** [[next-token-prediction]].
11. **Unsupervised — masked LM (denoising).** Predict masked tokens. **BERT family** [[masked-language-model]].

## NLP finetuning options

12. **Supervised finetuning (SFT).** Standard supervised learning on the task — input → label [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=9).
13. **Reward-model finetuning.** Define a reward, RL on it.
14. **RLHF.** Reward function based on **human feedback**. **Used in ChatGPT.**

## BERT — overview

15. **Full name.** Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers [[bert-model]] — [slide 10](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=10).
16. **Architecture.** Encoder-only Transformer.
17. **BERT-large.** 340M params, 24 encoder blocks, 1024-dim embeddings, 16 attention heads.
18. **Pretrained on.** BooksCorpus (800M words) + English Wikipedia (2,500M words).
19. **Deployment.** **Google Search in 2019.**
20. **Successors.** RoBERTa, DeBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, ELECTRA.

## BERT pretraining

21. **Two tasks.** **Masked language model (MLM)** and **Next-Sentence Prediction (NSP)** [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=11).
22. **Why mask.** Truly bidirectional models with concatenation **leak the answer** (each word would see itself from the other side). Masking lets the model use both sides safely.
23. **The 15% recipe.** Of the 15% masked: **80%** replaced with `[MASK]`, **10%** with a random token, **10%** unchanged.
24. **Why the 80/10/10 split.** `[MASK]` doesn't appear during finetuning → mismatch. The 20% non-`[MASK]` cases mitigate this.

## BERT inputs

25. **Special tokens** [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=12):
   - `[CLS]` — first token of every sentence; embedding used for classification.
   - `[SEP]` — separator between sentences (Q&A, entailment).
   - `[MASK]` — mask token for MLM training.
26. **Plus segment embeddings** (A or B sentence indicator) and **positional embeddings**.

## BERT finetuning tasks

27. **Sentence-pair classification** (entailment) — MNLI [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=13).
28. **Single-sentence classification** (sentiment) — SST-2.
29. **Question answering** — SQuAD v1.1 (predict span start/end).
30. **Single-sentence tagging** — CoNLL-2003 NER (B-PER, I-PER, B-ORG, etc.).

## BERT vs GPT

31. **Architecture.** BERT = encoder-only; GPT = decoder-only [slide 17](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=17).
32. **Pretraining.** BERT = MLM + NSP (bidirectional); GPT = next-token prediction (left-to-right).
33. **Best at.** BERT = understanding (classification, NER, Q&A); GPT = generation.

## GPT generations

34. **GPT-1** (2018) — 117M params, 12 layers, 512 max tokens, 768 hidden. Trained on BookCorpus (985M words). First decoder-only LM [[gpt-models]] — [slide 18](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=18).
35. **GPT-2** (2019) — 1.5B params, 48 layers, 1024 max tokens. **Multi-task learner**, zero-shot transfer [slide 19](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=19).
36. **GPT-3** (2020) — 175B params, 96 layers, 2048 max tokens, 12288 hidden. Trained on CommonCrawl (1T+ words). **Few-shot learner — the inflection point** [slide 20](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=20).
37. **GPT-4** (2023) — undisclosed details. **Multimodal** (text + image). 32768 max tokens (50 pages). Human-level on professional exams. Still hallucinates [slide 21](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014.pdf#page=21).

## RLHF

38. **What it is.** Use a reward function trained on **human preferences** to RL-finetune an LLM [[rlhf]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014b.pdf#page=1).
39. **The three steps.** SFT → reward model on preference pairs → PPO with the RM as reward.
40. **Why ChatGPT works.** GPT-3.5 base + RLHF = ChatGPT. Mock Q15.
41. **Trade-off.** RLHF does **mode seeking** (concentrates on most-rated responses) — gain reliability, **lose diversity**.

Drill these first.** ## Definitions | Term | One-liner | |---|---| | **Transfer learning** | Pretrain on big data → adapt to small target task | | **Pretraining** | Train on a large generic corpus (unlabelled or weakly labelled) | | **Finetuning** | Adapt a pretrained model to a specific downstream task | | **Body / head** | Pretrained backbone (body) + task-specific output (head) | | **MLM** (Masked Language Model) | Mask 15% of tokens, predict them — BERT | | **Next-token prediction** | Predict next token autoregressively — GPT | | **BERT** | Encoder-only Transformer, MLM pretrained | | **GPT** | Decoder-only Transformer, autoregressive | | **SFT** | Supervised Fine-Tuning | | **RLHF** | Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback | ## Two LLM phases (Mock Q14) | Phase | Goal | Data | |---|---|---| | **Pretraining** | Learn general language patterns | Massive unlabelled corpus (CommonCrawl, BooksCorpus) | | **Finetuning** | Adapt to a specific task / behaviour | Small labelled dataset (or preference pairs for RLHF) | ## BERT vs GPT (Mock Q11) | | BERT | GPT | |---|---|---| | Architecture | **Encoder-only** | **Decoder-only** | | Pretraining | **MLM + NSP** | **Next-token prediction** | | Direction | Bidirectional | Left-to-right (causal) | | Best at | Understanding | Generation | | Released | 2018 | 2018 (GPT-1) | | Successors | RoBERTa, DeBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT, ELECTRA | GPT 2/3/4/5, LLaMA, Mistral | ## BERT MLM recipe (Mock Q8) | Replacement | Frequency | |---|---| | `[MASK]` token | **80%** | | Random token | **10%** | | Unchanged | **10%** | **Why mask:** truly bidirectional models with concatenation leak the answer (each word would see itself). **Why 80/10/10:** `[MASK]` doesn't appear at finetuning → mismatch. The 20% non-`[MASK]` cases reduce this. ## BERT special tokens | Token | Use | |---|---| | `[CLS]` | First token; embedding used for classification | | `[SEP]` | Separator between sentences | | `[MASK]` | Mask token for MLM training | Plus segment embeddings (A/B sentence indicator) and positional embeddings. ## GPT generations | Model | Year | Params | Context | Highlight | |---|---|---|---|---| | GPT-1 | 2018 | 117M | 512 | Decoder-only LM template | | GPT-2 | 2019 | 1.5B | 1024 | **Zero-shot transfer**, multi-task learner | | GPT-3 | 2020 | 175B | 2048 | **Few-shot in-context learning** | | GPT-4 | 2023 | undisclosed | 32768 | Multimodal, human-level on exams | ## NLP finetuning options | Option | What it does | |---|---| | **SFT** (Supervised) | Standard input → label | | **Reward-model finetuning** | Define a reward, RL on it | | **RLHF** | Reward = human preferences. **Used in ChatGPT.** | ## RLHF (Mock Q15) ```mermaid flowchart LR Base[Pretrained LLM
e.g. GPT-3.5] --> SFT[Step 1: SFT
Human-written demos] SFT --> RM[Step 2: Reward Model
Trained on preference pairs] RM --> PPO[Step 3: PPO
Maximise RM reward] PPO --> Out[Aligned LLM
e.g. ChatGPT] classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0 class Out chatbot ``` **Trade-off:** mode seeking → reliability gained, **diversity lost**. ## Freeze vs finetune | Situation | Recipe | |---|---| | Target ≈ base domain | Freeze body, train head only | | Target ≠ base domain | Finetune all weights at low LR | ## Why pretraining matters (CV numbers) - ResNet18 from scratch on ImageNet = **3 days** (4 K80 GPUs, 30 epochs) - ResNet152 for 120 epochs = **4 months** (4 K80 GPUs) → Most teams can't afford this. Transfer learning is the default. ## Common traps - **"Pretraining and SFT are the same"** — false. Pretraining is the **first phase** (general); SFT is one option for the **second phase** (task-specific). - **"BERT can generate text"** — not naturally. BERT is encoder-only; GPT-style decoder is needed for generation. - **"GPT is bidirectional"** — false. GPT is **left-to-right** (causal masking). - **"RLHF replaces pretraining"** — false. RLHF is a **post-pretraining alignment step**. - **"Masking 100% of the chosen tokens with `[MASK]` is fine"** — false. Creates a pretrain/finetune mismatch — hence the 80/10/10 split. - **"ChatGPT was trained from scratch with RLHF"** — false. ChatGPT = **GPT-3.5 + RLHF** (RLHF is finetuning, not pretraining). - **"Zero-shot learning means no training at all"** — partially. The model is still **pretrained** — zero-shot means **no task-specific training examples**. - **"Few-shot learning needs extra training"** — false. Few-shot in-context learning gives the model a few examples **in the prompt**, not via training. ## Cross-references - [[../02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02]] — Transformer architecture (foundational) - [[../10-chatbot-evolution-and-rlhf/cheat-sheet|Cluster 10]] — Chatbot evolution and RLHF in context - [[../../exam-blueprint]] — per-question topic coverage

---
tags: [study-cluster, Chatbots, cluster-10]
cluster: 10
---

# Cluster 10 — Chatbot evolution, ChatGPT, RLHF, OpenAI APIs

**Mock 1 coverage: 1 question** (Q15 RLHF, also in Cluster 09). Lower priority; useful as historical context and for the "context as state" framing.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-05-evolution-of-state]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-14b-rlhf-overview]]
- [[../../../../10-Courses/Chatbots/lectures/lecture-15-evolution-of-chatbots]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/finite-state-machine-chatbot]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/chatbot-history-timeline]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/rag-pipeline]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/agentic-workflow]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/llm-evaluation]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/llm-guardrails]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/rlhf]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/llm-memory]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/openai-apis]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/llm-product-landscape]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-05]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-14b]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-15]]

## What this cluster is about

**The big paradigm shift.** Chatbots evolved from **Software 1.0** (rule-based finite state machines + regex — ELIZA, ALICE) to **Software 2.0** (Transformer + RLHF — ChatGPT). The technical lens: state went from a database variable to **the n tokens currently in the context window**.

**The historical timeline.** Turing test concept (1950) → ELIZA first chatbot (1966) → ALICE (1995) → Siri first worldwide (2010) → Google Now / Cortana / Alexa (2012-14) → Facebook Messenger bots (2016) → ChatGPT (Nov 2022, **fastest tech ever to 100M MAU — 2 months**).

**ChatGPT.** Pretrained GPT-3.5 + **RLHF**. RLHF biases the model toward human-rated answers — gain reliability, **lose diversity**. The lecturer's analogy: RLHF is "Page Rank for LLMs".

**Modern stack components** (Lecture 5):
- **RAG** (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — solves knowledge cutoff and hallucination by grounding answers in retrieved documents. Pipeline: Ingestion → Retrieval → Augmentation → Generation. **Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the LLM choice.**
- **Agentic workflows** — function calling (LLM outputs JSON to invoke tools), agent loop (autonomous iteration), ReAct (Thought → Action → Observation).
- **Evaluation** — LLM-as-a-judge, faithfulness, answer relevance, plus guardrails (PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering).

**LLM memory strategies** (Lecture 14b): buffer, conversation summary, entity memory, knowledge-graph memory, **vector-stored memory** (production default).

**OpenAI APIs.** Chat Completions (system/user/assistant roles) → Assistant API (deprecated 2026) → Responses API (current default, developer/user/assistant roles) → Reasoning models (o-series, "think before answering") → Function calling → Agents (instructions + tools + handoffs + guardrails).

**Major LLM products.** ChatGPT (OpenAI), Microsoft Copilot (Prometheus tech), Google Gemini (multimodal text/image/sound/video), xAI Grok (truth-seeking), Anthropic Claude (Constitutional AI), Meta LLaMA (open-weight). All decoder-only Transformers + alignment (RLHF / DPO / GRPO).

**Anthropic-specific concepts**: CLAUDE.md (project context), Skills (versioned slash commands), Agents (autonomous workflows with tools).

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../flashcards/lecture-05]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-14b]] · [[../../flashcards/lecture-15]]

## What this cluster is about

**The big paradigm shift.** Chatbots evolved from **Software 1.0** (rule-based finite state machines + regex — ELIZA, ALICE) to **Software 2.0** (Transformer + RLHF — ChatGPT). The technical lens: state went from a database variable to **the n tokens currently in the context window**.

**The historical timeline.** Turing test concept (1950) → ELIZA first chatbot (1966) → ALICE (1995) → Siri first worldwide (2010) → Google Now / Cortana / Alexa (2012-14) → Facebook Messenger bots (2016) → ChatGPT (Nov 2022, **fastest tech ever to 100M MAU — 2 months**).

**ChatGPT.** Pretrained GPT-3.5 + **RLHF**. RLHF biases the model toward human-rated answers — gain reliability, **lose diversity**. The lecturer's analogy: RLHF is "Page Rank for LLMs".

**Modern stack components** (Lecture 5):
- **RAG** (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — solves knowledge cutoff and hallucination by grounding answers in retrieved documents. Pipeline: Ingestion → Retrieval → Augmentation → Generation. **Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the LLM choice.**
- **Agentic workflows** — function calling (LLM outputs JSON to invoke tools), agent loop (autonomous iteration), ReAct (Thought → Action → Observation).
- **Evaluation** — LLM-as-a-judge, faithfulness, answer relevance, plus guardrails (PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering).

**LLM memory strategies** (Lecture 14b): buffer, conversation summary, entity memory, knowledge-graph memory, **vector-stored memory** (production default).

**OpenAI APIs.** Chat Completions (system/user/assistant roles) → Assistant API (deprecated 2026) → Responses API (current default, developer/user/assistant roles) → Reasoning models (o-series, "think before answering") → Function calling → Agents (instructions + tools + handoffs + guardrails).

**Major LLM products.** ChatGPT (OpenAI), Microsoft Copilot (Prometheus tech), Google Gemini (multimodal text/image/sound/video), xAI Grok (truth-seeking), Anthropic Claude (Constitutional AI), Meta LLaMA (open-weight). All decoder-only Transformers + alignment (RLHF / DPO / GRPO).

# Cluster 10 — Atoms (Chatbot evolution + RLHF + APIs)

> **Mock 1 directly tests:** Q15 (RLHF). Q18 (Seq2Seq for chatbots) lives in Cluster 02.

## Software 1.0 vs 2.0 (Lecture 5)

1. **Software 1.0 (rule-based era).** Logic = hard-coded FSMs + regex. "If user says X, do Y." Fragile, no nuance, doesn't scale [[finite-state-machine-chatbot]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2005.pdf#page=1).
2. **Software 2.0 (probabilistic era).** Transformer + self-attention. Programming → Training.
3. **Context as state.** State is no longer a database variable — it's the **n tokens currently in the context window**. Predicting next token vs navigating a graph.

## RAG (Lecture 5)

4. **Workflow.** Ingestion (chunk → embed → vector store) → Retrieval (embed query, top-K search) → Augmentation (inject chunks into prompt) → Generation (LLM answers based on context) [[rag-pipeline]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2005.pdf#page=2).
5. **Solves.** Knowledge cutoff + hallucination.
6. **Key insight.** **Embedding quality and chunking strategy matter more than the choice of LLM.**
7. **Tech stack.** LangChain / LlamaIndex (orchestration); ChromaDB (local) / Pinecone (cloud) / pgvector (SQL) (vector DB).

## Agentic workflows (Lecture 5)

8. **Function calling.** LLM identifies when it needs a tool and outputs **structured JSON** to trigger an API/SQL/Python call [[agentic-workflow]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2005.pdf#page=3).
9. **Agent loop.** Autonomous iteration — model evaluates its own tool outputs to reach a goal (e.g. Claude Code).
10. **ReAct.** Thought → Action → Observation triplets. Example: Thought "I need order status" → Action `get_order_details(order_id="123")` → Observation "shipped yesterday".

## LLM evaluation + guardrails (Lecture 5)

11. **LLM-as-a-judge.** Use a stronger model to grade a smaller model's output [[llm-evaluation]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2005.pdf#page=4).
12. **Key metrics.** **Faithfulness** (does answer match retrieved context?) and **Answer relevance** (does it address the prompt?).
13. **Guardrail layer** [[llm-guardrails]]: PII anonymisation, prompt-injection detection, output filtering.

## RLHF (Lecture 14b)

14. **Standard GPT loss = cross-entropy.** Distribution matching [[rlhf]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014b.pdf#page=1).
15. **RLHF instead does mode seeking.** Biases the model toward most-rated preferences provided by humans.
16. **Trade-off.** Lose diversity, gain reliability.
17. **Lecturer's analogy.** RLHF is **Page Rank for LLMs** — captures relevance the way Page Rank captured website authority.
18. **Bad for novelty.** Constrains the model to output common knowledge.

## LLM memory (Lecture 14b)

19. **Five strategies** [[llm-memory]] — [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2014b.pdf#page=2):
    - **Buffer.** All prior messages verbatim.
    - **Conversation summary.** Running summary updated over time.
    - **Entity.** Facts about specific entities (names, locations, products).
    - **Knowledge graph.** Structured relationships.
    - **Vector-stored.** Past convos as vectors; query top-K on each new message.
20. **Production default.** Vector-stored — scales unlimited history, cheap query, reuses RAG infrastructure.

## Chatbot timeline (Lecture 15)

21. **1950 — Turing.** *Computing Machinery and Intelligence* — proposed the Turing test [[chatbot-history-timeline]] — [slide 1](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=1).
22. **1966 — MIT's ELIZA.** First chatbot — keyword matching + scripted responses.
23. **1995 — A.L.I.C.E.** Heuristic pattern matching, won awards but **didn't pass Turing test**.
24. **2010 — Apple Siri.** First chatbot **available worldwide**.
25. **2012 — Google Now.** Predictive contextual recommendations.
26. **2014 — Cortana / Alexa.** Microsoft + Amazon voice assistants.
27. **2016 — Facebook Messenger bots.** Business-focused, automated.
28. **Nov 2022 — ChatGPT.** GPT-3.5 + RLHF. **5 days to 1M MAU; 2 months to 100M MAU — fastest tech ever.**

## ChatGPT (Lecture 15)

29. **Implementation.** Pretrained GPT-3.5 + RLHF [slide 2](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=2).
30. **Revenue trajectory.** $200M (end 2022) → $1.6B (2023, +700% YoY) → $3.7B (2024).

## OpenAI APIs (Lecture 15)

31. **Chat Completions roles.** `system` (set behaviour), `user` (prompt), `assistant` (response or stored prior) [[openai-apis]] — [slide 3](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=3).
32. **Assistant API.** Persistent threads, auto-truncate over context. **Deprecated 2026.**
33. **Responses API.** Current simplified default. Roles: developer / user / assistant [slide 6](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=6).
34. **Reasoning models** (o-series). Think before answering (chain of thought). Effort: low / medium / high [slide 8](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=8).
35. **Function calling.** Tool definitions in JSON; LLM picks tool + arguments [slide 9](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=9).
36. **Agents.** LLM + instructions + tools + handoffs (delegate to other agents) + guardrails [slide 11](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=11).

## Major LLM products (Lecture 15)

37. **Microsoft Copilot.** Prometheus tech — 3 phases: Bing orchestrator → grounding (fresh data + safeguards) → answer with citations [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=12).
38. **Google Gemini.** **Multimodal** (text, image, sound, video). Paper has 1300+ authors [[llm-product-landscape]] — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=13).
39. **xAI Grok.** Integrated in X. Multimodal (text + image). **Trained to maximise truth seeking.**
40. **Anthropic Claude.** Trained with **Constitutional AI** — embed ethical principles. Multimodal.
41. **Meta LLaMA.** **Open-weight** (close to OSS but not fully). Multimodal.

## Anthropic-specific (Lecture 15)

42. **CLAUDE.md.** Markdown doc at project root that gives Claude **persistent context** — read at start of every conversation [slide 17](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=17).
43. **Skills.** Reusable, versioned **instruction sets** invoked via slash commands (`/skill-name`) [slide 18](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=18).
44. **Agents.** Autonomous AI workflows — plan, execute multi-step, use tools (computer use, bash, file ops, MCP servers) [slide 19](../../../../30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots%20and%20Reco%2015.pdf#page=19).

# Cluster 10 — Cheat sheet (Chatbot evolution + RLHF + APIs)

> **Mock 1: Q15 (RLHF)**, also covered in Cluster 09. Lower priority but good context.

## The big paradigm shift

| | Software 1.0 | Software 2.0 |
|---|---|---|
| Era | Pre-LLM (ELIZA, ALICE, Siri-era) | LLM (ChatGPT-era) |
| Logic | Hard-coded FSM + regex | Trained Transformer + RLHF |
| State | Database variable | **n tokens in context window** |
| Failure mode | Brittle to phrasing | Hallucination |

## Chatbot timeline

| Year | Milestone | Tech |
|---|---|---|
| 1950 | Turing — *Computing Machinery and Intelligence* | Concept (Turing test) |
| 1966 | MIT's ELIZA — first chatbot | Keyword matching |
| 1995 | A.L.I.C.E. | Heuristic patterns (AIML) |
| 2010 | Apple Siri — first worldwide | Speech + intent + KG |
| 2012 | Google Now | Speech + behavioural signals |
| 2014 | Cortana, Alexa | Voice assistants |
| 2016 | Facebook Messenger bots | Business automation |
| **Nov 2022** | **ChatGPT** | **GPT-3.5 + RLHF** |

ChatGPT growth: **5 days** to 1M MAU; **2 months** to 100M MAU — **fastest tech ever**.

## RAG (Lecture 5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Doc[Documents] -->|chunk + embed| Vec[(Vector store)]
    Q[User query] -->|embed| QE[Query embedding]
    QE -->|top-K| Vec
    Vec --> Top[Top-K chunks]
    Top -->|augment| Prompt
    Q --> Prompt
    Prompt --> LLM
    LLM --> Ans[Answer]
    classDef chatbot fill:#fff3e0
    class LLM chatbot
```

**Headline insight:** **embedding quality and chunking matter more than the LLM choice.**

## Agentic patterns (Lecture 5)

| Pattern | What it is |
|---|---|
| **Function calling** | LLM outputs JSON to invoke tool |
| **Agent loop** | Autonomous iteration to a goal |
| **ReAct** | Thought → Action → Observation triplets |

## RLHF in one breath

> **GPT base = distribution matching (cross-entropy). RLHF = mode seeking. Lose diversity, gain reliability. Page Rank for LLMs.**

Three steps: **SFT** → **Reward Model** → **PPO**.

## LLM memory (Lecture 14b)

| Strategy | Stores | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Buffer** | All prior messages verbatim | Short chats |
| **Summary** | Running summary | Long sessions |
| **Entity** | Facts about entities | CRM-style bots |
| **Knowledge graph** | Structured relations | Domain reasoning |
| **Vector** | Past convos as embeddings; top-K query | **Production default** |

## OpenAI APIs

| API | Status | Roles |
|---|---|---|
| **Chat Completions** | Active | system / user / assistant |
| **Assistant API** | **Deprecated 2026** | Persistent threads |
| **Responses API** | Current default | developer / user / assistant |
| **Reasoning models** (o-series) | Active | Effort: low / medium / high |
| **Function calling** | Active | Tools as JSON |
| **Agents** | Active | Instructions + tools + handoffs + guardrails |

## Major LLM products

| Product | Company | Distinctive |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT | OpenAI | GPT-3.5/4/5 + RLHF |
| Microsoft Copilot | Microsoft | "Prometheus" — orchestrator + grounding + citations |
| Google Gemini | Google | **Multimodal** (text, image, sound, video) |
| xAI Grok | xAI | Integrated in X; **truth-seeking** |
| Anthropic Claude | Anthropic | **Constitutional AI** |
| Meta LLaMA | Meta | **Open-weight** (not fully OSS) |

## Anthropic-specific (Lecture 15)

| Concept | What it is |
|---|---|
| **CLAUDE.md** | Markdown at project root; persistent context, read every conversation |
| **Skills** | Versioned instruction sets via `/slash-commands` |
| **Agents** | Autonomous workflows with tools (computer use, bash, file ops, MCP) |

## Common traps

- **"ChatGPT was trained from scratch with RLHF"** — false. **GPT-3.5 + RLHF** (RLHF is alignment, not pretraining).
- **"RLHF preserves diversity"** — false. RLHF does **mode seeking** → loses diversity.
- **"RAG replaces the LLM"** — false. RAG is a pipeline that **grounds** the LLM with retrieved chunks.
- **"Open-weight = open-source"** — false. **LLaMA = open-weight** (weights released, training code/data may be private).
- **"Vector memory is just RAG over chat"** — actually true; that's why it's the production default.
- **"ELIZA passed the Turing test"** — false. **A.L.I.C.E. didn't either.**
- **"Apple Siri was the first chatbot"** — false. Siri (2010) was the first worldwide; ELIZA (1966) was the first chatbot.

## Cross-references

- [[../09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/cheat-sheet|Cluster 09]] — RLHF and ChatGPT in BERT/GPT context
- [[../02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02]] — Seq2Seq for chatbots (Mock Q18)

| **Reasoning models** (o-series) | Active | Effort: low / medium / high |
| **Function calling** | Active | Tools as JSON |
| **Agents** | Active | Instructions + tools + handoffs + guardrails |

## Major LLM products

| Product | Company | Distinctive |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT | OpenAI | GPT-3.5/4/5 + RLHF |
| Microsoft Copilot | Microsoft | "Prometheus" — orchestrator + grounding + citations |
| Google Gemini | Google | **Multimodal** (text, image, sound, video) |
| xAI Grok | xAI | Integrated in X; **truth-seeking** |
| Anthropic Claude | Anthropic | **Constitutional AI** |
| Meta LLaMA | Meta | **Open-weight** (not fully OSS) |

## Anthropic-specific (Lecture 15)

| Concept | What it is |
|---|---|
| **CLAUDE.md** | Markdown at project root; persistent context, read every conversation |
| **Skills** | Versioned instruction sets via `/slash-commands` |
| **Agents** | Autonomous workflows with tools (computer use, bash, file ops, MCP) |

## Common traps

- **"ChatGPT was trained from scratch with RLHF"** — false. **GPT-3.5 + RLHF** (RLHF is alignment, not pretraining).
- **"RLHF preserves diversity"** — false. RLHF does **mode seeking** → loses diversity.
- **"RAG replaces the LLM"** — false. RAG is a pipeline that **grounds** the LLM with retrieved chunks.
- **"Open-weight = open-source"** — false. **LLaMA = open-weight** (weights released, training code/data may be private).
- **"Vector memory is just RAG over chat"** — actually true; that's why it's the production default.
- **"ELIZA passed the Turing test"** — false. **A.L.I.C.E. didn't either.**
- **"Apple Siri was the first chatbot"** — false. Siri (2010) was the first worldwide; ELIZA (1966) was the first chatbot.

## Cross-references

- [[../09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/cheat-sheet|Cluster 09]] — RLHF and ChatGPT in BERT/GPT context
- [[../02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02]] — Seq2Seq for chatbots (Mock Q18)

  - `clusters/` — 10 clusters (overview + atoms + cheat-sheet each)
  - `practice-mocks/` — your original quiz, plus `mock-1-questions.md` and `mock-1-answer-key.md` with worked explanations

**The Mock 1 recalibration changed everything:**

| Pre-mock priority | Post-mock priority | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| #1: Cluster 01 (modern LLM) | **Skim only** | Lecture 16 not tested at all (0 of 19) |
| #2: Cluster 02 (transformer) | **#1** | 5 of 19 questions |
| #3: Cluster 03 (decision trees) | **Skim only** | 0 of 19 |
| #5: Cluster 09 (BERT/GPT) | **#2** | 4 of 19 questions |
| #4: Cluster 04 (reco data + metrics) | **#3** | 8 of 19 questions across two themes |

**Two flagged questions worth raising with the prof:**
- **Q9** — "Real-time architecture for single-digit request time" answer is A, but standard interpretation says batch + cache is fastest at request time. The lecture might frame "real-time architecture" as "low-latency response" regardless of whether scoring is precomputed. Worth confirming.
- **Q16** — "Purpose of user-item matrix" answer is A (store user preferences), but B (compute item similarities) and C (compute user similarities) are also defensible — the matrix is *used* for similarity computation. Pick A as the most foundational reading.

**The honest takeaway on the level**: yes, very high-level. If the real exam mirrors this mock, ~50% of the lecture content (deck 16 acronyms, decision trees, FM/FFM, GNNs, sequential recommenders, MF equations) is testing-irrelevant. Don't grind that material.

# Chatbots — Mock 2 (questions only)

19 MCQ. Single-select. Same style and difficulty as [[mock-1-questions|Mock 1]] — generated to test **different specific facts** while keeping the same topic distribution. Take closed-book; check against [[mock-2-answer-key|the answer key]].

---

## 1. What is the role of positional encoding in Transformers?

- A. To compute the attention weights
- B. To inject token order information into the model
- C. To improve gradient flow during training
- D. To normalise the embeddings

---

## 2. Which evaluation metric measures rating accuracy by penalising larger errors more heavily than smaller ones?

- A. Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
- B. Precision@K
- C. Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
- D. Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)

---

## 3. What does the `[CLS]` token in BERT represent?

- A. The end of the sequence
- B. The first token of every sentence, used for classification tasks
- C. A masked token to be predicted
- D. A separator between two sentences

---

## 4. When is a chronological split most appropriate for evaluating a recommender?

- A. When you have many cold users
- B. When the dataset is very small
- C. When user preferences change over time (e.g. fashion)
- D. When all users have rated all items

---

## 5. What does the softmax function compute in self-attention?

- A. A weighted sum of the value vectors
- B. A probability distribution over the attention scores
- C. The query and key projections
- D. The positional encoding

---

## 6. Which evaluation metric measures the proportion of recommended items that are relevant?

- A. Recall@K
- B. NDCG@K
- C. Precision@K
- D. Mean Average Precision @K (MAP)

---

## 7. What does "zero-shot learning" mean in the context of large language models?

- A. Training a model with zero labelled examples
- B. A pretrained model performs a new task without further training
- C. Training only the last layer of a neural network
- D. A model trained on zero categories of data

---

## 8. What pretraining objective is used by GPT-family models?

- A. Masked Language Modeling
- B. Next-token prediction
- C. Translation pair supervision
- D. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback

---

## 9. Which deployment architecture is best when you have a large catalog and need both freshness and scalability?

- A. Batch architecture
- B. Real-time architecture
- C. 2-step (hybrid) architecture
- D. Single-server architecture

---

## 10. Which diversity metric measures how surprising recommendations are to a specific user, by comparing them to items the user has already interacted with?

- A. Coverage
- B. Novelty
- C. Serendipity
- D. Diversity

---

## 11. What is the main purpose of the Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) loss?

- A. To predict the absolute rating value for each item
- B. To minimise the squared error between predicted and true ratings
- C. To ensure observed items rank higher than unobserved items for each user
- D. To regularise the model parameters

---

## 12. Which of the following is an example of implicit feedback?

- A. A 5-star movie rating
- B. A written product review
- C. A click on a product page
- D. A satisfaction survey response

---

## 13. Which architecture is BERT based on?

- A. Encoder-only Transformer
- B. Decoder-only Transformer
- C. Encoder-decoder Transformer
- D. Recurrent Neural Network

---

## 14. Why is LayerNorm preferred over BatchNorm in Transformers?

- A. It is faster to compute
- B. It does not depend on batch size, making it stable for variable-length sequences
- C. It produces better gradients during backpropagation
- D. It works better on image data

---

## 15. What is the primary purpose of negative sampling when training a recommender on implicit feedback?

- A. To balance the rating distribution
- B. To generate negative training examples from unobserved user-item pairs
- C. To reduce overfitting
- D. To compute the loss function

---

## 16. Which online metric measures user engagement by computing the ratio of clicks to total visits?

- A. Conversion rate
- B. Click-Through Rate (CTR)
- C. Lifetime Value (LTV)
- D. Monthly Active Users (MAU)

---

## 17. What is the purpose of an A/B test in production recommender systems?

- A. To compare model performance on a held-out offline dataset
- B. To compute offline ranking metrics
- C. To measure live performance of two model variants by randomising traffic
- D. To optimise hyperparameters

---

## 18. Why do Transformers use multi-head attention instead of a single attention head?

- A. It is significantly faster to compute
- B. It allows the model to attend to different representation subspaces simultaneously
- C. It reduces the number of parameters
- D. It produces calibrated output probabilities

---

## 19. Which tokenization method is the modern default in large language models?

- A. Word-level tokenization
- B. Character-level tokenization
- C. Subword tokenization (e.g. BPE, SentencePiece)
- D. Sentence-level tokenization

---
tags: [practice-mock, answer-key, Chatbots]
course: Chatbots
mock: 2
created: 2026-04-26
---
# Chatbots — Mock 2 (answer key + worked explanations)

19 MCQ. Source: generated practice — not from the prof. Designed to mirror Mock 1's style and difficulty while testing **different specific facts**.
Questions: [[mock-2-questions]]

## Score sheet

| Q | Answer | Topic | Lecture | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **B** | Positional encoding role | 13 | 1 |
| 2 | **C** | RMSE penalises outliers | 04 | 2 |
| 3 | **B** | `[CLS]` token in BERT | 14 | 2 |
| 4 | **C** | Chronological split for fashion | 02 | 1 |
| 5 | **B** | Softmax → probability distribution | 13 | 2 |
| 6 | **C** | Precision@K = relevant in top-K / K | 04 | 1 |
| 7 | **B** | Zero-shot = pretrained model on new task w/o retraining | 14 | 2 |
| 8 | **B** | GPT pretraining = next-token prediction | 14 | 1 |
| 9 | **C** | 2-step / hybrid for large catalog | 04 | 2 |
| 10 | **C** | Serendipity = surprising vs user history | 04 | 2 |
| 11 | **C** | BPR ranks observed > unobserved | 09 | 2 |
| 12 | **C** | Click = implicit feedback | 02 | 1 |
| 13 | **A** | BERT = encoder-only Transformer | 14 | 1 |
| 14 | **B** | LayerNorm batch-independent → variable-length OK | 13 | 2 |
| 15 | **B** | Negative sampling = generate negatives from unobserved | 02, 09 | 1 |
| 16 | **B** | CTR = clicks / visits | 04 | 1 |
| 17 | **C** | A/B test = randomise live traffic, compare variants | 04 | 2 |
| 18 | **B** | Multi-head = different representation subspaces in parallel | 13 | 2 |
| 19 | **C** | Subword tokenization (BPE / SentencePiece) is modern default | 13 | 1 |

## Topic distribution (mirroring Mock 1)

| Cluster | Mock 1 | Mock 2 | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transformer & attention | 5 | **5** (Q1, Q5, Q14, Q18, Q19) | Same count, different facts |
| BERT, GPT, transfer learning | 4 | **4** (Q3, Q7, Q8, Q13) | Same count, different facts |
| Reco metrics & evaluation | 4 | **6** (Q2, Q6, Q9, Q10, Q16, Q17) | Slightly more — wider net |
| Reco fundamentals | 4 | **3** (Q4, Q12, Q15) | Slightly less |
| Classical algorithms | 2 | **1** (Q11 BPR) | ALS not directly tested this round |

## Worked answers

### Q1 — Positional encoding — B

Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** — it has no notion of token order. Positional encoding (sinusoidal in the original Transformer; RoPE in modern LLMs) is **added to the token embeddings before the first transformer block** to inject order.

- A is what attention computes; positional encoding is *input* to it.
- C is residual connections.
- D is LayerNorm.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/positional-encoding]]

### Q2 — RMSE — C

RMSE = $\sqrt{\frac{1}{|K|}\sum (\hat r - r)^2}$. Squaring the errors **penalises larger errors more than smaller ones** (a 5-vs-1 mistake is much worse than a 5-vs-4 mistake). MAE uses the absolute value and weights all errors linearly.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-rating-metrics]]

### Q3 — `[CLS]` token — B

`[CLS]` is the **first token of every BERT sentence**, and its hidden state is used for **classification tasks**. The contrast: `[SEP]` is the separator (D); `[MASK]` is for MLM training (C).

- A — there's no end-of-sequence convention here; BERT is encoder-only.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/bert-model]]

### Q4 — Chronological split — C

Required when **user preferences drift over time**. The lecture gives fashion as the canonical example. Random and stratified splits don't respect time, so the model can train on data from "the future" relative to the test set.

- A → user-level holdout, not chronological.
- B is unrelated.
- D would describe a fully-observed matrix, irrelevant.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-data-splits]]

### Q5 — Softmax in self-attention — B

Softmax converts the scaled dot products $QK^T / \sqrt{d_k}$ into a **probability distribution over attention scores**. Then those probabilities weight the value vectors.

- A is what comes *after* softmax (the weighted sum).
- C and D are unrelated.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/self-attention]]

### Q6 — Precision@K — C

**Precision@K** = (relevant items in top-K) / K — the fraction of recommendations that were correct.

- A is **Recall** = (relevant in top-K) / (total relevant).
- B (NDCG) accounts for rank position.
- D (MAP) averages precision across users.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-ranking-metrics]]

### Q7 — Zero-shot learning — B

Zero-shot = the **pretrained model performs a new task with no further training**, often using only a prompt or a textual description of the task. Made famous by GPT-3.

- A confuses it with self-supervised learning.
- C is feature-extraction transfer learning.
- D is a misunderstanding.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/zero-shot-learning]]

### Q8 — GPT pretraining objective — B

GPT pretrains on **next-token prediction** with causal masking — predict $x_{t+1}$ given $x_1, \ldots, x_t$. Cross-entropy loss over the vocabulary.

- A is BERT's objective (MLM).
- C describes older supervised pretraining.
- D is **post-training** alignment, not pretraining.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/next-token-prediction]]

### Q9 — 2-step architecture — C

The **2-step / hybrid** architecture: batch generates a high-recall **candidate set** offline (e.g. top 100-1000 items per user); a real-time reranker scores the candidates with fresh features. **The industry default for large catalogs.**

- A (batch) is cheap but stale and doesn't react to live signals.
- B (real-time) is fresh but doesn't scale to large catalogs.
- D is a made-up term.

Note: this question disambiguates the slightly-confusing Q9 from Mock 1.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-architectures]]

### Q10 — Serendipity — C

**Serendipity** measures how *surprising* recommendations are to a specific user, by comparing them to items the user has already interacted with. Lecture 04 verbatim.

- A (coverage) = fraction of catalog recommended.
- B (novelty) = how rare items are across all users.
- D (diversity) = how different items in the *list* are from each other.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-diversity-metrics]]

### Q11 — BPR purpose — C

BPR is a **pairwise ranking loss**. For each user, sample observed item $i$ and unobserved item $j$; train so that $\hat r_{ui} > \hat r_{uj}$. The loss only requires correct *ordering*, never absolute scores.

- A and B describe pointwise (rating-prediction) losses — exactly what BPR avoids.
- D is what regularisation does, not what the loss does.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss]]

### Q12 — Implicit feedback — C

A **click** is implicit (behavior, not stated preference). Ratings (A), reviews (B), and surveys (D) are all explicit feedback.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/explicit-vs-implicit-feedback]]

### Q13 — BERT architecture — A

BERT = **Encoder-only Transformer**. GPT is decoder-only; T5/BART are encoder-decoder.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/bert-model]]

### Q14 — LayerNorm vs BatchNorm — B

**LayerNorm normalises across the feature dim within a single token** — independent of batch size. BatchNorm requires meaningful batch statistics, which fail for variable-length sequences and inference at batch=1.

- A is partially true but not the *primary* reason.
- C and D are wrong.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/layer-normalization]]

### Q15 — Negative sampling — B

Implicit feedback is **positive-only** (clicks observed, no "I disliked X" signal). To train a discriminative model, **sample unobserved (user, item) pairs as negatives**. Risk: missing positives sampled as negatives — addressed by [[../../../20-Concepts/bpr-loss|BPR]].

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/negative-sampling-reco]]

### Q16 — CTR — B

**Click-Through Rate** = clicks ÷ total visits. A measure of user engagement.

- A (Conversion rate) = conversions / visits.
- C (LTV) = total revenue per user across lifetime.
- D (MAU) = unique users in 30 days.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/reco-online-metrics]]

### Q17 — A/B test — C

A/B testing **randomises live traffic** into two arms (one model per arm) and compares production metrics. The only ground-truth way to evaluate whether a new recommender is actually better in production.

- A and B describe offline evaluation (a different thing).
- D describes hyperparameter tuning.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/ab-test]]

### Q18 — Multi-head attention motivation — B

Multi-head attention runs $h$ attentions in parallel, each with its own learned $Q, K, V$ projections, **letting the model attend to different representation subspaces simultaneously**. This is the canonical lecture answer.

- A — multi-head doesn't change the order of magnitude of compute.
- C — multi-head has roughly the same parameter count as single-head with the same total dim.
- D is unrelated.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/multi-head-attention]]

### Q19 — Tokenization — C

**Subword tokenization** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) is the modern default. Word-level explodes vocabulary; character-level produces very long sequences. Subword strikes the balance and handles unknown words by decomposing them.

→ **Concept:** [[../../../20-Concepts/tokenization]]

## How to use this mock

- Take it **closed-book on paper**, ~40 minutes.
- Mark yourself against this key.
- For each wrong answer, identify which **cluster cheat sheet** covers the topic and re-read it.
- Track your improvement vs Mock 1.

## Caveats

- Mock 2 is **generated practice** — not from the prof. The real exam may emphasise different specifics.
- Distribution mirrors Mock 1 but isn't identical — Mock 2 has slightly more metric questions and one fewer classical-algorithm question.
- If you ace both mocks, do a third pass on the **Q9 / Q16 ambiguities from Mock 1** since those are the questions most likely to trip you up under time pressure.

## Cross-references

- [[mock-1-questions]] · [[mock-1-answer-key]]
- [[../exam-blueprint]]
- [[../exam-style-guide]]
- [[../clusters/02-transformer-and-attention/cheat-sheet|Cluster 02 — Transformer & attention]] (highest priority)
- [[../clusters/04-reco-data-and-metrics/cheat-sheet|Cluster 04 — Reco data & metrics]]
- [[../clusters/09-bert-gpt-and-transfer-learning/cheat-sheet|Cluster 09 — BERT, GPT, transfer learning]]
